The US is not to blame for the Taliban, but the way that Americans were in charge of Afghanistan for 20 years definitely has something to do with the Taliban's ability — and they've gained some support from key parts of the population — to overthrow the Americans.

个体自决与集体自决的真正难题

— Yeah, I'm wondering if I could get your reaction to this sort of question, which is that in order to actually get agency and dignity accorded to each individual — that we are in fact one another's dignified equals — we need to have a very robust notion of self-governance. Rather than the idea that we need to have even like this idea of a strong, you know, minimal state — let's say we talk about the state being minimal in its scope and scale, but it has to be really strong. One of which is militarism, which is what you were just talking about. Like we tried to export supposedly free markets and democracy to Afghanistan, and instead what we did was created a counter-reaction, which is to have a society that doesn't accord dignity and respect to everyone.

Yeah. I mean, I think the really hard problem for classical liberals is the relationship between collective self-determination and individual self-determination. Liberals want to find a way to do both, to try to reconcile both at the same time, which is not an easy problem.

Whereas both the socialists and the imperialists are happy with just violating individual rights. The socialists do it to assert collective self-determination, and the imperialist militarists do it sort of not caring about collective self-determination. So the militarists violate both collective self-determination and individual self-determination in places like Afghanistan, where there's definitely not a regime of individual rights. And then the anti-liberal violent evil people like the Taliban sort of capture the mantra of collective self-determination even while they are also violating individual self-determination.

So it's like the militarists and the socialists, the violent revolutionaries, sort of agree on violating individual self-determination. They just disagree on collective self-determination. And that of course leads the Western actors to be very vulnerable to the charge that they're very hypocritical — they don't care about the freedom of the peoples that they're conquering.

— So do you think that the reason why liberals have had such a difficulty getting their position across is because they haven't figured out a way to resolve this tension between individual self-determination and collective self-determination? Would you put that as the silver bullet in some sense that needs to be solved?

Well, it's kind of a future research question. There are no silver bullets, of course. I mean, yes, it's a very hard problem to solve. You eventually try to get to some kind of liberal democracy in which the majority has a voice but they're also constrained on what they can do to individuals, and that constraint also has to be agreed on at some point by this consent of the governed. So it's not an easy problem to solve.

But I think it's more that just the hypocrisy of really not caring even about individual freedom. For example, at the time of the Versailles Conference 1919 after World War I, Wilson showed no interest whatsoever in this very pervasive problem of forced labor that had been happening in Africa, like most notoriously in the Congo under King Leopold. And so he could have sort of outflanked Lenin at that point with saying, "At least the West offers a regime which would be individual freedom of labor — consent to your own labor — whereas the colonizers were engaging in widespread forced labor." And Woodrow Wilson just didn't care about that. So he lost the battle on freedom before it even began. And that has something to do with Wilson being very much in the Alfred Marshall school, where you don't really care about the issue of consent and freedom — that's the moral issue.

— Well, it's the rule by experts, right?

古典自由主义的真正长处：擅长怀疑，而非蓝图

— So in this book, more than even in your other books I would say, you're sort of forced to deal with some rather deep philosophical issues having to do with what the nature of agency is, or the way you would put it, the nature of what it means to choose freely, what's involved in according dignity to each and every individual. There's some real fundamental puzzles that are involved in achieving this stuff. Like for example, what Popper calls the paradox of toleration — because, you know, do you have… that seems to be an issue that is being debated very vigorously right now across the world. Do you have any thoughts on those kinds of issues after doing all the research on this book?

You know, I think the key thing is that the classical liberals, both historically and still today, they're much better on being skeptical. Obvious violations of consent, obvious use of force and violence to achieve development — they're much better at being skeptical about that and making a huge contribution by challenging that view. They're better at that than they are at providing some kind of blueprint of what to do instead.

— Yeah.

And arguably it's sort of consistent with Adam Smith's concept of not relying on the experts. If the experts knew what to do, then maybe they have far more faith in putting them in charge. But beginning with Adam Smith up to the present day, the liberals just say, "Frankly, we don't know what to do. We don't know what the blueprint is. But you could at least end these more obvious forms of violence."

You don't have to invade other countries in the name of development like, in the modern period, Afghanistan, or today, Venezuela or Greenland. Just take away those most obvious forms of violence and compulsion, and that's already progress.

— Yeah, there's a low-hanging fruit bar, right? A friend of mine wrote actually a New York Times bestseller — we were college buddies together. His name is Matt Kibbe. He wrote a book called Don't Hurt People and Don't Take Their Stuff.

— Yeah, totally.

— So there is a kind of low-hanging bar that we need to do.

A lot of progress happens that way, right? Slavery is low-hanging fruit, but ending slavery is pretty important. Colonialism was sort of low-hanging fruit, but ending that kind of violence in the name of development was something that a lot of people wanted when that happened.

So you could argue that we economists, liberal experts, are a lot more useful doing that than we are trying to come up with our own plan for how to do everything.

— I very much like that aspect — when it starts in the beginning of the book with the contrast between Smith and Condorcet — that Smith says not only is he a critic of the effort to impose, but he has no desire to impose himself.

Yeah, that's right. There are very few economists that can meet that Adam Smith standard of being so self-denying.

废奴主义模型与"先停止伤害"

— I guess I wanted to ask you a little bit about that with respect to public policy in general. Maybe the abolitionist movement is the model in many ways — or the free trade movement with Cobden and Bright — which is that what you want to do is not so much impose, but get rid of the restrictions, get rid of the violations. How do you think about that in public policy in general?

Yeah, I mean, Frederick Douglass quoted the British abolitionist movement about slavery, just saying, "I don't have a plan for what's going to happen after the end of slavery. I don't need to have a plan in order to abolish slavery."

— Yeah.

Slavery is just an issue of "stop stealing" — was his quote from the British abolitionist. And so even after the Civil War, Douglass was very skeptical about philanthropic efforts to help the ex-slaves. He said, "Why do you have these kind of incompetent white experts coming from the North to the South trying to do good things? Why not put the people from the enslaved communities in charge of their own development?"

So I think that very much links into the modern debate that you don't have to have a comprehensive plan for what's going to happen after you end some form of violence or compulsion. You just end the violence. And that's the step forward.

— Yeah. That's a lesson, I think, that we might also take away from our more recent experience in Eastern and Central Europe — because there were all kinds of designs how to privatize, to privatize for what goal, to maximize revenue in some lens — that selling-off kind of idea — as opposed to simply creating conditions of freedom of entry and trade.

自由迁移、自由市场与现实政治的张力

So this comes back to another question I wanted to ask you with respect to: is the main sort of development model just simply the freedom to exchange goods and services and for people to vote with their feet across various areas, so that we end up by having individuals migrate to where they think is the best for them to live and whatnot? What do you think about that?

Well, of course I'm in favor of as much freedom of markets as possible domestically in every country, which seems like kind of the easiest way to achieve some kind of individual freedom in a mutually beneficial way.

Freedom of migration is kind of a harder thing. I would very much, certainly in general, be in favor of free migration. It seems like a lot of individuals choose a place where they want to live — that seems like another form of individual freedom. Of course, the problem has been in the recipient societies: what kind of consent do you allow for whether recipient societies receive the migrants or not?

— Yeah.

And most countries in the world do not have open borders — including countries inhabited by peoples that the advocates of immigration want to have freedom of migration in. The home countries of all the migrant groups, the home countries also not allowing freedom of migration into their own country. So there seems to be some big stumbling block that a lot of populations around the world don't want freedom of in-migration.

And what do economists do about that? I mean, you could morally oppose those immigration restrictions, but you still have to struggle with some idea of the consent of the governed in the recipient countries. It's not an easy matter to resolve. You can't just wish it away.

— I mean, it seems like it's become a major pressure point in democratic societies in the West at the moment, and the issue having to do with the democratic backsliding. I happen to be someone who's very favorable towards open borders and the moral aspect of that, because it's both the moral of the people who they should have the freedom to decide where they want to live their lives, but also the individuals here who should be freely able to contract with people that they want to interact with.

But I understand that there's very strong pressure points, as we see not only in the United States but also in the Nordic countries and other places where you've seen this kind of very strong reaction in societies, and therefore the rise of somewhat unsavory nationalism emerging.

But again, going back to your point about colonialism and postcolonialism, there does seem to always be a reaction-counter-reaction kind of idea. The fact that we decided to try to in the Middle East to export democracy at the point of a gun meant that sometimes people might not like democracy having a gun pointed at them, right? And so they end up by rejecting it and going the other way.

希望从何而来：自由总是先于经济学家

— So you've written this series of books in the field of development economics. You mentioned critics everywhere from Adam Smith in the old days to P.T. Bauer to Amartya Sen even raising issues. What would be your grounds for hope that the discipline itself will somewhat shift away from this notion of technical expertise to one in which — let's say we don't get rid of expertise, but we have expertise constrained by an emphasis on the moral imagination? Or do you think that that is just not going to happen, given what we decide as science and acceptable answers?

Yeah, I mean, what would be the grounds for hope when 100 years of economists arguing for this freedom as an end in itself has been unsuccessful in reforming the economics profession?

I mean, the good news is, increases in freedom did happen anyway. So economists sort of perpetually not really caring about freedom, and yet then, after the fact, having to sort of catch up with new forms of liberation, of increased freedom, have happened — through the rise of international trade, or through the end of colonialism, or the end of apartheid, or whatever.

So I think that's the good news, that's grounds for hope: that even if economists don't care enough about freedom, people do care about their own freedom.

— Yeah, they care about their own agency and dignity and self-determination.

So economists eventually have to kind of come around to that after it's already happened. But the faith in economists kind of leading the charge for freedom, I think, was not very well grounded.

— I mean, that last point that you make is an interesting one. It also goes back to Adam Smith and something that you captured there as well, in his examples like the butcher, the baker, and the brewer, because he didn't orchestrate that. Economists didn't orchestrate that. That was already human beings who did that, and then he's trying to understand it. There's a difference.

There's a great old book by David Colander called Why Economists Are Not as Important as Garbage Men. And he starts with a thought experiment: imagine if all the garbage men in New York City went on strike, and then compare how disruptive that would be for our life if all the economists decide to go on strike in New York. There — it wouldn't be that disruptive.

个人轨迹作为希望：从主流发展经济学家到自由的辩护者

And besides that aspect of the forces of hope, I think that your own career is an example of why young economists should have hope, right? Because as you pointed out, you were a traditional development economist, and then your position evolved as you confronted reality. It wasn't like you were a methodological critic from the beginning or an ideological critic from the beginning of the development exercise — but you've come to change your views on things over these years. Don't you see that as a path that is also very good reason for hope?

Uh, yeah, I'm not sure if I should be reason for hope or a cautionary tale and whatnot.

I'll immodestly say, when sort of the main thing I was doing was casting skepticism on whether aid really achieved material development or not, I think that I succeeded in getting some attention for that argument, convincing a few people. When I got more interested in shifting the debate onto the moral grounds of: is development actually based on the rights of the people being developed, on the rights of poor people? Have we forgotten about the rights of poor people?

— Yeah.

And should the aim of development be to promote those rights as well as promoting material development? I feel like it's been tremendously harder to make any progress on that debate. And I think — you know, if young people were acting purely in their own self-interest — I'd advise them to stick to talking about material GDP growth, and don't talk so much about freedom as an end in itself, because lots of people have failed to make progress on that before you.

— Yeah.

But still, there's always this kind of movie romanticism — that the most noble people are those, the heroes, who kind of fight against enormous odds, and maybe even to their inevitable doom, but still leave a heritage of fighting against enormous odds to future generations. That maybe a movie historical quest of the hero for freedom against enormous odds — I think maybe that could be appealing to some people as a way to go.

结语

— Well, I want to congratulate you on this book. I think it's one of the most important books published in the last several years, and I will do the best I can as a teacher to make sure that people are exposed to your positions. And I do think that individuals like yourself, your friend Lant Pritchett, other people, my colleague Michael Clemens — they are all doing tremendous work that leads us and gives us hope that we can learn from these careful studies ways to have a more humane and just society. So I want to thank you.

— Thank you, Pete. Appreciate it.

— Thank you for listening to the Hayek program podcast. To learn more about the research, scholars, and work of the Hayek program, visit hayek.mercatus.org. For more information about graduate student fellowship opportunities for students at Mason as well as at universities across the globe, please visit students.mercatus.org. We hope you recommend students to our programs or consider applying yourself.

item.description:
## 一、被忽视的真正"发展悖论"：暴力本身就是反证
最让我震撼的，是 Easterly 转述 Smith 的那个逻辑刀锋——**"如果你带给他们的真是好东西，为什么需要用暴力强迫他们接受？"** 这是一个被现代发展经济学几乎完全遗忘的"显示偏好"悖论。
经济学家擅长用 revealed preference 来分析消费者，却从不把它应用到被殖民者、被强制迁移的农民、被奴役的劳工身上。一旦我们把 Samuelson 的方法论严肃地外推到政治经济学，殖民者声称的"benevolence"就立刻在自己的逻辑下崩塌。**经济学最锋利的武器其实早就握在手里，只是从不对准自己的客户**。这是一种学科性的方法论失明。
## 二、跨领域连接：发展经济学是一种"医疗化的暴力"
听这段对话时，我突然想到一个意外的平行结构——**现代发展经济学和 19 世纪精神病学惊人地同构**。
在 Foucault 笔下的疯人院里，专家以"为你好"的名义剥夺患者的同意权，把强制约束重新命名为"治疗"；今天的发展话语里，专家以 GDP per capita 之名剥夺被援助国的同意权，把强制再分配重新命名为"援

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1427期】网红股HOOD大跌！微软OpenAI分家！聚焦联储会议！✨20260428#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、隐藏的"贝塔身份"：Robinhood 的真正商业模式

表面上看，Robinhood 是一家券商。但小陶给出的财报分析揭示了一个被市场长期忽略的事实：Robinhood 本质上不是券商，而是一只"市场情绪杠杆 ETF"——只不过是以股票形式存在。当加密涨 1%，HOOD 涨 2-3%；当美股回调，它先跌一步。这就解释了为什么管理层"无法对下季度营收给出强指引"——因为他们卖的不是金融产品，而是市场波动率本身。

更出人意料的是：投资 HOOD 在某种程度上等同于做多"散户的肾上腺素水平"。这与传统巴菲特式护城河逻辑完全相反——你买的不是稳定现金流，而是一种集体情绪的衍生品。所以当散户喊"100块以下就是捡钱"时，他们其实在为自己的情绪溢价买单。这是金融市场的一个递归讽刺：散户用情绪买入了一只把他们情绪证券化的股票。

二、跨领域连接：AI Agent 的"渠道战"=19世纪的铁路战争

小陶谈亚马逊 Quick vs 微软 Copilot 时点出了一个关键——软件开发成本下降了，但分发成本没有下降。这其实是一个被 AI 时代严重低估的洞察。

跨领域看，这与 19 世纪美国铁路兴起时极其相似：当时火车头技术（生产端）变得便宜，但轨道铺设权、车站位置、城镇分发节点才是真正的护城河。范德比尔特击败了无数技术更先进的对手，靠的不是更好的火车，而是已经存在的分发网络。

放到今天：Claude Code 让"造火车"变便宜了，但 Microsoft 拥有 Office 365 的 4 亿企业席位（=轨道），Amazon 拥有 AWS 上百万中小客户（=货运站）。真正的 AI 战争不是模型战争，而是"用户每天默认打开哪个图标"的战争。这也解释了为什么 OpenAI 拼命在做浏览器、做硬件——它知道自己有"火车"，但缺"轨道"。

三、最反直觉的观察：估值"先行"反而是基本面回归的死亡之吻

小陶谈星巴克时讲了"PE 先行→EPS 跟上"的两步逻辑，听起来很标准。但藏在里面有一个多数投资者没意识到的悖论：

PE 提前扩张到位，意味着市场已经把基本面回归的功劳预支完了。换句话说，CEO Brian Niccol 还没真正做出业绩，资本市场就已经把"他能做到"的奖金发给他了。这创造了一个隐蔽的陷阱——未来即使他做对了所有事，股价的上涨幅度也只能等于 EPS 增速本身，因为估值红利已经被透支。

更深一层的跨领域连接：这与诺贝尔奖经济学里的"理性预期假说"完全吻合——任何可预测的好消息，在它真正发生时都不再产生超额回报。所以投资星巴克这种"明牌反转股"的真正风险不是基本面失败，而是基本面成功但市场已经不再为成功买单。这也是为什么小陶从 UNH 切换到星巴克的两步框架——他在用"估值钟"识别这只表是几点了，而不只是看公司本身。

一个意外的延伸：可口可乐财报里"管理层有定价权 = 消费者依然坚挺"的反向推论同样精彩——定价权不是公司的护城河，而是消费者钱包健康度的体温计。当哪天可口可乐说"我们涨不动价了"，那才是真正的衰退信号——比任何 CPI 数据都早。

📝 转录文本

开场

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场分析员小陶，欢迎大家来到周二的更新。

今天这期内容主要就聊财报了： - 盘后发布财报的 Robinhood，发布完之后出现的下跌，那基本面是不是有问题； - 另外三家公司——可口可乐、星巴克，还有 Visa，看到当下消费者的健康状况还有公司的一个基本面； - 另外我们聊一下周三盘后发布财报的两家大科技：微软和亚马逊，分别是我持有的两家公司呢，也是排名持仓仓位是排名第二和第三的，聊一下基本面。因为在周二的时候，其实亚马逊有一个产品发布会，挺值得去讲的，然后周二也有 OpenAI 和微软之间这个协议的一个更新，那对于微软来说到底意味着什么。

好嘛，这些东西聊完之后，最后我们是因为周三还有美联储的会议，所以最后的话我稍微花一些时间和大家聊一下周三的美联储会，我们应该抱着怎样的一个期待。

OK 那我就废话不多说了，直接开始。在开始之前，拜托大家记得留言、点赞、加转发，那么可以让更多的人看到我们这期内容。

一、周二市场快速回顾

来先来看一下，快速的回顾一下周二资产价格的走势。美元美家市场的波动并不大，那在周二我们算是真正意义上吧，看到像样的一些回调，稍微像样的一些回调啊。

  • 标普 500 下跌 0.5；
  • 纳指下跌了 0.9；
  • 道指期下跌的幅度并不大；
  • 黄金出现了下跌；
  • 原油上涨；
  • 罗素下跌了 1 点哦，算是真正意义上稍微有点像样的下跌和调整。

大科技还是表现出了相对的强势，下跌 0.3%，等权指数下跌 0.5%。那周二主要美股的下跌、标普的下跌和纳指的下跌，是来自于芯片板块的回调，下跌了 3.7%。

关于大科技这边，前期大科技股 YTD 到现在为止，走势上涨了 1.8%，等权指数上涨 4.8%，还是正在慢慢的拉近大科技和等权指数之行的这个回报差。

二、Robinhood 财报分析

数据层面：Double Miss

先来看一下 Robinhood 的财报，先从数据去出发吧。营收和盈利： - 营收低于市场 6.5%； - 盈利 EPS 增速 EPS 体量低于市场 10.5%。

从他们营收的这个分布去看，其实无论是 option 期权、美股，还是 cryptocurrency 加密货币这一块，基本上这三类的营收都低于市场的预期。所以这一份财报是比较标准的一个 double miss。

券商类公司的特性：市场的 Beta

那像券商这一类的公司，它有一个特性就是随着市场去走，它基本上就只要美股走的好，然后只要加密货币市场走的好，那它的财报自然就会好。但是你会发现大多数的时候，只要加密货币涨或者美股涨，它的股价也会跟着上涨，所以它是一个随着市场波动而波动的一个个股。某种程度也可以理解为它是整个美股或者加密市场的一个 Beta。那比如说加密货币涨个 1%，那 Robinhood 可能就涨个 2%、3%。

所以很多时候即使在就当业务、当你本身业务和交易的体量或者和资产价格这个走势和波动非常相关的时候，那自然，很多时候在一个季度之前，你没办法对于下一个季度的营收或者盈利具有一个非常强的一个指引——说白了就是你是看市场吃饭的。

投资者真正应该关注的：护城河有没有变

那从一个投资者的一个角度，我们更加应该关注的是，Robinhood 就原来投资 Robinhood 这家公司，是因为公司在产品研发、产品推进这一块的速度非常的快，所以这一个护城河、这个基本面有没有改变。

从财报会议里面去找答案，那找到的这个答案是：这个基本面、他们这个护城河并没有改变。我去听了他们的财报会议，听完之后有两个重点嘛：

第一个重点：因为第一季度确实受到了战争的这个影响，然后 4 月份——因为 4 月份马上就要结束了嘛——他们所看到的 4 月份，是今年到现在为止交易体量最高的一个月，和历史上去比的话，所有的和历史上去比的话是第二高的这个月。所以马上将要结束的这个 4 月份，Robinhood 他们现在所看到这个数据是历史第二大成交量。对吧，所以从这个角度去看，第二季度的财报应该不会太糟糕。这是第一点。

第二点就是川普账户：那川普账户对于 Robinhood 短期之内，短中期、短中长期都不会有太大的影响。在一个超长期，十年、十五年之后，对于他是一个非常明显基本面的一个利好。因为当这一部分现在的新生儿，那么当他们以后成为一个交易员、成为一个投资者之后，Robinhood 是有可能成为他们首选的券商。所以等于是现在花一部分的钱去购买未来的这一部分客户。

除了这两点以外，产品上的更新和推出，然后地域上的这个 expansion，加拿大、新加坡都在如期进行。所以基本面并没有变糟糕，当然也没有变得非常的好。所以还是和以前一样，基本面并没有出现改变。

估值分析：PEG 与 Forward PE

那在这种情况下，短期之内要去找进场点或者离场点的话，可能就需要依赖于估值或者基本面或者技术面。如果能够基本面、估值和技术面找到共振的话，那这时候去加仓就会觉得非常的舒服。

首先是估值这一块：PEG ratio——Robinhood 未来三年营收的增速百分之，现在市场预期啊，我这边营收 16%、20%，然后 14% EPS 增速稍微慢一点。如果我拿他们现在的一个市值去除以他们 op income，或者拿 EV 除以他们 op income 的话，差不多这个 PEG ratio 增长率是在 2 倍。PEG ratio 在两倍的话并不低。

看一下 Forward PE：在去年的 6 月份、9 月份的时候，Robinhood Forward PE 是多少？70 多倍。那么当时市场说，我记得当时市场很多散户这个观点："100 块钱以下的 Robinhood 就是捡钱"。现在你会发现 80 多块钱，然后发布完财报之后又跌到、又跌回到 70 多块钱，前段时间也是已经跌到 70 以下了嘛。你还会就觉得 100 块钱以下是捡钱，那些人现在应该会觉得是非常非常便宜了，对吧。100 块钱以下都是非常的便宜的话，那现在 80、80 多、70 多的话一定是更加便宜了。

我觉得 Robinhood 是一个比较好的例子，就是任何的一家公司，它的估值没办法——就大多数的情况下——很少有公司它的一个估值可以保持在高位非常长的一段时间。除非市场的一个共识就是 30 倍、40 倍。那比如说一家公司估值在 70 倍、80 倍、在 100 倍以上的话，我们知道长期来说——当然有个例——但是大多数的公司来说，长期它是没办法保持非常高的这个估值的。所以很多时候基本面是一方面，估值是另外一方面，好公司是好公司，但是好公司不代表它一定现在是一个非常好的一个价格啊。

估值区间与分批加仓策略

所以回到估值这一块，比如说去年最高的时候是 70 多倍，那这段时间的一个下跌呃，现在已经回到——就截止到周二的收盘是 35.3 倍。那如果盘后数我现在看盘后出现下跌了 10%，跌完 10% 之后的话，估值差不多是在 32 倍。32 倍就是我第一次开仓的一个价格。

然后大家如果有仔细看的话，我这边画了一个红色的一个区间。这个红色的区间差不多是过去两年多的一个正负、一个正幅比较合理一个区间吧。那么上区间差不多就是在 43 倍，下区间的话就是在 25.7 倍。所以 32 倍可以理解为是这个区间的一个相对比较偏下面的这个位置。那如果再下面的一个位置，就是像今年 3 月份就在上个月，上个月 3 月份所回调到的一个低位，差不多就是在 25.7 倍左右。再往下去看的话，在 2024、25 年的 3 月份、4 月份的时候，当时最低的时候是 23 倍。

所以从我个人的这个角度去看，你想我第一个开仓的一个点位是在 32 倍，我接下去是准备加仓的。如果它继续往下跌的话，我记得在 3 月份下跌的时候，我也说过说，这个位置可能还没有那么的保守。如果在能够跌到 60 至 65 之间的话，我会愿意选择继续加仓，那 60 到 65 差不多应该是在一个下区间 25.7 倍左右。

所以大家比如说如果像我一样的话，那你完全可以把自己的仓位分为三份： - 第一份：我第一次加仓的一个点位 32 倍的这个估值； - 第二份：你完全可以把它设在一个估值的一个下区间 25.7 倍，如果跌到那个位置的话，那我选择进行加仓； - 第三份：你完全可以设到 2025 年相对的一个低位，23 倍以下可能 25 倍，要就 22.5 倍至 23 倍之间。

这样的话，你分三次进行之后，你能确保你最后一次加仓的一个时间点是在一个估值相对非常低的一个位置。这样的话，我们知道——当然这个前提一定是建立在基本面没有变的情况下的——这样的话就基本面没有变的情况下，我们还是看我这家公司的一个基本面。但是从估值这个角度去看，现在就变得非常的有吸引力。

技术面：RSI 与价格背离

然后另外一个角度——那这是估值的角度——另外一个角度就是技术面的这个角度。

那我这边给举了一个例子，比如说像今年的 3 月份，2 月份到 3 月份的时候，虽然 2 月份到 3 月份不是绝对一个低点，但是这边给大家一个参考，就是价格继续往下跌，然后 RSI 这边是出现了一个价格和 RSI 之间的这个背离。在 2 月份到 3 月份这段时间是出现了两次背离，那么就是价格连续剧破了——先破新低，然后接下去又破了两次新低，然后 RSI 的话，第三次破新低，第二次破新低和第三次破新低的时候，RSI 都没有破新低。这就是非常标准的 RSI 和价格之间的一个背离。如果出现这一个背离的话，那第三次背离或者第二次背离的时候，会是一个可以参考的进场点。

那在价格这一块，我也做一个推演的话，如果接下去价格往下跌——像现在看盘后，如果往下跌、往下跌完之后出现一个反弹之后，再往下跌，再往下跌的时候，如果 RSI 这一块第一次往下跌，可能 RSI 就看到了一个超卖了，RSI 看到超卖之后出现反弹，然后 RSI 再往下跌——随着价格的下跌，RSI 再往下跌嘛，如果这次再往下跌的时候 RSI 和价格之间出现一个背离的话，那第二次的背离就第二次的破新低或者第三次的破新低，可以成为一个加仓点。

那如果说这一个破新低成为技术面上的一个加仓点的一个价格，正好再回到前面 Forward PE 又吻合起来的话——比如 Forward PE 跌到了 25.7 倍或者说 22.5 倍至 23 倍之间的话——那这时候就出现了一个技术面、估值，包括基本面上的一个共振，这时候我们选择进场的话就会觉得非常的舒适。

三、微软与 OpenAI 的"分家"

好，然后我们来聊一下微软和 OpenAI 之间的这个协议。

其实，如果大家有追踪我这个频道的话，我在上一次聊微软财报的时候，我就有聊过我说：微软和 OpenAI 之间的这个分家，对于微软来说是正确的决定。微软现在的一个方向其实就是往亚马逊的这个方向去靠，就是他们并不去赌到底最后哪一家大模型可以走出来，而是 model agnostic——每一家大模型你们都可以来到我自己的云平台上面。

一旦我们有了这样的一个背景之后，再来看在周二早上的那条新闻之后，就会非常的理解、就会非常就可以去理解它背后的这个行业，就是分家就分得更远了。那就是彼此之间所谓 OpenAI 就是说我以后我 OpenAI 的这个技术，我可以放在其他平台上。然后就在周二的一个下午，就宣布了 OpenAI 和亚马逊之间更加深度的这个合作。

其实对于亚马逊、对于微软来说是一件好事。因为微软首先营收上它不再要和 OpenAI 去分成了，所以对于微软来说，短期之内，微软和 OpenAI 之间的这个订单，它的一个盈利毛利、它的一个利润率应该会上升，因为微软不需要再分钱给 OpenAI。从一个长期的角度，对于微软的影响是以后 OpenAI 的这个订单有可能会分派到其他的一些云厂商手里。

所以周二早上的这条新闻其实就是过去这一段时间，微软和 OpenAI 之间分家的这个延续的一个过程。我并不认为对于微软的这个基本面有非常大的一些影响。那我知道在周二盘前的时候，一度微软因为这条消息出现了 5% 的这个下跌，然后盘中的话是基本上全部收复掉了。

这说明了什么问题？这说明了最近这段时间整个市场对于软件板块包括微软到底有多么的敏感，只要是有任何的消息，市场的一个解读马上就是负面的。其实我相信大多数的这些资金可能都是一些量化或者是一些散户，因为你但凡对于微软基本面有些了解、他们的新闻有些了解的话，并不会对于这条消息或者这两者之间这个消息感到任何的意外。

四、亚马逊新品 Quick 与 AI Agent 之争

然后讲一下亚马逊。我不会花太多的时间，亚马逊在周二的下午推出了一些新产品，然后其中的一个产品叫做 Quick。

那这个 Quick，它其实是停留在 desktop——就是在台式机上面的一个 AI agent，它是在 desktop 上面的一个 APP。那这个 AI agent 或者这个智能体，其实就是建立在电脑界面上的一个智能体，就所有 APP 上层的一个智能体，它通过这个智能体去控制下面所有的 APP，然后去完成我们作为一个使用者所下的指令。

那它的一个竞品，这个 Quick 的一个竞品是谁？它就是微软下面的一个 Copilot，或者 Anthropic 下面的一个 Claude Code，或者说 ChatGPT。

我在今天发的这条推里面，我说当我看到这条消息的时候，我在吃饭的时候，我在听他们的产品发布会，然后我听到他们所宣布的这个产品之后，我马上想到的是：我说有没有可能亚马逊成为微软最大的这个竞争者，对吧。

但是我后来思考了一下，我想了一下，并不会。为什么并不会？是因为亚马逊和微软来说，亚马逊去和微软做对比的话，它缺乏的是微软的这个分发；和大模型去做对比的话，它缺乏的是大模型的这个网络效应。

什么意思？就是我们试想一下什么样的公司，他们会去选择——如果我要去 AI agent、我要用 AI 智能体的话，什么样的公司会去选择亚马逊的 Quick，就是亚马逊的 Quick 能够在什么种什么样的情况下能够让人们知道并且愿意去使用？

对于 AI 原生态的公司，他们会更加愿意去尝试这些大模型，他们所用的、他们所造的这些 AI agent，比如说无论是 Claude 的也好、GPT 也好，就他们是有这个意愿去尝试的。对于现有的些大公司来说，他们可能更加愿意的还是微软通过他们的分派，然后把这些产品递到他们手机、递到这些公司手里，更多的是一个被动的接受。

然后我和 Claude 验证了一下，那基本上 Claude 是同意了我的这个想法、就验证了我的这个想法：Quick 的一个获客路径是异常的艰难。那首先用户得听过 Quick 这个东西，然后用户得去试一下。那最重要的两点是： 1. 用户得信任亚马逊，允许亚马逊的这个智能体去读取自己的邮件、日历、文件，包括 Slack 的消息等等； 2. 用户得改变现有的这个习惯。

所谓现有的一个习惯，就是这些用户他不再下一次先打开 ChatGPT 或者说 Copilot，或者说 Claude 都是一样的，就是他就现在因为你 Claude 或者 GPT 已经有很大的一个用户基础在那边，已经形成了自己的这个网络效应，或者说像微软他们自己推出的一个 Copilot。

那么从亚马逊的这个角度，你要去打破用户的这个习惯，其实没有那么简单，因为你没有这个分派的渠道。但是也不是说完全没有分派的渠道，为什么？是因为，首先，亚马逊下面有几百万的中小型客户，那几百万的中小型客户，现在亚马逊在推出 Bedrock，基本上亚马逊所有 AI 的这个应用的落地全部是在 Bedrock 里面。所以他们下面的中小型客户，因为现在本身和亚马逊之间就有这个业务在那边，你把亚马逊看作是一个 enabling company，就是它是帮助中小型客户更好的去实现他们的盈利或者去增效。

也就是说，亚马逊本身它其实通过 AWS 这个服务也是 AI 的一个落地的一个表现之一。那随着 AI 的这个落地，随着你通过 AI 的一些工具可以帮助你的客户去增效降本增效，同时能够去增加营收的话，那在这时候顺便去推出你的 Quick，这是有可能会使得一部分的中小型客户愿意去接受亚马逊所推出的这个 Quick、愿意去使用吧。

所以最终我觉得在工作流程端的这一块，AI agent 应该不会是一家独大的，也不会是亚马逊或者说微软，或者说 OpenAI 或者说 Claude，我并不会认为是一家独大。

跨行业类比：Fintech 的"钱包入口"

然后另外一点，我们可以把它理解为是，比如说 Fintech 这个行业里面，有些公司可能是靠 cryptocurrency 先进来。最终的话，你想象任何一家金融的初创公司，它都是要掌握什么？都是要掌握用户的钱包，仅此而已。

然后在这——但是掌握用户的钱包的方式不一样，切入点不一样： - 银行最简单的普通的银行，它就是通过 debit、通过 credit，通过这个方式去进入你的钱包、掌握你的钱包； - 而现在的券商，你会发到券商也在做钱包这一块，那券商的一个切入点就是通过投资； - 然后 Bybit 上的切入点通过什么？就是通过加密货币这一块的交易。

所以这些公司本身它都是通过用户的一个痛点去切入。切入完之后，在这个基础上，用户习惯了——但他这个服务用你这个券商啊、用你这个 APP 习惯了之后——然后你在你现有的业务上再 expand，然后试图去增加你整个的这个 total addressable market、增加你整整个的一个 TAM。

那这一款 AI agent 也是一样的一个道理。因为每一家公司它都有每一家公司不同自己分销的这个渠道或 distribution channel，他一定是通过现有的业务去解决了用户一部分的痛点，然后在这个基础上再推出自己的这个 AI agent。

的确，现在有了 Claude Code 之后，软件的一个开发成本下降了很多。但是 Claude Code 没有办法改变软件 distribution——你在分配的时候，你所需要花的这个成本，或者你现有的这个分派的一个渠道，就是软件公司一部分的护城河。

五、星巴克：估值回归 vs EPS 回归

OK 然后我们来聊一下星巴克。

那星巴克发布完财报之后，财报我不多聊了，因为他们数据本身——我看他们 EPS 的一个体量比市场预期好了要 15% 以上的、17%——非常的好。盘后我看上涨了 5%。

我主要想聊一下，因为星巴克我记得上一季度财报我们有聊过——星巴克这家公司是一个非常典型的市场，因为我换了 CEO 之后，然后 PE 这边是 PE Forward PE 换了 CEO 之后，股价就出现了反弹。股价出现反弹，大部分是来自于 PE 的这个扩张。你看一下从最低 20 倍都不到，现在已经来到了 36 倍，算上盘后 5% 的这个上涨，现在可能来到了多少？37 倍、38 倍了。

所以那 37 倍、38 倍，其实从过去 7 年这个角度去看嘛，其实在一个相当高的一个位置。那为什么估值可以涨那么快？为什么？就是估值先走，市场其实就已经在定价。并且我认为现在市场已经非常充分的定价星巴克，它价值回归或者它的一个 EPS 增速的一个回归。

所以接下去我认为我们可以期待的是，我并不认为它的一个 PE 可以去到非常高的一个位置。我认为比较合理的——这种情况，当然，短期间内你没办法预估嘛，去到 40 倍甚至去到更高也是有可能。但是，你把时间线稍微放长一点，你把一年两年的这个时间线、这个眼光去看的话，我不认为 PE 可以长期保持在一个高位。从这个角度去看，你看比较合理的一个 PE 可能就是在 30 倍左右。

所以未来可能出现一种情况是 PE 往下降，但是 PE 往下降不代表它的股价就一定要往下降。那它可以出现一种情况是 PE 往下跌 10%，但是 EPS 的增速可以往上涨 20%。那 EPS 往上涨 20%、PE 往下跌 10%，最终的股价还是可以上涨 10%。那股价可以持续上涨。

也就是再说的简单一点，就是星巴克它 PE 的这个扩张已经结束了，这是第一步要完成。一般公司出现基本面反转的话，第一步一定是 PE 先行。PE 的这个扩张第一步已经完成了。接下去它的股价再要出现上涨的话，必须要依赖于 EPS 的一个回归，也就是真正基本面的这个回归。

所以第一步的一个估值回归已经结束了，然后第二步就是走 EPS 一个回归。有点类似于我投资 UNH 背后的一个逻辑，我和大家说过，我说 UNH 背后的一个逻辑：第一层的逻辑就是先看它估值回归，当它估值回归之后，然后第二步走的是 EPS 这个回归。我认为现在这段时间走的恰恰就是 PE 的这个回归。

六、Visa 财报：消费者的韧性

OK 接下去 Visa 的财报。

那 Visa 的财报主要看一下他们对于宏观的一些看法。Visa 他们的管理层认为，AI 现在的 AI 的落地，包括 agentic commerce 这一块的落地，他们期待是对于 GDP 的这个增速影响将会起到 0.8 个百分点至 1.5 个百分点的这个影响，所以将会增加对于消费的这个影响。

然后整个宏观环境在大中华地区的话，在过去这一个季度里面，他们是看到了有一定的一个改善程度。美国这边 payment volume 总共的交易的一个体量去看的话，同比增速 8%，其实是非常高的一个增速，然后反映了消费者的一个 resiliency 非常的强劲。最后，从必需品和非必需品消费这一块去看，还是表现出了相对的强势。然后对于相对 low spending 就消费比较低的这一部分客户人群，并没有看到任何放宽的信号。

所以截止到现在，我们看了 JPMorgan 的财报，我们看了美国银行的财报，我记得上一次我们看了一家房地产公司的一个财报——是 Rocky 是 Rocky Company 吧——我记得看了一下房地产公司的一个财报，然后看了现在 Visa 的一个财报。基本上我们可以得出一个结论：第一季度包括了 3 月份受到战争的这个影响，消费者的这个增速完全没有任何的放缓，就并没有受到任何战争的影响或者高油价的影响。

七、可口可乐：定价权与消费者健康

最后是可口可乐。

那可口可乐主要是看这一次战争对于化工原材料的这个价格的一个上涨，对于可口可乐的一个基本面有没有受到影响。那其实是没有非常大的一些影响，主要是因为可口可乐以前把他们的一个 bottling，就是装瓶的这一部分业务给分拆出去了。

那可口可乐现在只是单纯的是卖一个糖浆。卖糖浆的话，因为装瓶——因为所有的瓶基本上所有的塑料瓶子全部会受到化工品上涨率所导致的它的一个成本的一个上升——那可口可乐把这一部分业务给转化出去之后，所以这一次战争的一个影响、战争导致化工原材料价格的这个上涨，对于可口可乐的影响就几乎没有。

并且公司管理层这个期待，其实他们一季度这个 gross margin 卷是有一部分下降的，但是他们预计这是一次性的一个事件，管理层预计整个全年的话，他们的这个 margin 并不会出现下降，还是保持了他们这个 margin。也就是他们认为可口可乐——他们认为这是有一部分原材料或者成本的这个上升，他们也可以把这个成本转移到消费者身上。他们第一季度涨价涨了 4%，对吧。

对于这些必需领消费公司来说嘛，他们如果认为有绝对的这个定价权背后，也代表了消费者的一个消费水平是 resilience。消费者如果不坚挺的话，那即使是可口可乐也不一定有这个降价权。

所以总体来说，这一个季度到现在为止观察下来，基本上消费者还是非常的坚挺。即使是在高油价的这种情况下——当然这个高油价只维持了 3 月份 1 个月。

八、周三美联储会议预期

最后我们来聊一下周三的美联储会议。

那现在市场对于美联储会议的这个降息非常的有意思。你看，从现在起一直到 2027 年的 7 月份，将近一年——叫 15 个月左右吧——基本上市场预期在接下去的 15 个月里面是不会出现任何的降息，但是也不会加息。这个数字 -0.2 就是有 20% 的可能性会降息一次。你看到，到 2027 年的 7 月份，仍然只有 20% 的可能性会降息一次。也就是市场现在认为在未来的 15 个月的时间里边，美联储不会进行任何的加息或者降息。

我特地去看了一下 Nick 有没有释放一些信号。Nick 有，他写了一篇文章，他这篇文章主要讲的是看一下这一次的美联储会，他们会不会释放一个信号，是降息本身 off the table，就现在并不会考虑降息。

我认为是存在这种可能性的，就暂时来说没有必要去考虑这个降息，可能起码等到 9 月份、10 月份以后再看。

那势必明天因为有美联储的会议，然后盘后又有大科技的财报，所以从盘后去看，从明天的一个盘后去看或者周四开盘去看的话，市场波动一定会非常的大，到底往哪个方向去走，说实话我也并不知道。那对于大科技财报，我有没有信心？每一家大科技财报我都非常的有信心。

全球央行动态

然后另外这边我想聊一下的是，这边有在欧元区这一块，他们 4 月份因为——好像 4 月 30，他们也会有加息或者降息这个决议。现在市场预期是 11% 和 3%，那基本上也会保持利率不变了。日本央行这边 6 月 16 日要一个半月之后了——6 月 16 日，现在市场预期是有 66% 可能性会加息一次。所以日本央行在 6 月份是有可能进行加息的。

九、直播预告与 APP 工具介绍

那周三按照惯例，我是不会录视频的，但是周三也就是明天盘后，明天晚上美东时间 9 点半，我会在 APP 上面做一个直播。通常来说，直播都是只针对 VIP 的用户，但是这一次直播我和他们说全部公开，就是所有人都可以进来听，然后也可以进行提问。

所以大家记得，去——即使你不是 APP 的使用者的话、不是你 APP 的 VIP 的话——你也可以去通过画面里面的这个二维码去进行下载，或者无论是苹果商店、谷歌商店搜索 TOT 群都可以进行下载。所以记得，周三晚上美东时间 9 点半，我会为大家做直播。

第一次下载这个 APP 的话，我建议大家： - 那么可以去试一下贝塔计算器，它是免费的，把自己所有仓位放进去之后，可以了解一下当下承受的风险到底是多少； - 然后可以去试一下最新刚刚上线的这个 AI bot。这个 AI 机器人有点是类似于我的这个分身，那他们背后训练的数据是我所有的视频，然后我所有在 X 上的一些发言，然后我所有在 APP 里面的一些发言，所以基本上我让他们是复述我的一些观点，并不用强加于自己的一些理解。比如说大家好奇我对于任何一些标的一些看法，我在什么时候买入了这个标的、有没有这个标的、什么时候卖出，你问这个 APP 的话，他都会为大家进行解答； - 上个月开始还加入了研报的这个功能。前两份的研报讲的是 CALVE 和 NAVIS 两家公司的一个估值方法，最新上传的一份研报主要讲的是类似于陶氏化学这一类的公司，现在化工品原料价格上涨对于哪些板块会受到影响。

最后，这边从现在起至 5 月 14 日，APP 也在有一个限时的一个优惠，大家感兴趣的话可以去 APP 上看一下。

啊今天这期内容就到这边了，记得我们在明天也就是周三晚上美东时间 9 点半，我会在 APP 上做一个直播，是所有人都可以参与。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、隐藏的"贝塔身份"：Robinhood 的真正商业模式
表面上看，Robinhood 是一家券商。但小陶给出的财报分析揭示了一个被市场长期忽略的事实：**Robinhood 本质上不是券商，而是一只"市场情绪杠杆 ETF"**——只不过是以股票形式存在。当加密涨 1%，HOOD 涨 2-3%；当美股回调，它先跌一步。这就解释了为什么管理层"无法对下季度营收给出强指引"——因为他们卖的不是金融产品，而是**市场波动率本身**。
更出人意料的是：投资 HOOD 在某种程度上等同于做多"散户的肾上腺素水平"。这与传统巴菲特式护城河逻辑完全相反——你买的不是稳定现金流，而是一种**集体情绪的衍生品**。所以当散户喊"100块以下就是捡钱"时，他们其实在为自己的情绪溢价买单。这是金融市场的一个递归讽刺：**散户用情绪买入了一只把他们情绪证券化的股票**。
## 二、跨领域连接：AI Agent 的"渠道战"=19世纪的铁路战争
小陶谈亚马逊 Quick vs 微软 Copilot 时点出了一个关键——**软件开发成本下降了，但分发成本没有下降**。这其实是一个被 AI 时代严重

item.title: [Business of Sport] From Selecting Clubs to Signing Deals: Football’s Hidden Partnership | Chris Richards & Alan Redmond
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、那个出人意料的观察：Bayern Munich 的"拒绝"，本质上是一场身份套利

大多数人会把 Chris 离开拜仁理解为"年轻球员追求出场时间"——这是表层叙事。但真正反常的地方在于：他的"美国式天真"恰恰是他最稀缺的资产。

德国体系中的年轻球员被"拜仁=神圣"的符号系统深度规训，他们的选择被一种集体潜意识锁死——离开拜仁等于背叛某种秩序。Chris 没有被这套符号殖民过，所以他能做出一个在欧洲球员看来"不可思议"的决定：把品牌价值贴现成出场时间。

更深一层的洞察是：他在用美国式的"职业理性"对抗欧洲式的"图腾忠诚"，而这种文化盲区反而构成了他的认知优势。这和创业领域的"局外人优势"（Outsider Advantage）惊人一致——硅谷研究反复表明，颠覆性创新者往往是那些不知道行业规则有多神圣的人。Chris 的天真，是一种结构性的认知套利。

二、跨领域连接：Alan 的"精品代理模式" = 私人银行业的逻辑，而非大众零售业

Alan 反复强调"我们不要 800 个客户"——这听起来像一句营销话术，但如果你把它放进金融业的服务分层模型里看，会发现一个非常精妙的同构：

  • 大型代理公司 = 零售银行（规模驱动、低单价、高流转、客户即数据点）
  • Rok 这类精品代理 = Private Banking / Family Office（关系驱动、高单价、终身陪伴、客户即关系）

更隐蔽的是，他说的"我希望俱乐部多付钱"——这看起来违反代理人帮买方议价的常识，但其实暴露了一个深层机制：价格不是成本，价格是承诺装置（commitment device）。俱乐部花了 6500 万欧元买的球员，会被强制使用；花 200 万买的，会被搁置在替补席。

这和投资人圈子里的一句话是同构的："High valuation is a forcing function for board attention." 高估值不只是钱，它是注意力的强制机制。Alan 在做的，是把球员的薪资设计成俱乐部的沉没成本陷阱——一种行为经济学层面的"反损失厌恶"博弈。

三、被所有人忽略的真相：Chris 的"商业价值"打破了一个被默认了 30 年的足球营销假设

传统足球商业逻辑里，只有冠军球队的进攻球员才有大额商业代言——这是 Alan 自己说的"PSG、曼城、巴萨之外几乎没有"。

但 Chris 同时与 Crocs、Bank of America、Kellanova 合作。表层解释是"美国市场崛起"。这是不够的。

更深的洞察是：全球化的体育商业版图正在从"赛事中心制"转向"叙事中心制"。

  • 旧逻辑：你在哪个联赛、赢了什么 → 决定你的商业价值
  • 新逻辑：你代表哪个未被充分商业化的故事市场（story market） → 决定你的商业价值

Chris 之所以值钱，不是因为他在水晶宫拿了足总杯（虽然有帮助），而是因为他单独承载了一个尚未饱和的叙事赛道——"美国黑人 + 阿拉巴马州 + 全球足球 + 2026 本土世界杯"。这个叙事在五年前没有买家，五年后会被瓜分。他踩在了一个 18 个月的窗口期。

这与新消费品牌的"叙事经济学"完全同构：现代品牌不再为"最大市场"付费，它们为"最稀缺的叙事入口"付费。Chris 是足球世界的 alpha 版"地理叙事红利套利者"。

四、附赠一个隐藏模式：从拜仁到水晶宫，再到中场踢后腰——Chris 的整个职业路径，本质上是一个"反路径依赖"的连续决策链

每一次他都在拒绝"显赫但锁死"的选项，选择"不起眼但解锁更多自由度"的选项： - 拒绝马德里竞技（横向移动）→ 选择水晶宫（首发起步） - 拒绝联合柏林欧冠（短期诱惑）→ 留守水晶宫（长期累积） - 拒绝"中后卫专精"舒适区 → 进入中场拓展可塑性

这是经济学里的"实物期权"思维（Real Options Theory）：在不确定性高的环境里，最优策略不是最大化当下收益，而是最大化未来可选项的数量。Chris 在直觉层面运行着一套教科书级别的期权管理模型——而绝大多数球员的职业决策，是被身份焦虑和短期估值绑架的。

这个模式的意外之处在于：他的"低调"其实是高维度的进攻策略。

📝 转录文本

开场片段：决定离开拜仁与改变南伦敦

When I told Bayern Munich that I wanted to leave, they were like, "What do you mean you want to leave?" Like, "This is Bayern Munich. You don't always get a chance at a club like this." I think me being American and being a bit naive worked in my favor because ultimately if I wasn't playing, I wasn't happy.

I remember you saying to me, "Look, I might come through here, but realistically, they're giving me some minutes, but they're not giving me enough minutes to develop. I need to get out of here." A lot of people would have been like, "I'm at Bayern Munich." You weren't prepared to accept that — you wanted to play.

Craziest part about being in the changing room before the FA Cup final was that nobody was nervous. We knew that they would be a lot more confident because they had just beat us a few weeks before. So, we knew if we go in and do exactly what we had done all season long that we would get a result. This was our chance to change South London and change Crystal Palace forever.

That was the most beautiful part of it. You would look up in the stands and see grown men crying, families huddled together, hugging each other.

There's this cliche that pressure builds diamonds. And I think that this World Cup is a huge moment in the future of the sport in the US. We didn't just wake up one day and get here. It took years to get to this point. It's super exciting. This is a chance for this sport to really blow up back home. Business fascinating.

一、欢迎与开场：球员与经纪人的关系

Guys, it is so great to have you here. First off, thank you so much for joining us. We normally do like two-on-one. So, two on two. This feels much more even.

We've actually only done it once before, haven't we? With four people in. We had Patrice Evra with his —

Oh, yeah.

business partner. I don't think — and I was trying to think of other media even like coming into this — where the relationship between a player and an agent has properly been brought to life. Alan, what's it been like, I guess, with Rok when you're building that kind of agency?

二、Alan 谈精品代理模式：拒绝规模化，回归关系本质

Well, I think it's important for us from inception that we didn't mirror some of the trends that are taking place in the football agency space. So I think some of the football agents, some of the ones who've been around the longest, they have 800, 900,000 players. And that's absolutely not the model that we wanted to go down.

So we wanted to try to keep it exclusive. We wanted to keep a boutique nature to it. But the "boutique" word is — people throw it around, but the way it manifests for me is: you're working in a way that you've got time to spend with your clients. Because there's no point in going into a room with a deck and an amazing presentation and lots of verbosity if you're not going to have time to spend with the client after the ink is on the page.

And for me, when I look at the model that is prevalent in the market, I don't see how that works. And if it works for them, good luck to them, but it's not anything we want to pursue. We want to have the time to spend with — you sign a person who plays a sport.

And that's your start. You want to spend time with the person. If you don't want to spend time with the person, it's going to be a challenging relationship, even the contractual side of it. So for us, the quality of the person and the relationship you build with the person — that's the foundation stone to everything. And then obviously we try to send people who are incredible at their pursuit and we try to put a team around them to make sure they can reach the top of that pursuit.

三、识别青年才俊：命中率与"晚熟者优势"

Well, you said there about picking talent early. Of the picks, there's going to be ones that don't ever fulfill the potential that you thought they could. What is that hit rate like?

Um, I think our hit rate is quite good to be honest with you. There are always examples. I won't name a name, but there's a player very close to me from when he was age 15. He's a friend of yours, but at age 18, someone paid 12 million for him, a Premier League club, and he just had a very bad injury. And then while rehabbing for that, he had another very bad injury. And now he's still relatively young — he still has a chance to come back and have a career, but he's not having the career that he was almost certainly going to have.

So there's different factors. When we scout, the current level of that player who's 15 or 16 is relevant, but for us, you're looking to see if somebody is already overdeveloped or if they've got a physical advantage that's giving them that superiority in that moment. And for me, very often the kids we sign are the kind of scrawny ones who are not massively impacted at 15 — or they are in moments. But what we see is that when they grow into their bodies and the brain and the body are on the same wavelength, that's when the talent is going to really accelerate and jump off the page.

Do you remember when — was it Cleverley or Smalling — who was saying about actually the challenge in identifying talent early and they weren't the best?

Yeah. Chris Smalling, the former Man U player, he went as far as the very rare position where it was university or football contract, and it was: "I have my degree on one side. If I don't get picked up in this trial, it's not the end of the world." But he never went through the proper academy system. So that actually de-stressed him quite a bit because it didn't put his whole worth in succeeding in football.

四、Chris 的成长：阿拉巴马、晚熟与一次决定性的旅行

Well, Chris, you'll have seen that in growing up in the US. How many friends would you have had that were scouted in various sports because the US is quite unique with the college system. It's professionalized at a very early age.

Yeah, I mean, even myself, I was committed to going to uni at 18 actually, and then of course the Bayern thing came around. But my family always stressed education to myself because I was always a smaller kid until I was 17 years old. I grew like seven inches in one summer — six or seven inches.

If you would have come to me at 15, 16 years old, if I would have seen me at 15 or 16 — not going to say I was the hottest commodity. I was a smaller kid. But you see it in sports. You see kids who are on the top of the world at 15, 16 years old and then by the time they're 20 they've kind of burnt out. So I'm happy I went through that medium ugly stage at 15 or 16, because it showed me that I do still have a lot to work on. And maybe if I was at the top of my game at that age, then I wouldn't be as successful as I am right now.

Tell everyone about your story because it was a relatively unique pathway, very different because of the US into Europe.

So, I grew up in Alabama — sweet home Alabama. When people think of Alabama, you think of hillbillies and country and things like that, which is partly true, but we do have good sports going on there as well. At 16, I moved to Houston to play for my first academy. At 17, moved to my first professional academy with FC Dallas. I played basketball and football my whole life, but like I said, I was always a smaller kid.

I got to a point where I took a trip to Argentina and actually witnessed my first professional football match at 16, and I came home from that trip and I told my dad — who was an ex-basketball player — "You know, I'm just going to play football from now on." He says he wasn't devastated. I think he was. I saw it in his eyes. Yeah, still is. He doesn't want to admit it.

So I came back from that trip and told him, "I just want to commit to football." And he was like, "Okay, well, if you want to make this your life, you have to go 100%." So the next step is finding an academy or finding a higher level for you.

I went to try out for FC Dallas and didn't make the team. I was devastated. Then I got a call from a Houston, Texas number: "Hey, come play for our academy. You get to play FC Dallas twice in one year." So I went there, beat them twice in one year. And then a few months later, FC Dallas came back calling. Of course, developed a bit more physically. So I moved to Dallas the next year. And the day before my 18th birthday, I actually signed my first professional contract.

Three months later, I got a call from a German number asking me to come on trial for Bayern Munich. They signed me pretty much straight away. The rest is history. I'm here now. But I had no real insight on what professional football was until I was probably 17 years old.

五、第一份合同与那辆"绿色机器"

You said your first contract — might I be so blunt? It's very unish. What was the first pay for your first contract?

So, I'll say this. I went from making — or my parents gave me $50 a month for gas, food, everything like that, because that's all we could afford, and I have other siblings as well. Then my first payday — I got my signing bonus, which was 50K after taxes. I went straight down to the car dealership and bought a car.

I had a "green machine" — a 1989 Honda Accord that had a kit on the side that was dragging on the ground because I broke it driving over a railroad track and I couldn't afford to fix it. So the first day I went and got a new car. I remember seeing the 50k hit my bank account at 17 years old and thinking, "This is not real."

I do regret buying that car, because I moved to Germany 3 months later. But I made more money in one signing my name than my parents did in a year at that point. So it was amazing.

六、从拜仁的合作伙伴到登陆德国

Bayern Munich is one of the biggest clubs in Europe, right? So you go from missing out in a Dallas selection process to very quickly going to one of the biggest clubs in Europe. That is a massive jump. How did they know about you?

So, at the time, FC Dallas and Bayern Munich had a partnership, a club partnership, and they were sending players over to just train with them. The way that the MLS system works is that if you sign a homegrown contract — which means that you were in the academy for a year — then you can play for the first team. But because I'd only been in the academy for 7 months at that point, I couldn't play for the first team. So they sent me to Bayern on loan at first.

Then, like I said, after maybe 3 or 4 months, they came back to me: "We want to sign you on a permanent deal." It was a difficult decision for me because at the time I was thinking, "I want to go and play first team football. I don't want to continue to play under-19s, under-18 football in Germany." But I'm glad I was pushed to stay in Germany.

I was just thinking — you probably walk in there and suddenly you're with Lewandowski, Thomas Müller. Did you on your debut get an assist for Lewandowski?

I actually had that down here.

You got it?

Yeah. My full debut, I got an assist to Lewandowski. Honestly, being American, you don't quite understand the gravity of how big a club like Bayern Munich is. So when I got over there I was a bit naive to how everything worked, and I think it almost worked in my favor. I went in there first day, was a bit starstruck, but then I was like, "All right, cool. I'm here. I'm just going to give it 100% and whatever happens happens." That's one thing that everybody enjoyed about me — I didn't go in there and fear anybody. I'll just be myself.

七、不被光环裹挟：为什么 Chris 决定离开拜仁

I think that has stood to you throughout your career. You don't over-romanticize things. You have a great appreciation, and you know — winning the FA Cup, winning the Community Shield, and you progress, good chance, touchwood, of progressing to the final and hopefully winning the Conference League this year.

But sometimes players — let's use Arsenal as an example. You could have a young player at Arsenal, and when he's 15 or 16, he thinks he's playing for Arsenal, but he's at Arsenal's academy, which the same as any academy at a Premier League team — it's a boys' team with a somewhat loose affiliation to the first team. There's no guarantee you're going to step over the road to the first team and play any significant minutes.

But I think you've always had a great sense of: right, in order to progress, what do I need to do? You don't have that assumption that "I'm wearing the shirt, surely I'm gonna —" you know what I mean? Even at Bayern, you looked around — I remember you saying to me, "Look, I might come through here, but realistically, they're giving me some minutes, but they're not giving me enough minutes to develop. I need to get out of here." A lot of people would have been like, "I'm at Bayern Munich. All hail Bayern Munich." You weren't prepared to accept that — you wanted to play.

八、Alan 的代理哲学：让俱乐部高价投资，让球员被强制使用

Alan, from your perspective, if you're working with a player who you feel starts to have that approach, do you feel that's your task? Do you have a conversation?

We want to make sure a player is playing as much as possible. Occasionally a club will come to us and they'll express interest in a player, and we would always push it back and say, "Well, you've got three players in that position and one of them is, for example, the captain of the Dutch national team. So you're going to stick him on the bench?" And then they will say, "No, no, no, he's on his way to KSA this summer and this is going to happen."

You always need a pretty good rationale as to why they want to sign the player. Because the one thing for sure is: if you move a player to a team and the player doesn't play, in some sense it's your fault. Now, if the player doesn't perform well and isn't professional, that's by the by. As far as the player's concerned, justifiably to whatever degree you like, it is the agent's fault to some degree. So we always have to make sure that there is a strong rationale.

The other thing as well — sometimes a team will come to us and say, "What do you think the cheapest we can get this player is?" I don't really want that. I want them to pay through the nose whenever possible, because they're investing.

No, no, no. I mean to the other team — because if they are paying for the player a considerable amount, then they're invested in the player. If they get him two-for-one or in some kind of special deal, he's a cheap sub to have on the bench. Ultimately we don't want that. I want a club to invest in the player because then they have to commit to using him.

九、判断球员能否上场：研究、分析与教练话语权

Can I ask, when you're thinking about a next club for a player and you're trying to understand whether they will actually get playing time? What are the questions that you ask to actually uncover whether they will get game time?

In the initial stages, it tends to be the sporting director of the team, and then closer to the offer going in, you would tend to speak to the coach. Not always, but with some clubs the coach is very on top of the transfer policy, and other clubs the coach is not heavily involved. But for sure the coach picks the team every week, and you can have a situation where a player signs for a club because the sporting director loved him, but the coach doesn't pick him.

A lot of it is research and analysis before you even sit down to have the conversation. We have a player at the moment — a very good young player who's got an EU passport and he's got options all across Europe. He kind of chooses his club at the moment, and we are going through that process. I'm saying to clubs, "But you have the Dutch under-21 captain here. You've got a 25-year-old who starts every game for the Belgian national team. Where is this player going?" We do make them come back and explain how they're going to use the player.

And again, I like clubs to pay through the nose. Another thing is: clearly we like our players to be as well paid as possible in terms of salary. Part of that is yes, because we want them to be as financially secure as possible. But again, another part of that comes back to: the heavier the club is invested, the more chances they're going to use the player.

十、关于"球员-经纪人利益错位"的迷思

I always think that there's this misalignment between player and agent. You want players to move as much as possible because you obviously get a fee on the transfer. Is that wrong for me to assume there's a misalignment, or is that right?

Um, you're wrong.

No.

Like, what? Yeah. Why would it be?

Eventually it's managing — Gary, one club man for his own. Is that a good job? Yeah, you got an amazing talent, but he never moves.

If you were a local player who's come up through the academy at your local team and you go into the first team, even if you go on and win everything in sight, you're probably going to be one of the lowest paid in your club for most of your career. So you can suffer for being that local player, the one club man.

Most players, if they move, they tend to move because there are better opportunities — sporting and economic. But just continuing to move a player? No, it's not something we'd ever do. It's like a stress dream for me, to be honest. A player needs to settle. He's a person. At some point he'll have children — and you go and move school every year and the player will hate you. "What are you doing to me? I'm dizzy."

You do have certain players who don't settle. A big club signs them and they don't break in, so they go on loan the following year, and the club uses them for a season but doesn't have the money to buy them, and they move somewhere else, and then they move somewhere else. It can happen. There's a couple of fairly high-profile examples.

Balotelli — there's no club he hasn't played for.

Yeah, he's played for quite a few. He was an astonishingly talented player. There's certain players — these maverick guys like him — where the last three or four clubs they go to, it's maybe the ego of a manager going, "Nobody could tame him, but I can." And they fall in and they realize after 3 weeks that when he's setting fireworks off in the locker room that nobody can tame that person.

That great Jose Mourinho comment — you ever see it where he's like, "Balotelli walks in at halftime on a yellow card. I go, 'Mario, don't touch anyone. Don't do anything. Just stay up top, last man.' 46th minute, red card." You can't tame him.

No, no, for sure. But to fully answer your question: it's like any job. If you're working in insurance and you're changing employer every 12 months, it's going to be hard for you to even be recognized as being any good, because you're just moving all the time.

十一、关系胜于交易：日常沟通与精品环境

Okay. So for you two, you're established, happy in your club, contracts are long-term, you're not looking to move — let's just say, scenario. The relationship that you have with your agent, how does it develop when there isn't a contractual conversation to have? Are we looking at commercial? How broad is that relationship? And how often do you communicate?

To be fair, Rok is here in London, so I'm able to — honestly, I pop in pretty frequently and just sit down and get rid of him. Honestly, I think that's why he brought me here.

No, but it's nice because, like I said, one of the reasons why I signed with Rok is because it genuinely does feel like a family environment. I know if I'm ever Central, I can pop in and just sit there and have a coffee with them. That's one of the things that makes our relationship unique and special — because we are quote-unquote a boutique agency. I do feel like I'm cared for not just as a player, but as a person. And I feel the same way about them. I care about them as people as well. And whatever employee at Rok, if they ever need me, if they ever need tickets, whatever the case is, I'm always there to answer texts and phone calls.

So, one of the things is that it's almost good when I'm not speaking to him about contracts, because it means that I'm not worried about anything. And that's one of the things that Alan does really well, on top of other things, of course — he does all the dirty work in the background so that I'm not stressing when I get on the pitch.

It starts with the personal relationship. Listen, occasionally you will have a client where there's a coldness or there's just no automatic bond, and you got to work on it and try to develop it. But maybe this is not what a football agent is expected to say — but I would hate to work in a transactional way. I wouldn't like that. It's not the environment we've built or would want to work in.

十二、什么时候需要经纪人？以及家人当经纪人的局限

When does one get an agent? At what stage of the career? Is it when you sign your first contract? Is it when you get paid a certain amount?

Yeah, that's a good question. When I signed my first contract, I definitely needed an agent involved because I could have put pen to paper to anything. I just saw the number and I was like, "Okay, cool. Yeah, I finally can get a car that I want."

When you first sign that first deal — especially because there's no education about it growing up — knowing that there are people behind me that have done their due diligence, looked into everything, making sure that I'm not signing my life away. That was probably the moment.

So, it's like first professional contract.

Yeah. I will say I did have an agent before Alan when I signed my first deal. Great agent, but also I think ultimately when it got time to make those big-boy decisions, I did switch to Alan because we very much had the same kind of view on my career and view on the pitch. For me it was finding somebody that I really aligned with as a person, and then I knew that the deals would come, that everything on the pitch would come as well.

What do you make of families being agents? We see quite a lot of young players having dad as an agent or uncle as an agent.

It depends on the circumstances. For example, occasionally you might have a player of limited ability and dad gives up his job to be the agent, and I'm thinking, "Well, this is not wise, because I don't think the player will have a career in four years, and you've put all your eggs in this basket that's made of chocolate."

Sometimes you have a star player who the family acts as the agent. If the player intends staying in the same place, it's not the hardest thing in the world, in all honesty. You can look around and see what your teammates are making — well, I should be earning between this guy and this guy.

The issue, and it goes back to your question a few moments ago — does a player need an agent? When does a player need an agent? Even as a family, it's a little bit like being a single-issue politician. You're like "save the local river" and that's great, but then someone says, "Well, what do you think about this situation with this factory that wants to move in?" And you don't know anything about it.

As an agent, you have a broad view of the market and you have massive connectivity and contacts within the entire market. That's not a fault of families — it's just not practically feasible for them to have that impact in the market. So what happens a lot is the family agent ends up using another agent at some point for a transfer. There are certain things that we can say to a player that he wouldn't listen to from his family — so sometimes it's a double act. You work closely with the family.

十三、案例复盘：从拜仁到水晶宫的全过程

Can we put it into a real-world example then? If we say, right, the moment where you go from Bayern to Palace — what happens from that first contact being made to you actually walking into that Palace dressing room for the first time?

Well, again, when I said that we work — we try to keep the group of players that we work with quite exclusive. One of the reasons for that is that we're not spamming clubs. So when we sign a player, we can send a 90-second highlights compilation to, let's say, 40 sporting directors of the top teams in Europe, and we say, "Look, we've just signed this player. Here's a few clips." Typically they'll know the player, but we put some clips to them, and within 24 hours pretty much every club will respond.

Seriously?

Yeah. "When is he out of contract?" Sometimes they'll say, "Not for us. Thanks very much. But when does this contract expire?" "We don't need a right-footed center back. We need a left-footed center back. So if you have one of them, let me know." This type of thing.

You sign a player that you really believe in and you really believe is strong in the market — or will be strong in the market. Maybe they need some time, but it's a case of pushing the button at the right time.

With Chris — Chris wanted to come and play in the Premier League. I think it's fair to say he was a little bit frustrated that his game time at Palace, sorry, Bayern, was too sporadic. He'd get on the bench, but when you think about it, they don't substitute central defenders very often. It tends to be wingers and strikers who get subbed off, and goalkeepers never, and central defenders only when they're injured maybe sometimes.

And who's in front of you? Was that Upamecano?

So it was Boateng, Upamecano, Lucas Hernandez, Benjamin Pavard. I'm looking at them — and of course, "Oh, you know, trust the process. You're a young kid coming through our academy." I was like, "Yes, I understand that, but I also need to start my career at some point." This was a World Cup year that I was going into as well, 2022. So I'm thinking for myself — I was a big part of the US for the World Cup qualifiers. So I've established myself as a first-team footballer, but when I come back to club, I'm playing right back or left back, getting 10 minutes here, 10 minutes there. It's my turn. It's my time.

And you want to play in the middle?

Yeah, and I want to play in the middle. I want to play my own position. Like Alan mentioned earlier, when I told Bayern Munich that I wanted to leave, they were like, "What do you mean you want to leave? This is Bayern Munich." I was like, "Yeah, I understand that." I was 21, 22 at the time. I had to go and make some tough conversations happen. I remember knocking on the manager's door telling him I want to leave. At first it was on loan to Hoffenheim, and they didn't quite understand it at first, but ultimately they let me go. Then when I told them I wanted to leave on a permanent deal, they were like, "Are you sure? You don't always get a chance at a club like this."

Were you sure?

Yeah. Me being American and being a bit naive to how big of a club Bayern was worked in my favor. Of course I wanted to play at a big club like that, and I still do want to play at a big club like that one day. But ultimately, if I wasn't playing, I wasn't happy. This is my passion. This is something that I love to do. It felt like it was taken away from me at that point.

The loan to Hoffenheim was a really valuable experience because, one, he needed to play regularly, and we knew he was going to play at Hoffenheim. The other thing is there was interest in the UK market — quite a bit of interest — but that was only going to solidify and grow once people could see him play 90 minutes week in, week out, which he did at Hoffenheim. So that really helped in the end.

In terms of Bayern — you signed a new contract at Bayern, went on loan, and then left in the following season. Because he didn't have a huge remaining term in his contract, Bayern wanted him to sign a new contract before he would go on loan, which is typical enough. The reason is generally: if you came back and were out of contract, the work you were doing to hopefully improve your value would be compromised. That happens quite often. That's part of the course.

But at the time, when Bayern came with a financial offer on the new deal, we knew that he was going to be leaving on a permanent. It was a decent enough contract, but it wasn't showing us that he's going to be a starting player here anytime soon.

That's when — I say earlier on, we want them to pay because it's indicative of their ambition for the player. They really did love him. I remember speaking to Marco, the sporting director at the time, and saying to him, "No, he wants to leave," and the guy was like, in a state of disbelief. But you were wasting time there ultimately, because they were not going to put Boateng out of the team. They weren't going to put Upamecano out of the team because they spent 65 million on the guy. And they were actually bringing another central defender in if I'm not mistaken — they brought in De Ligt that same window. For big money. So it was a combo in that specific case. The fact that they were bringing in De Ligt, who at that point is one of the best in the world perceived to be — I'd have Chris over him any day of the week. But in any case, that was the situation.

Okay. So that then happens. It almost is good for you both, I guess, because it just clarifies your position.

Exactly. And sometimes all you need is clarity, because very often clubs will try to cling on to assets just in case they need them. But that's no good for a player. That's a disaster — to sign a contract and just be there in case of emergency, break glass.

十四、为什么选择水晶宫：被需要的感觉

So then are you two heads down together? You're going, "Right, okay. We know this is happening." You say what you want, the type of move you'd be really keen to explore, the country you may be keen to go and play in, and you'll go and act on that?

I think it's a combination of both. I had the dream to play in the Premier League, but also it was Alan being like, "Okay, you need to be a bit realistic. These are the kind of clubs that are coming in for you," whether it's in Germany or in Belgium or Holland.

It was making sure that we found the right fit. I had a few phone calls, but ultimately the conversations that I had with Crystal Palace were the best. They made me feel wanted straight away. Some clubs you'll get on the phone with them and they'll tell you, "Oh, like we see you in two years breaking into the team. We see you fighting for a spot with somebody," which I have no problem with competition. But it was just the fact that they made the time to have multiple phone calls with me. On top of that, met me in person. I was on the phone with Patrick Vieira within a month, and it was really cool. They really made me feel like family straight away from the first phone call, regardless of if I would have signed there or not.

And I guess after the experience at Bayern, the guaranteed playing time — well, nothing's guaranteed, but the chance to go and start your career as a starting player in the Premier League — that's a huge thing.

And do you remember the time Atletico Madrid had been in touch?

Yeah.

The issue with the Atletico Madrid one, it was very much a sideways move from Bayern. It seems like you turned down Atletico Madrid, but the point of it was that you were still potentially going to be a rotation player. He needed to go and start his career.

Also a team which has a fantastic record of developing talent.

Exactly. I knew that a big chance like that would come if I was able to do what I needed to do on the pitch. That was the number one thing. Alan from day one has always told me, "I can bring you this and that, but you're your biggest cheerleader. You go out there and you do your work on the pitch and then everything else will follow afterwards." It took him believing in me. But also it took a club like Palace believing in me for me to almost prove everybody else right. I knew it from day one, but it was showing everybody that I can also do this.

十五、教练在转会决策中的角色与俱乐部稳定性

How much — Patrick Vieira was manager at that time when you're making that move. I always wonder, in that discussion, how integral the manager can be to that conversation, understanding the environment of football now and the fragility of managers' positions. If you're relying too much on that relationship — change happens, system change happens, suddenly the move I've made here for the playing time can be completely destabilized.

Yes. You've got to look at the habits of a club and the stability of a club. Certain clubs behave like basket cases. Sometimes if they come with a good move for a player, you're thinking, "Well, they go in a different direction and they change sporting director and they change coach quite often. There's a lot of risk involved."

With Palace — I think we felt that they were a showcase for very good young players no matter who the coach was. While we anticipated Patrick was going to last longer than he did, at the same time we knew that in recent years — and it's been proven since then as well — the best players, they get their exits.

They're also straight shooters. Dougie Freedman, Scottish guy, doesn't sugarcoat anything. And Vieira also being French, he doesn't sugarcoat anything either. That's something that I really value in people — they just tell me like it is. Whether they think that I'm garbage, so be it. If they think that I'm good, also amazing. But one way or the other, I'll prove you wrong or right.

十六、合同里那些容易被忽略的东西

When you're going through contracts in this stage and moving, what are the important things in a contract that most people glaze over?

You usually have two bonus elements. Bonuses based on achievements, and then escalators based on achievements as well. So if the team wins a particular competition, the player gets a bonus, but the salary increases in addition to that. Or based on a number of — once the player has started X number of games, he gets a bonus, but the salary increases as well.

Is that very common to have bonuses and escalators in contracts?

Premier League, yeah, probably. One of the things that depends is the age of the player. For example, the term of the contract — sometimes you might have a player who's 27 and he's a free agent, and he might only want to sign a short-term contract because he might want to be a free agent again in two or three years. So you might want to sign a three-year deal. The length of contract sometimes can be a little bit of a put-off for the player.

With a young player, I don't mind a long contract because, having been an agent for 14 odd years, you trust the market to some degree. The best clubs come for the best players. Whether the player has two years left or four years left on his contract, the market does work generally.

What I would say is that I've been at clubs when they've received an email from an agency: "These are our players for this window who we think are suitable for your club," and it's got 40 players on it. That club is probably going to sign three players in the window — maybe five or six. So from an agent's perspective, what you want to do is make sure your communications with clubs are relevant and intelligent, and not just spam.

If we have a player who's a wing-back rather than specifically a left-back or right-back — he's really a wing-back — we're not going to pitch that player to a club that plays a back four. It's not relevant. And the sporting director will look at the player and go, "Great player, but we don't play like that." So you save yourself that step. We try to tailor our communications to clubs in a way that shows the clubs we understand them and we get them. And you may, in a conversation, say, "Well, look, in case you do switch it up and you go to wing-backs next season, we have this player."

十七、初到水晶宫：自我怀疑与拒绝转会

When you made the move, tell us about from those early stages to now. You're looking at a team at the moment where you're playing every week. But at the beginning, when you're starting out in that journey, you're not necessarily going into that team straight away. They play you a bit in midfield, I think at the beginning?

So once Roy came in, then I started playing in the midfield.

Roy Hodgson, the six.

Yeah, well, we'll call it like a five-and-a-half, four-and-a-half. But when I first came here, my first full season, I think I started four games. I remember summertime came around and I'm looking out like, "Did I make the right decision?"

There were a few moves and Palace shut them down. We had a few offers to get you out, actually for considerably more than Palace had paid in one specific case.

What was the most tempting?

I think Union Berlin qualified for the Champions League. That was a good offer.

And probably the fact there's a bit of German experience always.

Yeah, yeah. But there were a few. For different reasons — like Club Brugge is a brilliant trampoline club to go there and play for a season or two, and they've got a great record of selling players for multiples of what they paid. PSV was a good one to go and kind of win titles.

But Palace shut it down. And then, true enough to their word, the following season you became a starting player.

Hannah shut it down. They said, "Nope." They wouldn't accept it.

But then hopefully that warrants a conversation with you going, "This is why we don't want to accept it. You're going to feature."

Exactly. That was Dougie kind of coming to me. He was the sporting director at the time. They had the stigma of just sitting in their office all day long, and he actually made it a point to come speak to me in person at the training ground or whatever the case was, and was like, "I still see you as a big part of this team regardless of what manager is in. You will get your chance. Just keep doing what you're doing. Everybody sees you around here."

Dougie's a straight shooter, and I learned to trust his word. A few months later, I was playing every game, and ever since then, I've been in the starting 11.

十八、从中后卫到中场：临危受命的拓展

Did you have that moment — was it one or two performances? Because quite often that happens. Suddenly you get a chance, everyone says, "Oh, someone's been injured, you play three games, now you're starting routine." Was there a moment where that happened, or was it gradual?

Yeah, actually I remember one of our midfielders pulled out due to a family emergency. Again, I started four games the season before, and I feel like I played pretty well — I think I did okay. It gave the manager the confidence to put me in when one of our midfielders dropped out. I'm not going to lie, I don't think I had the confidence in myself to play midfield at the time because I had never done it before.

But I got in there and just kind of did whatever the team needed. It wasn't pretty, but that's exactly what the team needed. It gave the manager more trust, more trust. And then from then on, I probably started maybe the last 18 games of the season. I think probably 12 of those were in a position that I never played before. But everybody knew how hard I was working, but also how much I wanted to help the team succeed. Players like that is what every team needs. Sometimes doing the nasty stuff that doesn't quite get the praise, but just making sure that you do what's possible.

That is a huge thing for a central defender in the Premier League — to just step into midfield and start games in midfield. It goes back to that point I said: you're not necessarily overly respectful for the architecture of football. You'll just say, "Okay, I'm going to do that." And you do it. It's an amazing asset to have. It's really unusual.

Players tend to play in a particular position. City have these chameleons who can play here, there, and everywhere. But typically that's not the way. Apart from goalkeeper, central defenders are very often the people who would be least comfortable dropping into midfield.

十九、商业价值与全球化叙事

How do you think about the commercial side then?

Chris can speak most on this. The only thing I will say is that, globally for professional football players, there are really only a handful of players who are massive commercial assets. That's the reality. They tend to be attacking players at teams that win Champions Leagues more often than not — league titles and Champions Leagues. One of the incredible things about Chris is he has crossed a bridge that very often people don't see there is a bridge.

Is that true, sorry to interrupt you? Is that true on the players — only a select few are global assets and everyone wants them? Because you actually see the segmentation of audiences. Chris is a phenomenal asset given the rise of football in America. But also if you have African players, there might be large African corporates who really want to get behind them. Australian, LATAM — actually you see the segmentation of geographies.

No, I mean, to give you an example — and I won't name names, but let's say I'll randomly select Liverpool. They won the league a couple of years ago. If you went through their starting 11 of their league title, almost none of those players are really that commercially valuable. There's not many brands going, "I want to do a deal with that guy." They're not hugely in demand.

Whereas when you look at — like Palace — that Chris won two trophies in the last 12 months at Palace, but they're not winning league titles. But Chris has had deals with Crocs. He's got a deal with Bank of America at the moment. He's got a deal with Kellanova, who make Pop-Tarts and Cheez-Its and all of —

Yeah, but unfortunately, as an athlete, he has to pass it to me to sample.

That's unfortunate. My dad's reaping the benefits of that.

But the point I'm making is that Chris has got deals with these huge multinationals that are not typical for players outside of PSG, Man City, Barcelona.

二十、人格魅力与"领袖气质"

Embarrass him. Embarrass him.

Honestly, I don't think I can embarrass this man, as in he's just so great. You know — I'm sure you guys in your business, you do deals, and I'm sure to some degree you back yourself with this one expression that we all probably use sometimes: "If I get in the room, I'll get the deal." That very much applies to Chris. When people meet him, they want to work with this guy.

I joke sometimes — I call him "my president." Like, I'm joking, but if he wanted a post-career to go into politics, he could do it. Because the people are with him. When he walks into our office — we have some fairly big names who drop into our office occasionally and sometimes people are like, "Oh, there's —" and they greet them. Chris is like a rock star when he comes into our room. The staff are so happy to see him. He doesn't act like a rock star, but he's an amazing communicator. He's got a great look. And he has this foundation of —

The look's important though.

Yeah, of course it is. Because if you think about it, when you're a kid — maybe brands don't look at it in this exact way, but to some degree it's in the back of their mind: who do the kids want to grow up to be like? With respect, Peter Beardsley was an amazing player when I was a kid, but probably didn't have a great look. So did people want to grow up to be him? Not necessarily — they wanted to grow up to play like him.

Whereas in Alabama at the moment, any young soccer player there, or in America — not just specifically — or now even in South London, young kids see him and they want to grow up to be like him. I think that's been part of it. And it's not just play like him, but speak like him, walk into a room and have the same impact as him. All of these things coming together have made that.

二十一、品牌真实性与跨界合作

Can I ask, how do you think about authenticity with partnerships?

For example, like Kellanova — that's a fun one. Everybody can smile when you think of Kellanova, but these are actual products that I grew up — my family grew up eating and using. So for me it was really easy to get in the room with them, because I had a niche story about, "Oh, we used to put ice cream on our Eggos growing up," and they were like, "What? That's almost nasty." But we like it because it shows that this is just you.

When I do end up working with certain brands, they chose me for me. I bring a little bit to the room — like Alan said, I just like being myself. Now, some people love it, some people hate it, but it's not going to make me change who I am. So understanding that brands chose me for who I am, not just for what I bring to the table — that's what really makes me happy.

二十二、2026 世界杯：美国足球的临界时刻

We're in a moment now as well, right? We have the World Cup this summer. The whole eyes of the football world and beyond on the US in particular. You — congratulations, Player of the Year, right? Player of the Year last year. The position you're held in right now, the opportunity you have with the tournament coming there, the rise of football in the States — this is an amazing time for you to actually look at and go, "Yeah, do you know what? Like Landon Donovan or other people — like we can actually really do something cool here, something that may not have been done before." Do you think a little bit like that — capturing that moment?

Definitely. As an athlete, you think this is the moment to change the narrative of this sport back home. You think of the last World Cup — it was kind of what birthed our MLS. We want to be able to leave a platform where we can do something even bigger than that. Hopefully it's by winning. But if not, it's about inspiring the next generation to do better than us. That's just the on-the-field stuff.

Off the field, you think of: okay, this is the chance for myself to be in front of the six billion eyes. It can sound nerve-wracking, but at the end of the day, it's a chance to do the hard work now so that my name is always in people's mouths when they think of US soccer, when they think of US products, when they think of things like that.

I also understand that I'm representing more than myself. I'm representing my family. I'm representing my hometown. I'm also representing every kid that's going to come up after me. If people think about American footballers and they think of the next generation, maybe they're a little bit like myself. Maybe they're a little bit like Landon Donovan, Clint Dempsey, those kind of guys. So I'm understanding that it's more than just the current batch of guys now. It's what's coming afterwards.

It's actually a really good point. I haven't really thought about — for a top US sports person to have that proper global reach. So many sports in US — basketball, NFL, hockey — amazing massive sports, dominated by domestic eyeballs. Football, soccer — global game. Global stars coming home and bringing that in. Therefore it actually puts you in a very different bracket to some of the other relationships some of these companies have.

Yeah. A lot of US brands are starting to learn that now. This is so much more than just MLS. It's so much more than just Concacaf. It's global. Parts of the world that you've never touched before, or parts where you've probably heard of before, are going to be watching this game. So this is your chance to also get your name out there, get your brand out there, get your values out there. That's why the game — at least on the commercial side — is really growing right now in the US.

二十三、超越球员身份：投资家乡足球

The one thing probably worth saying — the word "authenticity" — I'm sure we've all sat in meetings where that's been used 18 times and none of us can see a hint of it anywhere in the room. Along with "alignment."

In terms of authenticity, to prove the point: Chris is invested in a team in his hometown.
