He's somebody from Hoover, Alabama, who is playing in the Premier League, is playing for his national team, has captained his national team, is current Player of the Year for his national team, has won significant trophies in the past year for Palace, is going into the World Cup as one of his country's best players — but he's actually investing in the game. That has another string to his bow. It shows that he's more than a player. He's a voice within the sport, and he's trying to develop the sport locally where he is.

Where would you say soccer is in terms of the rankings of sports in Alabama?

Oh man, probably a long way distant in certain — it'd be very far down. But again, like Alan mentioned, US Soccer and FIFA opened a pitch in downtown Birmingham, where I'm from. This is something that's never been done before. When people think of Alabama, they generally think of American football and college football. But I want to help — like I said — change the stigma, change the narrative around my hometown. It's my chance to do my part in bringing more awareness to this beautiful sport that we all love to my hometown, where it's not really ever spoken about, talked about, or even seen.

二十四、压力造就钻石：备战世界杯

Soccer in the US, football in the US, has been on an upward trajectory. People can often pin a lot of hope and focus on a World Cup as the moment. But in reality, quite often it's just another one of those steps, and you have to really make sure that when it's there, you capitalize on it. How's that feeling been within the country, and how is it right now as we're getting closer to ensure that this is the event you want it to be?

There's this cliché that pressure builds diamonds. Pressure is good. It's good to wake up in the morning feeling nervous about what you're going to do today, because it shows that you're doing something that really matters.

For me and my teammates, we understand that this World Cup is a huge moment in the future of the sport in the US. But also, we didn't just wake up one day and get here. It took years to get to this point. We understand that there's going to be a World Cup after this one. We understand there's going to be games after this one. But it's trying to make sure that when we go out there on this pitch this summer, we show the world that we have been prepared for this over multiple years. It's super exciting. We're trying not to put too much pressure on ourselves, but we do know that this is a chance for this sport to really blow up back home.

How's Mauricio Pochettino been?

He's been good. He's brought this kind of South American passion flair that maybe we were missing. If you watch the games, he's very animated, but I think it translates onto us on the pitch. When you have somebody like that on the sideline who's so well respected globally, again, it brings more eyes to the sport. We probably have a lot more Latin American viewers than we would have normally had.

US Soccer did their due diligence when it came to hiring him and understanding that he is a big name, but also somebody who's very passionate about the sport and has the same values as us — which is to grow the sport.

二十五、国际队建队节奏与精神凝聚

How do you look to build those relationships? He's come in not after years of you going to this World Cup, with years of experience with him managing you. The time you have together is really important — but what's the type of work as an international manager that goes in when you're not together? Is it constant communication, particularly building up to something like this?

Yes and no when it comes to the communication. He was a club coach, so he understands that if you're bombarded by both coaches — club and country — at the same time, it can get a bit confusing. But the time that we do have within these international windows, we typically spend a lot of time with each other. Not just coaches or players, but also coaches with players. And they're actually fun guys to be around.

When people look at coaches and players, sometimes it's like, "Get out of my face." But the fact that we don't see them every day makes it a little bit less like, "Oh, I can't stand this guy." It's good to be around these people because they bring something that we didn't have for years. Also, it's a learning experience for everybody. There's a reason why he was a coach at the top level. There's a reason why he's a player at the top level. If you can pick the 1% from this coaching staff and add it to your game and help translate it to the players around you, then ultimately as players and as a national team, we'll have grown from it.

With a World Cup, it's a different dynamic from league football. You go into a World Cup and the spirit in the camp is huge. Sometimes over a Premier League season, the spirit in the camp probably goes only so far because quality kicks in in the end. In the World Cup, the spirit in the camp is huge. How you prepare for each opponent is huge. That's something where he's going to be excellent. Him and his team are going to be great at preparing the team for the opponents. Beyond that, it's just each player stepping up in the big moments — and I think you've got some big personalities in there.

I'm very excited. I'm very excited for every World Cup, I won't lie. But I'm excited for this World Cup because Chris is going to go into it as one of the key players in the US. People in the US — when it's a home World Cup, they will have certain expectations — but I do think there's enough characters in there to actually deliver and get you through to the knockout stages. From the knockout stages onwards, who knows?

Well, CBS or someone did it — 50 people that are going to influence this World Cup. You were number 16.

There we go.

So there's a lot of expectation.

二十六、世界杯与商业机会的平衡

Do you guys have a conversation where, "Hey, this is a window where we can absolutely optimize for brands, partnerships because of the nature of the World Cup"? Or is it like, "No, no, focus on performance"?

No, you don't lose sight of the fact that performance is everything. But then you have dessert after a good meal sometimes — and the dessert is the commercial opportunities that come with it. Again, as Chris said, nobody just left them outside his door. He's earned them. So it makes sense to maximize that.

二十七、足总杯与社区荣耀

Speaking of earning them, trying to feed in naturally — but also fans will kill us if we don't talk about it — gone indulgence. You've put yourself in a position now at a perfect time. Two trophies — the FA Cup last season, incredible. Anyone watching football who's supported football, unless you're a Man City fan, was so excited to see you guys win that game. You then go and win the Community Shield. You're now in Europe, hopefully, fingers crossed, you could win that competition as well. How was it?

It was amazing. Of course, winning trophies is always nice, and winning accolades is always nice. But for what those wins did for the community of South London — that was the most beautiful part of it. You would look up in the stands and see grown men crying. You'd see families huddled together, hugging each other. That was the most beautiful thing.

Watching the BBC playback and hearing the audio and seeing the camera shaking and just seeing all the emotions from everybody in the stands — it was beautiful. Even to this day, I'll get in a black cab and the taxi driver will be like, "Oh, thank you for what you've done for South London." To this day I still feel what we did last summer and what we're continuing to do. We're thinking about, "Oh, we just want to win games," but it's so much more than that. It's a community. It's a club. It's a family that has not felt that kind of happiness for hundreds of years. And now we were able to do it and strike gold. It was beautiful.

Do you feel it's changed expectation? It's always the challenge with success — you do something exceptional, far beyond what people expected originally. Now they expect more. They want to feel that again.

We expected that from ourselves from day one. Especially with Glasner coming in — he's very ambitious. That Austrian-German mentality is like, "We have to win everything." It's exactly what we needed at that point in time. He came in saying, "Palace have this stigma of being a mid-table team. We want to win trophies now." And that's exactly what we did.

Going into this season, we were like, "Okay, we want to win the Conference League, we want to win the FA Cup, we want to win the League Cup. Of course, we want to win the Premier League. But if not, we want to qualify for Europe without having to win a trophy." Of course, there's a tighter race at the top than where we're at right now, but we still very much have a chance to qualify for Europe through our league play. On top of that, we're still in the Conference League. Sadly, not in the FA Cup, but regardless — we still have the chance to win silverware this year.

二十八、决赛更衣室：最不紧张的一天

What's it like in the dressing room before the FA Cup final? You're going against Pep Guardiola, Man City. You've been talking about this in a way where it's an expectation, as you said, from a manager. It's not from other people being outside going, "We're surprised to see you here." When you're actually in that moment before you go on pitch, how does it really work to gee you up — to take away any of the nerves?

The craziest part about being in the changing room before the FA Cup final was that nobody was nervous. We went in there as the underdog — I think deservedly. They beat us 5-2 maybe three weeks before. But it was almost like we knew that going into that game, they would be a lot more confident because they had just beat us a few weeks before. So we knew if we go in there and do exactly what we had done all season long — which is make it terrible to play against us — we would get a result.

I remember walking out on the pitch and seeing the tifo, seeing the fireworks, seeing everything that was going on there. I was like, "This is just another day." I'll probably be more nervous for the game on the weekend than I was for the FA Cup final. It was just because we all knew that this was our chance to change South London, change Crystal Palace forever. It's kind of a juxtaposition, but it literally was the least nervous I think I've ever been for a game.

I've been at World Cups, I've been at Champions League finals and Premier League stadiums the day that titles have been won on the last day. And honestly, being at Wembley the day of the FA Cup win — it was incredible. You touched upon it: grown men crying. They were all around me. I was probably one of them. There definitely were tears welled up. I can't say I bawled, but there were tears. It wasn't just the odd person — the whole stadium was such an emotional experience and everyone was just hugging each other when the final whistle went.

When the injury time board went up, we were like, "Oh god, they're going to get something here." Wasn't there a missed penalty?

Yeah, well, Dean kind of touched the ball outside of the box and we were like, "Oh no." We just figured it was going to go City's way.

Everyone was anticipating that "Oh, kind of the big bully is going to come along and nick it in the end" type of thing. You mean Haaland and his friends. But it was such a release.

Yeah, a lot of stuff happened. But that's like, "All right, so this is where they get back into it" — and then they didn't score. There was such a roller coaster, but then the release and the joy at the end of it — people were almost dropping to their knees and just everyone hugging each other.

The thing was that Haaland didn't take the penalty.

Yeah, exactly. I mean, you must have been on the pitch like —

Yeah, I was. Maybe he knows something I didn't. Because I remember at the time, everyone at home was going, "What?"

二十九、与哈兰德对位：身体怪兽与心理博弈

Is he a beast to play against?

He's a big lad to be fair, and he can move. He can very much move.

How do you prep for playing against him?

Just trying to minimize. The thing about him is that he has players around him that always find him. He's so smart with his off-the-ball movement. You kind of seem like he's not even playing — he's chatting with you for a little bit, kind of make you your friend, and then one second he switches on and he's gone, and you're like, "Come on, like I thought we were friends."

When you think "chatting" — like, "Do you watch Homeland on the weekend?"

Yeah, but he'll be like, "Yo, how you doing this and that?" And you'll be like, "Oh, cool." And you're like, "Oh, this guy's a nice guy." And you're like, "Oh no."

He's like, "I love Alabama."

If you went into a laboratory to create a striker, you would probably come out with Haaland.

Yeah. He is many things, but he is not the 2025 FA Cup winner. Pow pow.

That's it. Can't say that.

三十、Glasner 注入的胜者心态

We talked about the challenge of managers when they come in, when Glasner comes in and he puts that mentality in. Is it as simple as going, "Right, now we believe"?

I remember looking around the changing room and seeing guys who were playing for their national team, who were starting for their national team at high levels. We had two Colombians, English internationals, Japanese international, French internationals. We were like, "Okay, we aren't mugs. We're good players playing for our national teams."

He could have said that and we could have walked out of that meeting and been like, "Oh, he's just talking." But I remember looking around and we were like, "This is actually a team that can do it." Of course, maybe it took him saying that for us to ultimately believe. But every game he's like, "I don't care who we're playing. I don't care where we're playing. I don't care who is injured or isn't injured. We're going to go out there and give this team 90 minutes of hell, and we're going to try to win the game." That's something that we really bought into from day one of him.

I think with Palace, maybe in the past, they had relied on Wilfried Zaha. They were there or thereabouts. A lot of people who support other teams who are not direct rivals of Palace have a sympathy for Palace, because there is something great about the club, and the atmosphere is phenomenal.

I'm a Chelsea fan. I like Palace.

Sounds weird to say that.

Yeah. The experience of going there — it's quite unusual. Away fans sit on the side normally. You're behind the goal, and even just having that different type of experience in a stadium — I think it was the loudest stadium in whatever the decibel test is that they do. You feel that.

That wasn't Chelsea, was it? It's not hard.

Yeah, you really feel that. It comes through every touch point that you have, whether it's an away fan, a home fan, a player. It seems like a special place to be.

Definitely. It being one of the oldest stadiums in the league — you feel the history, but also the passion of the fans. It is a smaller stadium, so you hear every person chanting, you hear every person booing, you hear every person interacting with each other. That's what makes Selhurst beautiful. It's also one of the things that makes Palace beautiful — they speak about being a family club. I know a lot of clubs do speak about it, but from the staff to the fans to the players, everybody's involved. It starts at Selhurst being able to hear everybody. That's what makes it so special.

三十一、快问快答：吸引球员加盟的因素

Are you ready for a fun one? We've interviewed so many owners and they're always like, "Oh, we like to invest in the training ground, and it really attracts great talent." Rank one through three on what will attract you to a club.

Okay. Salary. No, I'm joking. Training ground / facilities, then manager. Salary, training ground, manager — one through three, one being most.

I would say manager.

Wow.

Salary, then training ground.

Wow.

I think of course you don't want to necessarily put all your eggs in one basket when it comes to a manager, because they can be sacked or whatever the case is. But the manager is the one person that really makes your time enjoyable at a club. When you're playing, it's beautiful. When you're not playing, it's not so beautiful. Ultimately, it comes down to the manager's decision.

When I remember getting on the phone with Vieira — even when I first got to Palace, I wasn't necessarily playing — he was still speaking to me, making me feel like I was seen. Without those interactions, I probably would have begged Alan to get me out of Palace pretty soon. Even at Hoffenheim — my coach at Hoffenheim, Sebastian Hoeneß, was my under-19s coach at Bayern and my under-21s coach at Bayern. So I already had that relationship with him, and he made my time at Hoffenheim so enjoyable. Number one would definitely be manager.

三十二、被低估的队友：Thiago

Who's the best player you've played with who's not obviously the star, like dirt count, just put in more yards than anyone? Who's the Trojan horse?

That Bayern team was pretty hefty. Not that he's underrated at all, but playing with Thiago — I think he didn't hit quite his form when he went to Liverpool, but when he was at Bayern, I've never seen a player like that. Not just as a player, but also as a person. He spoke English, German, Portuguese, Spanish, a little bit of French. He's a guy who really kept the changing room together, but also led by his actions on the pitch.

Even when I came from Bayern to Palace and he went to Liverpool, I remember being in the tunnel before the game, and I hadn't spoken to him. I was a youth player at Bayern, so he could have easily been like, "I don't know who you are." And he came up to me before I even saw him and gave me a hug. He's like, "Oh, good to see you. Let's chat afterwards, this and that." Something like that — it's an interaction that will always live with you. He's a person you always remember because of who he was as a person, rather than a player.

It's the small things, right?

Exactly.

He's still got it. He played in that senior game at Liverpool the other week, and the ball is still glued to his foot.

Also loves padel. Plays padel all the time.

三十三、想合作的品牌与发型故事

Are we going to do a quick fire? I have so many good quickfire ones. This is for both of you. Which brand do you not work with today that you would most like to work with? You'll both have a different answer, I imagine.

Which brand do I want to work with? This is — you want Chris to work with?

I — oh, sorry for Chris. I was going to say — and you as well.

Okay. The people of Flahavan's Porridge in Ireland. I love your approach so much. Come on. Please send me a talent.

No, for Chris, that's a good question. I would say fashion. Something more on the fashion side. I think that's probably the missing piece at the moment for me.

I would say — again, I don't want to pinpoint a brand, but I think maybe something revolving around hair. After games, fans will be like, "Can I touch your hair?" Even people back home — they're like, "Oh, the Afro is such a cool thing." There's even a mini figurine that the National Team Players Association is selling and it has an Afro on it. Like a bobble hat.

Yeah, almost like — bubble hat. It's cool because of course this is just naturally how my hair grew, but it's cool because it's like — when I'm on the pitch, my grandma, who can't see as well as she used to, she's like, "Oh, there's Chris right there." It's just something really cool — it's my look.

So we're not going to see you in the World Cup walk out with it braided or something?

I don't think you will. No, that's not the plan. Suddenly, I'm going to change. The plan is to keep the Afro.

三十四、最佳球场、人们对经纪人的误解、以及当父亲

Interesting. Which is the best stadium you've ever played in?

Atletico Madrid Stadium — their brand new stadium is amazing. Oh, man. We played them in Champions League when I was at Bayern, and it was during COVID, so there were no fans, but it felt like a museum. It was massive, massive, massive — just beautiful stadium.

What does everyone get wrong about football agents when they think about the agent relationship?

I think they miss all the bits in the middle. You look at transfers as being the key thing — they're very important. Sometimes renewals are just as important as a transfer, when things are going well. When the player's progressing, a renewal is important. But also, it's dehumanizing the whole sport and just looking upon it as a series of transactions and greed. And it isn't. There's a lot of love in it. We do it because we love it, not because we love cash.

How many employers? We also love cash.

Yeah, we like both.

How many players do you actually work with yourself?

So core group of about 40 players.

40.

Yeah. And then we have — we've got Brazil. They have about another 100-odd players there. But in terms of players who I'm doing transfers for and renewals for — yeah, around about 40.

What do you do with your spare time?

I play with my daughter a lot. That takes up a lot of time.

How old is she?

She's 16 months now.

What does no one know about being a parent that they should know?

Oh, get the sleep. No. I think it definitely takes a village. There's that saying, but it's so true. Being far away from my family, I've very much learned that it takes more than just the parents — it takes the people around to also help.

三十五、文化冲击、传奇教练、最紧张的比赛

What's the biggest shock when you went from US to Germany?

Other than the autobahn, I would probably say just how regimented German people are. Sometimes you can get away with being one minute late back home, two minutes late, or being fashionably late, per se. But in Germany, it was very much like, "If you're not 10 minutes early, you're already late." So just pretty much how disciplined they are.

Is that the kind of thing where you go, "Right, okay, I made that mistake or learned that once, then I don't do it again"?

Oh, yeah. I'm usually 30 minutes early to wherever I need to be. My girlfriend, she doesn't like that. My dad doesn't like it either. My mom doesn't like it. But I'm like, if I have to be somewhere at 2:00 p.m., I'm out the door by 1:30.

So you're not one of these people that turns up 45 minutes before a flight?

No. That stresses me out. I got to be there at least 2 hours, sometimes 2 and a half hours before.

Much more enjoyable.

Exactly.

Which manager have you not played for that you would love to play for?

I would have loved to have played for Klopp. Again, I love that kind of German, Austrian mentality. You can see that he was very passionate and that he loved the players that he coached.

What was the game you were most nervous before playing?

I would say the first time I ever played Mexico with the national team.

I'd be scared too.

Yeah, it was. It was home. But every time you play Mexico at home, it feels like an away game because there are so many Mexican immigrants that live in America. Every time I play Mexico, it's between 50 to probably 75, 80,000 fans, and a majority of them are Mexican fans. We have such a long history with playing them with the national team. Especially this generation that we're in now — we've pretty much always beat them. It's a big rivalry on the pitch, and there's always scraps. There's always scraps in the stands. It's fun, but the first time playing them, you're like, "Oh my gosh, this is the game."

三十六、世界杯主办城市与决赛对手

In the US, with the different cities hosting the games, which ones are there some where you go, "That's going to be intense," and some where you go, "That'll be a little more gentle, a little nicer experience"?

I think games in Houston are going to be tough. Of course, it's in the middle of summer in Houston, which will probably get up to like 45 degrees — exaggeration, but it'll be hot. Even though they're playing indoors, they'll definitely feel it. I think LA also will be that way, just because of how culturally hot LA is. There'll be people from all walks of life there. That will also be a huge one.

Mexicans again.

Yeah, exactly. That's what I'm saying. But Houston is a different beast.

You can win the FA Cup or the Europa League — Conference League?

I think FA Cup. It wasn't just because we beat City — I think it was the fact that we were the best team in England at that point. And again, what it did for this community. I would redo that.

三十七、最难缠的前锋与英超的全球吸引力

Of all strikers to come up against, which are you most like, "Oh no"?

I will say honestly, one of the toughest ones I've ever played against was Michail Antonio. We played them in World Cup qualifiers in Jamaica. Look at me now and think of me maybe 15-20 pounds lighter going up against a full-strength, full-health Michail Antonio. He bullied me. He bullied me.

It's funny now to see him doing well with Sunderland — Brian Brobbey. I remember playing him when I was in the academy at Bayern and he was playing for Ajax — the exact same size that he is now. He was like, "Come on, we're going to go for a walk." Just had my shirt the whole time. He's been that size since he was probably like 16 years old. I watched him play as an under-15 at the Marvel tournament in Netherlands — he was the same size. He was the guts of two feet taller than the other kids.

It's like the photos of the young Lukaku — seeing that against when he was in the youth team.

Exactly. "I give up."

But he was very good.

Yeah. Sometimes it's just a physical advantage. He was good as well. At that point in my career, I was still playing minutes here and there for Bayern, so I hadn't necessarily got my foot into grown men's football yet. I remember that game being like, "Oh my gosh, this is another level." Not necessarily because he was super techy or whatever, but it was just the fact that he knew exactly how to play the game. He knew exactly where he wanted to be. He knew exactly where he wanted to go. That was definitely a moment where I was like, "Wow."

It's one of the great things about the Premier League. For a defender like Chris, one week he has Mohamed Salah running at him, leaning one way, going the other. He's up against Haaland the following week. You're up against some of the best in the world on such a regular basis. It's an amazing experience. I think it's why so many players — everywhere I travel a lot for work and everywhere I go, every player wants to get into the Premier League.

Guys, this has been amazing. Thank you so much for doing it. I've loved having you on.

No, thank you all for having — thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、那个出人意料的观察：Bayern Munich 的"拒绝"，本质上是一场身份套利
大多数人会把 Chris 离开拜仁理解为"年轻球员追求出场时间"——这是表层叙事。但真正反常的地方在于：**他的"美国式天真"恰恰是他最稀缺的资产**。
德国体系中的年轻球员被"拜仁=神圣"的符号系统深度规训，他们的选择被一种集体潜意识锁死——离开拜仁等于背叛某种秩序。Chris 没有被这套符号殖民过，所以他能做出一个在欧洲球员看来"不可思议"的决定：把品牌价值贴现成出场时间。
更深一层的洞察是：**他在用美国式的"职业理性"对抗欧洲式的"图腾忠诚"**，而这种文化盲区反而构成了他的认知优势。这和创业领域的"局外人优势"（Outsider Advantage）惊人一致——硅谷研究反复表明，颠覆性创新者往往是那些**不知道行业规则有多神圣**的人。Chris 的天真，是一种结构性的认知套利。
## 二、跨领域连接：Alan 的"精品代理模式" = 私人银行业的逻辑，而非大众零售业
Alan 反复强调"我们不要 800 个客户"——这听起来像一句营销话术，但如果你把它放进**金融业的服务分

item.title: [Invest Like The Best] Lessons From a Life in the Markets | Paul Tudor Jones Interview
item.dc:creator: Invest Like The Best
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：他的"交易天才"其实是一种童年创伤的代偿性延续

Paul 反复强调"流动性是我的 DNA"，归功于祖父那句"你只值你明天能开出的支票"，以及亲眼看到 Bunker Hunt 在六周内从世界首富沦为破产。但更深层的线索藏在他 2 岁半在菜市场走丢的故事里——那是他第一个童年记忆。一个让他"恐惧到极点"的失控瞬间，被一位陌生黑人老人的善意"接住"。

注意这里的隐藏对称： - 童年的恐惧 → 被一个外人解救 → 形成 4000 次的祈祷复读 - 交易中的恐惧 → 被流动性解救 → 形成 50 年的"绝不持有"信条

他自称是 NFL 里的"右护卫，每天在战壕里搏斗"，而把 Buffett 描绘成"信仰美国就好"的人——这不是单纯的策略偏好，这是一个永远等不到母亲的孩子，无法允许自己进入"信任并等待"的状态。Buffett 的 50% 回撤"会对他造成实质性影响"——他承认了。这是他自己说的，但他可能没意识到这句话的深度。

最意外的洞察：他用慈善（Robin Hood、特许学校）疯狂"复制"那位陌生老人的善意，用交易的"绝不持有"反复演练他祖父的不安全感——他的两条人生主线，其实是同一个 2 岁半男孩在用两种语言说同一句话。

二、跨领域连接：新闻写作 = 主成分分析 = 交易决策框架 = 临终回顾

他随口提到的"新闻 101 应该是必修课"，看似是个闲聊建议，但当你把他这一段话和他描述的交易方法、以及最后回答"伟大人生的主成分"时的方式并列，会发现一个惊人的同构：

领域 第一段（最重要） 排序原则
倒金字塔新闻 Who/What/Where/When/Why/How 信息衰减递减
交易决策 当下最 actionable 的那个变量 催化时刻（catalytic moment）
人生回顾 God, Family, Friends, Fun, Service 临终视角倒推

他是在用同一种认知架构同时管理资金、信息和人生。这就是为什么他能日均处理 80 万封邮件还能"在血流成河时买入"——他大脑里有一个永远在运行的层级排序器。

更妙的是：他对日元的判断（"被低估 24 个月，但需要催化时刻"）和他对人生的判断（"善意要 4000 次的 reps 才会从 I should 变成 I am"）用的是同一个时间结构——长期价值 + 触发事件 = 释放。他在用交易员的方式理解品格的复利。

三、一个他自己都没有说穿的悖论：他羡慕 Buffett，但他成为 Buffett 的方式比 Buffett 更彻底

表面上他在道歉："Warren，你才是复利之王，我是个傻瓜。"

但深挖一下：Buffett 复利的是资本。Paul 复利的是善意——4000 次祈祷那个无名老人 → 看到 Eugene Lang 的瞬间共振 → I Have a Dream → Robin Hood → Bedstuy Charter School → 全美第一名小学。这是一个 70 年的复利曲线，年化"善意收益率"惊人。

他说"我希望成为 Buffett 那样信仰美国的人"——但他其实信仰的是一种更稀缺的资产：人类相互拯救的能力。Buffett 把 9 岁就懂的复利用在了股票上；Paul 把 2 岁半就懂的善意用在了人身上。

最反讽的连接：他对 AI 最大的恐惧是什么？是"build-break-iterate"模型用在尾部风险上的灾难性，是无法分辨真假的水印问题，是脑机接口模糊人机边界。但仔细看——这恰恰是"善意复利"被破坏的场景：当你不知道屏幕另一端是不是人，4000 次的 reps 就无法形成；当 50 亿条算法信息每天涌入，"在血流成河时买入"的那种人类直觉就被淹没。

他对 AI 的不安，本质上是一个用善意复利打造了一生的人，看到自己最珍视的复利机制正在被解构。这才是为什么他在镜头前反复回到"一个简单的善举"——那不是道德说教，那是一个对冲基金经理在公开宣告他认为最被低估的资产。

📝 转录文本

开场：投资者 vs. 交易员的羡慕

"My number one bullet point is you're going to make your money by writing a trend for the very very longest time. Or you can be like Warren Buffett. You can be one of those value investors who won't spend a nickel. And I used to just sit there and rail on Warren Buffett year after year after year. And he just happened to be in the right place at the right time and caught this bull market. I was just thinking, 'Oh my god, why couldn't I be Warren Buffett? Just believe in America and just went you're down 50% who cares cuz America's going to bring you through.' Warren, if you happen to hear this, I'm deeply apologetic. You are the OG of compound interest."

There was something that we talked about when we last met that made me laugh and I thought it'd be a fun place to begin our conversation today, and it was about the difference between being an investor and being a trader, and you mentioned something basically saying you wished you could be an investor. Life would be so easy. I'd love you to describe the difference between the two and the life of a trader.

I promise I'll do that. And we'll talk about markets and AI and everything, and at the end I'll say "were you not entertained" and I bet you'll say yes. But of all the things that you do on your podcast, the one that I think is the most important, if you don't mind, I'd love to start with that one — which is that question you ask everyone at the end.

I'm in. I love it.

一、最难忘的善意：从蔬菜市场到 Harlem

It's so funny — I gave this commencement address one time to what is now Rhodes College in Memphis. I remember when I was writing it, I was in there thinking, "Damn, who gave my commencement address?" And I couldn't remember. It was funny. No one remembers. Can you remember who gave your commencement? You're so young. You might.

It was the president of Ireland at the time. I went to Notre Dame.

And you're Irish, right? Okay. But it was so funny because no one remembers who gave their commencement address. And I was sitting there thinking, "God, I'm getting ready to do this for 15 or 20 minutes and no one's going to remember a damn thing. How do I make it special?" And so on this podcast, I'm assuming people listen to millions of podcasts and they probably can't really remember, but fractions of them. So if there's anything they remember from this, I hope it's this part.

All right. So everyone that listens knows that the question is: what is the kindest thing that anyone's ever done for you?

1957 年的菜市场

It's such a great question because the kindest thing was also my very, very, very first childhood memory. I was probably two and a half or three. It was probably 1957, something like that. And I was with my mother at this place called the Curb Market. And I got separated from my mother. And boy, you can imagine being 2 and 1/2 years old and separated from your mother — you're terror stricken.

And this elderly black gentleman came up — and I was sitting there bawling my eyes out cuz I thought my mother had left me — and said, "What's the matter, little boy?" And I said, "I lost my mama." And he said, "Here, don't you worry. We're going to find her." And he grabbed my hand and we started walking up and down the aisles. It was an outdoor vegetable market. And gosh, I can almost still smell fruits and vegetables.

We come around the corner finally and I see my mother and I was so happy, and my mother saw me, and at first she just started laughing. I remember her, she was laughing so hard. So she comes and she gets me and she says to that man — she tries to give him $5, which was in 1957, a huge amount of money. And he said, "No, ma'am. I know you'd do that for my child too."

It was such a simple act of kindness, but it was so profound to me at the time. So that night, I had a prayer list with my mother that she used to go through with me every night. So it'd be "God bless Mama, Daddy, Peter, Paul, Alberta, Grande, Pete, and Judy Lyn, and Sid." And then we added my mother. I said, "What was that man's name?" She goes, "I never got his name." For the next 10 to 12 years, right in that prayer list would be that man. So probably four or 5,000 reps.

Wow.

Every night, for that man.

1986 年的 60 Minutes 与 "I Have a Dream"

Now fast forward to 1986. I'm living in New York City and I'm lying on my couch, 32 years old, and I'm watching 60 Minutes, and Harry Reasoner is interviewing this guy, Eugene Lang. And Eugene Lang was a businessman who had gone back to give the commencement address to his elementary school up in Harlem, which over the course of the intervening 60 years had completely transformed from a gentrified area into an African-American Hispanic community with almost a population entirely consisting of people of color.

And Gene asked the principal, "How many of these kids, these 12-year-olds are going to go to college?" The guy said, "Statistically, maybe — I don't know — eight or nine percent, something like that." He could not believe that, because obviously he had graduated from that school, gone on to college, and then done very well. So right there on the spot, he decided, "I'm going to promise every one of those kids — if they graduate from high school, I'll put them through college." And that resonated with me so much. He adopted that class and he created something called the "I Have a Dream" program.

I said, "I can do that. I can do that." And the next day I called him up and he said, "You know, it's funny. Three or four other people have called me and we're all meeting at my apartment on Tuesday night." I got there late. I was seeing myself up in Harlem or somewhere on the lower east side Manhattan — I got Bed-Stuy, the highest crime-ridden community in New York at that time, even higher than the Bronx.

And that began this journey. I put my heart and soul into it. I'd go over there every Tuesday. I went to the commencement address of this elementary school, did the same thing that he did, told them that if they graduate from high school, I'd put them through college. Of course, everybody was jubilant. It was a big surprise that parents were so happy.

14 年的踩坑与 Bed-Stuy Charter School of Excellence

That began this big journey for really the next 14 years because I kept adopting successive classes. I had tremendous passion. We were doing after-school programs. We were playing sports, teaching life skills. About 3 years in, I realized these kids aren't doing that great now. They'd gone on to different junior highs. They're not doing that great. So I started hiring some tutors, figured that out. And then probably four years in, I had one kid who got killed. I had a bunch of girls become teenage moms. So now all of a sudden, I'm dealing not just with the academic challenges, but also with the social challenges to boot, of which there are many.

Along the way, I learned so much by failing. I learned so much about what it takes to actually beat poverty. And by the way, 1987, the year after that was when Robin Hood started. And then that informed me — certainly was extraordinarily instructive. And as we built Robin Hood out and began applying metrics and making sure we had goals and standards and a variety of things, it also led me ultimately to start one of the first charter schools in the late '90s in Bed-Stuy, right next to that spot. Actually, I started a charter school called the Bed-Stuy Charter School of Excellence. The first one started out as an all-boy school. We named it "Excellence" because I wanted these boys to know that we were going to demand excellence of them. And we completely totally crushed it. We had hired the absolutely dream team of educators to do it. And within about four or five years, we were number one out of 543 elementary schools in New York City.

The point being that you can have all the passion in the world, but you have to have a plan. There has to be a great pedagogy in what it is that you're trying to do when educating a kid.

善意的"乘数效应"

Why is all this relevant? Well, just think about this. So here was this one simple act of kindness of this elderly man helping this young child. In that case, a black man helping this young white boy. And so when I saw Harry Reasoner and I saw that elderly man helping those young kids, it was the photo negative of what had happened to me. And I thought, "Wow" — it just was instinctive.

The point here is that one simple act of kindness can have waves of betterment. It can be so transformative and so multiplicative. There's no doubt in my mind that 4,000 reps of thanking that elderly man to help me as a child inspired me when I saw Harry Reasoner interviewing Gene Lang to emulate that in a sense.

I keep thinking how instructive it would be if intentionally we just all start every day with a goal of one simple act of kindness. It doesn't have to be anything big. It can be as something as small as what happened to me when I was three. And look at the possibilities that come from it. Just imagine if 350 million Americans intentionally set out every day for one simple act of kindness.

What I will say, particularly to the young people that might be listening to this — I'll tell you, before 2000, I would say 2000 was kind of the turning point — all this demonization and criticism and the attack mode that we seem to be in, this didn't exist. It's not the way, by the way, that this generation that's perpetrating it and promoting it was raised. It's not the way we were raised. It's not the way of the 70s, 80s, 90s. You didn't have these vitriolic attacks. There was a much higher level of civility and respect. And I think down the road we'll get back to it. But so I would just say to young people: you don't have to accept today as what this country historically has been about, because it wasn't that way when I grew up. It certainly wasn't that way, and it's not going to be that way in the future.

二、commencement address：射出那一箭

Absolutely incredible story. Maybe my favorite version of this answer that I've ever had. First time I've ever done at the beginning of the episode, which makes it fun, too. What was your message in the commencement speech that you ultimately gave? What did you decide was the message to leave them with?

So I got up there and I looked on the dais and I said, "How many of you — there's like 20 people over 50 or 60 — how many of you remember your college commencement address?" None of them. Not one of them could remember.

I love to hunt and fish. So I go through and I talk about just the normal challenges they're going to face in life. And I also talked about how going through this process, I wanted to make sure I was going to say something that was memorable. So right there at the end, I said, "So whatever you do," — and I pulled out my bow and I knocked an arrow — "so whatever you do, aim high and shoot straight." When I went like this, the whole crowd goes "Woo! Aim high and shoot straight." And I had an apple on a table over here and smashed it.

Knocked it.

Yeah. So I don't know whether they still remember or not, but I know when everybody was ducking, I thought, "Oh, they're going to remember that." I told the president of the university, I said, "Listen, you don't have to tackle me or anything like that, okay? This is part of it. I'm just trying to let these people have a night that they'll remember for some time."

三、交易员的诞生：Bunker Hunt 与白银的崩塌

And it's an excuse for me to ask my other question about this difference between investing and trading. So I'd love you to explain the life of a trader in contrast to that of an investor.

If I just think about when I started, I started in 1976. Inflation was raging. I started on the floor of the commodity pits. Stuff was literally going up and doubling and cutting in half every year. The volatility was insane.

To give you an example of how crazy things got — Bunker Hunt was squeezing silver at the time and he bought about 200 million ounces at an average price of about $3.12. And between 1976 and 1980, we had crazy monetary policy. Inflation started ripping and silver went literally through the roof. Ultimately, I started out in the floor of the COT exchange. I bought a membership on COMEX, which was the metals exchange at that time. I got in the pit and was executing some orders for them sometimes along the way.

And the bottom line was, it was just the most amazing bull run. I want to say by like '79, silver was around $30 an ounce. And all of a sudden he was worth about five or six billion, which made him worth three times the next closest person in the world. Bunker says, "Well, I think this silver here is the most valuable asset and resource on earth." Someone asked him, "What are you going to do with all that silver?" And he goes, "I'm going to bury it. I'm going to bury it. In fact, what I'm going to do is I'm going to buy 20 million more ounces and I'm going to bury that too." So he buys 20 million ounces at $35. And when that happened, it just roofed. Well, it goes to $50. So at $50 now, he's worth about $11 billion and he's got a multiple of five or six on the next closest guy. I just couldn't even believe what I'd seen and how much money this guy had made.

Anyway, so that was when COMEX decided — the commercials had been just eradicated because they're sitting there holding physicals and having to put up this margin every day and their bankers going, "Yeah, I can't do it." So anyway, they made it liquidation only. Silver collapsed. It went from $50 to under $10 in the space of about eight weeks. And that had a searing impact on me — to see him go from the richest guy to virtually bankrupt in the short space of six or seven weeks. Right then and there, I would never own anything for the [long run]... or trust anything for the rest of my life.

祖父的格言与流动性 DNA

My grandfather, when I was really young, said, "Son, you're only worth what you can write a check for tomorrow." That also had a really deep impression on me. So liquidity has always been something — it was in my DNA as a child, because that was my grandfather's favorite saying all the time. And then also just my early trading days — my early trading days, I had a $10,000 account. I'd run that thing to a hundred, run it back down to zero. And also living in my early 20s, I had this friend, he's such a character, he could take $2,500 or $5 grand and run it to $2 million. Of course, the market moves were crazy like no one I'd ever seen. We were brokers at that time at EF Hutton. And we called him a mortician cuz he'd get an account with $10,000, churn about $100,000 commissions, take it to a million bucks, and then have it in deficit.

So you learned that liquidity was really important because the volatility was so huge and we're all living on the edge. The idea of owning something for the long run was laughable, because look how much money you could make by trading in the short run, because the volatility profile was off the chart.

50 年讲台上的 #1 bullet：ride a trend for the longest time

One of my mentors who got me into trading was teaching a class on investments in Virginia and asked me to come be a guest lecturer. I started in '82 and I've been doing it every semester since. I remember one of my favorite stories to tell that class — I would go through how all the greatest fortunes in the world were made. I'd always ask them, "Who's the richest guy in the world?" And back then it was Bill Gates and Warren Buffett. How did they get there? And my point to them would be: they got there by riding a trend for the long run. And that would be my big lesson to them. I'd always have two or three bullet points. My number one bullet point is: you're going to make your money by riding a trend for the very, very longest time.

I said, "There are many different ways to achieve it. You can own a company like Bill Gates or Steve Jobs, whatever. Or you can be like Warren Buffett. You can be one of those value investors who won't spend a nickel." And I used to just sit there and rail on Warren Buffett year after year after year. And I would self-congratulate myself for trashing him, going, "He just happened to be in the right place at the right time and caught this bull market. If that guy had been in Japan, forget it. Never would have happened. If it started 1989 in the Nikkei, forget it. Never would have happened. Different story. Right place, right time. Bull market genius."

Alpha vs. Beta：BBI 基金 40 年的 -0.12 相关

Oh yeah, I guess this will be my 50th year. And the difference between my trading and investing — I just take our fund, BVI fund, for 40 years it has a minus .12 correlation with the S&P 500. So you can see the difference between investing and trading. So 100% of our returns are alpha — 100%.

And I was just thinking, "Oh my god, why couldn't I be Warren Buffett? Just believe in America." And just went, "When you're down 50%, who cares? Cuz America's going to bring you through." And not that he doesn't — he works as hard as any human being — but it's that belief system that's so fantastic. As opposed to: I feel like I'm a right guard, and I've been a right guard in the NFL for 50 years where I'm just every day fighting in the freaking trenches every day. And every time someone says to me, "Gee, I want to go into trading," I said, "Go long-short, go on the equity side, go do [that]." I've always envied that belief system. It's such a great belief system and it's worked so well for so long. But I would have huge difficulty to have been in Warren's shoes in 2008-9, have a 50% drawdown. It would have had a really material impact on me. I don't think I have his calm and patience and fortitude to be able to do what he does.

9 岁的 Buffett 与"What I Realize Now"

And so anyway, I was listening to the Acquired podcast on Berkshire Hathaway and for the first time I learned that at 9 years old — 9 years old — he understood the power of compound interest. And actually when I listened to that podcast, I just went, "Oh my god, this guy is a genius and I have been the biggest fool." I've always wanted to write a book called What I Realize Now, which will go through the successive mistakes of beliefs that I've had my lifetime. What I realize now is what an idiot I was. That guy is a flipping genius cuz he understood the power of compound interest, which I somehow managed brilliantly to avoid my entire career. He understood it at nine. He went out at 17, went to Columbia, and sought out Benjamin Graham. What a genius.

Now the great thing from that podcast is that he was also so smart that he partnered with Charlie Munger. Charlie was clearly a tremendous genius in his own right because whereas Warren would buy 50-cent dollars, right, Charlie understood the power of compounding for companies that were growing all the time. And I think the two of them together — man, what a combination. But anyway, if Warren, if you happen to hear this, I'm deeply apologetic. You are the OG of compound interest and I wish I was one-tenth as smart as you are.

四、AI 与风险管理：尾部事件的 Build-Break-Iterate

Didn't you talk to him about what he thought about AI soon thereafter when we first talked about — what was that conversation like?

You cannot be a trader, investor — whatever term we use — and not be a really good risk manager. Anyone that's really succeeded investing or trading is first and foremost a great risk manager.

I went to this conference about 18 months ago and I just was so alarmed by what I heard, and then I mentioned on CNBC — and he watches CNBC every day — and sent me a note and said, "I agree with you 100%, but the genie's out of the bottle. I don't know if we can get it back in." I think he's completely on board with the belief that we have some real threats from AI.

The problem with AI is — if you think about the way AI is being deployed, practiced and deployed right now is the build-break-iterate model. Build it, break it, fix it, and iterate. If you think about it, that's been the invention model since the beginning of man. Build, break, iterate. But we've never been in a situation where the tail event — the break — can do so much damage. It could be hundreds of millions if not billions of lives.

What's so scary — when I was at this conference, which is only about 35 or 40 people with one modeler from the four biggest model companies — and when I was able to ask them pointedly, "How do you think that AI safety gets resolved?" the pretty much consensus answer is, "I think we'll finally do something about it when 50 or 100 million people die in an accident." I mean it's crazy.

My big problems with AI are: one, there's no plebiscite on this. There's no vote. There's no way for the public to say yay or nay on the pace at which we're going. And that's the way that most innovation occurs. Again, this one has a tail event on it that's so large.

I go back to when the nuclear bomb was dropped — 18 months later, our Congress and our administration was smart enough and forward-thinking to create the Atomic Energy Commission to begin to try to regulate something that had huge tails. Here we are, we're three years in and "regulate" — what are you talking about?

If there ever was a leadership position that needs to be taken by any president, it's on AI regulation right now — not just the United States, but convening with China, with all the other purveyors, to make sure that we don't do something that has catastrophic consequences. And that's just on the safety side, not even what's going to happen to social disruption. Matt Schumer came out with a very lengthy essay on how the two new models that were released six days ago are going to be so unbelievably disruptive to the workforce.

So the news in my mind keeps getting more alarming. If this was anything to do inside Tudor — this thing would have been so contained so long ago. I mean, that's what a good risk manager does. And yet there's zero risk management here.

水印、深度伪造与脑机接口

Your point about great investors and traders are great risk managers — in the face of AI, how do you think about this exogenous variable lurking?

The one thing that I think should be part of this next election — the simplest, most important thing that we can do — is we can demand that all AI is watermarked. That's the single most transformative thing that we could possibly do for the country, for the world. Make it a felony if someone knowingly violates that three times and put them in jail.

I want to know what's authentically human and what's not. And when that happens — talk about restoring trust in the country, which I think is one of the biggest problems. I would say two times since this year began, I've had very serious people call and say, "Did you see XYZ?" And of course, it turned out to be a deep fake. I think just so we can get back to some truth and honor and normal discourse, I think we've got to do that.

And the reason why it's so important is, if I go back to that conference 18 months ago, I would say a significant portion of the scientists there envision a future where humanity is going to have a chip in our brain that's going to allow us to access so much knowledge, power, etc. So I'm just kind of looking down the road and thinking — okay, we really need to know what we're reading, what we're looking at. Because again, that group, without having any feedback from the rest of America, thinks that a blended human with a machine is perfectly acceptable, is the future, should have inalienable rights. For me, I don't think that's the case. I'd vote no. I think most humans would vote no.

五、Eli Tullis 与"棉花地里的微笑"

So coming back to some of these themes of investor versus trader, risk management, etc., I know you learned a lot from this guy, Eli Tullis. And I love these lessons that were formative for you, for what became the trading legend that you now are. What did he teach you? And was there any specific episode from your time with him that taught you the biggest or most important lesson?

God, he was so strong. He was really good at executing at the maximum apogee of fear as well as greed. He was so good at smelling that, because he traded hardly anything but cotton. He would just sit there and focus, and he could just — he was just so good at waiting for exactly when there was just too much elation or when there was too much fear.

And then I would say the number one thing I learned from him was — oh, we were so long cotton one weekend, and there was a great drought, spectacular drought. And of course it rained all through the belt over the weekend. Walk in — market was limit down. He'd gotten absolutely smashed. I thought, "Oh my god, it's over."

That day for lunch, his wife brought in four of her friends. He had the most beautiful office I've ever seen in my life. And he came out and just — oh my god, he had a smile on his face. "Oh, ladies!" — and flirting with the wives. I mean, I was sitting there going, "Are you kidding me? This guy's just broke and he's acting like he's Rock Hudson or something."

Wow. Boy, I tell you what, I'll never forget that one to this day. When the going gets tough, the tough get going. And you wear it here, and you have that confidence you're going to come back. Hugely important.

六、交易是什么：拳击比喻、催化时刻、日元与比特币

Feels like the right time to ask you just what trading means to you. You've said before that the whole world is just this interconnected series of capital flows and part of the job is being on top of that and taking positions based on what's going on in the world. But mostly I interview investors who buy stakes in companies. I very rarely interview someone that is moving and putting on major positions in any sort of asset class or trading instrument. And so I would love to just hear you describe — like, what to you trading is, what is the literal act that you are doing every day with such excellence for decades.

拳击比喻

There's a couple of metaphors that come to mind. Well, let's start with boxing, because you have an opponent. In this case, it's the market. You're going into the ring with this opponent who's after you the whole time. And I'm thinking more of a classic one as opposed to a Mike Tyson one. But you're kind of pairing, jabbing, feeling each other out, looking for an opening. And then every now and then you'll have a great opening and you take a big shot and you may land one.

If I think about big shots — Bitcoin 2020, a knockout. Two-year rates 2022, knockout. You have these incredible opportunities at times. If you just sit and wait the whole time in the interim — some of it you're just gathering information, looking for those openings. You're always trying to make progress. You're always trying to win the round. But there'll be those few opportunities where you can do something really material.

催化时刻：Dollar/Yen

I love the boxing analogy and the examples you gave. Bitcoin, two-year rates, precious metals, so many things you've been through. What is going on in one of those? Like, maybe pick one and I'm literally curious what you are doing in the moments, what you are looking at, what you are studying, what supply-demand imbalances you see. Like what is the literal nature of one of these windows that opens every so often?

If you think about all the really big moves, it's probably because the market has gotten too carried away, or there's been some imbalance that's gone on for too long, or a central bank does something that they shouldn't be doing, or a government does something they shouldn't be doing. So that's I think the genesis of most of the big moves. Typically, it's going to be inspired by central bank or central government.

A good one right now in the making that's going to be interesting, I think, is dollar-yen. The yen's grossly undervalued. Has been for some time. What's the catalytic moment? That's really the point you've got to ask. What's the catalytic moment? And that catalytic moment was this new prime minister that was just elected. She has all the characteristics of a Ronald Reagan or a Margaret Thatcher or Donald Trump when he got elected the second time. And if you look at what those local currencies did, they all appreciated pretty quickly 10% off the bat.

I want to say Japan has $4.5 trillion — a $4.5 trillion net international investment position to the good with the rest of the world. Probably 60% of that's in the US and most of that is unhedged. So they just have this massive dollar liability. And all of a sudden, we've now got the most dynamic leader in certainly half a century in Japan, who's "Japan first," is going to have a very entrepreneurial way that she is going to remake that economy.

So you're looking for something that's underowned, undervalued, way out of whack. People have gotten complacent on it, and you're looking for that catalytic moment.

2022 两年期国债 & 2020 比特币

In the case of the two-year rates in '22, we had way too much fiscal stimulus. And then Powell — he overstayed being easy way too long because he wanted Biden to reappoint him. So as soon as Biden reappointed him, it was go time to get short two-year notes because you knew the Fed was going to normalize.

And then in 2020, when you saw again all the interventions, both the central bank and the Treasury, you just knew that the inflation trades were going to take off. And of all of them, what was the best one at that point in time? It was Bitcoin. Bitcoin is unequivocally the best inflation hedge that there is — more than gold, because Bitcoin is finite. There's only so much Bitcoin that can be mined.

The problem with it as an inflation hedge is — if you got into kinetic exchange, there's clearly going to be cyber warfare, and anything that you have to deal with electronically is going down, including Bitcoin. So strike one. And then secondly, quantum computing. Who knows if and when, with AI advancing as fast as it is, that we may actually have quantum computing. Now, quantum computing — someone can come in and can hack any bank and hack anything they want to. So in terms of it being a great inflation hedge — gold increases supply every year by a couple of percent; Bitcoin, there's a finite amount that can be mined, it's decentralized. And so in that sense it has the greatest scarcity value of anything.

七、泡沫诊断：1987 / 1998 / 2000 与今天

If you think about the big ones that you've lived through — '87, the global financial crisis, COVID, etc., various asset bubbles through history — maybe say a little bit about those general experiences. You're very famous for what happened in 1987, of course. And I'm curious about those ones because I'm curious what you think about today's environment. "Are we in a bubble?" is a very popular question, mostly answered by investors, less so by traders. And so I would love your perspective on the big ones you've experienced historically and how those lessons apply to today.

共同的火药：杠杆与衍生品

If I think of some really really big accidents, most of them have the same underlying reason, same underlying foundation, which is too much leverage somewhere. Most of the leverage, most of the time — if I think of the big ones — were derivative-inspired, either futures or options.

1987, that crash was 100% portfolio insurance — 100%. Had they had limits, which they didn't, it would have been a 10%, maybe 15% max. But that was 100% derivatives.

If I think about 1998, Long-Term Capital — big derivatives, they had a huge balance sheet with a lot of derivatives they were off-sides on.

2000 was a little different. 2000 was the easiest bear market I've ever seen in my whole life. It's got so many similarities to right now in the sense that the bear market of 2001 and 2002 was a consequence of all the IPOs in '99-2000. And then as they unlocked, you just had this never-ending cascade of selling.

当下的 IPO + Unlock + Buyback 反转

cascade of selling — that's a great way of putting it.

And we're getting ready... I want to say that the contemplated IPOs in the next year are going to be 5–6% of market cap. So why are we where we are right now? Because we've been retiring 2 or 3% of market cap — probably a little less than 2% market cap every year without fail for the past 10 years.

Through buybacks and things like this.

Through buybacks. And so now all of a sudden, you're going to completely reverse that math. I don't think it necessarily happens instantaneously with the IPOs, but then there'll be the unlocks. So you could see a situation where, okay, maybe go through some kind of rolling top, and then 18 months from now, six months — we'll have to look at the buyback, the unlock schedule. But you're going to want to watch those because that'll just be adding equity supply. And you're already going to be diminishing the buybacks because of all the commitment to capex from the hyperscalers. They're already going to be eating into their cash flow. So I think that's why tech has dogged it and why it will continue to dog it, because much of the funding for these IPOs is going to come out of existing tech stocks.

主权债泡沫 & 252% Market Cap / GDP

When you say "are we in a bubble" — I don't know if we're necessarily in a bubble. We're clearly so leveraged in equities in this country. We're so dependent upon firm equity prices at this point in time. And when I say leveraged — we're 252% of stock market cap to GDP. So 1929, we were I think at the top, we were 65%. And then in '87 we got to about 85 or 90%. Then 2000 we got to 170%, and now we're at 252%.

So you can just imagine — if you think about the periodicity of significant bear markets since 1970, we get kind of a mean reversion on average every 10 years. When I say mean reversion, let's say mean revert to the past 25 or 30 year P/E. So if we did that here — and again these are elevated P/Es way elevated, beyond the 20th century — that would be, say, a 30-35% decline. Well, 35% on 250% of GDP is 80-90% of GDP — the reverse wealth effect. Oh my gosh, 10% of our tax revenues are capital gains; they go to zero. So you can see the budget deficit blowing up, you see the bond market getting smoked, you can see this kind of negative self-reinforcing effect. And so it's troubling. It's troubling.

So are we in a bubble? We're clearly in a sovereign debt bubble. In the stock market, we're over-equitized as a country, have the highest individual equity weightings in the history of the country. And then the real problem is that — if you look at private equity in 2007-2008, that was about 7% of institutional portfolios. Now it's about 16% of institutional portfolios. Real estate's gone up. Infrastructure bets have gone up. We're so much less liquid than we were in 2008. So you have to be cognizant of that fact when you think about how you have your money deployed.

给"长期投资者"的反驳：22 倍 P/E 之后的 10 年

I had a friend ask me — he was a wealth manager and he hates hedge funds because of the fees. He just says, "We just need to put money in the S&P 500." He said, "If you were investing for 20 years, what would you tell somebody?" And because he knows that I'm supposed to say, "You just buy the S&P, close your eyes" — well, the problem is that if you buy the S&P at this current valuation, the 10-year return is negative when you buy with the S&P P/E of 22. That's what history shows.

So yes, the S&P is a spectacular long-term [investment] if you have a 100-year view. But that's because that's an average of 100 years, including times when the S&P 500 P/E was six and seven and eight, or one-third of what it is right now. So valuation matters a lot, and the stock market's really high, and it's going to be really hard to make money from here, I think, with any kind of long-term view.

八、一天的样子：6:15 起床、信息过载、exquisite execution

If I came and shadowed you on a given day today, how does it go? What are you doing? What does a day look like today? I know you have a system that you can be heard by all times by your execution traders. I know it's intense, but just walk us through a day in the life.

I get up at, say, 6:15. I work till 7 o'clock. I go work out till 7:45. I try to do 45 minutes of hard cardio every day. I get in front of the screens for the opening. I typically won't have any meetings till 10:00, and then I'll probably do meetings till about 12:00. Typically have lunch with someone, have a meeting after lunch, try to make sure that I've got an hour before the close and an hour after the close to be able to map out what I want to do the next day — because, again, I want to have a plan — as well as think what's going to happen that night in Tokyo, Hong Kong.

I'll go home around 5. Walk with my wife for an hour. Go up, work for an hour, come back down and eat. I'll normally watch the news and the most mindless entertainment that I possibly [can]. I used to read a book and a half a week, and then once the internet came, forget it. I'm so tired of reading by nighttime. I've read one book in the last year, which is going to be — by the way, I recommend everyone read it. It's David Wood. He's kind of a newsletter writer, but he has written what's going to be a spectacular bestseller on globalization markets. It's fantastic, and I'll bet you it gets made into a Netflix series. I bet you it's going to be a banger.

Anyway, I'll try to watch something on Netflix. Then I'll work from say 9:30 to 10:15 and then go to bed. Then I'll wake up at 6:15 and do it [again].

No, no, no.

I'll wake up at probably 2:30 or 3:00 and I'll work for half an hour, watch London open for 45 minutes, half an hour. I'll do a lot of analytical work. Then it's a quiet time. It's good. Then I'll go back to sleep and wake up at 6:15.

Wow. And you've been doing that for 50 years?

I've been doing that — yeah, I've been doing that certainly since the '80s. I feel like I work so much harder than I did 40 years ago, 30 years ago because there's more information.

800,000 封邮件与 Exquisite Execution

Oh my god, I get 800,000 emails a day. If I think about when I was a pit trader — I think about even the '80s when there was so much less information, I could spend more of my time intently focusing on what the highs and lows were going to be in the day, which are really important for execution. Doing what my boss Eli did — just waiting, paying attention, focusing. Are we at a point of maximum pain? Is there so much fear right now, which is a great time to buy? Is — does it look like it's going to go up forever, a great time to sell? You have to be intentional to be able to pick those points in a day. And when you're trading 25 different instruments, instrument by instrument by instrument — a lot of times they'll be correlated, sometimes they're not — you have to be very intentional about that.

So if while you're doing that there's 48 emails coming in at the same time, all of which could be actionable information... I think today, for me at least, it's a lot harder, I think, because the information overload distracts me from exquisite execution.

What does that mean, "exquisite execution"?

Am I buying when there's blood on the ground? And am I selling when there's complete elation? Let's take last Friday, where we had the largest down day in the history of gold and silver. So you've got to be paying attention every minute. Because when you have a 33% move in silver in one day, you just have to be so focused on that day's activity, what you're going to do on the opening, what you're going to do if the price moves through a certain price that you weren't anticipating that day.

It gets back to what I learned in Bed-Stuy. You better have a plan. You better have a plan ahead of time and it better be self-executing and you better have thought through that. And I hear it from my other friends who are macro traders. They go, "God darn it, I just feel like I'm two hours late, three hours late." And last Friday was a great example. Can be so material.

九、激情、概率与"用进废退"

If I think about what's required to do that day in day out, there's got to be just an enormous amount of passion for markets and what you do. Can you just talk about the notion of finding, nurturing, the importance of passion in a life's work?

70% 是天生的：概率论与游戏狂

It's so funny when we talk about traders, great traders — and again, I'm talking about people who are alpha generators, not investing — we had a dinner about, I guess it was a year ago Christmas, and I had four or five of my best risk-takers at dinner, and we had the debate: is a great trader born or made? I'd say it was unanimous agreement at the table that 70% of it is nature.

And when I say "born with it" — by the time I was 21, I had played [everything]. I loved games. I love chess, backgammon, Parcheesi, Monopoly, battle, gin rummy — you name it, I played it. Then I started gambling in college. I had a degree in probability theory and had never taken a math course on it, but I knew it. I was such a game fanatic and I loved it so much.

If I had to pick out the number one traits — Type A personality, incredibly curious and inquisitive, and loves competition, loves games. Because, again, our whole business is just another form of probability theory. And I still love today — I play bridge with my friends all the time. I can't get enough of that. I love any kind of game of chance. And I love trading.

棕榈滩 GP 的箴言："You retire, you die"

The other reason that I love trading — I'll never forget, my wife's Australian and we first got married back in 1989. She said, "Well, I know you guys live in New York, but I came from the beach. So when our last child graduates from college, you're taking me to the beach." So sure enough, my son turns 18, my fourth child, 2014, we're going. So we ended up down here in Palm Beach. She gives me a GP — a general practitioner. So she makes me go in. I see the guy and this guy's 83 years old. And I say to him, "You're living here in the land of the walking dead. Everyone here is basically fossilized. What is the secret to longevity?" And he goes, "It's real simple. You retire, you die."

And that had a really profound impact on me, because I realized as I get older — and maybe when you get older, you will too — boy, you better [keep moving]. If you don't use it, you're going to lose it. And that's why I try to work out two hours a day. And it's why another reason that I enjoy trading is I want to keep my mind sharp because there's too many things that I want to do when I'm — my father lived to be 100 — when I'm in my 90s that I want to do. So I think trading is great therapy for me too.

The other reason that I really enjoy trading is I'd like to make an absolute pot of money so I can give it away. I got so many causes that I want to give it to that I actually feel like this is the pursuit of nobility. So I enjoy [it] — I feel it's a privilege to wake up and I just hope I freaking kill it so I can give it away.
