四、超越"成本加成"框架

Patrick: If forced to go beyond that definition, there's only so many of those, right? You've probably invested in companies effectively attacking each category. What else would I find if I went digging on this same thread?

Scott: Airbnb is a classic huge example. When Founders Fund invested, it was still crossing from kind of a weird backpacking, couch-surfing, air-mattress-in-someone's-living-room type thing to what it is today. Not that many people were interested in sleeping on air mattresses in people's houses, but that was something that team was really into. How do we turn this into something much larger where people can meet each other and have a really authentic experience when going somewhere instead of just staying in a hotel?

That wasn't something many people were thinking about. And yet when you actually looked at it, you realized how big the market could be, and if they could cross over to a mainstream thing, it could be huge.

Sean Parker 与 Spotify

Sean Parker was on the Founders Fund team right when I joined and led the Spotify investment. The internal memo on the Spotify investment was just so well-reasoned. It was because of this history of understanding music and doing the Napster thing, and then years and years of trying to find the right company that was taking the right formula. That led to him seeing the potential of Spotify and why it was the perfect geography to start in and the perfect licensing strategy.

It's often just a really deep interest in something that's of a personal interest to a founder. They believe it should exist. They believe there should be some way to solve this and everyone's done it the wrong way and here's the right way. Sometimes people are sitting with those ideas for five or ten years.

如何从第一次会议判断

Patrick: Across all the meetings you did — first meetings — how did you improve at conducting that meeting to figure out if this was the type of person and problem that you could get interested in?

Scott: It probably wasn't even the meeting itself. It was probably just trusting your judgment more.

  • Day one: "I don't know anything. I'm going to take a lot of meetings. Some of these seem good, some seem not so good, but we need to do the work."
  • Early on: Intuition was all you had to go on, and I think is usually correct.
  • Next couple years: You get a little bit better at the job, get better at trying to analyze things. That might actually lead you astray — you start doing the analysis when you kind of already know. You should just concentrate into the fewest number of companies possible. Don't dilute your average returns by indexing.
  • Over time: Get better at asking the right questions to harness the intuition — "Okay, my gut's telling me we should dig into this."

Airbnb 案例：直觉未即刻亮起

Patrick: Could you think of a single investment that your gut was not flashing "yes" almost immediately, that you worked your way to get there and did it?

Scott: Airbnb was one of these. We did a lot of work on it. Founders Fund did a small angel check early on and then a much bigger check in the next round.

At the angel check, it was still very informal air mattresses. There had been something where some guest completely destroyed a home, a whole bunch of controversy around that. Then the company took a hard stance and said, "We will reimburse the host and we are professionalizing this." That was maybe the moment that you could tell it was going to go mainstream, and then Founders Fund made a huge investment.

If you just look back at the past, you might say, "Oh, this seems like a niche thing." But if you did the work, you could see a bunch of different trends: - Demographics were shifting to a slightly older crowd, not just backpackers out of college - Market share in different markets was increasing a lot

We sliced market share, marketing spend — you could see that all these markets they were in, they were just taking share and becoming the dominant thing. You analyze the data and it was like: "They're winning. It's over. They're going to win."

五、估值、资本密集度与"不要追便宜货"

Patrick: There are two other components of the "no competition" idea. One is valuation and price — what you have to pay. The second is capital intensity. You've invested in lots of stuff that requires lots of money to get the thing up and running and to revenue and to profitability. How did you learn about those two dimensions?

Scott: The low-competition thing typically would be associated with lower valuations, but I think trying to find value deals in venture is a dumb idea. It's not the right plan.

Maybe if you have a very small fund and you can pick up some interesting IP or: "Hey, this company maybe will never be that huge, but this is a really good deal" — and you look at multiples, and it starts to look a little bit more like PE — that can probably be okay. But for true venture, I think it's dangerous.

Either it says something about the company's ability to raise capital — that they're unable to at market prices — and unless this is the last round they need to raise or they really are going to be a capital-efficient business, that's probably a risky thing. Maybe the team is incredible at debt financing but terrible at venture financing, so they're going to switch to debt and that's going to be amazing — you could imagine a situation like that. But typically, if you're meeting a company and it's a crazy value deal, it's probably just not going to end up being that good, is what I've observed empirically.

美元部署 vs. 交易数量

Patrick: Do you think if we did an analysis of the actual dollars deployed by Founders Fund, that more of the dollars would have been deployed once the company was already popular?

Scott: That may be the case. If you look at actual dollars deployed, it's probably more the "concentrate into the winners" strategy. The way that can still be a good strategy even if the company's popular is:

  1. It's popular but not as popular as it will be.
  2. Up rounds — anchoring on the past versus looking to the future. Peter has said this many times and has guided the Founders team to think this way. If you've got a company that was growing steadily, but then there's a big up round, it's probably the case that that up round is not even enough up. If it's like a 2x up round, maybe it should be a 4x up round.

"上涨越陡，低估越严重"

Patrick: There's that famous quote of his: "The steeper the up, the greater the undervaluation." What is actually happening that makes that true?

Scott: People are just anchoring on the past. They're like, "Oh, last round was this. I guess it should be reasonable compared to last round's price." And in reality, all that matters is next round price. How do we make sure the next round's an up round? What are the catalysts going to be for further increases?

You don't get paid as an investor based on how close you were to last round's price. It's ultimately against exit price. But the only thing you have to go off of that's actual data or empirical is the past — so people are much more anchored on that.

六、创始人应该爱上自己的想法，投资人不应该

Patrick: What have you learned about how much to be in love with the problem itself? This is a good excuse to talk about General Matter too. Are you inherently fascinated by/in love with uranium enrichment, or is there some other big thing going on behind the scenes?

Scott: From the investor seat, I don't think you want to be in love with an idea. That's a risky thing. You're going to try to find a way to express it by investing, and you're going to maybe make some compromises on the team because you know this idea is just so good, its time has come. But then if the team isn't there, 90% of the time it doesn't work.

Then there's a whole thing of "horse and jockey" — can you swap out the team? But the whole founders thesis was always: no, you need the founder to run the company to have this vision and to see it through. So from the investor side, being in love with the idea is really dangerous. It can cause you to make all sorts of compromises that come back to haunt you, and it can cause you to put good money after bad despite the writing on the wall.

But on the company side, you have to be in love with the idea. It's not that rational to start a company. There are a lot easier, more comfortable ways to make money if that's the goal. Smart people who want to make money — there are so many good jobs in the finance world. Or people who just want to build — there are lots of places you can build. But if you want to actually start a company, it better be something you're really passionate about, or you think the problem is really important.

Scott 自己的故事：并非爱上铀，而是爱上核能

For me, enriching uranium never was something I was specifically excited about. I was always into nuclear energy. I always thought this was a no-brainer. The two things from sci-fi from the '60s — not just sci-fi, but what our country thought — was: we're going to be going to space and doing things in space, and we're obviously going to have nuclear energy. We went from burning wood, to chemical bonds, and now atomic energy was clearly so much more energy-dense and powerful and should be lower cost.

I never had a specific interest in uranium enrichment. But through the course of investing at Founders Fund, I went from 2010 looking at all sorts of different hardware companies — first investment I ever made was a satellite company, Planet Labs — then did a lot of different things that were outside of pure software. Last couple years at Founders Fund, I drifted back towards almost pure hardware by the end.

Then really energy — we invested in Crusoe Energy and understood the whole stranded supply of flare gas and what you could do with that. Then invested in a company called Radiant, which was the inverse stranded demand — how could you serve that demand? Maybe you could serve it with a small microreactor. Even if that microreactor's output was expensive, the stranded demand had to pay crazy rates anyway for diesel generators in a remote Alaskan village, for example, or an army base. That's a good starting point fitting the whole founders thesis of: start with a really small market and grow into a bigger market. Don't worry about your TAM, worry about owning that market and then grow from there.

My path to understanding the bottlenecks in nuclear energy was: having invested in Radiant, having met so many other advanced reactor companies along the way. All of them said the exact same thing: "We're going to make nuclear affordable. We're going to make it scalable. We're going to take this from huge construction projects to factory-built. And yet the one thing that's the hardest is not licensing. Everyone thinks the NRC is impossible to get through, but no, it's not that. We cannot get the fuel. The fuel comes from Russia. Only Russia makes it. We have to import it" — and this was even pre-Russia ban.

I spent all of 2023 looking into this, trying to understand: of the five steps of making fuel, which one is stopping it? Is it all of them? Is there not enough uranium? Is it something about the process? It was the enrichment step. I looked for a company in the enrichment step to debottleneck nuclear, and could not find anything for an entire year. Finally decided: if this is going to exist, it's got to be a new company. It's not an incumbent. It's not a government. It needs to be a new private company. This was the important company that nobody was building. This was the important problem nobody was solving that I could somehow actually contribute to.

七、政府与技术的关系

Patrick: In all the work you did, what did you learn about the relationship between governments and technology? So much of the history of technology in the US was actually military-rooted — so much of what we developed was for a military purpose and then became commercial. The two seem like they have always been deeply intertwined. What surprised you? What interested you?

Scott: My experience through SpaceX was: the government wants to work with private industry and wants to solve problems.

At SpaceX, I worked on the engine systems under Tom Mueller and did a lot of the structural thermal work on those, to help make sure they would stand up to the environments. Low cost and high performance — that's ultimately what we were optimizing for. Once those engine systems were working really well, I moved over to the Dragon capsule.

On Dragon, we were on the NASA COTS program — Commercial Orbital Transportation Services. It was a multi-hundred-million-dollar program to bring back two capabilities: launch to the space station, first a cargo vehicle and then ultimately a crewed vehicle that could dock with the space station.

The last year or two I was there was really focused on Dragon and working very closely with NASA, because we were going to dock with I guess the most expensive asset mankind had ever developed. The last thing anybody wants is any sort of issue with a private company's cargo capsule docking with the space station, either smashing into it or something.

Patrick: Collision.

Scott: You would think collision — yes, that's an issue, but the way orbits work, there's some ability to avoid it even if things are off-track. The harder thing to really get a handle on was things like thermal and pressure. Okay, you've got this vehicle, it has solar panels, what's the heating on the vehicle, and what thermal load is that driving back to the space station, and can that handle it? All these interface requirements.

We were working closely with NASA on those. These were incredibly smart people who believed in space, who had been working at NASA for decades in some cases, despite not a ton of growth in space activity. They were there because they believed it and they loved it. The opportunity to work with a company — "Okay, we have the space station, we have this program, how do we get you guys to the next step in the milestones? How do we collaborate to make sure this is safe, but that it actually happens?" — super collaborative, very positive.

My takeaway was: in industries where you have true believers in the government agencies who have been doing this for a long time, they're there because they want it to happen. There's a lot of openness and excitement to working together, if the company is credible and cares as much about safety and performance as the agency does.

八、在 SpaceX 学到的"快速造出好东西"

Patrick: In your operating time at SpaceX, what did you learn about making great things quickly — great things that work quickly?

Scott: All the classic things — there's the "Elon algorithm" at this point. I don't think it was explicit back then. It was just: "Hey, we have to get this rocket launched. Let's make sure it works. Let's not over-optimize or have analysis paralysis. Just define what the goal is, come up with a good solution. This looks like a good solution. That's a good plan. Let's run with it. Let's not deliberate for months and months over things. Let's just decide and move forward. If we're wrong, we can always go back, because there's extensive testing along the way."

Use good engineering principles, think from first principles, move fast, get it to 90–95%, not 99%. Get operational, and you can make it better later. But if we just never launch, if we never get operational, this isn't going to work.

垂直整合与本土制造

Patrick: One of the things that's most interesting to me today is this whole "learning by doing" thing — now carefully studied that it's in the iterations that you gather lots of your learnings, and there's a literal predictable curve. We've outsourced so much of this, especially in manufacturing, overseas. It's not that we do innovation and someone else does manufacturing — it's more that the manufacturing creates a lot of the innovation. How important do you think that is in the next decade or two in the US?

Scott: There's the onshoring piece, but it's maybe even simpler as just vertical integration.

Domestically you have companies in the nuclear space, aerospace, defense — many sectors where subcontracting is the norm. You subcontract a subsystem to somebody who subcontracts a component, and that component has different inputs, and they subcontract all the way down.

In aerospace at the SpaceX days, there were 30 layers of subcontractors in one system — from the space shuttle, some crazy number. In the nuclear space recently, there was one analyst call where a company was bragging about having something like 900 subcontractors — so many that they needed regional organizers of the subcontractors.

On one hand, it's like: "Okay, clearly what you're doing is really complicated. There are deep barriers to entry." But every one of those interfaces that crosses another company is typically a fixed interface that's not going to move very quickly. Everyone's designing their individual piece against preconceived interface requirements, and you end up with a really calcified architecture. You can't optimize across layers for the overall goal.

If you bring all that in-house and don't have a lot of subcontractors, you can — as an engineering team — optimize with every iteration, trade off interface requirements. "Hey, this thing you asked of me over in the electrical team, for me on the mechanical team is going to be really hard. Do you mind giving me a little bit of breathing room on that? I'll make it up somewhere else." You can have those conversations much more easily when two people are sitting side by side at a desk than when you have two separate companies across the country.

At a minimum, you need to pull the engineering together and vertically integrate — sign up for doing more of the engineering yourself and not outsource it.

Part two is the manufacturing. If you're actually going to make the thing and you're trying to design for manufacturing, you want your manufacturing co-located with engineering. That's what all the great hardware companies are now doing — at least your first-of-a-kind manufacturing co-located with engineering under the same roof. You basically talk it out: "Here's why this thing you just designed is hard for me to make. If you can make it this way, I don't need a six-axis CNC. I can use this with a laser cutter and make this part. We're going to get 10x throughput at one-tenth the cost."

At large scale you can maybe push it somewhere else and go to a lower cost center, as opposed to Southern California. But I think it's mandatory — you've got to bring it all together.

九、能源与文明

Patrick: What have you learned about the role that energy plays in a civilization or a society? It's funny — we didn't really talk about energy for a long time. The per-capita growth of energy was kind of flat for a couple of decades prior to this recent surge because of AI and data centers.

Scott: Two of my favorite charts are on this topic.

图表一：GDP 与能源消费的相关性

One is GDP per capita versus energy consumption per capita. The R-squared on it is certainly over 0.8. You cluster every country on earth, plot GDP per capita, plot energy consumption per capita, and there's a very obvious line through them. There are outliers, but it's just so predictive. Energy use in production is the ultimate proxy for human prosperity, for economic activity. This has been pretty well understood for a while but completely under-discussed.

图表二：美国电网停滞

The other chart I always look at: we grew our grid for a long time and then starting around the '90s, essentially no growth until today. China was growing a long time — in 2010 we were equal, neck and neck. This year they now will be triple the US on total energy production. So the US just needs to do something if it wants to continue to be relevant economically. You need more energy production if you want to grow your economy. Purely outsourcing stuff overseas just gives up capability, especially as it gets to new manufacturing techniques — how to scale manufacturing rapidly.

因果关系：哪个方向跑？

Patrick: How do you think the causality runs? If China surpasses us on a per-capita basis — let's say they get to double — is there some rule that will just mean they'll find ways to use that energy that raises GDP per capita? Or is the causality the other direction — as GDP rises or we find new innovations, energy catches up? If I just air-dropped three times the US per-capita energy capacity on some random country, would that country necessarily become prosperous?

Scott: The whole argument from the '90s almost until 2020 was something like: even if it would increase the economy, would you want to? Do I want to have dozens of aluminum smelting facilities in my country, or is that better done overseas? It's low margin and not interesting and not going to accelerate your economy. Why do you have people working on that when they can be in the services world and do more interesting things?

Certainly that was an argument. But I think we're seeing right now what happens when you're not proactive about it — you don't have the capability to expand rapidly. You don't want to be caught completely flat-footed when there's a large demand for energy production. Now here we have it with AI and data centers. Some of these curves look like data centers could consume, if they were allowed to, the entire grid by 2030 at this growth rate.

In theory, energy production will come online as we need it. But in practice, if you don't develop it, you may bias yourself towards other things because your energy costs are higher than other countries'. Then they take those industries and the front of the supply chain and just start marching forward where they can dictate a lot of things about the economy.

The classic view was: economies are efficient, markets are efficient. If there's a demand for energy, we will bring it online when we need to. But physical-world stuff has a timeline with it. In the US, it can be very hard to get stuff permitted. When there's unexpected demand that's very rapid in its increase, you might be caught completely off-guard.

A few years ago, if you looked at a data center and thought, "Where are they going to place it?" the answer was: where there's stranded power — stranded wind in West Texas, stranded flare gas in South Dakota, North Dakota. Now those stranded assets are gone, and it's time to build new capacity. Data centers are completely backed up on getting natural gas turbines, and nuclear takes a few years to get installed. From an energy stability, economic robustness point of view, you want to have capabilities and at least the ability to stand those up as you need to.

十、能源现状：水平但方向堪忧

Patrick: How do you feel about the state of things overall? The state of energy in the United States — both state and direction.

Scott: The state's been fine until now, and then the direction is completely flat. The issue is we have not increased at all — decades on the supply side. So the direction doesn't feel great. The state, I wish we had more. I wish we were not one-third of China. We could be doing a lot more things if energy was not just abundant but cheap. Making more high-cost energy also won't bring back certain industries.

劳动力 vs. 基础设施瓶颈

Patrick: Do you think energy is the bottleneck to us bringing back some of these industries that have left, or is it more labor?

Scott: Labor is actually the one that's probably the most responsive. If there are really compelling jobs in a field, people will shift over to that. The classic one the last couple years has been electricians — massively undersupplied. Can't get enough to build all the data centers. They're commanding very high wages — can make more than people who went and got a master's. It's an incredible career, and now you're probably going to get a lot of people shifting into it.

Labor responds. It probably takes a few years, but it's faster than building infrastructure. Infrastructure is probably the bottleneck. And is it just energy? Probably not just energy. Probably permitting can take a while. There are a bunch of different bottlenecks to getting things online.

十一、为什么相信核能

Patrick: Why do you believe in nuclear? Why that specifically?

Scott: If you look statistically, it's always been the safest, cleanest base load.

基载：经济的基础

For the economy, what you really want is base load — meaning always on, very reliable supply of electricity, not intermittent, but something you can actually design an industrial process around as the foundation of the economy. Even data centers want base load. Solar with enough storage could be base load, but typically it's not anywhere near that amount of storage. Usually solar is more intermittent, more for peak shaving. If you really want base load that businesses and industry can rely on, it has to be on almost all the time, highly reliable. Nuclear is the most reliable.

环境与安全

People only in the last 10–20 years have started caring about climate as much as they do now. If you care about climate — carbon, particulates — nuclear is the cleanest by far.

Safety is the part people question. Nuclear is the safest by far of base load too. A fossil fuel plant has known impact on human well-being from emissions. Nuclear has none of the carbon emissions. There have been high-profile accidents — Chernobyl, Three Mile Island, Fukushima — but the actual risk associated with nuclear, despite those high-profile incidents, is still far, far lower than any other form of base-load energy.

成本：真正的关键

Okay, so why have we not had more nuclear? Over time we did less and less and less — probably starting in the '70s — and you see the cost of nuclear going up as we do less of it. Where the cost is today is more than fossil fuels. Unless you really care about safety (which is acceptable from all forms) or carbon emissions, is it really worth it? Are you really going to do nuclear when it can take 10–15 years to build a reactor and they can be double the expected budget? That's really hard for a utility to stand up to.

On first principles, nuclear should be one of the lowest cost. You've got much more powerful physics, potentially smaller reactors outputting a lot of energy. Look at a pellet of nuclear fuel — the amount of coal that's equivalent to is like a ton of coal. I can take all this space and pack it in so much smaller. I don't need as much material for my reactor. I'm not going through as much mined product. It's an order of magnitude different. On first principles, nuclear should be extremely affordable — potentially much more affordable than fossil fuels. It has not been at all, but that's the goal.

十二、先进反应堆的分类

Patrick: Can you create a taxonomy of the advanced reactors? Everyone thinks of nuclear as the massive plants and stacks that they're used to seeing pictures of, which cost $10 billion to make. What are the things being tried on the reactor side?

Scott: Everyone thinks of the cooling towers. You have cooling towers in other industrial processes too — all it is is steam cooling off, hot water cooling off so it can be reused as a coolant at the start of the cycle. Nuclear is known for it. That's typically about a gigawatt-scale reactor — often an AP-1000 Westinghouse gigawatt-scale reactor design in the US.

The interesting buckets are around size, because they link to applications and markets:

Bucket 1: Gigawatt 级（电网）

  • Grid: Want to battle it out on the grid, you need gigawatt scale because it moves the needle on the grid, and you're getting the scale that can bring cost down as low as possible (at least for now).

Bucket 2: Micro-reactor（偏远社区/军事）

  • Micro reactors: On the other end of the spectrum — a stranded community doing diesel generators, bad for environment, not that cheap. You can beat them on cost. Think 1 MW scale — megawatt, not gigawatt.

Bucket 3: SMR（数据中心）

  • 100–300 MW SMRs (Small Modular Reactors) — the middle. I think all three buckets are going to be important. Gigawatt scale is for the grid, megawatt scale for remote communities or government installations. The middle scale is going to find its niche behind the meter with data centers over the next 5–10 years.

"Behind the Meter" 数据中心

Patrick: Where one unit could be dedicated to one data center — something that doesn't interrupt the cost that I'm paying as a ratepayer in whatever district I'm in, even if there's a data center there.

Scott: Exactly. The ultimate behind-the-meter thing is just an island. Imagine a fence around a few hundred acres. You've got a data center and its nuclear power plant there, or whatever other form — peaker plants, natural gas turbines, maybe even solar. It does not even touch the grid. Not impacting the grid, not doing anything to the communities — rate cost completely separate.

I actually think there's a huge opportunity to improve communities through this. If you have a data center putting billions into both compute and power production — let's say 50/50 — can we increase that power production 10%, which is only a 5% project increase, and bring (in the case of a gigawatt data center) an extra 100 MW to a community? That's huge. That would plummet utility prices for that community. At a minimum, we're going to see people going behind the meter — not disconnected from the grid — and ideally we see them actually feeding the grid and feeding base load.

BYOE: Bring Your Own Energy

Patrick: That reminds me of your BYOE concept. Maybe just spell that out a little bit.

Scott: The concept: we're doing all this investment in the grid for the first time in decades. Private companies are making the investment. They'll make the investment with "bring your own energy" — BYOE. Their biggest worry is: "Will I be allowed to build this data center in this community?" Over the last year, that's been the start of a discussion.

If they want to get to the right answer, they can tell a community: "Hey, we're going to be over here on this unused land. Are you okay with us being here and running a bunch of compute, and being net positive to your grid? Can we just plug in and give you some extra power in return for inviting us to be a neighbor?"

To me, that's a complete no-brainer. A slight cost increase during a time when hyperscalers building data centers are all about speed to power — they want to be online as fast as possible because this is the contest, who can get to the greatest scale first — is a very low cost compared to the advantage of deploying quickly in a community and being invited there versus being rebuffed. Modern data centers can do completely closed-loop cooling, so the water issue is not an issue. It's really just this power thing.

先进反应堆可能失败的原因

Patrick: What would be the explanation for these new advanced reactors ultimately not working? What keeps you up at night? This is out of your control — you're not designing these advanced reactors yourself.

Scott: 1. They don't have fuel to operate. That's a showstopper. We're trying to eliminate that risk. 2. They're too expensive. The physics will work — I don't think anyone's too worried about that.

You have dozens of incredible companies with great engineers trying to solve the advanced reactor problem. They'll typically pick a form factor of the right size to focus on a certain market — micro reactor for remote, SMRs for data centers, very large gigawatt scale for the grid. SMRs ultimately have the plan of being cheaper than gigawatt-scale construction projects for the grid. The question is going to be: is their energy production cost low enough?

For these advanced reactors, it's not just a fuel availability problem — it's a cost question. The fuel can be half of their total cost, and long-term, enrichment will be half of the fuel cost.

十三、General Matter：产品、业务、公司

Patrick: For General Matter specifically, I'd love to hear at three levels how you're thinking about building this thing: the product level, the business level, and then the company itself. Starting with the product — how have you figured out what the market wants or needs?

核燃料供应链的五步

Scott: This goes back to understanding the fuel cycle — the fuel supply chain for nuclear. There are five steps:

  1. Mine uranium out of the ground
  2. Convert it into a gas
  3. Enrich it
  4. Deconvert it back into a solid
  5. Form fuel pellets

Five steps to go from ore in the ground into a little pellet into a fuel rod that goes into a reactor. The US has good capability across the board except in enrichment. It's the one area that we don't have any commercial-scale capability. There's some R&D capability but nothing commercial-scale or commercially competitive with Russia and Europe.

三个核燃料悬崖

We decided to hone in on the enrichment step. This bottleneck is leading to the three "nuclear fuel cliffs":

  1. Cliff 1 — HALEU supply chain: The same problem we heard from all the SMR companies. HALEU is the fuel that advanced reactors need — uranium enriched to about 20% U-235 (the fissile material). They want it at 20% because it helps their reactor be smaller. If there's no reliable supply in the next few years, it's going to make it very difficult for them to scale up. So far, they have small amounts from the DOE to prove their reactors work, but not to scale.

  2. Cliff 2 — 2028 LEU cliff: When the US's ban on Russian uranium imports goes into full effect, January 1, 2028. Through an act of Congress, we're no longer allowed to import Russian uranium. Today Russia is about 25% of US imports, so 25% immediately goes away in 2028. Utilities will start eating into inventories and trying to work out deals to import more from Europe. This cliff is LEU — low-enriched uranium at 3–5% enrichment.

  3. Cliff 3: At some point in the future, the US stockpile of enriched uranium for propulsion for the Navy runs out.

We're focused on the nuclear energy side: LEU and HALEU. The most urgent cliff is HALEU for advanced reactors. Coincidentally, this is the small, emergent market we can go after that we don't think any incumbent will go after on the same time frame. We can serve those advanced reactor customers that we've known for years. Phase two: LEU production for the 94 reactors on the grid today — a $2–2.5 billion market in the US.

与武器级浓缩的区别

Patrick: What's the relationship between this kind of enrichment and a more weapons-oriented type of enrichment?

Scott: All enrichment is for the most part the same. Think of it as a refining or distillation process — you're just refining further. The product we make is enrichment services. You can run material through from natural uranium as it comes out of the ground, to any arbitrary level of enrichment. Commercially relevant: LEU 3–5% for existing reactors, HALEU 20% for advanced reactors.

The fundamental unit of enrichment capability is measured as separative work units (SWU) in the enrichment field. We provide that service and sell it to utilities and advanced reactors.

When you see countries going to weapons grade, they're trying to go north of 20% — the internationally agreed limit. There's strong international consensus we should all stay below 20. You see countries going to 60%, saying it's for their nuclear energy industry — it's pretty suspect. Weapons grade is well over 90%. It's not different technology, but applied in a different way and in a completely non-international-consensus setup.

Patrick: Is the percentage the right way to think about that — like purity?

Scott: Yes. There's U-238, U-235 as it comes out of the ground, and other isotopes. You basically want to filter out U-238 to get U-235 — the fissile isotope.

铀矿储量

Patrick: What do we know about the stock of ore in the world?

Scott: Certainly hundreds of years of supply in the ground. US has supply. We have active mining in the US. Canada has even more supply, higher grade. Australia and Kazakhstan are huge deposits — US imports from Kazakhstan. On the ore side, not an issue.

业务构建

Patrick: How do you build a great business on top of this product?

Scott: I wouldn't say it's so easy at all. The latent demand has not been obvious until the last couple years. Any large latent demand is in the last 24 months — linked to the thought that there's going to be a lot more data centers doing AI inference or training. Before that, there wasn't a lot of new nuclear being talked about. There was need for HALEU, but that's a small market.

In the 2010s, really no new capacity building anywhere — certainly not for LEU production, certainly not for HALEU. The fact that this is a growing market — we're still betting on that. It's not the case that we're currently deploying tons of new reactors in the US, but we think that's coming. The market we believe in deeply — I don't know the entire industry believes the demand is there. They've been through nuclear renaissances before that didn't pan out. Their point of view: "We'll build it when we know there's a need."

On ease: it's a proven thing — people have done this before, but it's still not easy. Some retro technologies were done in the past, and we lost a lot of capability because we didn't do it for so long. Enrichment has been an industry without a lot of change since the '90s — not a lot of progress, not a lot of leveraging of new technology. There's a lot of hard engineering work to get back up to modern.

Even once that's done, you're building facilities that are a million square feet — multi-billion-dollar infrastructure projects. In the same way that building the first Tesla Roadster was hard, the real challenge was how to scale up. It's extremely underappreciated how much goes into standing up a huge industrial facility. That's the hard part.

北极星指标：$/kg-SWU

Patrick: How do you think about the North Star metric for the business? If I equate it to SpaceX — cost per kilogram to orbit — what's your chart going to look like?

Scott: They had cost per kilo to orbit, specific to low Earth orbit. You can decompose the orbital piece into a velocity — almost like cost per kilo to some velocity.

Our version is cost per kilo of uranium to some enrichment level — cost per kilogram to 3–5% or to 19%. Which can be described as cost per separative work unit (SWU), where SWU is the industry measure of enrichment: basically kilos times some entropy reduction or separation or organization of the material. Our North Star metric is dollars per kg-SWU.

是否对客户成功至关重要？

Patrick: Does that map onto the value creation cycle of your customer?

Scott: Depends on the reactor type:

  • Gigawatt scale AP-1000 light-water reactor (3–5% LEU): cost of fuel as a percent of overall cost is quite low. It doesn't really matter. What they want is availability, reliability, diversity of suppliers. Most of the cost is capex for the huge $10B project.
  • Advanced reactors (HALEU, 19.75%): matters a lot. Fuel can be more than half of their energy production cost. It requires more input material to refine all the way there.

Fuel has been purchased as five different services. A utility purchases uranium and upgrades to the uranium as it goes through the supply chain. It's really a tolling business — they own license to the material, and book transfers all the way through. They pay different people per unit of service. The service we provide is priced in dollars per SWU.

For LEU, enrichment is maybe a little less than a third of fuel cost. For HALEU, even more — we think it's going to be the dominant cost driver of fuel cost.

把选择与 Founders Fund 框架联系起来

Patrick: So back to tying it to the Founders Fund stuff — you've found the highest cost segment of the workflow, sold to the highest percentage of cost, new customer that's emerging, small, that you can go own. That's how you ultimately build a great business — by driving all of that force through the narrowest possible choke point.

Scott: If you want to put it in investment terms and business strategy terms, that would be it. But if you go back to the very beginning, it just turned out that this was the thing most necessary. This enrichment step was why we didn't have the more enriched fuel that advanced reactors needed. You realized the US had lost the ability despite being number one in the world during the '80s. This bottleneck was going to be the bottleneck to all of nuclear energy if you believed in advanced nuclear energy, and this is the thing we had to solve.

We did it because no one was doing it, and it was extremely important and urgent. I think that ended up corresponding to a very good entry point for the market. But the two are really linked. It's not a coincidence that if you're solving a problem that's urgent, that no one else is doing, on a small but emerging market — it will completely fit that framework.

十四、构建伟大公司：团队即公司

Patrick: The final step is building a great enduring company — by which I mean not just a flow of cash flow, but a collection of people, a set of impacts on the country. What does that mean to you?

Scott: It comes back to the team. Naval has said this many times: "The team you build is the company you build." To us, it's team DNA.

选址决策：在哪里建公司？

We thought about where to put the company — a big question at the very beginning. You look at who you actually need on the team — of course nuclear engineers, but also great mechanical, electrical, software, chemical engineers. Every type of engineering.

Turns out the percent of the team that's nuclear engineers is single digits. We don't need that many. We're not doing a reactor. There are actually no nuclear reactions in our process. We need to make sure there are no nuclear reactions — but to do that, you don't need a huge number of nuclear engineers.

Do we go where the nuclear engineers are, or where all those other types of engineers are? On dimension one — nuclear engineers, you don't need many. You need great ones. Where do we find them? They're just scattered everywhere. There's no one place to go. So we had to go the other option — where all the other engineers are, specifically hardware and aerospace: Southern California.

工程驱动的团队 DNA

The team DNA we wanted to set up: this is an engineering-driven company. We're not doing a science project. No new physics. No going down a multi-year R&D path that's uncertain. We need to get this operational as fast as possible. This is an engineering problem.

  • Engineering cost out of the system
  • Engineering performance up
  • Engineering cost of capital down
  • Engineering schedule to be as tight as possible
  • Even engineering our own buildings — taken from the Tesla playbook

Don't hire a GC and outsource everything — you lose total control, the schedule is not in your hands anymore. You need to build your own in-house engineer-procure-construct (EPC) firm as a team to run your construction projects. It's one of the hardest things — not just engineering, not just manufacturing, but actually construction of millions of square feet on schedule, on budget.

The whole DNA of the company: drive to deliver for the industry as quickly as we can, while being safe, reliable, every other dimension. Go quickly because the industry needs it. Don't have analysis paralysis. Don't deliberate over things that don't matter. Get a service live that can deliver for the US industry, then bring cost down over time. Everything's oriented around schedule and cost.

运营公司：Scott 的一周

Patrick: How do you run the company? If I were to follow you around for a week, what would it look like?

Scott: It changes over time. My strong preference is internal-facing — to be there helping to do the work internally.

Right now we're in a phase of company building and team building — rapidly hiring. A lot of my days right now is interviewing people. I'm the last step in the interview. What I'm screening for is: does this person have the attitude? Skills have already been screened for. Do they care about this problem? Do they know there are lots of places they could work, but this may be the only place they can work on this problem in a private company? Do they know there are probably places they could go make more salary, have an easier job, a more predictable schedule?

We're trying to do this mission that requires us to go as quickly as possible and to deliver before end of decade — for both the advanced reactor industry and the existing reactors on the grid (which is 20% of our grid). This is going to mean a lot of late nights, weekends, just working as hard as we possibly can. Is that something they've done before? Is it something they like doing? Do they know what they're signing up for? Are they really motivated by this?

A lot of my day right now is making sure we have the right early people on the team to build the right culture for them to then go recruit and hire the next people. The early team you build is the company you build. That drives a lot of my external-facing stuff — got to get the word out to the right people so they know what we're working on, why this is important, why this is the bottleneck to scaling nuclear and scaling energy in the country, and why they should join our team.

十五、核能的恐惧与现实

Patrick: How do you reason through what seem to me like illogical, data-free fears that people have about nuclear in general? You started to address them earlier — the amount of waste is actually quite small, fits in like this room. Bad events — Chernobyl and Three Mile Island — were bad, but if you dig into the data, nuclear is very safe. These fears really do seem to be the reason we don't have a lot more of it. How would you pitch people on not being worried?

Scott: You're pointing out the difference between acute and chronic events. Acute events that are attention-worthy — people remember those. The things that are just low-level in the background — people don't think about. Even car crashes every day.

You can think of it in terms of catastrophic events. You can think of it in terms of your health — what are we chronically doing that's unhealthy, taking its toll, versus acute things. People have a strong bias towards the acute. But often the right thing is to look at the data.

I don't think most people find that compelling. The more compelling thing in nuclear for me is: think of a world in which we are not constrained by energy. We can all agree that's good, because it's base load, very affordable, brings down your rates, safe, and clean. We can debate those and look at the data, but imagine that future world.

The way to make nuclear really compelling is actually to check the final box: cost. Again, why have we not had more nuclear? The schedule has been uncertain. Costs often above projected. When you look at the total package, it's not cheaper than coal, natural gas, or hydro. So why do we want this?

If you told someone: "Nuclear is safe — we can dig into that. Understood you're not interested if it's more expensive. But what if it's cheaper? What if your utility bill got cut in half?" I think people would suddenly find that extremely compelling for something that could be tens of miles away from their house, still powering the grid.

十六、为什么没有更多 Founders Fund、更多 Scott Nolan？

Patrick: To bring it back to where we began — why do you think there are not more Founders Funds and more founders pursuing some radically different unique vision for the future? It doesn't seem like there are many of these people. There's only one Founders Fund. Few investors have taken on this mantle of big, contrarian, unique projects. It seems so strange.

Scott: It's all case by case, even for Founders Fund. It's not a programmatic thing that Founders Fund has tried to do. It's almost the opposite. Founders Fund recruits for people that want to be investors — that don't want to be entrepreneurs. If they want to be entrepreneurs, they should go be entrepreneurs. The two roles are extremely different. For people who have done both, they realize how different operating is than investing. Founders Fund explicitly selects for people that want to be investors.

And yet once in a while a company will start — it almost feels like a disservice to not go start it. In this case, a decade-plus of investing, met all the reactor companies, they all say the same problem, no one's doing it. You look at the final thing: important problem, no one's doing it, maybe I can do it. You look at what it takes to actually go do that — wait, my background is really aligned with that. If I don't do this, then — if the goal is to have an impact on the world, we can have positive impact through investing, but a much greater impact through starting this specific company — it's actually wrong to not go do that.

There's a desire to not start companies, full stop, and then only with extreme exceptions will we start them. If it's an extreme exception, it makes sense that it doesn't happen that often.

投资人 vs. 运营者的生活质量

Patrick: Have you experienced it that way so far?

Scott: I like what we're doing a lot, but in terms of quality of life, it's not as good. Any entrepreneur would tell you that. Once you start a company, you're really taking the harness and latching on. Whatever it is you have to go do, it's not optional. If you're an investor and a deal comes across your plate but you're a little bit too busy, it is easy to let that one go.

When you're running a company, stuff comes up you just have to deal with. That's also a function of the time horizons. When you're operating, you see the feedback cycle very quickly. As an investor, that company you might have chosen to not meet with to preserve quality of life — that could be the next great company. You might not realize you're not doing well until five years from now when you don't have that return.

Some people are just wired a certain way. Even in the investing role, I would take 10 meetings a day — to the point of definitely diminishing returns, but wanting to work hard and make sure you see everything.

The other thing about investing when properly understood: you actually don't need to invest in that many companies. While you can brute-force it and boil the ocean, try to meet everyone and be extremely helpful to everybody, all that ultimately matters is a few companies per vintage, and you just have to be into those. Certainly as a VC — much more than private equity or hedge funds — you don't actually have to work that hard meeting everybody. You can dive in as much as you want into specific companies, value-add, help them, be their preferred investor. But it is not a job of labor. It's really a job of ideas and thinking. The quality of life of being surrounded by smart people, talking through ideas and thinking, can be a lot more comfortable than rolling up the sleeves and diving in.

十七、结语与最后一个问题

Patrick: I for one am glad you're now working on the problem you're working on. It seems enormously high leverage, and I certainly hope the future you might enable is the one we get to see. There's very little argument against more, cheaper, cleaner energy. An incredible project.

When I do these interviews, I ask everyone the same final question: What is the kindest thing that anyone's ever done for you?

Scott: The recent example completely linked to this conversation is the transition from Founders Fund to General Matter. You have to say: what was this thread that led me here? It goes all the way back to being recruited into Founders Fund by Peter, then support through a decade of investing, and then as I wanted to shift focus to this — total support for doing that.

Obviously Peter tried to beat up the idea and make sure it's good, but being along for that journey and then ultimately joining our board as one of very few boards that he's on — really appreciate that.

Peter 最难回答的挑战

Patrick: What was the hardest part about him beating up the idea? What was the hardest aspect to get through?

Scott: All the abstract meta-level questions. Going back to: we haven't had any new nuclear in a long time — why is that? Is nuclear just regulated to death? Is regulation meant to stop it, basically make it illegal? All these questions of: why is it really the case we're going to get more nuclear now?

That challenging forced us to ask a lot of hard questions that we feel great about our answers to. Even the one you asked — are you dependent on every SMR succeeding to make a real market? On the HALEU side, yes, HALEU production ultimately will be SMRs that create that demand. But to make HALEU, you have to make LEU — and LEU is what goes into the grid today. Our technology works in LEU also. We'll be building LEU capacity. There's a $2B-plus US market and a similar-sized market in our allied partners that we can sell into. There's a known good market.

Worst case, there's an existing market and we can start a business there, and time is then on our side for when and if nuclear grows — which we think it will grow very rapidly.

A lot of these conversations with Peter were in 2023 — before the AI data center boom. Now it's extremely obvious why we need this. We're in a good position.

Patrick: Scott, this has been so much fun. Thank you so much for your time.

Scott: Thank you.

【赞助结语】

Your finance team isn't losing money on big mistakes — it's leaking through a thousand tiny decisions nobody's watching. Ramp puts guardrails on spending before it happens. Real-time limits, automatic rules, zero firefighting. Try it at ramp.com/invest.

As your business grows, Vanta scales with you, automating compliance and giving you a single source of truth for security and risk. Learn more at vanta.com/invest.

Ridgeline is redefining asset management technology as a true partner, not just a software vendor. They've helped firms 5x and scale, enabling faster growth, smarter operations, and a competitive edge. Visit ridgelineapps.com.

Every investment firm is unique, and generic AI doesn't understand your process. Rogo does. It's an AI platform built specifically for Wall Street, connected to your data, understanding your process, and producing real outputs. Check them out at rogo.ai/invest.

The best AI and software companies — from OpenAI to Cursor to Perplexity — use WorkOS to become enterprise-ready overnight, not in months. Visit workos.com to skip the unglamorous infrastructure work and focus on your product.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、隐藏的系统性发现：Scott 的职业轨迹其实是"逆向瓶颈追踪"
表面上看，Scott 从 SpaceX 到 Founders Fund 再到 General Matter 是一条从工程师到投资人再到创业者的"常规进化"。但真正的系统性模式是：**他每一次转换都在追溯更上游的瓶颈**。
- SpaceX：解决"把东西送上太空"的瓶颈 - Founders Fund：解决"让正确的公司获得资本"的瓶颈 - General Matter：解决"让所有先进核反应堆能够运转"的单点瓶颈（燃料浓缩）
这里有个**令人意外的观察**：Scott 实际上是在执行一种"反向康威定律"——组织不是塑造产品，而是产品中的瓶颈反向塑造了他的组织归属。每一次他不是"选择下一份工作"，而是**被系统中最窄的节流阀吸过去了**。这解释了他为什么说"不去做就是失职"——当你把自己视作流体，瓶颈就是你的宿命。
## 二、跨领域连接：浓缩铀的商业模式 ≈ 半导体 EUV 光刻机 ≈ 咖啡烘焙
这可能是整场对话中最被低估的洞见。General Matter 做的不是"卖铀"，而是卖**"分离功单位"（S

item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最意外的观察：这不是一场"战争"，而是一场"叙事套利"

表面上大家都在问"川普为什么打伊朗"，但 Allison 的回答藏着一个更颠覆的结构——他自己也不知道，连白宫内部都给出了"六个开战理由、五个胜利定义、无限个结束时间"。这不是信息不足，而是刻意的叙事过剩。

这其实和 Allison 在达沃斯对川普的描述形成了一个隐秘的闭环：先制造入侵格陵兰的戏剧张力，再"把枪从桌上拿走"，最后宣布"我们已达成框架协议"。伊朗战争的剧本结构，和格陵兰真人秀的剧本结构，是同一个。 区别只是——格陵兰的演员是记者，伊朗的演员是 B-2 轰炸机。

跨领域连接——这种"叙事冗余"在金融市场里有个名字叫 optionality pricing（期权定价）：当你不知道哪个结局会发生时，同时持有多个互斥的故事本身就是一种战略资产。川普不是一个"糟糕的沟通者"，他是一个把行政权力期权化的操盘手。六个理由不是混乱，是六个看涨期权——哪个涨了行权哪个。这是华尔街交易员的直觉，被搬进了战争决策室。

二、被忽略的"镜像结构"：BB 是伊朗最高领袖的对偶

Allison 反复强调"这是 BB 的战争"，用亚哈船长追白鲸做比喻。但有趣的是——BB 用二十年单一议程（摧毁伊朗核计划）绑架了四届美国总统的外交政策，而哈梅内伊用同样单一的议程（摧毁以色列）绑架了伊朗八千万人的命运三十多年。

这两个人是彼此的心理镜像：都是靠"存在一个必须被消灭的宿敌"来获得国内政治合法性的领导人。一旦对方消失，自己的权力基础也会瓦解。这就是为什么 Allison 说"BB 会一直打到政权更替"——因为没有哈梅内伊的 BB，和没有 BB 的哈梅内伊，在政治心理学上是同一个空位。

这里有一个惊人的系统性真相：长期敌对的两个强人领袖，构成了一个共生系统。他们彼此需要对方存在，胜过需要自己的人民认可。这种结构在商业里叫"宿敌双寡头"（Coke vs Pepsi, Boeing vs Airbus），在政治里叫"永久战争经济"。

三、最反直觉的结构性洞见：80-80-9 框架是人类文明的"幸存者偏差"

Allison 提到的三个数字——80 年没有大国战争、80 年没有核弹爆炸、仅 9 个核国家——他说这是"史上最长和平"。但真正让人后背发凉的是他随口一句："本来应该已经发生了。古巴导弹危机时就可能发生了。"

我们生活在一个应该已经毁灭但没有毁灭的时间线里。这不是乐观主义，而是统计学上的极端尾部事件——就像你醒来发现自己连续 80 次抛硬币都是正面。

跨领域连接——这和塔勒布的"黑天鹅"理论形成了一个未被发现的对偶：我们通常担心"尚未发生的坏事"，但 Allison 让我们思考一个更诡异的问题——"尚未重演的坏事"。核战争不是低概率事件，它是已经被人为压制了 80 年的高基线概率事件。这和生物学里的"免疫系统维持"同构——不是疾病没来，而是有一套极其昂贵的机制在每时每刻阻止它。非扩散体制、相互确保毁灭、情报预警——这些是文明的"免疫细胞"，而我们正在一个接一个地削弱它们（北韩、巴基斯坦，现在可能加上更多）。

最大的风险不是"会不会有新的核国家"，而是我们已经忘了是什么让旧秩序延续了 80 年。当 Allison 说"应该给建造它的人感恩"时——这是一个 86 岁的学者对即将失忆的文明发出的最后提醒。

四、一个隐藏的经济学底层联系

Allison 在结尾谈到美国贫富差距的不可持续时，看似离题，其实闭合了整场对话的环。注意他的措辞："如果最上层 10-20% 拿走了 70-80% 的蛋糕，这在民主制里不可持续。"

把这个数字放到他前面讲的 80-80-9 框架旁边——你会发现一个惊人的共振：美国国内的财富分配比例，和全球核武器的国家分布比例，结构几乎相同（9 个核国家里，5 个拿走了绝大部分权力）。

这不是巧合。垄断结构在任何尺度上都倾向于自我复制——从个人财富到国家权力，从芯片制造（96% 在台湾一个岛上）到稀土加工（大部分在中国）。Allison 所有的地缘政治焦虑，本质上都是一个学科的同一个问题：过度集中的脆弱性。

这解释了为什么他同时担心 DSA 社会主义者当选和中国崛起——它们是同一种系统失衡的两种症状。

📝 转录文本

开场与嘉宾介绍

Welcome to another episode of the All-In Interview. I'm really excited to be joined by uh back to All-In Harvard professor Graeme Allison. Professor Allison, welcome back to AllIn.

Thanks for having me.

We last had you at the uh summit in Los Angeles, so it's great to have a conversation. A lot's happened since we last got together. For the audience, Graeme Allison's the founding dean of the Harvard Kennedy School. He's advised every Secretary of Defense since Kessinger. He's helped dismantle 12,000 Soviet nuclear weapons and his book, Destined for War, remains the definitive framework for understanding the US–China relationship. I think it's worth kind of bringing forward some of the things that have happened in the world since we last met. I'm going all in.

赞助商口播：Airwallex

If you were building a global financial system from first principles today, you wouldn't build it on 50-year-old legacy rails. You'd build airwalls. It's the single platform for global accounts, cards, and payments that treats the entire world like a local market. Stop paying the legacy tax and start building the future at airwall.com/allin. Airwallix. Build the future.

一、伊朗战争：迷雾中的五点判断

Let's start with Iran. The US and Israel are in the middle of an attack on Iran and the Supreme Leader is dead, but the regime is still holding on to power. Where do you think this conflict will take us? And are we sleepwalking into World War II?

Well, thank you again for having me back. I You'll have a great podcast. I enjoyed the summit and I'm sorry we're not there in person to to catch up but I think about a man uh how much to say so this could be a long lecture let me just do four or five quick points.

第一点：巨大的不确定性

First point, most important point, is that there's more questions than answers though there's a huge level of uncertainty currently about what's happening and about what's likely to happen. Part of this is Clausitz's famous fog of war, but there's a fog of war that's actually increased because we got two big fog machines uh adding to the confusion — namely Trump and the administration on the one hand and BB on the other — and then we got all the chattering class around this.

So I would say: why did Trump decide to go to war now? There's six different reasons he and the administration have given and each one they back out. What's the objective? Uh there's five different objectives backing around. And when is this war going to end? You know, a day, a week, a month, who knows? So, it's very uncomfortable to recognize how uncertain things are. And most of the conversation about it tries to make it more confident. But I think the place to start is there's a huge amount of uncertainty.

第二点：美以军力与情报的惊人表现

Having said that, I'll still give you my prejudices about a few of the points. Point two, this has been an extraordinary uh demonstration of supreme military power and supreme intelligence power. US military and the intelligence community and the Israeli counterparts have been way way way off the charts and I think they should make all Americans proud of what's been built over this decade of two decades of investment but especially the past decade.

第三点：不必为伊朗政权流泪

Uh third point, there's no tears to be shed for Ham and Islamic Republic regime. It's an evil leader, an evil regime. There's no bad thing that could happen to them that we shouldn't be cheering out of that.

第四点：摧毁容易，建设难

So, next point though, breaking something's a lot easier than building something. And destroying targets is something that our military knows very well how to do. Building a new regime, regime change is something that we know historically uh doesn't work very well, at least in Iraq and Afghanistan. We had every word said that's been said in this case. And we then went in all-in and spent more than a billion dollars of many, many thousands of American lives. And when we went through Afghanistan, the Taliban were ruling. When we left Afghanistan, the Taliban were ruling. So I would say unfortunately it's against a backdrop that's hard.

第五点：这是 BB 的战争

The next point slightly more controversial, but I think this is BB's war. If your reader or your listeners don't remember Mumby Dick, they should go look at it again. Ahab had this fixation obsession with the white whale and tried to find a way to kill the white whale whenever he could. For the last two decades, that's been BB's number one, number two, number three agenda. He's tried to sell that war to uh Obama, uh to Trump won, to Biden, and how he succeeded in mesmerizing Trump, whom I thought had his number, I'm surprised.

So, I'm pro-Israel, but anti-BB in this respect. And I think that uh I understand he's brilliant politically. But I don't think this uh uh the arguments that were made that uh that Trump has actually repeated — that Iran was about to attack us. I see no evidence for that. That Iran was about to get a nuclear weapon. I see no evidence for that. That Iran was building an ICBM that was going to attack the US. I see no evidence for that. There's many many bad things about the Iranian regime, but not most of the claims that that were made. So, I I look at this and I hope it turns out well, but I remember that in wars very frequently, it's easy to get in and it's quite difficult to get out.

二、川普的动机：战略、直觉，还是 BB 的魔术？

Let me double click on that point. You mentioned there's multiple reasons that the administration has messaged for doing this, multiple views on what success looks like, multiple timelines on ending it, and that this is BB's war. How do you think Trump was motivated to do this? What is the real motivation? And I will ask this in the context of the big delegation and visit to China coming up next month. Is Trump motivated fundamentally to create leverage with China and use this as a tool to try and maximize his negotiating leverage going into the Chinese negotiations?

That's a great question and I wish that the answer was this was all part of a grand strategy. That would make me feel hugely better. But uh I try to do Trumpology and I was actually having dinner last night with Bill O'Reilly, a former student who I think is one of the better Trumpologists, were trying to interpret and we were going back and forth through this.

It's easy enough to see why the window tactically the window in which this attack occurred was chosen because here you got 40 you got the supreme leader and 40 of the other leaders of Iran out in public. So that's an opportunity as a target and the best opportunity that's come along. The question is why would you be attacking them and especially Trump given his uh campaign and his previous views about not repeating the mistakes of the people that took us into endless windless wars.

And I think it's — I mean I I think BB is a magician. I think his single-mindedness uh is impressive. I think his agility in making arguments and developing arguments is impressive. How he managed to persuade Trump this was a good idea for the US. Now I don't have a good idea. And I think that uh I think if I listen to the reasons why even the people closest around them have given or that Trump have given, they don't seem persuasive.

Now partly Trump is erratic. Uh partly he sort of you know acts on the basis of instincts — uh somehow it came to him that this was uh you know something that could be done. I think actually BB painted a pretty uh — I mean the upside of this picture if if it's successful and I hope now that we've gone into it it would be would be redefining security in the Middle East for a generation. So getting rid of the Iranian regime, as the BB and company have gotten rid of the proxies of the regime earlier — which again since October 7 been extremely impressive — could possibly you could saying well there's going to be a whole new Middle East. And if the Middle East were all like the UAE, uh that would be fantastic, fantastic for all the human beings that live there. So I think maybe he got excited by the upside, not quite uh careful enough about the risks or the downside.

Because the number of ways in which this could go wrong — you know, if if we woke up tomorrow or today and a couple of ships have been sunk or a couple hundred of Americans have been killed, this going to have a very different image.

三、马杜罗行动、中国访问与"魔法棒"心理

And you think that this is independent then of the action with Maduro and independent of the big delegation headed to China for a big negotiation next month.

Well, I wouldn't say entirely independent. I would think with Madera — so Trump I think this again this is my whatever armchair Trumpology — but I would say initially he thought tariffs were a magic wand and every leader thinks if you have a magic wand that's fantastic. He's tried the tariff magic wand and discovered it didn't quite have all the effects that he imagined on liberation day.

Uh he then discovered the American military which genuinely is a huge one, and the Maduro operation was just spectacular. I mean again it's a military and intelligence operation. Brilliant. No other country in the world could even imagine doing something like that. And that actually you know makes all of us stand taller. It can also provide a little — or historically encourages hubris and an imagination that this magic wand can work in many other arenas.

I don't mind at all the proposition that every other country should take account of the fact that the US has the most remarkable military and intelligence community in the world. And that's not a bad backdrop for China and for a China trip. So, I agree that things may not be completely disconnected, but I think in terms of the timing — I mean, right now, when is the war going to be over? If you talk about 3 or 4 weeks, we're — we'll already he'll be in China March 29. So, my inclination again, if we were just doing prediction markets, is that he's going to find a way to declare this over before then because he needs a little time to get his mind set on that. But if you ask the folks at the defense department or if you ask the people in Israel, they're thinking this is another month or beyond this award.

So again, just things I don't think are quite, you know, in sync.

四、如果伊朗走向民主？——理想与现实的落差

Jason's joined us. Professor Allison, it's always a treat to have you on.

Nice to see you. It's just amazing to have you on here and to give us this time. If things were to go extremely well and Iran went from a monarchy to a dictatorship and now in this third act here in the modern day to a democracy and you actually had a democracy with 93 million citizens in the Middle East, what impact would that have on the region? We've seen the Gulf monarchies evolve human rights, but they're still monarchies. You have one other I I guess democracy in the region in Israel, but this could be extraordinary if it was a democracy. But what would that mean to the to the global chessboard?

Well, it's a good question. I mean, if this could occur, it would be spectacular even I think but I would say this is way way way too ambitious. There's a hundred things that would be wonderful outcomes better than what's likely that would be short of that.

I remember uh in Iraq after Bush failed to find nuclear weapons — which was the initial I would say somewhat cucked up rationale for our going into Iraq — Kandi Rice who was the secretary gave a speech about well how wonderful it'll be when Iraq is a democracy and market economy, quote, "radiating stability through the Middle East." So we're pretty good at doing "I have a dream," you know, in that version. I think that's a way way way stretch.

I would be very happy with a regime there that simply was not about uh building nuclear weapons, was not about building missiles that threaten the US and our friends and allies in the region, and not about supporting proxies. And if it's a pretty cruel regime, unfortunately, that's unfortunate for the Iranian people, the Persian people. Uh when I'm a student of history, I love Persia. You know, I remember uh Cyrus and I remember uh the Pelpeneisian war. I remember, you know, this is a fantastic culture in history. They've been hijacked by a terrible, terrible group of people. And if those people can be somehow ousted, you know, wonderful for Iranian people and wonderful for the world.

I — if I were betting it again, I'd say go back to the first point: questions, uncertainty, finding signals in the noise extremely difficult. But if I rebuilding it, it'll end up that the guys with the guns will in some version uh be the next iteration. And uh if they're tamer and less uh uh determined to threaten our interests, will this have been worth it? Well, again, we'll have to look at over the longer run.

五、战争的外溢与不可预测后果

I think the other thing that we know about wars is that they're unpredictable and that they have many unanticipated consequences. Now I think as we watch what's happening now and see what impact does that have on oil and gas prices — predictable so you can see. And going well what impact does that have on other countries — huge. So the number of countries now whose economies are being disrupted by this impact and therefore blaming us for it.

So I had a call from someone in Taiwan uh yesterday saying wait a minute, did anybody think what it's doing for us, since they had about half of their electricity comes from their natural gas that that is now come you know now not not coming. Uh if you ask about what is this meaning for Ukraine, all the patriots that were to hope to prevent missile strikes from Russia on Ukraine are now in the Middle East.

So that's many more target. So there's just unfortunately it unfolds in so many different directions. Now I don't want to seem too pessimistic about it because again I think we've been shocked fortunately on the upside by how uh professional our military has and intelligence folks have been, and also how pathetic uh or hollow the Iranians have been. First day of the attack, I was at the defense department and I gave them a list of 11 things that I would do and be capable of doing if I were playing the Iranian hand. And uh they seem to be — I mean I I I hardly even want to say out loud, but they act like a paper tiger.

六、过度成功的陷阱：会不会再造一个阿富汗？

Do you think there's more damage that can be done by being more successful? You know, if we think back to Iran, Iran is twice as many people, 100 million versus 50 million, four times the land area, a much better equipped military, and arguably the military system, the regime is embedded in every aspect of industry. So the more successful the United States is in dismantling the Iranian regime — and as was advised by Condisa Rice who apparently was just in the White House today and advising the president and publicly stated "it's time to finish them off, it's time to go all the way" etc. — does that not then leave a country with no infrastructure, no leadership, 100 million people in tatters, and that's the classic breeding ground for some of these extremist groups to seize control and seize power? And we don't have the resources to go in and have another multi-decade Afghanistan Iraq problem.

I'm afraid I agree. Yes, I think the uncertainties about that — I mean you can do four or five different scenarios for how this might conceivably end. It could end up being an extended civil war in Iran among the various national groups even some of them possibly seceding. So again, fairly chaotic. Look like Syria plus. Okay. It could look like Iran or Afghanistan. Well, excuse me. We were there for more than a decade with unlimited resources. Hardly any constraints on the number of people. And how did that work out?

So I say that I'm a small C conservative and kind of think history is not always — certainly not the only guide — but is a good place to start. So I would say nervous. Yeah.

Is it too late to have a Venezuelan outcome where we've taken out the head of the snake and we've simply replaced it? The snake can continue operating and living without too much destructive damage and effect. But it seems like we may be a little too far gone now in Iran and that to take this all the way might be very difficult to manage.

Is it too late? If I were again that we're all in the dark and all the uncertainty — so back to point one — but after that I would say Trump is erratic, impulsive, but he has some coordinates and one of them is his power.

November 3rd is a big day for him. He's focused on that. The economy is a crucial element of that. That's part of the framing of the of the China relationship. The impact of this on the economy is already evident. Okay. And could become more evident.

The public opinion again. I think he's — I saw today in the journal that he's sent Higs Higsmith out to sell the war.

Mhm.

Good luck. I'm not sure he can sell. Uh so the public opinion of this — it's about six to four unfavorable —

because they didn't make the case for the war in advance.

Uh the impact of this on other parties including parties that we care about is negative. Uh so I can easily imagine in the next week even declare — if you look at that sheet the way in which he's left himself room to declare victory at having, as you say, cut off the head of the snake, destroyed their nuclear capabilities, destroyed their ability to project power — or in the defense department we always say "destroy or degrade." Degrade has a lot of dimensions as you would point out. And now it's up to the Iranian people to seize their own government and take advantage of themselves.

The difficulty with that will be BB wants to fight on till the regime is destroyed, and that'll be an interesting struggle to see how that goes. Earlier Trump demonstrated that when he thought it was in American interest he was prepared to pull BB's chain. So we'll see.

七、MAGA 的分裂与对以色列的态度

This is uh I think in before we move out of the Mina region — a topic that's coming up — there's obviously no support for this war in America. Most Americans put foreign affairs today at one or two % of their concern. Economy and many other issues are high on their list. And he's been pretty clear, "Hey, we're not going to put boots on the ground." And then there's this anti-Semitic underpinning that's happening in America that's become pretty acute, and you had Rubio who we mentioned earlier say, "Hey, we did this because the Israelis were going in anyway and we had no choice." And then they walked that back. Explain to the audience how we should look at that turn of events, where there's a group of people in America, specifically people in the MAGA party, who believe Trump has betrayed them and that he's captured by the Israeli government in some way and he's doing their bidding. I'm not saying that — that's my perspective — but that is an undertone that we're seeing and that's something new in our lifetime.

I think it certainly is. So, this is a big topic, but I'd say when I talk about it, I say I'm pro-Israeli and anti-BB. So, BB is not Israel. The people whom I know mostly and respect mostly in Israel are the national security barons — the people who've been the chief of staff or the head of Mossad or the head of Shinbet or the people that work in those structures — and they believe that BB is actually destroying the Ben-Gurion's Israeli democracy that they cared about.

Or if take Tom Friedman, it's pretty hard to accuse him of being anti-, you know, anti-Semitic. Uh but he's been I think very clear and very courageous in pointing out that what BB's doing to Israel's democracy is really destroying it, and the impact of that on Jewish kids in America. I mean I say this on campus where they want to be proud of Israel. They deserve to be proud of the country that has been built over these years, but they look at the behavior of these crazy right-wing settlers and their activities in the occupied West Bank, or they look at some of the activities in Gaza, or they look at now the kind of unlimited uh war without any even attempt to make arguments about proportionality, and just say, "Wait a minute, that's not the country I want to be proud of. I want to be proud of Rabin's Israel. I want to be proud of Ehud Barak's Israel. I want to be proud of Ben-Gurion's Israel." And I'm pretty much of that persuasion.

I think what BB risks in this and risked earlier uh with interfering in American politics invites push back. So I think this could have a big long-term negative effect both in the Democratic party and the Republican party, especially among younger people. If you look at the polling for the under-30s on Israel, it's just like shocking. Yeah.

八、中国与台湾：为什么未来两年入侵概率只有 5%？

Can we move to China because I think that again this is going to be the big story over the next month. You've said publicly that ousting Maduro from Venezuela could embolden China to accelerate a Taiwan takeover. The CIA, it has been reported in media, warned Tim Cook of Apple and other tech companies about a possible invasion as soon as next year. You've separately met publicly with Chinese officials. What's your view on the likelihood and the timeline of a China attempted takeover of Taiwan and what are these kind of motivating factors that might make that happen sooner?

So, let me clarify. I'm not sure or I may have been misinterpreted, but obviously the Chinese watch very carefully. They study wars and they will learn lessons from what's going on here, but I don't think that is a — I don't think the Venezuelan operation other than making them feel envious about the capabilities will impact their likelihood of attacking Taiwan.

So Taiwan — number one, I think the likelihood of an attack on Taiwan this year or next year or even into 28, but let me just do 26 and 27, absent some major provocation by Taiwan or some other third incident, is very low. I put it at about where the prediction markets are — 5% or something low. So I'm not counting on that.

Second, why is that so low? I'm just curious why 5%.

Yeah. Okay.

原因一：和平统一的剧本

Because first, they have a theory of the case of what they call peaceful reunification and they think things are evolving in that direction.

The theory of the case is that the current government which is a DPP government currently is stymied by its opposition in the parliament. So they can't even buy the arm sales that we previously gave them. There's 11 billion arm sales that the Chinese have complained about. But Taiwan's legislature probably won't buy half of that. Okay. So the current president is struggling. There's going to be an election in January 28th and they think it's likely that the former party, the KMT, will be elected — somebody they'll be much more sympathetic to China and to an evolving relationship. So that's number one.

原因二：军队大清洗

Number two, the Chinese have been engaged in a deep deep purge of all of their military leadership. I mean, it's hard to believe and you read about the paper from time to time that he fired this guy, he fired this guy. He's fired the equivalent of every four-star in our whole political system and every combatant commander and every commander who would be commandeering the forces over you know to deal with Taiwan.
