I'm not letting the ministers who are taking this class with me graduate until they build and deploy agents. I've told them they're not getting their graduate certification.

部长们来家里上课

Harry: Have you told your wife that you've got 26 ministers coming to your house?

Anj: She let me co-host — date night, Anj — she let me co-host them at our house in SF a few weeks ago. And I'm very lucky to Viv. I don't deserve Viv, I'll tell you that. But she's very mission-aligned, and we both believe that the best thing we could be doing with our time is educating at scale.

Dario 为什么这么强

Harry: What makes Dario so good that other people don't see from the outside?

Anj: One — sheer scientific brilliance. Truly world-class technical ability in his domain. An obsessive desire for truth-seeking — to keep reasoning, reasoning, reasoning, doing experiments until — he's a physicist at heart. I think Dario is a physicist at the end of the day. He's not actually a computer scientist. And so a world-class physicist — an applied physicist — tries to derive general laws of reality by looking at data and running empirical experiments. He's an empiricist, and he has an obsessive desire to be a good empiricist.

And the third is mission alignment culture. He says: this is our focus, this is our mission, no drift, we won't take shortcuts, we are willing to make huge tradeoffs to hit this mission. And that attracts the best talent — incredible talent. In the face of criticism of people saying, "you're a mercenary, you're blah blah blah, you're just doing this for profit" — no, actually it turns out there's a ruthless desire to stay focused on the mission. And that results in hard tradeoffs and priorities. And if you're not aligned on that mission, then you'll just think he's crazy or he's evil or whatever. It's crazy how much ad hominem attacks people I've seen against him. But he's got that clarity of mission.

过去 12 个月改变的看法

Harry: What have you changed your mind on in the last 12 months?

Anj: The biggest one is health. I've had some health experiences between both my family members and myself that made me realize we all just don't know how much time we have on Earth. And that makes you stop taking for granted how much time we have. And so I started taking time much more seriously.

Every lecture I do at Stanford, we talk a lot about scaling laws and technical stuff, but I also give the kids an Andre's life scaling laws lesson. I'm very inspired by Richard Feynman — Feynman's lectures always kind of combine technical education with a little bit of life coaching.

And my number-one scaling law for them, for the students, was: take life seriously, but don't take it so seriously that you forget what makes it worth living. Which is: have fun with friends, work on interesting projects with people you love, don't take relationships for granted. It's humans that make the world go around. And if you're so focused on your next fund or your next raise or whatever, you just take for granted the one thing we all don't know how much we have, which is time with each other.

And so I just started valuing my time more, my relationships with people. My parents — I left my parents behind in India to move to college at Stanford, and I have gone weeks of my life not calling them or texting them, and now they're in their 60s, and I've —

Harry: I would give you a hug if we were in person.

Anj: [__] I'm so sorry, man.

Harry: Don't worry. It's okay. Oh Jesus.

You know, the first money we ever made from the show, we made it because my mom has MS and we couldn't afford treatment for her. And the only way that I could pay for it was by putting adverts in the show. And that was how we did it. And they still pay for it. Thank you to Vanta for paying for Mom's MS.

Anj: Thank you, Christina. Yeah. Thank you for the corporate sponsors. The trade-offs, you know, the sacrifices are — parents are amazing.

Harry: Parents are insane.

跳下金钱跑步机

Harry: How do you escape the money treadmill? I didn't have money when I grew up, and I was like, "I'll be happy when I get x amount of money." Any advice on escaping that money treadmill?

Anj: I was very lucky. I went to Singapore on a government scholarship. Lee Kuan Yew, who was the founding father of Singapore — I'm a big Lee Kuan Yew-ist — realized that they didn't have many resources, they didn't have money as a founding nation. They had nothing basically other than themselves and their strategic location. And he realized we need to build a talent program. We need to run this country like a company. And we would recruit the best talent from across Asia.

Because I was the top 10 or something in some public exam in the 10th grade in India, I was tapped to be a scholar in Singapore. I was a government scholar. Now, I didn't have to — I was lucky enough that my parents could have paid for it, had a family business in telecom. But it was very important to me to be independent from my parents, because in Indian culture and a lot of cultures, if you don't have financial independence, you are always beholden to somebody else. And in community cultures like India, there's a lot of pressure to adhere to their values.

And I think I did subconsciously — I'm very lucky I have a sister who lives in London who fought my battle for me. She's 7 years older and I got to see that she was a rebel, and she wanted to do all kinds of things — including she wanted to go to fashion school and they didn't want her. She had to go to law school because they were paying for it. And she fought some of my battles and made me realize: the more independent I was, the more freedom I had. And freedom matters to me a lot.

And so I have always defined my goal, my financial goals, through independence. That's what has always mattered to me. I'm willing to make big tradeoffs in money to retain my independence. And anytime I find my independence feeling threatened, I go, "You know what? I need a change."

That's what matters to me. You need to figure out what is your mission. What matters to you more than anything else. So you're willing to just turn down all kinds of money and job opportunities, because that clarifies a lot where you spend your time, basically.

Harry: My mission is really to enrich the already very rich family offices of Europe. That's it.

Anj: Okay. Well, then you've got a ways to go on the treadmill, brother.

Harry: Was that not a bug? I didn't read the memo. [__]

Anj: You know, I also think that people are just not very funny anymore. We lack a little bit of humor in a lot of society.

Harry: It's so sad.

Anj: Yeah. I was with my partner the other day, I'm like, "You speak like AI." And he's like, "I know."

Harry: And you know what? I talk to my wife like I talk to Claude, and she [__] me. I'm like, "Yeah, that's not a good thing."

墓志铭："He Was Right"

Harry: Dude, a final one. It's a bit morbid, but what do you want to be remembered for? What do you want Anj's legacy to be?

Anj: You know, Viv asked me this three years ago at a party — I think it was at our anniversary or something. We were with 10 of our closest friends, including some of the co-founders of Anthropic. And it was one of these classic San Francisco kind of dinner parties, and she puts me on the spot. I just blurted out — I think what she had asked was, "what do you want it to say on your tombstone?"

And I blurted out: "He was right."

And the room just went dead quiet, and they were like, "Yep."

And it's because I have this obsessive desire and need to try to learn where the future is going and then tell everybody about it. And then everybody thinks I'm like some snake oil salesman or whatever. And then — now that's changed, because now it turns out I was so right. I made LPs so much money that they're all now asking me, "Anj, can you —" People — I was invited back to teach this class at Stanford. So people have realized, "Okay, Anj might know a thing or two about the future. Let's go get his take."

Rishi Valley 与数字节制

I went to this boarding school in India called Rishi Valley.

Harry: Seven years of no tech.

Anj: No tech. Seven years. Rural India.

Harry: No wonder. No wonder you're a happy and adjusted person.

Anj: It's taken me a while to get here.

Harry: Would you let your children have social media?

Anj: Yes. I think it'd be crazy to not let them have access to social media, but I think it has to be done in moderation. And most parents have a really hard time moderating it with their kids. And then it's really hard to moderate.

At Rishi Valley I had access to a computer once a week. And so you need to enforce something like that where you don't take it for granted. It's within a structured environment, and then you develop good habits and protocols and practices to not be dependent on it. I would plan my Wikipedia sessions — you got one hour a week in the computer room in Rishi Valley, so you really got to plan the highest use of that time. And so you're not dependent on it, but you use it as a high-leverage strategic asset.

That's how I think technology should be viewed. You shouldn't take it for granted. The problem is, people keep saying, "we're going to have the singularity." Have you realized we've been at this for like 10 years? Half of you outsourced your brain and thinking to this device.

收尾

Harry: Oh dude, I so enjoyed doing this. Thank you so much for putting up with me. You've been utterly fantastic.

Anj: No problem, man. Thank you for doing the consistency with which you've kept up this. You're an institution now, right? Like, the hard thing is — I can't believe this — you've been at this, we've gotten old together, right? When you were doing this, like you said, you were a kid. I was much younger.

Harry: Dude, me and Pat Grady — Pat Grady was like one of the first people I met in Venture, and he was an associate, and I was like a 17-year-old. I laughed with Pat. I said to him the other day, "Dude, you've gone from associate to head of Sequoia, and I've gone from podcaster to podcaster."

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 当被拒绝 21 次的不是"项目"，而是"认知带宽"
大多数人把 Anthropic 早期的"21 个 VC 说 No"解读为"VC 看走眼"。但 Anj 的叙述里藏着一个更尖锐的真相：那些投资人不是在评估风险，而是**根本没有加载底层概念**——他们甚至不知道 GPT-3 是什么。这不是判断失误，而是**认知基础设施的缺失**。
这里有一个反常识的推论：在前沿技术的早期，资本的真正稀缺性不是钱，而是**能理解你在说什么的钱**。Anj 不得不把融资目标从 5 亿美元"重新锚定"到 1 亿美元，不是因为 Anthropic 不值 5 亿，而是因为市场上只有 1 亿美元的人听得懂这个故事。**估值是听众理解力的函数**——这是大部分早期创始人从未意识到的融资本质。
## 算力与电力的跨时空镜像：我们正活在 1885 年的英国
Anj 把当下的算力市场类比为 1885 年的电力革命，这已经足够敏锐。但真正让我觉得惊艳的是更深一层的模式：**每一次基础设施革命都经历"囤积 → 标准化 → 普及"的三段式，而 boom-bust（繁荣泡沫）不是偶发事故，是缺乏标准化的必然副产品**

item.title: [Invest Like The Best - Videos] How AI Is Changing Warfare | Palantir CTO
item.dc:creator: Invest Like The Best - Videos
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

异端者的"反向熵"：痛苦不是成本，而是信号

Shyam 关于"冠军表现感觉糟糕"的论述埋着一个反直觉的系统性真相：大多数组织把痛苦视为需要消除的摩擦，但真正的建设者把痛苦当作导航仪。 Rickover 被安排在女厕办公、被海军系统性羞辱——这些通常被视为"文化病灶"的信号，在异端者的操作系统里反而是"你走在对的路上"的证据。这里有一个让人不安的推论：任何一个"工作得很顺利、没什么阻力"的军事/制度创新项目，大概率是假的。 因为真创新必然触动存量利益结构，不触动就不是创新。这解释了为什么 Palantir 最初的"办公室"是政府方不想买单、操作员却离不开的那道缝隙——缝隙的存在本身就是产品市场契合的证明。

跨领域连接：Palantir 的"本体论"其实是神经可塑性的企业级对应物

你听到 Shyam 谈 ontology 时，表面是数据工程话题，但深处是认知神经科学。他反复说"美国文化的优势是思维可塑性（plasticity）"——这恰好是脑科学里描述神经突触可重塑的核心术语。而 Palantir 的本体论层的真正功能，不是"映射数据"，而是让一个组织的集体神经系统保持可塑：当现实变化时，企业能够像健康大脑那样重新连接突触，而不是像僵化的官僚机器那样需要每 5 年做一次"脑部手术"（重组）。这也揭示了为什么他讨厌 cost-plus 合同——那种合同结构等价于给大脑打麻醉针：你被剥夺了学习的反馈回路。更惊人的是，他批评的"美国国防工业基地财务化"和神经系统中"髓鞘化过度导致学习能力下降"在系统行为上同构：两者都是用效率换灵活性，最终以丧失适应力为代价。

一个你可能没意识到的黑色幽默：美国国防部是"最后的共产主义堡垒"

Shyam 把五角大楼称为"除古巴之外最后的共产主义堡垒"——这句话轻描淡写，但它翻转了整个冷战叙事的底层逻辑。美国赢了冷战，却把敌人的组织形态吸收进了自己最核心的安全机构。 这是一种极其诡异的历史反讽：为捍卫自由市场而建立的军工复合体，内部是计划经济；而正在挑战美国的中国，在制造业的执行层反而比美国更像"企业家的市场"（World-class manufacturing 意义上）。这暗示了一个跨越军事、经济、组织理论的更大模式——当一个系统成为唯一的超级力量时，它会自发地"共产主义化"，因为没有竞争压力就没有了保持市场机制的必要性。Palantir 的生意本质上是在做"再市场化"：把制度里"失灵的决策链"重新接通反馈信号。从这个角度看，Palantir 并不是一家软件公司，也不是国防公司——它是一家制度复活公司（institutional resurrection company），专门对抗熵增造成的"组织脑死亡"。再往前推一步：这意味着 Palantir 真正的竞争对手，从来不是 Snowflake 或 Databricks，而是官僚制本身。

一个出乎意料的观察：Forward Deployed Engineer 不是销售模式，是"反身性对冲"

大家都把 FDE 当成一种高端顾问式销售策略来模仿。但如果你仔细听 Shyam 的语言，你会发现 FDE 的真正功能是对抗创始人的自我欺骗。创始人最容易死于"我相信我的产品有价值因为有人买了"——这是一个闭合的自我强化回路。FDE 机制是故意在产品和现实之间插入一个反身性验证通道：工程师去工厂车间看到所有人都用 Excel 绕开你的软件，这个"羞辱性信号"是任何营收数据都无法提供的。换言之，FDE 是一种反脆弱的认知架构，不是销售部门。 这也解释了为什么多数模仿者失败——他们把 FDE 当成一个成本中心去优化，而没有意识到它的产品功能是"让创始人无处可逃"。

📝 转录文本

How AI Is Changing Warfare | Palantir CTO

嘉宾：Shyam Sankar（Palantir CTO） · 主持：Invest Like The Best

开场介绍

My guest today is Shyam Sankar, the CTO of Palantir Technologies. I think you'll find by listening to Shyam that the business itself, their mission, and how they've built it—and indeed him and his life—are completely fascinating.

We talk about his entire worldview, which I find personally the most interesting: this need to re-industrialize the United States after decades of moving so much of our production—and, as he would argue, therefore our learning of how to innovate and make things—off of our own shores. We talk about the history of heretics: people who have, against the odds, designed solutions that have driven so much of our success around the world.

Please enjoy my conversation with Shyam Sankar.

一、异端者（Heretics）的定义

Q： Shyam, I think the most fun place for me to begin is an area that you're interested in that I'm fascinated by: a history of people in American military lore who you call heretics. People like Hyman Rickover and Andrew Higgins—who designed 90% of the boats that landed at Normandy—John Boyd, who invented the OODA loop (one of the great biographies of a military figure that I've ever read). Talk to us a little bit about how you would define a heretic in your use of that term, and why you're so interested in that group of people in US history.

Shyam： Well, I think they're really founders. You know, they're founder figures, and they get obsessed with delivering something that makes no sense—because they're fighting, particularly in the military context, the bureaucracy. You're going to end your career. You pay extreme prices for it. So there's almost a pathological obsession with winning, which I really relate to both personally, but also that's what I see in great founders.

In the present moment, it's really important to encourage the hidden heretics who are in the military today—to recognize: your heresy matters. And frankly, if you look back at history, the only shit that ever worked, the things that helped us win all the wars, were the things that the heretics actually did. Nothing that went through the machine delivered anything.

I think all change comes from these heretics, and they only later become heroes. If you think about Billy Mitchell, who invented the Air Force—he was court-martialed and died penniless and depressed, and only after his death did we invent the Air Force based on his original heresy. I'm just fascinated with what motivates these folks to keep doing this. And I guess I relish a little bit in their rebellion and the victory that the rebellion brings about.

二、Rickover 的故事：一个异端者的完整案例

Q： If you could have people study any one heretic story to understand this general idea, which would you pick?

Shyam： I'd probably pick Rickover.

移民起点。 So Hyman Rickover—born in a shtetl in Poland, came to the US when he was six, had one of these near-miss moments where, in those days, when you landed at Ellis Island, you had 10 days for someone to come pick you up. His mother gave someone money to go send a telegram to the father who'd already been here. And that guy just pocketed the money. On the 10th day, there was another guy who arrived at Ellis Island who bought them one more day, ran out, got the father, got them. It's this near miss where he almost didn't even end up here—he would have been sent back to Poland.

性格与早期生涯。 He was kind of this feisty character—I think he was like 5'2", very short. He went to the Naval Academy. He was so unlikable that in the yearbook at the Naval Academy, they had torn out his picture. You know, he's just one of these difficult people. In World War II, he drove a coal ship. He was not some exceptionally high-performing senior military officer commanding a destroyer or carrier.

核电潜艇的执念。 But after the war, he went to Oak Ridge and observed the vestiges of the Manhattan Project, and he had this idea: we could build nuclear-powered submarines. He'd had this experience on diesel-powered submarines, which suck—they're basically surface ships that happen to go underwater for like an hour. This became the thing he was obsessed with.

Talk about chutzpah—like, Oppenheimer himself thought this idea was going to fail. So you have Oppy telling you you're stupid and this isn't going to work, and you're just going to go forward. He started on this project and built the first nuclear submarine in seven years, start to finish.

羞辱与韧性。 The Navy hated him so much that his first office for this project was literally the women's restroom. They're like, "What can we do to humiliate this guy into quitting?"—but he just kept going. If you actually look at his personal memoirs, you can see that the humiliation got to him, but in a way that he could channel it to be motivating. It wasn't like he was somehow inured to the pain of it all, but he could push through it. And I think recognizing that every major project you do is going to push you in these sorts of ways—like, how do you dig deep? How do you grind through the pain?

不对称优势的遗产。 He created a really unique culture in Naval Reactors which is alive today. Part of what's really cool is that our nuclear submarine force is one of our last remaining asymmetric advantages against the Chinese, and we built it in the 1950s. What a legacy for this guy to have built.

He was in this unique role of both being a human who would invent the technology and therefore was qualified to think about how to regulate it. Whereas the Russian submariners would spend roughly six months at sea and then six months in Sochi recovering so their white blood cells could regenerate, because the radiation shielding sucked—we've had no deaths due to nuclear incidents in our submarine force. He designed it to a standard which is: I'm designing this thing so my son could be in it, and it's 100 times safer than what we believe the minimum standard to be. That sort of obsession is not a manager's view of the world; it is a founder's view of the world.

One of his legacies: he was an admiral for 30 years, and even as an admiral he was very difficult. Zumwalt, who was the Chief of Naval Operations for a while, said: the Navy has three enemies—the Soviet Union, the Air Force, and Hyman Rickover.

【赞助商片段 1】Ramp / Rogo / WorkOS

Most software companies try to maximize your time on their app to juice engagement. Ramp does the exact opposite. Ramp understands that no one wants to spend hours chasing receipts, reviewing expense reports, and checking for policy violations. So they built their tools to give that time back, using AI to automate 85% of expense reviews with 99% accuracy. And since Ramp saves companies 5%, it's no wonder that Shopify runs on Ramp, Stripe runs on Ramp, and my business does too. To see what happens when you eliminate the busy work, check out ramp.com/invest.

Every investor should know about Rogo, because Rogo AI's platform is not just another generic chatbot. Instead, it was designed to support how Wall Street bankers and investors actually work—from sourcing, diligence, and modeling to turning analysis into deliverables. For me, three key things differentiate Rogo. First, it connects directly to your systems, so it can work with your actual data. Second, it understands your workflows—how work really happens across a deal or an investment. And third, it runs end-to-end and produces real outputs the way the best people do: auditable spreadsheets, investment memos, diligence materials, and slide decks that match your standards. This all comes from the fact that Rogo is built by finance professionals for finance professionals, and it's already being adopted by some of the most demanding institutions in the world. To learn more, visit rogo.ai/invest.

OpenAI, Cursor, Anthropic, Perplexity, and Vercel all have something in common: they all use WorkOS. And here's why. To achieve enterprise adoption at scale, you have to deliver on core capabilities like SSO, SCIM, RBAC, and audit logs. That's where WorkOS comes in. Instead of spending months building these mission-critical capabilities yourself, you can just use WorkOS APIs to gain all of them on day zero. That's why so many of the top AI teams you hear about already run on WorkOS. WorkOS is the fastest way to become enterprise-ready and stay focused on what matters most—your product. Visit workos.com to get started.

三、Shyam 自己的"不友善"（Disagreeableness）

Q： I remember reading the book about John Boyd—three times he was sort of the best fighter pilot, then he helped develop the F-16, then he came up with the military strategy concept that was instrumental in some of the US wars in the '90s. He was a bastard as well—highly disliked, a really bad father, highly disagreeable, but incredibly important in US military history. You don't seem to have that kind of personality. By all accounts, you seem very likable within Palantir and outside. Where does your disagreeableness surface?

Shyam： It surfaces in certain ways. As Karp would say, I'm not everyone's cup of tea. Where you can very clearly see it is in the early days of Palantir: you'd be dealing with the government, and it's easy to think about as a monolith, but you have operators who want to use your software but are not in charge of buying it. And you have the IT people, or the program, who's in charge of building what you would be building. So you're a structural threat to them.

The advice is: go along and get along—hey, those guys are the people paying you, why don't you just do what they want? The problem is, if I did what they want, it wouldn't work. And so that's where the disagreeableness comes in. It's being ornery about: no, I'm going to deliver the thing that actually works for the operator. I'm going to piss off literally everyone along the way. They are knives out going to try to kill you. We had to sue the Army to compete at some point.

In that vein of being truly committed to is the product excellent? what else could it be doing?—you could think about the whole OODA loop of forward-deployed engineering as continuously solving through back-propagation of what the product should be. That's the motor of innovation.

四、世界观的形成：家族史与成长环境

Q： If I think about Palantir's story and success and your part of that story, your personal worldview seems really important. I would love to hear the formative experiences that contributed to that worldview.

Shyam： There's no single event. It's a combination of family history and the environment I grew up in.

家族逃亡经历。 Starting with the family history part—we fled violence in Nigeria, where my father, we frankly almost died, to settle in the US. That happened pretty young in my father's life, in his early-to-mid-30s. It's a pretty jarring experience; it gives you a whole new lease on life. You have a deep amount of gratitude and perspective on the trials and tribulations that may come ahead. But it also grounds you in a profound understanding of the counterfactual: what makes this country so special. Sometimes it can be unmooring not to have those experiences, because it's easy to take it for granted, it's easy to become cynical.

My father didn't have the classic immigrant story where he came here and became successful. That's a great thing if it happens to you, but it's also a full journey—it's easy to appreciate. Dad started businesses that went bankrupt; it was very hard. But having that rooting experience created a graciousness and perspective in him—he was always so thankful for the opportunity. I took a lot away from that over time, especially as I grew up.

奥兰多的乐观主义。 The second thing is growing up in Orlando in the '80s and '90s—it was this profoundly optimistic period. In the shadow of the Space Coast, you were bombarded with the idea that technology was going to make the world better, that there was a structural positive-sum view of everything. It was so motivating, and people had ambition around these things. That really set a frame for how I thought about things and how I wanted to spend my life and energy.

建设者的承诺。 Part of what's interesting about the heretics I'm drawn to is that they're all builders of some sort. The conversion from heresy to heroism is a result of an empirical thing they built. John Boyd was alive to see the success of his innovations for Gulf War I, where the US destroyed the fourth-largest army in the world. It's hard to think about now, because we think about it as almost a foregone conclusion, but leading up to it there was a lot of uncertainty.

That worldview is centered in an understanding: look, I think the US is the greatest force of good that exists in the world. As a country, we understand that there's something special about founders—there's a reason we called them the Founding Fathers. Just empirically: Europe has created zero companies worth more than €100 billion from scratch in the last 50 years. It's almost astonishingly bad. We've created all of our trillion-dollar companies from scratch in the last 50 years. The primacy of people, and being in a culture and environment that allows that to happen—that doesn't subjugate the human, doesn't snuff out human flourishing, that allows you to pursue these things. That may seem trivial, but the heretics are all a version of that.

五、父亲的品格

Q： What was your dad like personally?

Shyam： Every spare moment this man had—even though he was so busy and was just grinding—he would be on the phone trying to help someone. I have this weird memory: my dad had this sort of "engineering autism" that a lot of us have, but he met someone who had totally disfigured teeth, and he just had the actually profound kindness to tell him: you should get cosmetic surgery. And that guy is so profoundly thankful that no one else had the balls to say that. It actually changed his job prospects. It changed his life. You could clearly see the positive-sumness in him: hey, I'm just going to keep paying it forward.

六、美国为何伟大：信念 + 思维可塑性

Q： If you had to identify the component parts that make America the greatest country in the world, what are the key components?

Shyam： 1. A belief in exceptionalism and greatness. No army that lost its morale ever won the war. Believing in yourself, believing that greatness is possible, is a precondition to being able to express it and realize it. A lot of cultures don't have that. 2. Complete plasticity of thought. Culturally, America is a place where people do change their minds. Hey, this isn't working—let's do something different. Or: I observe that thing working, even though it goes against the orthodoxy of everything I've learned—we're moving, we're pivoting. In Europe, people start arguing about shit that happened 300 years ago. Some cultures are so rooted in the past that they can't update their priors. A culture capable of learning—you could call it plasticity, the first derivative of learning—how can you make progress without that?

七、伟大（Greatness）的含义

Q： What does greatness mean to you?

Shyam： An aspiration that's substantially bigger than oneself. The focus of American greatness is on American prosperity and the American worker. How can we do things that are actually enriching our civilization, our people, raising their own aspirations, inspiring them to go on and do great things?

There's this unstable equilibrium in civilization that pulls you towards nihilism. Greatness is the antidote—the counterveiling force saying: no, actually it's worth investing in our institutions. It's worth going through this painful journey because what we're going to get on the other end is something better, something we all aspire for, something you will be proud to pass down to your children.

Q： An example of greatness you've witnessed firsthand?

Shyam： The obvious one is Alex Karp. His ability to manage and unlock talent is something I've never seen before. From the very early days, he modeled our entire company as an artist colony. We have more in common with a Hollywood talent agency than with a typical software company—in our approach to looking at humans, in this first-principled approach to the individual, and this deep resistance to a cargo-cult-like process that corrodes all the things that actually create value. That's important because if you drive process to the extreme, you actually get something that looks more like the Soviet Union.

How do you maximize the potential of the people in front of you and advocate for their growth? You have to have a structural personality where you're not threatened by other people's successes—you actually relish them. That's the parallel between Alex and my father.

八、解锁人才：超能力与氪石

Q： How do you unlock talent?

Shyam： 起点：理解人才是不均衡的。 Really talented people are highly uneven. They're very good at some things, okay at another set, and really bad at some others. Usually the trick is: where are they in their own journey of understanding what those things are? Particularly for high-achieving young people, they misattribute what their superpower is. They think there's this thing they do that requires a fair amount of effort, and therefore they get a dopamine hit when they succeed. It's probably something they're not even bad at—they're just okay at.

超能力的定义。 Superpowers are effortless. In some sense, it's almost not even rewarding to exercise your superpower. My analogy: Superman could fly. He could see through walls. But that wasn't arduous for him—it's just something he could do. You learn it comparatively: oh, this thing that's lizard-brain, effortless, almost thoughtless for me, other people who are really smart can't seem to do, or I do way better. Embracing that is the first step. All of your contributions to the world are going to come from your superpower—everything else is basically a waste of time.

氪石（Kryptonite）。 There are some weaknesses you have that aren't just I'm kind of average or a standard deviation below average. It's like: you're six standard deviations below average here. It's not something you can work on. The only strategy for Superman around kryptonite was to avoid it. Can you be okay with that? As the artist, what is your process of accepting that? How do we help you accept that actually, at the point of acceptance, you become more valuable?

环境的作用。 You have to create an environment that supports them through that, because the discovery of kryptonite usually involves you being exposed to it. You're going to make some really bad mistake that will have real consequences. Hopefully you learn from it. You don't want a culture that says you fucked this up, I gotta fire you. It's: oh, I'm so glad you learned that this is not what you— exactly.

I remember doing something I really screwed up once that had consequences for the company. I sheepishly went into Alex and was completely honest. There was no grand strategy behind this other than my commitment to always being honest. He was very appreciative. He was also in pain as he internalized what this was going to mean. But he valued the fact that I wouldn't try to hide it. I took away how important that environment was going to be.

Q： What do you think your superpower is?

Shyam： There's actually kind of a narrow sliver. In the Palantir context, the intersection of forward-deployed engineering and product—there's an interface there. Owning that one inch on both sides of that interface, almost as a dictator—that's my superpower.

九、"伽马射线"式的成长：深水区扔人

Q： I heard a story once about someone at Palantir who joined and was very quickly given an extraordinarily difficult project around which they had no relevant experience, and relatively limited resources, solving a problem truly global in scale. They described it as the most formative, important, and most stressful professional experience they'd had. How do you balance how much leash to give someone?

Shyam： Let me start with an analogy. How did Bruce Banner become the Incredible Hulk? It wasn't progressive overload. It's not like he lifted a little more weight every week. It was a near-fatal dose of gamma rays. 50% chance he died, 50% chance he turns into the big green monster.

There's absolutely an element where we're taking people and just irradiating them. Prospectively, you're not sure if they're going to come out the other end, but you have some belief that they have the raw talent and potential. Then you want to create an environment that has enough transparency and flow of information that, if you see this thing is going off the rails, without them having to seek you out, you're able to push in and help them. A lot of environments don't have that by default.

反事实。 The alternative model doesn't work. The structured career ladder with progressions is perfectly designed to make you feel comfortable that you're growing—it gives you some belief there's a linear path to follow—but actually it's all fake. On the other hand, if you're willing to suspend disbelief, throw yourself off the deep end, your maximum rate of learning will be coincident with your maximum ability to tolerate pain. You're in over your head, working on a problem you don't even know. This is the environment you're going to learn at the fastest possible rate. You're only limited by your motivation and ability to endure the discomfort.

Then, if you're willing to do that serially—just keep jumping off the deep end, go after problems you're frankly not qualified to go after but have the raw potential to do—you turn into a superhero. That's why Palantir is a founder factory.

Q： Did you have your own gamma-ray moment?

Shyam： The article talks about the deployment at the KOIC. That was seminal. I was leading it; it was all on me; it was never obvious until basically the last second whether it was going to work. I internalized: oh, success looks like a lot of pain—and that instinct to figure out how to reduce the pain, how do I do this the next time with less pain, is exactly the wrong instinct.

十、如何发现值得下注的人才

Q： If you were giving a commencement speech—how can people expose themselves to that test?

Shyam： It's the conspiracy of the people around you who give you that chance. I don't know why Kevin Hartz hired me—some Stanford grad student coming to talk to him—but the fact that he took the bet and just threw me out there, that was the first time I got to jump off the deep end. You're assessing the people who are hiring you as much as they're assessing you: is this an environment that's going to bet on me, or is it an environment that wants me to be a veal-cow? Will they tolerate my eccentricities, or will they try to squash them?

Q： How do you know someone's worth betting on?

Shyam： At some point, it becomes lizard-brain—you get enough reps. But early indicators: first-derivative proof of some sort of sui generis thing—did they create anything from 0 to 1? And expression of agency and initiative. The simplest way I'd say it: if I give this person an inch, can they turn it into a mile? The worst bet to make is: I gave this person a mile, and somehow they turned it into an inch.

十一、Palantir 未来 10 年的文化愿景

Q： What do you hope changes most in the next 5–10 years at Palantir?

Shyam： Culturally—because all value creation is downstream of culture—continuing to be an environment where a new person can come in and just have huge responsibility, be in charge of people who've been here 10 years.

I call it a quantum org structure. I don't think a flat org structure is optimal—it doesn't actually work. What you want is an org structure that can crystallize as the right structure to solve the problem as it exists today, and is able to reform as the problem mutates tomorrow. You can see the pathology of big companies as they go through some big reorg every five years. Even if you steelman it and say this is the right org structure for this company today—every day entropy is against them. Every day it's slightly more wrong than it was before. If you're so ossified that you have to wait until it's totally broken before you reorganize, that's a fundamental weakness. That plasticity—your commitment to working backwards from the problems—is how we generate value.

Q： What critique of Palantir do you take the most seriously?

Shyam： When you have a structure like this, you don't get to spend nearly as much time with people as you'd want. The rate of irradiation is very heterogeneous. There's a bimodal distribution for how long people stay at Palantir: the probability you'll stay for a decade-plus if you've stayed three years is incredibly high. But around the three-year mark, everyone faces this crucible where they look around and say, this place is so fucked up and crazy—and they have to understand: oh, but that's the feature of it. Then they stay (sign up for it) or they'll say, this is the bug of it—I gotta get out. The critique I take most seriously is that with a little help, a lot more people could make that hump. Those people are the future of the business.

十二、Forward Deployed Engineering（FDE）

Q： You pioneered the success of this model at Palantir. Tell us the story of what this term means, where it came from, and why it's powerful.

Shyam： 对传统软件业的批评。 My critique of the conventional software industrial complex: you're sitting in Palo Alto, building a piece of software, and your essential feedback loop on is this software valuable? is can I sell it? If someone's willing to pay for it, it must be valuable. That's an okay feedback loop. There's a stronger feedback loop: is the software valuable? did it deliver the outcome? The only way to assess that is in the field—with the end user, on the factory floor, in the foxhole.

Palantir 的困境。 In our case, it wasn't going to be enough. You have this weird division between the IT buyer and the operator. The validation is going to come from the operator. The idea was to build software through back-propagation. How do you go to the person who has the problem? You're showing up with your best thesis of what you've built in metaphorical Palo Alto, but you're not just sending a sales engineer whose job is to ring up the sale. You're sending someone obsessed with whether it's going to generate an impact in the world, willing to continuously solve backward, and technical enough to understand the full stack.

两类工程师。 There really are two types of engineers: people who know how to build the right thing, and people who know how to build it the right way. Their dopamine hits come out of different things. Right thing people are more like MacGyver—I solved the problem. In their mind, solving the right problem is 80% of the work. Right way engineers are more like artists: don't you see how beautiful the thing I built is?—architectural elegance, scalability—that's what gives them the high. Never mind that it doesn't solve a problem anyone cares about. Don't you know it's art on its own?

Think of it like vector math. FDEs skew toward the hacker mentality; product engineers skew toward the artist mentality. You have an unstable equilibrium where you're trying to align these vectors to go upwind. If either side dominates, you don't really have a business. At that seam you need a dictator-like figure to manage and decide.

幂律与秘密。 This model is super valuable when you think about your customers as existing on a power law in terms of how useful the next marginal thing they're asking for is. If it's a Gaussian, just survey all your customers and average it out. If some percentage of your customers are living in the future—yes, only a handful have this problem today, but you can assess that it's a really valuable thing to solve that no one else has solved—you should solve it now and effectively have a monopoly on it. You'll be 5 years ahead. You can only surface that through forward-deployed engineering.

关于秘密。 Peter talks about the importance of secrets: if you're going to do something really impactful, there has to be something true about the world that you believe and other people disagree with. Warp Speed is born out of one of those secrets. For the better part of 15 years, our software has been used to make things in the world. The one thing you could not tell anyone before COVID is that their manufacturing software doesn't work. People would point at their ERP system: look how great it is, I spent $5 billion, $10 billion. But as a forward-deployed engineer, when I'd go to the final assembly line on the factory floor, everyone's using Excel. There's a contradiction no one is forced to reconcile: the software is so illegit that I hit the eject button, pull the data into Excel, and hand-roll my own solution. That should be an obvious sign.

谁不该用这个模型。 I'd say: you have to believe you're going to be able to capture meaningful value for the problems you're solving. The problems have to be really big, implying a lot of value behind them. To get at-bat, you weren't going to get paid a lot up front—you were going to invest a huge amount into the customer. Our economics are downstream of our customers' economics.

The other part: your ambition extends beyond a linear box. We always struggled when we came into the commercial world where you had the Gartners of the world: well, this is my enterprise architecture, I've broken it up into all these boxes. What box do you fit in? The answer: we don't fit in any box. We fit in all the boxes. You're solving for all the problems in the seams—for the fact that that architecture diagram was made by some mid-level product marketing manager, never used or validated in anger, never actually solved a problem in the real world. But the cargo cult of the IT buyer just keeps believing they should buy it. If you fit into that, doing forward-deployed engineering would be crazy. But if you don't, you need some way of scraping and clawing to deliver enough value that the institution has to confront: even though it's heterodox, even though it's heresy to my enterprise architecture—it works.

十三、Palantir 到底是什么？

Q： Could you just explain what Palantir is? It's such a basic question, but until I read a detailed account, I probably couldn't have done so.

Shyam： This is actually like the hardest question in the world for me.

We have built an enterprise operating system. Our core thesis is: what makes this stuff really valuable is decisions, not data. It goes to John Boyd's OODA loop. What we're providing is an operating system that allows you to bring all of your enterprise data together. It solves the impedance mismatch: all this data was collected and stored in a way that makes sense to the underlying transactional system, or to some part of my institution, but doesn't actually represent how the humans of my institution think about the problems we're facing.

So we represent what we call the ontology—not just a model of the data, but also a model of the actions (the kinetics). If you're going to make a decision, you might be allocating inventory—how do you do that? This ontology layer almost becomes like an API layer for the business. It makes the business programmable, which changes the rate at which you can compound on top of it. Now you have an abstraction layer to build applications that manage your value chain from the hand of your supplier to the hand of your customer. Call it a value chain; also call it a decision chain. Your whole business, if you squint, is just a series of decisions you're making. For every one of those decisions, you want an OODA loop: I want to make the best possible decision today, but more importantly, I want the first derivative to be good—I want to learn tomorrow how to make a better decision than the one I had today.
