十四、Airbus 案例：从本体论到决策链

Q： Could you give an example of a customer—the circumstance of their data or systems, what you did, the process of establishing the ontology, and what transformation it enabled?

Shyam： Two things will make this make more sense. First: we want to make software that makes you more different, not more similar. We think of it as alpha, not beta. Second: we are a wildly inductive company. We would never show up with a preconceived notion of this is the aerospace ontology. Rather, it's about understanding what makes your aerospace company different from all the others—that's the differentiation you're competing on.

Airbus A350 的背景。 When we first came to Airbus, Airbus was ramping the A350. The A380 was a wonderful plane—structurally unprofitable program. The A350 was the bet the whole company was effectively riding on in 2015. The first time you make an airplane, you're always going to have issues—there's a learning curve. One of the most important things to figure out as quickly as possible: what is an issue that is just a non-conformity—hey, I'll give this guy some training and he'll figure it out, teething pain that'll go away—and what is a recurring issue that is actually a design defect I need to work with my suppliers or upstream on? How quickly you can solve that becomes fundamental to the shape and cost of the ramp.

从工厂车间开始。 The first thing we did: we just started sitting with users on the final assembly line in Toulouse. These are wrench-turners, effectively. What makes your life suck? What's hard about this? What's the challenge working on these non-conformities? You look: basically they're going through Excel, looking at reports in SAP, trying to figure out how to bucket all the issues. So we helped them automate all that. The ontology at that point related to parts, sequencing of work, defect rates—everything related to quality.

进化到决策链。 Once you had this immense quality asset, you were able to pivot it. Now I'm up the learning curve. It naturally leads to the next problem: production planning. Quality → production planning → in-service. Now you have the greatest data asset possible for this tail. As this plane enters service, how do I maximize the uptime of this asset for my customers? How am I going to compete against the other aerospace manufacturer? That's the natural evolution: you start with one problem, but of course they exist in the context of this connected decision chain.

十五、军方客户 vs. 商业客户

Q： What's the difference between dealing with military/government customers versus commercial customers?

Shyam： A lot more non-market forces in the military. The military is the only institution in the world that profoundly divides up supply and demand. The beating heart of most companies is integration—sales and operations planning. You have the combatant commands that deal with real-world events—you're fighting Russia, that's European Command, EUCOM. Then you have the services—Army, Navy, Air Force, Space Force, etc.—whose job is to man, train, and equip. That's the supply side. So you have a group with no responsibility for demand essentially deciding what to build, how much, how to man, train, and equip it. Then they present these forces to the combatant commands: this is what you have to solve this problem. That adds complexity. If the demand side says hey, I really want this capability—they can't buy it.

One thing really advantageous about being both commercial and government-oriented: the intrinsic rewards on the government side are so high, so motivating, it enables you to get through and solve really hard problems—a rising tide for the whole business.

十六、美军的现状

Q： How would you define the state of our military today?

Shyam： 人的层面。 We are the best military in the world. Our uniformed service members are better than we deserve. Every day, someone tells me: hey, when are you going to stop being shocked at how good this E-4 is at building this app or doing this thing? The amount of talent is eye-watering. I think we encumber them in a lot of process. We deprive them of agency. I have a lot of critiques of the bureaucracy.

威慑的丧失。 But the structural challenge we have, observing from a distance: the whole point of the military is to deter our adversaries from conflict. If you look back over the last 10 years, we've not been able to deter a lot of conflict: the annexation of Crimea in 2014; the militarization of the Spratly Islands after they told President Obama we will not militarize it; the pogrom in Israel; the invasion of Ukraine; unprecedented gray-zone phase-zero operations by the Chinese in the South China Sea.

威慑的恢复。 In the last year, we've started to restore deterrence. Whatever you think about it, Midnight Hammer was a massively deterring operation. Maduro is massively deterring. On multiple levels: with Midnight Hammer, the overwhelming strength, precision, incredible complexity—no other military in the world could have done that. But we need to view it as a single strike. Deterrence against a peer adversary is: can you generate that strike every single day until the enemy submits to your will? With Maduro, it also shows our capability: literally the raid happened the day the Chinese were in town. Maduro has all these Chinese and Russian weapons in Venezuela—and they don't seem to work at all. That sends a very strong message to everyone else who thinks their security is coming from buying Chinese and Russian weapons.

十七、"18 Theses"：异端者与工业基础

回到根因。 If we start by saying we've lost deterrence—in the last year we've started regaining it—what happened? We had it for a long time; we were the sole superpower in the '90s. We spend a fair amount every year on defense (trillion dollars, right?). It would be reasonable for the American people to expect—I think there's something much more profound.

People want to talk about narrow things like acquisition reform or the mix of weapons. I think it comes back to people. It comes back to the heretics.

二战的动员模式。 At the dawn of World War II, we were the best at mass production—effectively, our country invented it. To create an industrial base, we took the number two at GM, who was previously the number two at Ford—Bill Knudsen, a Danish immigrant, the perfecter of mass production—and we direct-commissioned him as a three-star general and put him in charge of war mobilization. Knudsen was a world-class engineer. We gave him the authority to go meet other world-class engineers around America.

We had one key advantage in WWII in timing: Lend-Lease. There was a period where we actually mobilized production when we were not fighting—we were building stuff that we gave to our allies, the Brits in particular and the Soviets. That allowed us to build factories and retool existing ones and hit a production ramp so that after Pearl Harbor, we were basically at full-rate production.

Put this in context of our adversary, the Germans. They were actually better engineers than we were. They just made a very small number of very exquisite things.

现在的倒置。 If you come to the present moment, we sound like the Germans. We make a very small number of truly exquisite, eye-wateringly exquisite things. And our adversary is the world's best at mass production. This should give us all a little discomfort.

工业基础的变质。 The second part: the nature of the industrial base. Today we call it the defense industrial base. It used to be the American industrial base. Until the fall of the Berlin Wall, only 6% of spending on major weapon systems went to defense specialists—companies that only did business with the DoD. 94% went to dual-purpose companies. Look, a missile is single use—there's no dual-use missile. But the idea that Chrysler would build missiles and minivans meant all the R&D on production, material, equipment, machine tooling could be leveraged—subsidizing our national security—recognizing that freedom and prosperity were two sides of the same coin.

We're so far away from that world today. General Mills, the cereal company, used to build torpedoes and inertial guidance systems. Kodak, the film company, was a key supplier to Corona, our first spy satellite. This is the industrial base that won World War II, won the early Cold War, and really deterred our adversaries. Only as a consequence of becoming the sole superpower did everything change. That 6% number today is 86%—86% of major weapon systems come from defense specialists only.

创始人消失了。 The other part: the nature of the industrial base. We think about it as Northrop Grumman. More accurately, it was Jack Northrop. It was Leroy Grumman. It was William Lear. It was Henry Ford. It was Henry Kaiser. You had founder figures—their own heretics, what we'd recognize in the Valley today. That's where this talent was: in the industrial companies of America. And we were betting on these people, not just their institutions.

"最后的晚餐"。 In 1993, there was a very famous dinner at the Pentagon called The Last Supper. We had won the Cold War. In a democracy, it's very reasonable for the people to say we want to spend less on defense—what exactly is our threat? So we slashed the defense budget. At this dinner, the Secretary of Defense told a subset—I think 15 of the 51 primes; we had 51 primes, today we have 5—you're all not going to survive. The budget's getting cut. We give you permission to consolidate. Maybe some of you should exit the defense business altogether, and know that some of you may go out of business, and we're not going to save you. This set off a merger frenzy.

Most people look at this and take away the wrong conclusion: oh, this is when we lost competition—when we had competition, it was much more dynamic. The much more profound consequence: this was the moment of profound financialization and conformity in the industrial base. You lost the crazy people. They went to tech, to other parts of our economy where there was enough positive-sum energy and growth—not just about dividends and buyback ratios and financial engineering, but about real engineering.

Kelly Johnson 的例子。 Why should we celebrate a Hyman Rickover or a Kelly Johnson? Kelly Johnson was the founder of Skunk Works at Lockheed. In his lifetime, he built 41 airframes. That is insane—many of which we still fly today. He built the U-2 in 13 months. The timelines we're talking about are just incredible. You can't look at that and think anything other than: this is an artist. This is divine, what he's able to do.

So much of what we've gotten wrong: those things were messy, chaotic, hard, frictionful. Over time, we've tried to install lots of process to make them less messy. As a consequence, they end up not working. We need a little more crazy back to get these things to work.

十八、重建工业基础：为什么与如何

Q： How do we undo this? A lot of what's happened is that we've shipped the capability to produce physical things offshore. We've become primarily a country whose market cap rests on technology, IP, and extreme talent. We've done an amazing job at small numbers of exquisite things, exquisite people, exquisite researchers—but we don't make much here anymore. How can we reverse that?

Shyam： It is daunting but critical. So we have no choice but to lean in and start. The best time to start would have been yesterday; the next best time is today.

为什么必须。 The biggest lie we bought from globalization: we will do the innovation and they will do the production. What you have to recognize: innovation is itself a consequence of productivity. To put it in terms your audience would understand most directly: what motivated Google to do the research behind the Attention Is All You Need paper in 2017? If you ask them, they'll say the team was working on an incremental 3% improvement to Google Translate. You cannot think of something more banal leading to something more revolutionary than that. If you don't have the input stimulus on improving production, you're actually not going to be able to capture the innovation.

That's literally what we're seeing play out in the marketplace: they went from making a battery to making the whole car. WuXi went from being a cheap pair of hands for pipetting contract research in pharma to now 50% of all clinical trials being drugs created in China. We ceded the innovation—it's not that it was taken away from us. We ceded it because we had completely incorrect precepts.

美国的不对称优势。 I don't think it's true that we're not great at building things in this country. Look at Elon and the progeny of Elon—we do know how to manufacture things here, and we're going to have to think about manufacturing in new and novel ways. In addition, it doesn't all have to be new. Ford, General Motors—these are world-class manufacturers too. We have that capability, but we also have new ways of thinking about this: much deeper vertically integrated production; thinking about the production process itself as more iterative and software-like; co-locating R&D with production.

AI 是大卫的弹弓。 Probably the last piece: AI is David's slingshot here. If we can give the American worker superpowers—if you can make the American worker 50 times more productive than a worker anywhere else—you're going to change the efficient frontier of what can be made here. We should be re-industrialization maximalists. The free-traders like to say maybe we can friend-shore. I think that stuff lets us off the hook too easily and fails to solve the problem.

苹果与中国的对照。 From Patrick McGee's book Apple in China—people love to say well, we don't have the machine-tool engineers in America. You could hardly get two people in a room to talk about it. You could fill a whole stadium in China. But look: Apple has spent the equivalent on an inflation-adjusted basis in the last five years of two-and-a-half Marshall Plans building talent and capacity in China. How about we try to spend one Marshall Plan here? The problem is we're not even trying. Partly because we're daunted, partly because parts of our economy don't recognize this isn't going to keep working. You're going to lose everything—not just something.

未宣布的紧急状态。 That's why I feel a need to urgently call to action: we are in an undeclared state of emergency. It's not just manufacturing physical things. Think about drugs: 80% of our generic drugs come from China in one way or another—whether the APIs or the finished product. That is a huge problem. Today your child has an ear infection—pretty trivial, you think, I'll have some generic antibiotics and it'll go away. If you have great-power competition with China, and the will of the American people to fight when they really contemplate hey, my five-year-old might die of an ear infection… We need to take these things much more seriously, further down the supply chain.

I'm glad we're starting with rare earths, but there you could say we had to get punched in the face to mobilize. One galling thing: we've been staring at this rare-earths problem for 15 years, and we more or less have solved it for about two billion bucks. It's roughly a rounding error. Part of it is just starting. A marginal improvement of even 10% dramatically reduces the adversary's leverage.

十九、如何理解中国这个对手

Q： Can you give your perspective on how you think about China as an adversary, as a country?

Shyam： 历史背景。 When we won World War II, we spent our own money to rebuild Germany and Japan. Microelectronics moved to Southeast Asia—as Apple in China documents and Chip War documents—because we helped move it there. Yes, we got cheaper goods in return, but the economic development of these countries, their flourishing to create global stability, was part of the calculus. Same thing with admitting China to the World Trade Organization.

欺骗。 The problem is they cheated the whole way through. Even going back to pre–Deng Xiaoping era, they called us the big enemy. Part of it is our own naiveté—our own belief that capitalism and human flourishing are a natural consequence of prosperity. Okay, they're saying these things, but as they get richer, they're going to change their perspective, and we're all going to find some way of having a positive-sum view of the world. That's essentially what didn't happen.

美国必须失败。 The challenge with the CCP: it's not enough for China to be prosperous. America must fail. You can see this through simple acts. In agriculture, it is totally their prerogative—a business decision—whether you buy American soybeans or Brazilian soybeans. I do not begrudge them one bit. It's a totally different thing when you're trying to smuggle in agricultural funguses so we can't grow soybeans. When you start going down the laundry list of what's actually happening, that's the shape of it.

战争观的差异。 They're a formidable adversary. Our conception of war is kinetic—the lone heroic cowboy against the odds, winning through heroism. Their conception of war is deception—the general who tricked the adversary into the valley, then flooded it. You want to win without firing a shot. A lot of what's happening is below what we'd consider the threshold of conflict, but it absolutely is conflict. It is, in their system, system-destruction warfare—waging war through all means, where they have asymmetric strength.

美式 OODA。 The American Calvinist sensibility is structurally positive-sum. We turn the other cheek time and time again. There will be some line at which we just react. When we react—the best quote I have from an admiral is: it's no longer John Boyd's OODA loop (observe, orient, decide, act). It's the American OODA loop: observe, overreact, destroy, apologize. What we want is to prevent that by having enough deterrence that we're not even provoked into a position where we need to react.

二十、不对称优势：中国 vs. 美国

Q： What are the most important asymmetries—where do they have an asymmetric advantage against us, and where do we have it against them?

Shyam： 他们的：长期计划。 Their principal asymmetric advantage is long-term planning. They actually can't pivot on a dime. Go back to Gulf War I. They saw the devastating violence we did in basically four days to the fourth-largest army in the world and said: okay, we have to systematically map all the critical dependencies of America's military technology and invest against it. They have been doing that since '91. The frog has kind of boiled. It's a slightly easier game because they're playing a home game, but that's the map they've been on—a steady journey. They broadcast their intent the whole time. There were never any surprises.

我们的：疯狂。 Our asymmetric advantage is that we're crazy. The AI phenomenon was not in their long-range plan. It wasn't in our long-range plan, but when it happened, our whole economy pivoted on a dime. Even the Chinese models are a result of distillation. Our ability to be unpredictable because we ourselves do not know what we're going to do—that's our key strength. That comes back to the heretics.

Where does that unpredictability come from? Not our bureaucracy—that's incredibly predictable. Not the steady-Eddie managed decline. It's the crazies.

二十一、如何激励更多异端者

Q： How do we create the right incentives for more of our most talented people to go be heretics in this part of the American story?

Shyam： Two incentives.

一、承认与名望。 Recognition that we need the heretics. Hyman Rickover enjoyed his celebrity for a big period of time; just being known as the guy who did this is itself a reward.

二、金融。 The cynical view of the defense industrial complex is these are greedy war-mongers. Well, they picked the wrong industry. On the greedy side, you should be in tech or some other business. These are crappy businesses—they have 9% operating margins, valued at less than two times revenue. We actually need to make our primes more valuable; that would help us attract the heretics.

How? The facile critique is: the primes are the problem, the system's the problem. The primes are just downstream of the incentives the government gives. The worst incentive is cost-plus contracting: your upside is capped, you're not supposed to take any risk, the government basically pays for your R&D, you have no skin in the game. You can still make a small number of exquisite things, but that system is anti-heresy—you can only do the conventional thing the government agrees you should do.

Think about the Higgins boat. Andrew Higgins built that boat on his own. There was no spec—that's why the Navy kept rejecting it. It wasn't the Navy's idea. But 92% of all boats in WWII are the Higgins boat. The heresy is the boat that won the war. How do you create an economic incentive where that's the case? Companies like Anduril are pioneering that on the hardware side: hey, I'm building a product, these are my specs, these are my opinions, I'm absorbing the risk. You should buy it if you like it.

乐观的理由。 Part of the reason for profound optimism: I would not have started all this advocacy if I thought I was screaming into the wind. Having done this for 20 years, for the first solid 10 years we were alone in the darkness—SpaceX on the launch side and us. Now there are hundreds of founders building in the national interest with a US capital stack—one of our other advantages—over $100 billion invested in projects changing how the Department thinks about the future. Increasingly in conversations I have in the Pentagon, I hear these new entrants talked about as Plan A. Not Plan B, not hey, we'll see—maybe this will work in the future. It's: no, no, this is the plan, and we'll have to have backups if it doesn't work. That is unrecognizable from the world I started in in 2006.

二十二、"总统一日"：狭窄但撬动性的主意

Q： If you were president for a day tomorrow, what would be your executive orders?

Shyam： Narrow ideas—but I'd try to blow them up.

药品生产回迁。 You want to bring pharmaceutical production back to the US. For generics, the business case will never work—too hard, too much subsidy. But what you really want is to redevelop a skilled workforce that understands how to make drugs again, and the production capacity where in a time of crisis you could pivot from branded to generics.

So why don't we manipulate patent length? Say: look, drug companies, if you make your worldwide supply of your branded drugs here in the US, I will extend your patent length such that you recover 2x your capex investment to do that. That's a simple business case every pharma company would underwrite in a heartbeat—and it provides the catalyst to generate that capacity.

更宏大的模式。 There are a lot of things in the American economy that look like that. Going back to prosperity and freedom: Chrysler built minivans and missiles, Ford built satellites until 1990, General Mills, Kodak—you go down the list, the entire economy looked like that. It's a consequence of being the sole superpower, and the Pentagon—the old Pentagon—being the last bastion of communism except for Cuba. Every country gave up on communism including Russia and China except for Cuba and the Department of Defense. That made it both a bad business and seemingly no threat, so you could be a single-purpose company that just cared about being a company. I'm not sure that's a stable or healthy society. We have obligations across freedom and prosperity.

二十三、美国文化的另一面 & Palantir 内部文化

Q： You mentioned the US ability to pivot. What are the negative aspects of US culture? And zoomed in on Palantir—how do you cultivate the culture you want?

Shyam： 美国层面。 Some problems require sustained effort. If you can pivot really quickly, maybe you pivot to solving the problem—but that means you can pivot away from solving it and get distracted. It's feature-bug. If you're in a competition, you want to play to your strengths, which usually involves things the adversary is unable to do. It helps that the nature of populating the US was that all the heretics from the old world came here—we have an unfair advantage on that.

维护文化的铁律。 The critical lesson I've learned over and over: the entropy of the universe is against you. If you don't invest in your culture every single day, you will lose it. Nothing about culture is self-sustaining. That's as true for America as for your company.

The best cultures are really almost cult-like. Your culture is highly differentiated—it should actually be hard for someone to inorganically insert into your culture, otherwise it's not very differentiated. That requires flag-bearers, true believers. Starts with recruiting. Not every human has a disposition to be part of a cult. Some are structurally mercenaries, evaluating life through the lens of what's my next job? You want to build with people who think of the present job as the calling—which engenders the primacy of winning. They're always thinking about how to win today, because that's the whole game. If you win today, you have the right to win tomorrow. Rather than getting lost in what I wish the world could be—unmoored aesthetic rather than how the world does work and what is required to win today.

二十四、Shyam 最常纠结的权衡

Q： What do you personally wrestle with the most?

Shyam： The most important decisions from my seat: what projects are worth outsized investment that we think are going to define the future of what our customers need—and therefore the future of the business—over the next 5 years? How do you get the right talent on those projects? How do you discover what those projects are?

I think about it as: my backlog is something I re-prioritize dynamically relative to reality. Yeah, here's my roadmap—it's an approximation at a point in time of the most valuable things to build. The second some new data comes in, I'm going to rip that up and re-prioritize. Having a culture that lets you do that—because that is really fucking painful. People don't like it. Their world is shaken up. It generates a lot of chaos. But it's the right thing. Managing that, so your culture enables it and there's conviction that the cost is worth it because the return is so outsized—that's the whole business.

Q： How would you coach someone dealing with the whiplash of pivoting?

Shyam： 一、痛苦是冠军的常态。 There's a great Greg LeMond quote—world-championship cyclist. Someone was asking him about a championship performance; he almost looked incredulous: you know, it doesn't get easier, right? You just go faster. What do you think a championship performance feels like? It feels shitty. It's really painful, really hard. Your ability to win is basically: can you survive more pain than your competitors can?

So there's this weird phenomenon: if you're doing something world-class, it's really painful. Also, if you're totally failing, it's really painful. The discrimination to know which of these two worlds am I in? The worst world is where you have no signal—you're just plodding along, but managed decline is really what's happening. Is the pain we're experiencing generating results or not?

二、不要一次性推动整个公司。 You can't move the whole company at once. Even a company as small as ours—only 4,500 people—every change is a mini-insurgency inside the company. Think about it like campaigning: who are the people who already believe what you're saying? How do you add incremental weight behind what they're doing? How do you help them succeed more? Are you trying to generate momentum, or deal with the drag of bringing everyone along? My experience: momentum is the thing that aligns the whole company. You should be waking up every day in the midst of these pivots just working on momentum. People will catch up.

二十五、内部"起义"机制

Q： Your favorite methods internally?

Shyam： Very tactically: celebrating the heretics, celebrating the successes, creating venues where everyone has some ability to revolt.

"当面让我滚"。 Every employee's first AMA with me, I end it by telling them to tell me to fuck off to my face. It's very uncomfortable for them—they're almost not sure if this is entrapping them into something that'll get them in trouble. But how can you say you have a flat culture if you can't tell the boss to fuck off? It's a small gesture, but it sets the conditions to understand: we are a company committed to taking the very best idea regardless of where it comes from, and putting all our wood behind those arrows. Most of those ideas are usually some variance from what we're currently doing—otherwise it'd already be the current best idea.

"起义周"（Weeks of Revolt）。 We've institutionalized—got to be careful how much you institutionalize—minimally two times a year, we have weeks of revolt where people are allowed to go build whatever they want. I want the people to have a chip on their shoulder—the whole point is to prove we've been fucking up and doing it wrong. Inspire us with the thing you've built. Then, if you have a culture that is profoundly truth-seeking, the truth is so charismatic that now that I've seen it, I can't unsee it—this is obviously the right answer, let's go.

二十六、AI 时代：自我颠覆为唯一出路

Q： The AI time is so remarkable. How do you receive this new technology and make sure you get better and don't get disrupted?

Shyam： The only way you can do that is by disrupting yourself. To the point of institutionalizing rebellion—it is the perpetual motor of self-disruption. The most valuable thing is not to defend moats, but to go charge after the new opportunity.

初学者心态的优势。 One thing unique about AI: how much it plays to the beginner's mind. The first adopters of AI for us were the youngest hires. To the point of generating momentum, I didn't bother with people who were cynical or dismissive. I let the young people generate overwhelming productivity that in some sense humiliated or inspired other people to get on board.

There was this almost out-of-body experience: as someone who's been there 20 years, or someone who'd been in Palantir three years—you know what, the best people doing this are really the young people—by which he meant people who had been there like three months. That recognition—here we have someone who's not a gatekeeper; he's actually trying to find the insurgents and empower them, place them where they can go forth and do badass shit, and we can all learn together by doing—has been really fun with the rate of transformation AI enables.

二十七、AI 价值将在何处累积

Q： Who captures the value in the AI stack?

Shyam： We think value accrues at the chips layer and the ontology layer.

堆栈视角。 If you squint at the stack: chips, model providers, AI infrastructure (where we think ontology sits), then AI applications. What you observe: the model companies have to run up the stack away from providing just a pure model—the model is commoditized; it's everything they build around the model that creates value. The AI pure-play applications are running down—as they scale, they have to deal with more customers and more customer data, and they're reinventing AI infrastructure, essentially AIP from first principles.

我们的领先。 Through some divine intervention, we've built this platform over the last 20 years—the meat of it since 2015 with Foundry leading to AIP. That gives us a huge head start. Even if these models help you code faster, there's the code, and then there are the secrets behind why we built the code. So where are we going to be in two years? And two years after that?

交付速度的复利。 The way we measure this: how quickly can I deliver value for my customers? How quickly can I deliver enterprise autonomy, fundamentally change my customers' businesses? That number has been awesome. Two years ago, we could do that inside of eight weeks. Now it feels like a week. That rate is compounding dramatically.

二十八、从幕后走到台前

Q： For so long you were the if-you-know-you-know guy, very much behind the scenes. Why the shift to being an everybody-knows guy?

Shyam： I'm a bit reluctant about the shift, but I can't look my kids in the eye and not say that I'm ringing the bell that we're in an undeclared state of emergency.

The first major public thing I put out was the 18 Theses: we're in an emergency, we've lost deterrence. I've been doing this for roughly 19 years at that point—here is my diagnostic of what's wrong. The shot clock is ticking. We've got to coalesce around this problem.

On a note of profound optimism: I've seen more change in the Department of War in the last year than the prior 19 years. To the point of Americans do the right thing, having exhausted all the other options—we are definitely in the place where we're starting to do the right things, and the first and even second derivatives are positive, going faster and faster.

二十九、"18 Theses" 的核心

Q： How would you sum up the 18 Theses?

Shyam： 1. We've lost deterrence. 2. This is not going to go the way WWII did, because we don't have the industrial base we had in WWII. 3. Recognize our unique American strengths: the person is the program. Don't forget Edward Hall built the Minuteman; Schriever was the Air Force general who oversaw our ICBMs; we call it the Apollo program, but it was actually Gene Kranz's program. The primacy of those people—getting the right people in those roles—is determinative. It would be like thinking you could found a company by recipe, as opposed to the company's future being determined by the founders. Of all cultures, we should understand that. 4. Downstream of that: there is no process out of this. The reason we're screwed right now is we prayed at the altar of process. We thought if we follow this requirements process, let the bureaucracy work, it will lead to a good result. The only thing that leads to is decline.

历史证据。 Look at all the great military innovations through history: Churchill built the tank as head of the Royal Navy because the British Army wasn't smart enough to realize horses were not going to win the next war. He called it a land ship because he could only build ships. That's not a one-off. Literally every major military innovation—including the Sidewinder missile, a rogue project some guy built with lighters and a new sort of seeker that totally humiliated the Air Force's missile in Vietnam, and now we think of it as the standard.

When Bill Perry became assistant secretary of defense, he was staring down this thing. I can either try to fix the system and lose, or I can take the two things I really care about and make sure they work: GPS and stealth. At every crossroad, the person who just wants to engage with the system—we lose. The person who's willing to just put it all on the line to make it work—wins. It was a call: if you're a latent heretic in the Department, if you're a latent heretic in defense tech, this is the moment to express your heresy.

三十、对 Palantir 最大的误解

Q： Anything about your worldview, Palantir, etc. that would be surprising to an outsider?

Shyam： First of all, we don't really think of ourselves as a defense company—we're a software company. The fact that half of our business is in government, two-thirds of that half in defense, is actually more normal to a historical pattern than the present.

关于数据与监控的误解。 Outsiders misunderstand: they somehow think we're in the business of collecting data or doing surveillance. That is probably the part least well-understood about what we're doing.

核心问题：制度合法性。 The core problem we're set up to solve is the legitimacy of our institutions. Why do doors fall off planes? Why does it seem like our basic government services don't work? If you don't address those problems—build state capacity, private and public—if you don't help these institutions get better, it breeds nihilism: we should just burn it all to the ground, nothing is worth investing in. That doesn't actually work. It's strictly a worse world. That energy is what you're fighting against.

"Disneyland 丛林游方向盘"。 Give the C-suite, government or private sector, the benefit of the doubt. They have this steering wheel and are diligently trying to steer. What they don't understand is: it's a Jungle Cruise prop from Disneyland. It's not connected to anything. Then you have the people down below looking up saying, how could my leaders be so dumb? That is where the nihilism starts.

操作系统的意义。 What is the point of this operating system? Why are we trying to make these decisions better? We're trying to create something more responsive for the people leading these institutions and the people who do the work. So much of the 20th century has been a wild managerial revolution that has concentrated power away from both ends to an amorphous middle blob. That's where all the disconnection in the steering wheel happens. Reasserting that our institutions work for the people they're designed to serve, and our companies are the best in the world and functioning and competitive—that's how you underwrite freedom and prosperity.

三十一、被塑造为反派

Q： The idea that you have all this data, you're doing surveillance, you're a panopticon—does that weigh on you?

Shyam： It used to weigh on me. Part of it is: okay, you've been doing it for 20 years—how long can this stuff keep weighing on you? It would be fair to say it weighs on every new employee when they come in; there's a journey of dealing with that. But it's the lot of the man in the arena.

To do or to be. John Boyd's advice to other officers: you can either be somebody or do something, but you can't really have both. If you're going to be somebody, you play this game—it's all theater—you're going to get promoted, get the accolades, feel good about yourself. Where you actually do something, it's going to feel really shitty, no one's going to appreciate it—but you'll have the intrinsic reward of knowing you did something.

We're trying to do something.

三十二、父亲的影子

Q： Would you be willing to reflect on your relationship with your dad?

Shyam： It would be hard to overstate the impact dad and his example had on me. Such a selfless man who gave everything to his family, could weather any humiliation in service of his children and his wife. When you're a kid, you learn this stuff subconsciously—you're like any normal kid, this thing dad did is annoying, you don't fully appreciate it. But the culmination of all those events over time profoundly shapes who you are. I hope I can set an example that inspires my kids to do the same. A lot of fatherhood is being a man that inspires your family to be better.

三十三、收官问题：最大的善意

Q： What is the kindest thing anyone's ever done for you?

Shyam： The greatest, covered in the story: Miss Ethel Danhoff, the head of admissions for a private school in Orlando. My dad took me for a tour of the school, having learned it was the best. That was the other fixation my dad had: education was how you'd achieve some prosperity. I'm going to give my son every opportunity I possibly can.

Turns out we'd missed the admission window by a mile—everything was kind of done. I was very reluctant to even go on the trip and see the school; I was happy at the school I was at. But when I saw the science facilities, I was like, I need to go here. I need to learn this stuff. This is inspiring. Now I was all spun up and excited—and of course I'm learning: hey, you totally screwed this up, you'll have to wait a year.

And Miss Danhoff—with no data, it's not like I had some standardized test scores—she said, give this kid the admission test. Just do it. Maybe she had a modicum of data after that. We were a family that needed financial aid—here it is, let's admit a kid we're going to have to subsidize to come here. That opened the entire future.

If you look later in life, maybe people have cynical reasons to help you—you're further along, they think there might be something in it for them. Here there's nothing in it for her. Just a profound act of kindness that fundamentally changed my life.

Q： Great place to close. Shyam, thanks for your time.

Shyam： Thank you.

【赞助商片段 3】Ramp / Vanta / Ridgeline / WorkOS / Rogo

Most software companies try to maximize your time on their app to juice engagement. Ramp does the exact opposite. Ramp understands that no one wants to spend hours chasing receipts, reviewing expense reports, and checking for policy violations. So they built their tools to give that time back, using AI to automate 85% of expense reviews with 99% accuracy. And since Ramp saves companies 5%, it's no wonder that Shopify runs on Ramp, Stripe runs on Ramp, and my business does too. To see what happens when you eliminate the busy work, check out ramp.com/invest.

As your business grows, Vanta scales with you—automating compliance and giving you a single source of truth for security and risk. Learn more at vanta.com/invest.

Ridgeline is redefining asset management technology as a true partner, not just a software vendor. They've helped firms 5x and scale, enabling faster growth, smarter operations, and a competitive edge. Visit ridgelineapps.com to see what they can unlock for your firm.

OpenAI, Cursor, Anthropic, Perplexity, and Vercel all have something in common: they all use WorkOS. And here's why. To achieve enterprise adoption at scale, you have to deliver on core capabilities like SSO, SCIM, RBAC, and audit logs. That's where WorkOS comes in. Instead of spending months building these mission-critical capabilities yourself, you can just use WorkOS APIs to gain all of them on day zero. That's why so many of the top AI teams you hear about already run on WorkOS. WorkOS is the fastest way to become enterprise-ready and stay focused on what matters most—your product. Visit workos.com to get started.

Every investor should know about Rogo, because Rogo AI's platform is not just another generic chatbot. Instead, it was designed to support how Wall Street bankers and investors actually work—from sourcing, diligence, and modeling to turning analysis into deliverables. For me, three key things differentiate Rogo. First, it connects directly to your systems, so it can work with your actual data. Second, it understands your workflows—how work really happens across a deal or an investment. And third, it runs end-to-end and produces real outputs the way the best people do: auditable spreadsheets, investment memos, diligence materials, and slide decks that match your standards. This all comes from the fact that Rogo is built by finance professionals for finance professionals, and it's already being adopted by some of the most demanding institutions in the world. To learn more, visit rogo.ai/invest.

item.description:
## 异端者的"反向熵"：痛苦不是成本，而是信号
Shyam 关于"冠军表现感觉糟糕"的论述埋着一个反直觉的系统性真相：**大多数组织把痛苦视为需要消除的摩擦，但真正的建设者把痛苦当作导航仪。** Rickover 被安排在女厕办公、被海军系统性羞辱——这些通常被视为"文化病灶"的信号，在异端者的操作系统里反而是"你走在对的路上"的证据。这里有一个让人不安的推论：*任何一个"工作得很顺利、没什么阻力"的军事/制度创新项目，大概率是假的。* 因为真创新必然触动存量利益结构，不触动就不是创新。这解释了为什么 Palantir 最初的"办公室"是政府方不想买单、操作员却离不开的那道缝隙——**缝隙的存在本身就是产品市场契合的证明。**
## 跨领域连接：Palantir 的"本体论"其实是神经可塑性的企业级对应物
你听到 Shyam 谈 ontology 时，表面是数据工程话题，但深处是**认知神经科学**。他反复说"美国文化的优势是思维可塑性（plasticity）"——这恰好是脑科学里描述神经突触可重塑的核心术语。而 Palantir 的本体论层的真正功能，不是"映射数据"，而是*

item.title: [Lex Fridman - Videos] Infinity, Paradoxes, Gödel Incompleteness & the Mathematical Multiverse | Lex Fridman Podcast #488
item.dc:creator: Lex Fridman - Videos
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、Cantor的精神崩溃与连续统假设的"诡异对称性"

让我先抛出一个让人不寒而栗的观察：Cantor用尽一生想要证明的连续统假设，最终被证明是"既不能被证明，也不能被否证"的——这本身就是一种近乎残忍的宇宙级反讽。他的疯狂不是因为他错了，而是因为他试图回答一个在他所处的公理系统中根本不存在答案的问题。这就像一个人耗尽生命试图测量"红色有多重"。

更深的洞见在于：Cantor的崩溃可能不是数学带给他的，而是他无意识地撞上了哥德尔不完备性定理的边缘——在哥德尔正式证明之前的几十年。他是数学史上第一个感受到"真理之墙"的人，却没有语言去描述它。Hamkins后来的多元宇宙观，本质上是一种心理疗愈式的哲学解构：与其说"有一个正确答案我们找不到"，不如说"答案是复数的，每个宇宙各有各的真"。这是数学界的"哥白尼革命"。

二、跨领域连接：Hilbert的酒店、量子叠加与佛教的"空"

这是一个让我兴奋的隐藏连接——Hilbert酒店中"满了还能再装"的悖论，与量子力学中的叠加态测量、佛教中"色即是空"的辩证逻辑，三者共享同一个深层结构：整体与部分的非二元性。

  • Euclid原则说"整体大于部分"——这是经典物理直觉
  • Cantor-Hume原则说"双射即相等"——这是无限的真实结构
  • 量子力学说"测量即坍缩"——观察者与系统不可分
  • 般若心经说"是诸法空相，不增不减"——这难道不就是Hilbert酒店的诗意版本吗？

最让人意外的观察：Hamkins在访谈中说"我活在Platonic realm（柏拉图实在界），我不太理解物理宇宙"。这句话表面是数学家的浪漫宣言，但它揭示了一个反直觉的认识论真相——抽象对象的存在比物理对象的存在更清晰。当你越深入研究一个苹果（原子→夸克→波函数→...），它越变得神秘；而当你越深入研究"5"，它越变得清晰。这彻底颠覆了"具体优于抽象"的世俗偏见。

三、证明 vs 真理：一个被低估的元系统性洞见

最容易被读者忽略但最具系统性破坏力的是Hamkins关于LLM不可靠的论证——他指出LLM被设计为"产生看起来像证明的东西"，而不是"产生证明"。这与他描述自己年轻时用LaTeX排版作业的故事形成了一个完美的镜像：漂亮的排版欺骗了他自己的批判力，因为"漂亮的数学通常是正确的数学"这一关联被他的大脑悄悄学习了。

这里有一个让人脊背发凉的元层级洞见：整个人类文明可能正在大规模重演Hamkins的LaTeX错误。我们的批判系统是基于"形式与内容的统计相关性"训练的——当形式足够像真理，我们就放下了警惕。LLM的危险不在于它会撒谎，而在于它完美利用了我们认知系统中"形式即真理代理"的捷径。

跨领域的爆炸性延伸：这同样解释了为什么精致的政治演说、漂亮的商业PPT、流畅的YouTube科普都能绕过我们的批判性思维——它们都遵循"看起来正确"的优化函数，而不是"是正确的"。Hamkins强调的"truth vs proof"的哲学区分，在AI时代不再是数学家的内部游戏，而是人类认知主权的最后防线。

最终的系统性反转：你可能以为这次访谈是关于无限、悖论与集合论的。但它真正讲的是——当我们试图用有限的工具去把握无限时，我们最珍贵的发现不是答案，而是"答案的形态本身是多元的"这一觉醒。这正是Hamkins多元宇宙观的隐喻力量：它不只是数学命题，它是一种生存姿态。

📝 转录文本

开场白

The following is a conversation with Joel David Hamkins, a mathematician and philosopher specializing in set theory, the foundation of mathematics and the nature of infinity. He is the number one highest rated user on MathOverflow, which I think is a legendary accomplishment. MathOverflow, by the way, is like StackOverflow but for research mathematicians. He is also the author of several books, including Proof in the Art of Mathematics and Lectures on the Philosophy of Mathematics. And he has a great blog, infinitelymore.xyz.

This is a super technical and super fun conversation about the foundation of modern mathematics and some mind-bending ideas about infinity, nature of reality, truth, and the mathematical paradoxes that challenged some of the greatest minds of the 20th century.

I have been hiding from the world a bit, reading, thinking, writing, soul-searching, as we all do every once in a while. But mostly, just deeply focused on work and preparing mentally for some challenging travel I plan to take on in the new year. Through all of it, a recurring thought comes to me, how damn lucky I am to be alive and to get to experience so much love from folks across the world. I want to take this moment to say thank you from the bottom of my heart for everything, for your support, for the many amazing conversations I've had with people across the world. I got a little bit of hate and a whole lot of love, and I wouldn't have it any other way. I'm grateful for all of it.

This is the Lex Fridman Podcast. To support it, please check out our sponsors in the description, where you can also find ways to contact me, ask questions, give feedback, and so on. And now, dear friends, here's Joel David Hamkins.

一、不同大小的无穷：从Cantor到Galileo

Some infinities are bigger than others. This idea from Cantor at the end of the 19th century, I think it's fair to say, broke mathematics before rebuilding it. And I also read that this was a devastating and transformative discovery for several reasons.

So one, it created a theological crisis. Because infinity is associated with God, how could there be multiple infinities? And also, Cantor was deeply religious himself.

Second, there's a kind of mathematical civil war. The leading German mathematician Kronecker called Cantor a corrupter of youth and tried to block his career.

Third, many fascinating paradoxes emerged from this, like Russell's paradox, about the set of all sets that don't contain themselves, and those threatened to make all of mathematics inconsistent.

And finally, on the psychological side and the personal side, Cantor's own breakdown. He literally went mad, spending his final years in and out of sanatoriums, obsessed with proving the continuum hypothesis.

So, laying that all out on the table, can you explain the idea of infinity, that some infinities are larger than others, and why was this so transformative to mathematics?

— Well, that's a really great question. I would want to start talking about infinity and telling the story much earlier than Cantor, actually, because, I mean, you can go all the way back to Ancient Greek times when Aristotle emphasized the potential aspect of infinity as opposed to the impossibility, according to him, of achieving an actual infinity. And Archimedes' method of exhaustion where he is trying to understand the area of a region by carving it into more and more triangles, say, and sort of exhausting the area and thereby understanding the total area in terms of the sum of the areas of the pieces that he put into it.

And it proceeded on this kind of potential understanding of infinity for hundreds of years, thousands of years. Almost all mathematicians were potentialists only and thought that it was incoherent to speak of an actual infinity at all.

Galileo的悖论

Galileo is an extremely prominent exception to this, though he argued against this sort of potentialist orthodoxy in The Dialogue of Two New Sciences. Really lovely account there that he gave. In many ways, Galileo was anticipating Cantor's developments, except he couldn't quite push it all the way through and ended up throwing up his hands in confusion.

The Galileo paradox is the idea or the observation that if you think about the natural numbers — I would start with zero but I think maybe he would start with one — the numbers one, two, three, four, and so on, and you think about which of those numbers are perfect squares. So zero squared is zero and one squared is one and two squared is four, three squared is nine, 16, 25, and so on.

And Galileo observed that the perfect squares can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with all of the numbers. I mean, we just did it. I associated every number with its square. And so it seems like on the basis of this one-to-one correspondence that there should be exactly the same number of squares, perfect squares as there are numbers, and yet there's all the gaps in between the perfect squares, right? And this suggests that there should be fewer perfect squares, more numbers than squares because the numbers include all the squares plus a lot more in between them.

Galileo was quite troubled by this observation because he took it to cause a kind of incoherence in the comparison of infinite quantities. Another example is, if you take two line segments of different lengths, and you can imagine drawing a kind of foliation, a fan of lines that connect them. So the endpoints are matched from the shorter to the longer segment, and the midpoints are matched and so on. So spreading out the lines as you go. And so every point on the shorter line would be associated with a unique distinct point on the longer line in a one-to-one way. And so it seems like the two line segments have the same number of points on them because of that, even though the longer one is longer.

And so it makes, again, a kind of confusion over our ideas about infinity. And also with two circles, if you just place them concentrically and draw the rays from the center, then every point on the smaller circle is associated with a corresponding point on the larger circle in a one-to-one way. And again, that seems to show that the smaller circle has the same number of points on it as the larger one, precisely because they can be put into this one-to-one correspondence.

Cantor-Hume原则与Euclid原则

Of course, the contemporary attitude about this situation is that those two infinities are exactly the same, and that Galileo was right in those observations about the equinumerosity. We would talk about it now by appealing to what I call the Cantor-Hume principle, or some people just call it Hume's principle, which is the idea that if you have two collections, whether they're finite or infinite, then we want to say that those two collections have the same size. They're equinumerous if and only if there's a one-to-one correspondence between those collections.

Galileo was observing that line segments of different lengths are equinumerous, and the perfect squares are equinumerous with all of the natural numbers, and any two circles are equinumerous, and so on.

The tension between the Cantor-Hume principle and what could be called Euclid's principle, which is that the whole is always greater than the part, is a principle that Euclid appealed to in the Elements many times when he's calculating area and so on. It's a basic idea that if something is just a part of another thing, then the whole is greater than the part.

So what Galileo was troubled by was this tension between what we call the Cantor-Hume principle and Euclid's principle. It wasn't fully resolved, I think, until Cantor. He's the one who really explained so clearly about these different sizes of infinity and so on in a way that was so compelling. He exhibited two different infinite sets and proved that they're not equinumerous; they can't be put into one-to-one correspondence.

It's traditional to talk about the uncountability of the real numbers. Cantor's big result was that the set of all real numbers is an uncountable set. Maybe if we're going to talk about countable sets, then I would suggest that we talk about Hilbert's Hotel, which really makes that idea perfectly clear.

二、Hilbert酒店：可数无穷的奇迹

— Yeah, let's talk about Hilbert's Hotel.

— Hilbert's Hotel is a hotel with infinitely many rooms. Each room is a full floor suite. So there's floor zero... I always start with zero because for me, the natural numbers start with zero, although that's maybe a point of contention for some mathematicians. The other mathematicians are wrong.

— Like I mentioned, I'm a programmer, so starting at zero is a wonderful place to start.

— Exactly. So there's floor zero, floor one, floor two, or room zero, one, two, three, and so on, just like the natural numbers. So Hilbert's Hotel has a room for every natural number, and it's completely full. There's a person occupying room N for every N.

But meanwhile, a new guest comes up to the desk and wants a room. "Can I have a room, please?" The manager says, "Hang on a second, just give me a moment." You see, when the other guests had checked in, they had to sign an agreement with the hotel that maybe there would be some changing of the rooms during this stay. So the manager sent a message up to all the current occupants and told every person, "Hey, can you move up one room, please?" So the person in room five would move to room six, and the person in room six would move to room seven and so on. And everyone moved at the same time. And of course, we never want to be placing two different guests in the same room, and we want everyone to have their own private room. But when you move everyone up one room, then the bottom room, room zero, becomes available, of course. And so he can put the new guest in that room.

So even when you have infinitely many things, then the new guest can be accommodated. And that's a way of showing how the particular infinity of the occupants of Hilbert's Hotel violates Euclid's principle. It exactly illustrates this idea because adding one more element to a set didn't make it larger, because we can still have a one-to-one correspondence between the total new guests and the old guests by the room number.

— So, to just say one more time, the hotel is full.

— The hotel is full.

— And then you could still squeeze in one more, and that breaks the traditional notion of mathematics and breaks people's brains when they try to think about infinity, I suppose. This is a property of infinity.

— It's a property of infinity that sometimes when you add an element to a set, it doesn't get larger. That's what this example shows.

Hilbert巴士与Hilbert火车

But one can go on with Hilbert's Hotel. Maybe the next day, 20 people show up all at once. We can easily do the same trick again, just move everybody up 20 rooms. And then we would have 20 empty rooms at the bottom, and those new 20 guests could go in.

But on the following weekend, a giant bus pulled up, Hilbert's bus. And Hilbert's bus has, of course, infinitely many seats. There's Seat Zero, Seat One, Seat Two, Seat Three, and so on. And so all the people on the bus want to check into the hotel, but the hotel is completely full. So what is the manager going to do? When I teach Hilbert's Hotel in class, I always demand that the students provide the explanation of how to do it. So maybe I'll ask you. Can you tell me, what is your idea about how to fit them all in the hotel, everyone on the bus, and also the current occupants?

— You separate the hotel into even and odd rooms, and you squeeze in the new Hilbert bus people into the odd rooms and the previous occupants go into the even rooms.

— That's exactly right. That's a very easy way to do it. If you just tell all the current guests to double their room number, so in Room N, you move to Room 2 times N. So they're all going to get their own private room, the new room, and it will always be an even number 'cause 2 times N is always an even number. And so all the odd rooms become empty that way. And now we can put the bus occupants into the odd-numbered rooms.

— And by doing so, you have now shoved an infinity into another infinity.

— That's right. So what it really shows is that we can define that a set is countable if it is equinumerous with a set of natural numbers. And an easy way to understand what that's saying in terms of Hilbert's Hotel is that a set is countable if it fits into Hilbert's Hotel. So to be equinumerous with a set of natural numbers is just the same thing as to fit into Hilbert's Hotel.

And so what we've shown is that if you have two countably infinite sets, then their union is also countably infinite. If you put them together and form a new set with all of the elements of either of them, then that union set is still only countably infinite. It didn't get bigger.

So there's another way to push this a bit harder, and that is when Hilbert's train arrives, and Hilbert's train has infinitely many train cars, and each train car has infinitely many seats. And so we have an infinity of infinities of the train passengers together with the current occupants of the hotel, and everybody on the train wants to check in to Hilbert's Hotel.

So the manager can again send a message up to all the rooms telling every person to double their room number again. And so that will occupy all the even-numbered rooms again, but free up again the odd-numbered rooms. So somehow, we want to put the train passengers into the odd-numbered rooms. And so while every train passenger is on some car, let's say Car C and Seat S, somehow we have to take these two coordinates C, S, the car number and the seat number, and produce from it an odd number in a one-to-one way.

An easy way to do it is to just use the number 3 to the C times 5 to the S. 3 to the car number, then you multiply 5 by itself the seat number of times, and then you multiply those two numbers together. That's always an odd number, 'cause the prime factorization has only 3s and 5s in it. There's no 2 there. And it's always different because of the uniqueness of prime factorization.

— And prime factorization is every single number can be decomposed into the atoms of mathematics, which is the prime numbers. You're showing 3 and 5 are both prime numbers, odd. So through this magical formula, you can deal with this train, infinite number of cars, with each car having infinite number of seats.

— Exactly right. We've proved that if you have countably many countable sets, then the union of those sets is still countable. It's quite remarkable. When I first learned this many years ago, I was completely shocked by it and transfixed by it.

整数格的视觉化

— Have you been able to internalize a good intuition about countable infinity?

— Let's think about the integer lattice, the grid of points that you get by taking pairs of natural numbers, the upper right quadrant. Each row and column has a countable infinity of points. I can imagine a kind of winding path through these grid points, like up and down the diagonals. Winding back and forth. So I start at the corner point and then I go down, up and to the left, and then down and to the right, in such a way that I'm going to hit every grid point on this path.

So this gives me a way of assigning room numbers to the points. Because every grid point is going to be the Nth point on that path for some N. And that gives a correspondence between the grid points and the natural numbers themselves.

— Yeah, it's a really nice visual way to think about it. So can you speak to the uncountable infinities?

三、不可数无穷与Cantor的对角线论证

— Maybe there's one more step I want to insert before doing that, which is the rational numbers. A lot of people maybe have an expectation that maybe this is a bigger infinity because the rational numbers are densely ordered. Between any two fractions, you can find another fraction. The average of two fractions is another fraction. And yet, the rational numbers are also still only a countable infinity.

The way to see that is exactly the same as Hilbert's train again, because every fraction consists of two integers: the numerator and the denominator. So if I tell you two natural numbers, then you know what fraction I'm talking about. So you can still do 3 to the P times 5 to the Q.

You might think, "Well, every set is going to be countable because there's only one infinity." But it's not true, and that's the profound achievement that Cantor made — proving that the set of real numbers is not a countable infinity. It's a strictly larger infinity.

实数与超越数

— So let's talk about the real numbers. What are the real numbers? Why do they break countable infinity?

— With the real numbers, we have the algebraic numbers. We have all the rational numbers. The integers and the rationals are all part of the real number system. But then also, we have the algebraic numbers like the square root of 2 or the cube root of 5. Numbers that solve an algebraic equation over the integers, those are known as algebraic numbers.

It was an open question for a long time whether that was all of the real numbers or whether there would exist transcendental numbers. The transcendental numbers are real numbers that are not algebraic.

It was Liouville who first proved that there are transcendental numbers. Cantor also famously proved that there are many, many transcendental numbers. In fact, it follows from his argument on the uncountability of the real numbers that there are uncountably many transcendental numbers. So most real numbers are transcendental.

Some of the famous transcendental numbers would include the number π (3.14159265...), and Euler's constant e.

— So you could say that some of the sexiest numbers in mathematics are all transcendental numbers?

— Absolutely. Although square root of two is pretty... And if you have a kind of simplicity attitude, then zero and one are looking pretty good too.

— Sorry to take that tangent, but what is your favorite number? Do you have one? Is it zero?

— Did you know there's a proof that every number is interesting?

— What's that proof look like?

— Yeah. Zero's interesting because it's the additive identity. One is the multiplicative identity. Two is the first prime number. Okay. So one can go on this way, but I want to prove as a general principle that every number is interesting. Suppose, toward contradiction, that there were some boring numbers. But if there was an uninteresting number, then there would have to be a smallest uninteresting number. But that's a contradiction, because the smallest uninteresting number is a super interesting property to have. So therefore, there cannot be any boring numbers.

— That's a beautiful one. Back to Cantor's argument.

对角线论证

— Cantor wants to prove that the infinity of the real numbers is different and strictly larger than the infinity of the natural numbers. Suppose that the real numbers can be put into one-to-one correspondence with the natural numbers. So therefore, for every natural number N, we have a real number, let's call it R sub N. Our assumption allows us to think of the real numbers as having been placed on a list, R1, R2, and so on.

Now, I'm going to define the number Z. The integer part is going to be a zero, and then I'm going to put a decimal place, and then I'm going to start specifying the digits of this number Z: D1, D2, D3, and so on. And what I'm going to make sure is that the Nth digit after the decimal point of Z is different from the Nth digit of the Nth number on the list. And I'm going to make it different in a way that I'm never using the digits zero or nine.

Looking at the Nth digit of the Nth number, those exist on a kind of diagonal going down — for that reason, this argument is called the diagonal argument. We've made our number Z so that the Nth digit of Z is different from the Nth digit of the Nth number. But now it follows that Z is not on the list, because Z is different from every R sub N. And that's a contradiction because we had assumed that we had every real number on the list.

— So it's a kind of proof by construction.

— Exactly. There's a kind of philosophical controversy about whether Cantor's construction is constructive or not. Given a list of numbers, Cantor gives us a specific means of constructing a real number that's not on the list.

This diagonalization idea has proved to be an extremely fruitful proof method, and almost every major result in mathematical logic is using in an abstract way the idea of diagonalization — including Russell's paradox, the halting problem, and the recursion theorem.

四、集合论：现代数学的基础

— Can we just step back a little bit? This infinity crisis led to a kind of rebuilding of mathematics. Set theory became the foundation of mathematics. The axiomatization of mathematics, the paradoxes forced mathematicians to develop ZFC and other axiomatic systems, and mathematical logic emerged. Can you explain what set theory is and how does it serve as a foundation of modern mathematics?

— Set theory really has two roles. On the one hand, set theory is its own subject of mathematics, with its own problems and questions and answers and proof methods. From this point of view, set theory is about the transfinite recursive constructions or well-founded definitions and constructions.

But set theory has also happened to serve in this other foundational role. In its foundational role, set theory provides a way to think of a collection of things as one thing. That's the central idea of set theory. A set is a collection of things, but you think of the set itself as one abstract thing. So when you form the set of real numbers, then that is a set. It's one thing. It has elements inside of it. So it's sort of like a bag of objects.

选择公理

— And axioms are facts that we assume are true, based on which we then build the ideas of mathematics.

— The history of the current set theory axioms, known as the Zermelo-Fraenkel axioms, came out in the early 20th century. Zermelo in 1904 offered a proof that what's called the axiom of choice implies the well-order principle. That was extremely controversial at the time. There was no theory, there weren't any axioms there. Cantor was not working in an axiomatic framework. And Zermelo didn't either. His ideas were challenged so much with regard to the well-order theorem that he was pressed to produce the theory in which his argument could be formalized, and that was the origin of what's known as Zermelo set theory.

The axiom of choice principle is completely natural. If you have a bunch of sets, then there's a way of picking an element from each of them. The difficulty is that when you can't specify a rule or a procedure by which you're making choices, then it's difficult to say what the function is that you're asserting exists.

Russell describes a rich person who has an infinite closet with infinitely many pairs of shoes, and he tells his butler, "Please go and give me one shoe from each pair." The butler can do this easily because for any pair of shoes, he can just always pick the left shoe. We can describe explicit choice functions.

But the problematic case occurs when you think about the infinite collection of socks — if we assume that socks are sort of indistinguishable within each pair, then the butler wouldn't have any kind of rule for which sock in each pair to pick. That's when you need the axiom of choice.

ZFC的公理

— Can we maybe speak to the axioms that underlie ZFC?

— Zermelo introduced most of these axioms to formalize his proof from the Axiom of Choice to the Well-Order Principle. The axioms express the most fundamental principles of the understanding of sets. For example, extensionality says if two sets have the same members, then they're equal.

— There's also: there exists a set with no elements (the empty set). For any two sets, there's a set that contains exactly those two sets as elements. For any set, there's a union set, and a power set. The axiom of infinity: there exists an infinite set.

— I read some historical accounts about Zermelo's proof of the well-order theorem. The historians were saying never before in the history of mathematics has a mathematical theorem been argued about so publicly and so vociferously as that theorem of Zermelo's. People were objecting. But then Zermelo and others actually looked into the mathematical papers of some of the people who had been objecting, and found in many cases that they were implicitly using the axiom of choice in their own arguments.

We know that the axiom of choice itself will never be the source of inconsistency in set theory. If there's inconsistency with the axiom of choice, then it's already inconsistent without the axiom of choice.

— A consistent axiomatic system is that there are no contradictions.

— A consistent theory is one for which you cannot prove a contradiction from that theory.

五、Russell悖论与Cantor的一般定理

— Russell's paradox and another anthropomorphizable proof of uncountability...

— Cantor actually proved a much more general fact: for any set whatsoever, the power set of that set is a strictly larger set. The power set is the set containing all the subsets of the original set.

Suppose X and the power set of X have the same size. We can associate to every individual of X a subset. Now let me define a new set D — the subset of X that contains all the individuals that are not in their set. D is a perfectly good subset, and because of the equinumerosity, it would have to be attached to a particular individual — let's call her Diana. Now we ask, is Diana an element of D or not? If Diana is in D, then she should be in her set. But D was the set of individuals that are not in their set. So if Diana is in D, then she shouldn't be. Contradiction.

委员会与水果沙拉

The anthropomorphizing idea: For any collection of people, you can form more committees from them than there are people, even if you have infinitely many people. Suppose not. Every committee could be named after a person in a one-to-one way. Form what I call committee D, which consists of all the people that are not on the committee that's named after them. That committee has to be named after someone — let's call her Daniella. Is Daniella on the committee that's named after her? If she is, then she shouldn't be. If she isn't, then she should be. Contradiction.

When I was teaching at Oxford, one of my students came up with the fruit salad version. Consider all possible fruit salads from a given collection of fruits. We want to prove that for any collection of fruits, there are more possible fruit salads than there are fruits. Form the diagonal salad, which consists of all the fruits that are not in the salad that's named after them. That diagonal salad would have to be named after some fruit — let's say durian. Is durian in the salad named after it? Same contradiction.

This is exactly the same logic that comes up in Russell's paradox. Russell is arguing that the class of all sets can't be a set, because if it were, then we could form the set of all sets that are not elements of themselves. There's no universal set.

Frege的悲剧

Before Russell's paradox, Frege was working on his monumental work — the philosophy of logicism, the attempt to reduce all of mathematics to logic. His basic principles happened to imply that for any property whatsoever, you could form the set of objects with that property — the general comprehension principle.

Russell wrote him a letter when he observed the work in progress, that there was this problem. Russell wrote this letter to Frege, and it was just at the moment when Frege was finishing his work. It was already at the publishers. It must have been such a horrible situation for Frege.

Frege had put in the appendix of his work a response: "Hardly anything more unwelcome can befall a scientific writer than to have one of the foundations of his edifice shaken after the work is finished. This is the position into which I was put by a letter from Mr. Bertrand Russell as the printing of this volume was nearing completion."

— It's heartbreaking.

— My view is that the main goals of logicism are basically completely fulfilled in the rise of set-theoretic foundationalism. When you view ZFC as the foundation of mathematics, the principles of ZFC are fundamentally illogical in character. So the fact that set theory is able to serve as a foundation means that mathematics can be founded on logic.

六、Hilbert纲领与哥德尔不完备定理

— Can you explain Gödel's incompleteness theorems and what they teach us about the nature of mathematical truth?

— It's one of the most profound developments in mathematical logic. To understand the theorems, you really have to start a little bit earlier with Hilbert's program.

Hilbert was famously supportive of set theory. There's this quote: "No one shall cast us from the paradise that Cantor has created for us."

— This minefield of paradoxes.

— Hilbert proposed: we're going to have this strong theory that we want to be proving our theorems in. He wanted it to be as strong as possible — to answer all the questions. There's another famous quote: "Wir müssen wissen, wir werden wissen" — "We must know, we will know." Secondly, we want to combine that with a very weak finitistic theory, in which we prove that the reasoning process of the strong theory is safe.

形式主义的证明观

To make sense of that, you basically have to invent the philosophy of formalism. A proof is itself a finitistic kind of object — a sequence of assertions which can be viewed as sequences of symbols that conform with certain rules of logical reasoning. Even though the contents of those statements might be referring to infinite uncountable objects, the statements themselves are just finite sequences of symbols.

The main thing about formalism is that you divorce the process of doing mathematics from the meaning of the mathematical assertions. The reasoning process consists of writing down sequences of symbols on your page, following finitary rules.

Hilbert wanted to prove in this purely finitary theory that if we follow the rules of that game, we're never going to get a contradiction. So those were the two aims of the Hilbert program.

不完备定理

If Hilbert had been right, we would have this theorem enumeration machine that would just spit out theorems all day long. The nature of mathematical investigation in Hilbert's world is one of just turning the crank, devoid of creative thinking.

But the fact of Gödel's incompleteness theorem shows that that is not the nature of mathematical reality.

  • The first incompleteness theorem: you cannot write down a computably axiomatizable theory that answers all the questions. Every such theory will be incomplete, assuming it includes a certain amount of arithmetic.
  • The second incompleteness theorem: no such theory can ever prove its own consistency.

Would you trust a theory that proves of itself that it's consistent? It's like the used car salesman telling you, "Oh, I'm trustworthy."

— Just for clarification, you used the word theory. Is it synonymous with axiomatic system?

— In mathematical logic, "theory" is a technical term. It means any set of sentences in a formal language. So if you say axiomatic system, it's basically synonymous with theory.

七、真理 vs 证明：核心区分

— What is the difference between provability and truth?

— This is a really core distinction. It's fascinating to me to go back and read even the early 20th century people before Gödel and Tarski — they were totally sloppy about this distinction between truth and proof. To be true for them meant to be provable. Because of the incompleteness theorem, we realized that actually there's quite subtle things happening.

Truth is on the semantic side of the syntax-semantics dichotomy. Truth has to do with the nature of reality — mathematical reality. We have a concept of something being true in a structure.

Tarski gave this absolutely wonderful account of the nature of truth in what's now known as the disquotational theory of truth. The sentence "Snow is white" is true if and only if snow is white. We can use this idea of disquotation to give a formal definition of truth in a mathematical structure of a statement in a formal language.

Proof, on the other hand: a proof is a certain sequence or arrangement of sentences in the formal language that accord with the logical rules of a proof system. There are certain modes of reasoning that are allowed. If you know A and you know A implies B, then at a later step you're allowed to write B as a consequence — modus ponens.

The proof systems are generally sound (if the premises are true, the conclusion is true) and complete (whenever a statement is a logical consequence, there is a proof of it). And there's a hidden third adjective: it should be computably decidable whether something is a proof or not.

— A mathematician comes to mind that said he has a proof, but the margins are too small to continue. So that doesn't count as a proof.

独立性现象

— What is, again, the tension between truth and proof?

— The incompleteness theorem says: you cannot write down a computable list of axioms that is complete. There will always be statements that you cannot prove and you cannot refute. They are independent of that theory.

— How traumatic is that?

— My view is that this isn't traumatic at all. This is rather completely eye-opening in terms of our understanding of the nature of mathematical reality. The incompleteness theorem tells us, look, we just can't write down a list of axioms that is going to be consistent and is going to answer all the questions. It's impossible.

The provability problem can be formulated as a decision problem. This is the Hilbert-Ackermann Entscheidungsproblem, appearing in the title of Turing's 1936 paper. It is an undecidable decision problem, equivalent to the Halting Problem.

八、停机问题

— Can you describe the Halting Problem?

— Given a program, will it halt? The Halting Problem is computably undecidable. There is no computable procedure that will correctly answer all instances. We can get half the answers — if a program halts, you can run it and eventually see. But to give the "no" answers, you need to have a kind of deep insight into the nature of the program.

Suppose toward contradiction that we had a procedure for determining whether a given program halted on a given input. I'm going to describe program Q, which takes as input a program P. The first thing it does is it asks the halting subroutine, "Would P halt on P?" If yes, Q jumps into an infinite loop. If no, Q halts immediately.

Q's behavior on P was the opposite of P's behavior on P. Now, what would Q do on Q? Q would halt on Q if and only if Q does not halt on Q — contradiction.

— What a beautiful proof. Simple.

用停机问题证明哥德尔定理

— I view this as the simplest proof of Gödel's theorem. Suppose we could write down a complete theory of elementary finite mathematics. Then we could construct a theorem enumeration machine. Now I have this theorem enumeration device on my desk. You give me a program and input, and I'm going to answer the halting problem by waiting for the statement coming out of the device that asserts either that P does halt or that P does not halt. But we already proved that you cannot solve the halting problem. Therefore, you cannot have such a complete theory of arithmetic.

九、证明的艺术

— You've written a wonderful book about proofs. What can you say about proving stuff in mathematics?
