— I wrote that book when I was teaching such a proof-writing class in New York. I wasn't happy with most of the other books because they would concentrate on the totally uninteresting parts of what it's like to write a proof — these mechanistic procedures.

— We dedicate the book to my students: "may all their theorems be true, proved by elegant arguments that flow effortlessly from hypothesis to conclusion while revealing fantastical mathematical beauty."

一个示例证明

Let's do a proof. Suppose you're gathered with some friends in a circle, and you can point at each other however you want, or yourself. Could we arrange a pattern of pointing so that everyone was more pointed at than they are pointing at others?

Suppose it were possible. We're going to give a dollar to everyone that we're pointing at. Everybody made money, because everyone was pointed at by more people than they're pointing. But it's obviously impossible for us to make money as a group by just trading money with ourselves. Therefore, it can't be possible.

This proof illustrates one of my habits: to anthropomorphize your mathematical ideas. You should imagine that the mathematical objects are people, or active animals.

It's interesting to think about, well, what if there were infinitely many people? Then it's not true anymore. The theorem fails. In fact, you can arrange that everyone is strictly more pointed at than pointing. If everyone has even just one dollar bill, then you can arrange that afterwards everyone has infinitely many dollar bills.

十、数学对象的存在与柏拉图主义

— To what degree should we think of infinity as something real?

— Asking about infinity specifically is not that different than asking about the number five. What does it mean for the number five to exist?

There seems to be this desire to reduce mathematical existence to something we can experience physically. But my attitude is that it's very backward, because I don't think we have such a clear understanding of the nature of physical objects, actually.

Imagine a certain kind of steam locomotive. We talk in detail about every detailed aspect. Then suppose I say, "Now tell me what would it mean for it to exist physically?" What could you possibly say? It is a profound mystery. In fact, it becomes more and more mysterious the more physics we know. Newtonian → atoms → electrons/protons/neutrons → quarks/leptons → wave functions...

Whereas we do seem to have a much more satisfactory account of the nature of abstract existence. I can talk about the nature of the empty set. It doesn't get more and more mysterious — the more that you say, it becomes only more and more clear.

结构主义

— What is structuralism?

— Structuralism emphasizes that what's important about mathematical objects is not what they're made out of, but rather how they function in a mathematical structure. We should only care about our mathematical structures up to isomorphism.

To ask "What is the number four really?" is an anti-structuralist thing. If I have the natural numbers, I could replace the number four with this bottle of water playing the role of four, and it would be isomorphic.

Frege had a famous question — the Julius Caesar problem: we don't seem to have any way of telling from the Hume principle whether Julius Caesar is a number or not. The structuralist response: "You give me a number system. If Julius Caesar isn't a number, then I can take the number 17 out of that system and plug in Julius Caesar for that role, and now Julius Caesar happens to be the number 17." The question of whether Julius Caesar is a number or not is irrelevant to mathematics.

十一、数学的进步

— Do you think there's breakthroughs a thousand years from now on the mathematics side?

— I have my feet in two worlds of mathematics and philosophy. Mathematics has huge progress. We simply understand the mathematical ideas much better. We understand the nature of infinity now better than they did 100 years ago. Whereas in philosophy, there's a less of a sense of progress.

A thousand years from now, the mathematics that they will be doing would probably be completely unrecognizable to me.

MathOverflow

— You are a legend on MathOverflow, ranked number one all time with currently over 246,000 reputation points.

— MathOverflow has really been one of the great pleasures of my life. I've been on there since 2009. I'm interested in any question that I find interesting. There was an enormous amount of interest in topics that were logic-adjacent. A question would arise in group theory, but it had a logic aspect. I was often able to figure out an answer by learning enough about that other subject matter. It really allowed me to grow enormously as a mathematician.

十二、连续统假设

— Can you lay out what is the continuum hypothesis?

— The continuum hypothesis is the question that arises so naturally whenever you prove that there's more than one size of infinity. Cantor proved that the infinity of the real numbers is strictly larger than the infinity of the natural numbers. Immediately one wants to know: is there anything in between?

The continuum hypothesis is the assertion that there is no infinity in between the natural numbers and the real numbers.

For all the sets that anyone ever could define or pick out, it was always the case either that they were countable, or else they were fully equinumerous with the whole real line.

Cantor的纲领

Cantor had a program for proving it. The continuum hypothesis holds for open sets — easy to see. Remarkably, Cantor proved it also for the closed sets using the Cantor-Bendixson theorem. In this theorem actually was the origin of the ordinals.

The Cantor program was a sort of working up. You did it for open sets and for closed sets. Then maybe you go into the Borel sets. The continuum hypothesis has been proved for the Borel sets. Then one wants to go beyond — to the projective hierarchy. It turns out that one begins to need large cardinal assumptions. With enough large cardinals, the projective sets also are always either countable or equinumerous with the whole real line.

— Hilbert put the continuum hypothesis as number one on his list of 23 problems.

独立性结果

The continuum hypothesis remained open until 1938. Gödel proved that if the axioms of set theory are consistent, then there is a set theoretic world where both the axiom of choice and the continuum hypothesis are true. This is the constructible universe, Gödel's L.

But that's not the same as proving it's true. In 1963, Paul Cohen invented the method of forcing and proved that if there's a model of set theory, then there's a model in which the continuum hypothesis is false.

— It seems like a way to escape one mathematical universe into another. Can you explain forcing?

十三、集合论多元宇宙

— One of your more controversial ideas, as laid out in the paper The Set-Theoretic Multiverse: there may not be one true mathematics, but rather multiple mathematical universes.

— We have thousands of independence results. Practically every non-trivial statement of infinite combinatorics is independent of ZFC.

If you're following the universe view or the monist view, you might say, "ZFC is a weak theory, and there's the true set theoretic reality out there, and we need a better theory."

When I was a graduate student in Berkeley, another graduate student was working on a question that turned out to be independent of ZFC. The attitude of his non-logic professor was, "Oh, I guess I asked the wrong question." But my attitude was: when you ask a question that turns out to be independent, then you asked exactly the right question, because this is the one carving nature at its joints.

大基数

For any theory we can write down, there's going to be things we can't prove. So we're already aware that there will always be these independent phenomena. This is the consistency-strength hierarchy. And how lucky we are to find the large cardinal axioms that instantiate exactly this feature of increasing consistency strength. Yet it's also known that the continuum hypothesis is independent of all of the known large cardinal axioms.

多元宇宙观

I take the developments of set theory over the past half century as evidence that there isn't a unique set theoretic reality. Rather, what we've been doing is producing more and more alternative set theoretic universes in which the fundamental truths are differing.

Given any model of set theory, there's a forcing extension where the continuum hypothesis is true, and another one where it's false. You can sort of turn it on and off like a light switch. That's the fundamental nature of the continuum hypothesis.

This led me to my views on the multiverse view of set theory and pluralist truth.

The choice of the philosophical perspective doesn't actually have to do with the mathematical developments directly. It tells us where set theory should go. Hugh Woodin, who was also my graduate supervisor, has a very strong universe view — his theory of ultimate L. Whereas if you have a pluralist view, you're going to be attracted to questions about the interaction of different set theoretic universes.

This led to set-theoretic potentialism and set-theoretic geology (with Gunter Fuchs and my PhD student Jonas Reitz). Jonas said, "I want to undo forcing." And it turned out to be quite fruitful.

借力虚空之地

You can prove something in a forcing extension and bring it back to the ground model. To pick a more elementary example: in the early days, mathematicians solving algebraic equations would be led to the square root of minus five in the middle of their construction. They didn't have any meaning for that, but they would do it symbolically, and eventually all the complex parts would cancel out and they'd end up with an actual answer. You start in the real numbers, march on through the land of nonsense, and end up with an answer that was real again. Forcing arguments work similarly.

十四、超实数

— What are surreal numbers?

— The surreal number system is an amazingly beautiful mathematical system that was introduced by John Conway. It provides a number system that unifies all the other number systems. It extends the integers, the natural numbers, the rational numbers, the real numbers, the ordinals, and the infinitesimals. It's a proper class.

It's generated from nothing by a single rule: we proceed in stages. At any stage, in all possible ways, we divide the numbers we have into two collections — a left set and a right set, so that everything in the left set is less than everything in the right set. We create a new number that fits in that gap.

At the beginning, we don't have any numbers. We divide nothing into two empty sets. Everything in the empty set is less than everything in the empty set vacuously. We create a new number — the Big Bang of numbers, the surreal genesis — when the number zero is born.

Then we can put zero in the left set with empty right — that's one. Or zero in the right with empty left — that's minus one. Then we have minus one, zero, one. Four gaps → four new numbers: two, 1/2, minus 1/2, minus two.

It's a transfinite process. At day omega, every real number will be born, but also the ordinal omega itself, minus omega, and the number epsilon — the firstborn number that's strictly bigger than zero and strictly less than all the positive rational numbers, an infinitesimal.

You can define arithmetic operations naturally. The surreal numbers form an ordered field, and a real closed field — every odd degree polynomial has a root, and you can take square roots.

Conway最大的失望

The surreal numbers are fundamentally discontinuous. They don't have the least upper bound property; there are no convergent sequences. So the ordinary use of calculus based on limits doesn't work — but you can still do calculus using non-standard methods.

I once organized a conference in New York with John Conway. Someone asked him, "What is your greatest disappointment in life?" Conway answered: the reception of the surreal numbers. He had ambition that the surreal numbers would become a fundamental number system used throughout mathematics and science.

Philip Ehrlich mentioned to me once that Conway was his own worst enemy with regard to that very issue, because in the Conway style, everything is a game. He treated the surreal numbers as a kind of plaything.

生命游戏

— Speaking of Conway turning everything into a game — the Game of Life is a fascinating invitation to an incredibly complicated and fascinating mathematical world.

— The Game of Life is a sort of playground for computably undecidable questions. You can prove that the question of whether a given cell will ever become alive is computably undecidable — equivalent to the Halting Problem.

十五、停机问题的黑洞

— Can we say something about how many programs we know something about in terms of whether they halt?

— Once years ago, Alexey Myasnikov asked me whether the halting problem has a black hole — a decision problem possibly difficult in the worst case, but the difficulty concentrated in a tiny region. We proved there is one. There's a computer procedure that decides correctly almost every instance of the halting problem.

The proof: when programs are run, you can calculate that 1/e² ≈ 13.5% of programs don't have any instruction transitioning to the halt state — they obviously can't halt.

Then we hit the jackpot. The head sits on the leftmost cell. Half of the time, the instruction says move left. But if you move left from the leftmost cell, the head falls off the tape — the computation crashes. With a lot of states, the next state is very likely to be new. You get a random walk behavior. By Pólya's recurrence theorem, a one-dimensional random walk is very likely to come back to where you started. When the head returns to position zero, half the time it falls off.

Using this analysis, the probability-one behavior of a random Turing machine is that the head falls off the tape before it repeats a state. That's the stupid proof showing how to solve the halting problem on almost all instances.

十六、P vs NP

— Do you think P versus NP will ever be solved? Could P equal NP?

— P is an asymptotic property. The coefficients on the polynomial could be enormous. For small values of inputs, it doesn't make sense to talk about polynomial time feasibility with respect to the problem inputs we will ever give.

Sometimes overblown remarks are made about P=NP being incredibly important — but it's not about practical things at all because of the asymptotic nature. And we already have approximation algorithms that solve all the actual real engineering problems.

— SAT solvers work amazingly well in lots of cases.

十七、最伟大的数学家

— Who is the greatest mathematician of all time?

— Personally, I don't really think this way about ranking the mathematicians by greatness. If you forced me to pick someone, it would probably be Archimedes because he had such incredible achievements in such an early era. But I also have the view that I want to learn mathematics from whoever can provide it. There's a kind of luck aspect to who was first.

— What do you make of completely separate discoveries made at a similar time?

— Certain ideas are in the air. This is the nature of growth in knowledge.

我的工作方式

— What's your own process when you're thinking through a problem?

— My mathematical style: I don't really like difficult mathematics. What I love is simple, clear, easy-to-understand arguments that prove a surprising result. The question of whether it's a new result is somehow less important to me.

If I prove a new result with a complicated argument, that's great, but I still want to see the beautiful simple argument because that's what I can understand. I'm naturally skeptical about any complicated argument because it might be wrong.

My process: just a kind of playful curiosity. Whenever there's an idea, I just play around with it — change little things, understand a basic case, then make it more complicated, apply the idea to my favorite example, see what happens. This often leads to insights, then to methods, then pretty soon you're making progress.

You imagine a set-theoretic model as a place where you're living, and you might travel to distant lands by forcing. Those metaphorical ways of understanding often help in realizing strategies for finding the answer.

— What do you think of Andrew Wiles spending seven years on Fermat's Last Theorem?

— A totally different style than my own practice. I have pushing towards a hundred collaborators. For me, mathematics is often a kind of social activity. I enjoy this kind of social aspect, and it's not just social — that's the nature of mathematical investigation as I see it.

关于Perelman

— What do you think of Perelman declining the Fields Medal and the Millennium Prize?

— Mathematics is full of a lot of different kinds of people. My attitude is: it doesn't matter. Maybe they have a good math idea, and so I want to talk to them.

— I do think there's a similar thing observing Olympic athletes. The greats do the thing they do, take on these problems for the love of it, not for the prizes.

— My daughter introduced me to her boyfriend. I wanted to know, first of all, what is his chess rating, and secondly, what is his MathOverflow score.

十八、无限象棋

— Can we talk about the math of chess and infinite chess?

— Chess ordinarily is played on a tiny eight by eight board. We want to play infinite chess on the integer board, infinite in all four directions. We don't play from a standard starting position — instead you present a position with a lot of pieces already on the board.

Probably people are familiar with the mate-in-two genre. In infinite chess, you can create a position which is not mate in N for any N, but white has a winning strategy that will win in infinitely many moves. White is going to win, but black controls how long it takes. Black can say, "I know you're going to win, but this time it's going to take a thousand moves at least" — or a million.

规则

The pieces move just like you'd expect. There's no edge, so no promotion. The threefold repetition rule is replaced by: infinite play is a draw. The only way to win is checkmate at a finite stage.

This position has game value omega — white is going to win, but black can play as though counting down from omega. The nature of counting down from omega is that you take a giant step on the first count, then subtract one each time.

与Corey Evans合作

I had a co-author, Corey Evans — a US national master chess player and philosophy professor of law. I met him because he was my son's chess coach. The proofs in infinite chess are extremely finicky. I would create the positions and Corey would say, "Hey, this pawn is hanging" or "this bishop can leak out of the cage."

We made positions with values omega, omega-squared, omega-cubed, and in a three-way collaboration with Norman Perlmutter, omega to the 4th. Since that time, it's been improved — we now know that every countable ordinal arises as the game value of a position in infinite chess.

十九、AI与LLM

— Do you see AI as a potential great collaborator?

— I've played around with it but I haven't found it helpful at all, basically zero. My typical experience is that it gives me garbage answers that are not mathematically correct. It's frustrating — like if I were arguing with a person who insisted their wrong argument was right, I would simply refuse to talk to that person again.

I know that several prominent mathematicians whom I have enormous respect for are saying that they are using it in a way that's helpful, and I'm often surprised. Maybe I just need to improve my skill.

I'm skeptical for another reason: the AI is trying to give me an argument that sounds like a proof rather than an argument that is a proof. The motivation is misplaced.

一个个人故事

When I was an undergrad here at Caltech, LaTeX was a pretty new thing. I was typing up my homeworks in LaTeX. I would print it up and submit it, and the grades would come back terrible. The copy was so beautifully typeset that it looked like the kind of mathematics you find in a book — and that mathematics was almost always correct in a book. I had lost my critical eye. So I was making bonehead mistakes in the proofs.

This kind of effect is very much real with the modern LLM systems. They're not designed to make a logically correct argument. They're designed to make something that looks like a logically correct argument. It's easy to get fooled if you're not skeptical. I worry when people rely on AI for mathematical arguments.

— There is a real skill in providing the LLM system with enough information to be a good collaborator. For me, in the realm of programming, it provides not the answer but the inspiration.

— I also find it likely that MathOverflow answers are part of the training data. So you're talking to yourself, essentially.

二十、最美丽的数学思想

— What idea in mathematics is most beautiful to you?

— The transfinite ordinals. The idea of counting beyond infinity. You count through zero, one, two, three... then comes omega, then omega plus one, omega plus two... then omega plus omega, then omega plus omega plus one... You can always add one. Eventually omega times three, omega times four, then omega squared — the first compound limit ordinal, a limit of limit ordinals. Then omega squared plus one, and it never stops.

It's absolutely beautiful and amazing. It forms the foundation for transfinite recursive constructions — the Cantor-Bendixson theorem, the V hierarchy, Gödel's constructible universe, Zermelo's proof of the well-order principle. The idea of just counting past infinity is so simple and elegant, and has led to so much fascinating mathematics.

哲学中最美的思想

— What about philosophy? What is the most beautiful idea?

— I have a foot in both fields. In some contexts I'm required to choose. My training is in mathematics, but I turned myself into a philosopher over the years. When I went to Oxford for the first time, my main appointment was in philosophy. That's also true now at Notre Dame, where my wife Barbara is also a philosopher.

I remember when I told my daughter I was going to become professor of philosophy in Oxford, she looked at me plaintively and said, "But Papa, you're not a philosopher."

The most beautiful idea in philosophy: the distinction between truth and proof. It's so profound and gets at the heart of so many philosophical issues. Truth is about objective reality. Proof is about how we come to know the things that we know. To focus on proof is to focus on the interaction we have with objective reality — the mathematical reality I live in.

— And the gap between the two is full of fascinating mysteries — in the Platonic realm, in physics, in human psychology, sociology, geopolitics. That's our journey whatever field we're in. I for one am grateful for how marvelous of a philosopher, mathematician, and human being you are. It's truly an honor to speak with you today.

— Well, thank you so much. It's such a pleasure to be here.

结尾

Thanks for listening to this conversation with Joel David Hamkins. To support this podcast, please check out our sponsors in the description where you can also find links to contact me, ask questions, get feedback, and so on. Thank you for listening. As always, happy New Year. I love you all.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、Cantor的精神崩溃与连续统假设的"诡异对称性"
让我先抛出一个让人不寒而栗的观察：**Cantor用尽一生想要证明的连续统假设，最终被证明是"既不能被证明，也不能被否证"的**——这本身就是一种近乎残忍的宇宙级反讽。他的疯狂不是因为他错了，而是因为他试图回答一个**在他所处的公理系统中根本不存在答案的问题**。这就像一个人耗尽生命试图测量"红色有多重"。
更深的洞见在于：Cantor的崩溃可能不是数学带给他的，而是**他无意识地撞上了哥德尔不完备性定理的边缘**——在哥德尔正式证明之前的几十年。他是数学史上第一个感受到"真理之墙"的人，却没有语言去描述它。Hamkins后来的多元宇宙观，本质上是一种**心理疗愈式的哲学解构**：与其说"有一个正确答案我们找不到"，不如说"答案是复数的，每个宇宙各有各的真"。这是数学界的"哥白尼革命"。
## 二、跨领域连接：Hilbert的酒店、量子叠加与佛教的"空"
这是一个让我兴奋的隐藏连接——Hilbert酒店中"满了还能再装"的悖论，与**量子力学中的叠加态测量**、**佛教中"色即是空"的辩证逻辑**，三者共享同一个深层

item.title: [Invest Like The Best - Videos] GLP-1s, Peptides, and The Trillion-Dollar Health Revolution
item.dc:creator: Invest Like The Best - Videos
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：GLP-1 成功的真正原因不是"瘦得更多"，而是"瘦得刚好"

大多数投资者和媒体把 GLP-1 叙事简化为"减肥竞赛"——谁减得更多谁赢。但 Alex 在数据里看到了一个完全相反的信号：消费者并不在寻找"减肥核弹"，他们在寻找"可持续的微小优势"。高剂量=高副作用=高脱落率。这意味着整个华尔街的估值模型可能都搭错了轴——他们在用"疗效最大化"给一款"合规性最大化"的药定价。

这里藏着一个更深的跨领域隐喻：GLP-1 市场的逻辑更接近 SaaS，而不是传统药品。传统药是一次性"治愈事件"，定价逻辑是急性价值；GLP-1 却像 Netflix 订阅——留存率（compliance）才是真正的 LTV 驱动。当口服版把价格打到 $150/月、新增处方从每周 20 万飙到 30 万时，这不是医药故事，这是一次典型的"价格弹性 × 订阅留存"的消费级拐点。制药工业第一次被迫用消费品思维重新做自己。

二、隐藏的系统性真相：医疗创新的真正瓶颈已经从"发现"迁移到"分发"

Alex 反复回到一个让人不安的事实——过去几十年人类预期寿命曲线几乎是平的，哪怕我们已经有了 statins、PCSK9 抑制剂、GLP-1、抗淀粉样蛋白药物。换句话说：大多数能救我们命的药早就被发明出来了，我们只是没在吃。

这是一个你很少听到的系统性诊断：医疗的下一个万亿级机会不在实验室，而在"从处方到合规"那段最后一公里。他把它拆成三个维度——complexity、cost、compliance——这三个词其实是每一个 DTC 消费公司都在优化的东西。所以一个大胆的跨领域连接：下一代最大的"生物科技公司"可能长得一点都不像生物科技公司，它更像 Amazon + Shopify + Peloton 的混血——Hims、Ro、Lilly Direct 已经在验证这一点。PCSK9 本该是比 GLP-1 还大的市场，但因为它"沉默"（你感觉不到胆固醇在杀你），所以它卖不动。这揭示了一个残酷的系统性真相：药品的销量与它的救命能力几乎无关，而与它能否制造"可感知的反馈回路"强相关。 这是行为经济学在分子层面的显现。

三、一个你可能没意识到的"反身性循环"：AI 正在改变科学本身的认识论

Alex 提到一个容易被忽略的事实——科学文献里相当一部分实验是无法复现的。这意味着如果你直接用 PubMed 训练生物 AI，你得到的是 garbage-in-garbage-out。于是真正有护城河的 AI 药物公司必须做一件极其反常的事：自己生产"科学 tokens"——用机器人湿实验室在闭环里制造原生数据。

这里有一个极其漂亮的跨领域呼应：这和 Tesla 的 FSD 逻辑一模一样。Tesla 的护城河不是算法，而是它的车队每天产生的原生驾驶数据——别人没有也买不到。未来最有价值的生物 AI 公司不是"懂生物的 OpenAI"，而是"拥有全世界最大、最干净、自生成的科学数据工厂"的公司。这还引出一个更深的哲学颠覆——科学方法本身正在被自动化成一种"可并行执行的工业流程"。过去科学家的核心稀缺资源是"大脑容量"和"双手"；AI + 机器人把这两者都解除约束后，科学家的角色正在从"执行者"变成"假设空间的策展人"。这是五百年来科学方法的第一次底层重写。

四、一个对你私人生活有意外启示的洞见

Alex 的个人故事里有一条隐线非常值得玩味——他父亲"从不坚持一件事"，他母亲"we always commit and we never quit"。他把这两种看似相反的基因合起来，变成了他投资哲学的内核：高风险容忍（来自父亲的失败史） + 极端韧性（来自母亲的完成主义）。

这里的跨领域启示是：最好的投资者、科学家、创业者都不是"单一心智"，而是"内部辩论的产物"。他们能同时持有"下一个 hypothesis 会失败"和"但我一定会跑完全程"这两种对立信念。这与 GLP-1 的故事奇妙地呼应——这个药的真正突破不在于"效果更强"，而在于"足够温和以至于你能长期坚持"。长寿的秘密、科学的秘密、投资的秘密可能是同一件事：不是最大化强度，而是最大化你愿意不放弃的时间。

📝 转录文本

GLP-1s, Peptides, and The Trillion-Dollar Health Revolution

Invest Like The Best — with Alex Carnell

开场介绍

My guest today is Alex Carnell. Alex is probably the most talented bio and healthcare investor that I've ever met. And as you'll see, he is an encyclopedia of all things going on in life sciences. Alex has been teaching me this topic for more than 5 years. And every single one of our conversations is about as deep as this one that you'll hear today.

We cover every single aspect of what makes today's life sciences world so interesting. Starting with this concept of the health stack, the things that we can do both offensive and defensive to extend our life based on the evidence that he sees in the most exciting companies of today. We talk about cardiovascular disease, neurodegenerative disorders, metabolic disorders, cancers and beyond. And finally, we finish with his remarkable personal story. I hope you enjoyed this great conversation.

一、2025：医学史上最激动人心的一年

You and I have had these conversations now for many, many years. We've been partners and friends for a long time. And so it's hard to know where to begin the conversation because I suspect we'll go for hours talking about drugs and bio and healthcare and outcomes and a million things that affect us all. You have this cockpit-like view into what's going on in the state of medicine today. How would you describe the state of the union? The daily cockpit view that you get of the whole industry — give us a dispatch from that room. What do you see today?

When I look at this moment in terms of where we are in medicine, and I look back over my 20 years of building and backing biotech companies, I would say that 2025 was probably the single most exciting year in my entire journey.

So to unpack that: what we're seeing is GLP-1 medicines are showing us what happens when we get to the root of disease. These medicines have the potential to do everything from protect us from being diabetic after being pre-diabetic, all the way through lowering our risk of having a heart attack or a stroke, protecting our kidneys, and soon we're going to see the opportunity to protect us from developing addictions to alcohol and drugs.

A profoundly important single medicine — because these medicines are incredibly powerful and they can do wonders for people's long-term health. What we're seeing is that the adoption of these medicines has exceeded almost everyone's expectations, including my own, which were pretty darn high. I think it's easy now to conclude that this as a class of medicines will easily be in excess of $100 billion a year in revenue.

And so what gets me fired up about that is not necessarily this once-in-a-decade hundred billion dollar revenue opportunity that exists within GLP-1s. What gets me fired up is that it's actually the first commercial proof that we are ready for what I think we're going to look back on in time as a once-in-a-lifetime trillion-dollar revolution in all of public health.

发明与影响的鸿沟

In order for that to be true, there's going to be layers of attack that are needed to help us live our longest and best lives. One of the things a lot of people don't appreciate when I meet them and we start talking about where medicine is and what it can do is that incredible scientists have already cracked the code on most of the medicines we need to protect us from most of the diseases that will claim most of our lives.

That's a really important point because what that means is that there are tons and tons of people that have traveled through all different mazes to arrive at the biological target and come up with a medicine against that target that can help us to live longer and better lives.

And so when we think about what is the gap that exists in the world today — between the reality that when we look at any expected lifespan charts, they haven't budged in decades. In fact, the last major advancement we had in inflection was decades ago on the back of antibiotics, vaccines, and better hygiene. We've gone nowhere since then.

The gap is not necessarily needing more medicines. It's actually pointing those medicines at the impact that they can have. To me, what I'm excited about about the GLP-1 opportunity is we're seeing the first commercial proof that we're ready to head in that direction. The reason that those sales have exceeded our expectations is people are taking into their own hands their future. People are basically voting with their feet saying, "I'm done waiting to reactively go and treat myself for the diseases that I backward-lookingly manifest. I'm ready to go get after and help myself to live longest and fullest."

二、Health Stack：健康技术栈的五大防御层

I wanted to zoom in on a concept that you and I have been riffing on a lot recently, which we're calling health stack. Stack is a term in technology — your tech stack is all the various components that you use to create your overall thing. We thought this idea could be applied to health. Maybe riff a little bit to begin on this notion of the modern evolving and emerging health stack.

What a health stack looks like has a couple of different elements to it. It has elements of offense and defense.

On the offensive side of the equation are some of the basics that we all know about — proper nutrition, strength training, proper monitoring and testing, and doing those activities in a systematic way and tracking that data and building off of it over time. Later on we could talk about how AI can play a big role in enabling that to reach its final chapter of what it takes for impact.

What I find most interesting about it is: if we think about five core dimensions, not only are they within our hands to be proactive about protecting ourselves, but we have the medicines to do that. To me, the five key layers are:

  1. Lipid optimization
  2. Cardiometabolic health
  3. Neurocognitive health
  4. Inflammatory health
  5. Blood pressure

1. 血脂优化

When we think about lipid optimization — our bodies produce cholesterol. There's a type of cholesterol called LDL cholesterol that's really dangerous, because this cholesterol builds up in our bodies over time. It's slowly accumulating in our vasculature. And as it accumulates, it gets to a point where it causes a blockage — that's a heart attack and a stroke.

We have medicines today — whether they're statins, the PCSK9 inhibitors — that can do wonders to reduce our level of cholesterol. Most middle-aged men and women walking around have somewhere between a 30 and 50% probability of having a heart attack or a stroke sometime between the time that they turn 40 and the time they turn 80. To me, that's tragic because we have the medicines that exist that can help us dramatically lower that risk to sub-10%.

2. 心脏代谢健康

Looking at cardiometabolic health, what we're talking about is the glycemic environment in our bodies combined with visceral fat. These are two major drivers or impediments to our long-term health.

To give you an intuition: the higher the level of glucose in our bodies, think about it as making our vasculature more brittle. So if you are both having lipids accumulate in your body and your vasculature is getting more brittle, you're only force-multiplying the risk that you ultimately have one of those horrific heart attacks or strokes.

We can do something about that. We've got amazing GLP-1 medicines that — from clear data out of Eli Lilly — show a 94% reduction in your risk of moving from being pre-diabetic to diabetic. We see amazing data in terms of what it does to help us lose weight and have category shifts going from obese to overweight, from overweight to normal weight. It's a game-changer in terms of our long-term health.

3. 神经认知健康

Moving down to neurocognitive health. The data is not out yet, but later on this year we're going to see the next step for what are called anti-amyloid medicines — medicines that can go right at the heart of the accumulation of plaques, this time not in our vasculature, but in our brains that lead to all sorts of damage and cognitive decline.

We've got medicines today that can go right at those plaques and bust them. What Lilly is going to probably show later on this year — it's my guess, but I think the data really supports it — is that getting at those plaques earlier before they accumulate as significantly is going to show dramatic effects on protecting us from developing Alzheimer's.

4. 炎症健康

Moving on to inflammatory health. One of the things I think people don't appreciate is that the food we eat is actually quite inflammatory. Because we have over time moved from being hunters and gatherers to being able to have as much access to Pop-Tarts as we want — and I love them too, you don't get one, you get two — we overeat to a point where it drives an inflammatory response in our bodies.

You can think about that whole equation: all right, if we have both lipids accumulating and our body has a high glycemic environment making our vasculature brittle, and the same food that we're eating is driving an inflammatory environment around it — oh my goodness, it's like a ticking time bomb for all of that to go off.

Not only is the inflammatory environment of our body implicated in driving an increase in cardiovascular events, strokes and heart attacks, but also a whole set of inflammatory diseases that span everything from atopic dermatitis to ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease.

5. 血压

Finally, blood pressure. Maybe this one's very intuitive — if we're living high-stress lives, if we're not working out, if we are developing obesity, we have this high glycemic environment so our vasculature is brittle, our LDL is mounting, and on top of that the pressure going through that system is going higher and higher as we're aging and doing nothing about it. Oh my god, that's just another force multiplier.

All of these axes that I define as the five key layers — each of them has medicines available to help us control our fate. Each of them has medicines available that if we could get on them early and be proactive about where we're going with our health, they will undoubtedly add an extra decade of life to our expected lifespan and have the potential to take curves that have been dead flat for decades and drive one of the first inflections ever.

[Ad break: Ramp, WorkOS, Felix by Rogo]

三、GLP-1 商业化的深度解构：2025 的三大发现

I want to spend a little bit of time at each of the five levels to let you explain what you're seeing and learning, especially from your perch as an investor. One of the things that makes you so unique is that ultimately you're trying to make money on all this stuff. There's money on the line here — this isn't just you reading something and trying something; it comes out on a scoreboard.

I want to start with GLP-1s because it's obviously the thing everyone understands the most. It's visceral. What specifically are you seeing in 2025 and 2026 that is so exciting?

发现一：消费者并不在追求"更多减重"

Maybe one of the things most people don't realize is that there are a couple different injectable GLP-1s on the market today, and we have some of the first orals coming to the market now. These medicines exist at lots of different doses.

One of the most interesting discoveries I made over the past year was this: Wall Street is very focused on the next GLP-1 having more weight loss and more weight loss and more weight loss. And I'm sitting back thinking, I don't know if that's the right focal point.

One of the most interesting findings from diving into the data is that people are not solving for this massive bazooka. People are solving for something that helps them to lose some weight, stabilize, and be there. That's really interesting because what it means is what people really want is something that's going to give them a health advantage, but it's going to be incredibly tolerable.

What we find is that you go higher and higher in doses, the unintended consequence is yeah, you're going to have more weight loss, but there's a whole host of other side effects that come with it. That is different than most medicines where we're trying to maximize efficacy. What people are maximizing for here is: how do I get on these things and then stay on them?

发现二：直接面向消费者（DTC）的商业模式

Typically the way medicines are commercialized is you have big pharmaceutical companies — Eli Lilly, Novo Nordisk — with armies of sales reps out calling on doctors, pitching the attributes. It's a very capital intensive exercise, a very human-talent intensive exercise.

What we saw is that Eli Lilly pretty early on last year pushed more aggressively into a non-traditional way to get these medicines into the hands of people. They added to their legions of salespeople a digital front end in Lilly Direct that allowed people to get a prescription from their doctor and get the medicine direct from Eli Lilly.

As you fast forward towards the end of 2025, what we started to see was that more than half of the new people joining are coming in directly.

发现三：价格弹性巨大

The third point from 2025 was all sorts of theater and dramatics around compounded GLP-1s. What we learned was that not only do people want something that's going to defend them and be tolerable, not only do they want something they can get through all the frictions of their system and just hit a button and have it come to their homes — a real consumer-like product — but price matters a ton.

Through most of 2025, the average monthly cost of a GLP-1 was north of $400 or $500. Thanks to our administration, those prices are coming down. Compounded GLP-1s cost about half as much per month as the traditional approved GLP-1s by Lilly/Novo. We were collecting data suggesting somewhere between 15-20% of the market that you couldn't capture through scripts was actually flowing through groups like Hims and Hers and other sources — people willing to take risk of safety and manufacturing unknowns because they wanted the benefits but couldn't afford $400-500/month but could afford $200-250.

2026：口服 Orforglipron 的拐点

In 2026, we now have an oral version of Wegovy. This medicine has launched over the last couple months. Every week, the oral version of Wegovy is setting record after record.

You might think, yeah, of course — who wants to inject themselves once a week for the rest of their life? But I don't really think that's what's driving an almost 4x relative launch cycle for the oral Wegovy versus the most recent injectable launch, Zepbound. People want these medicines at the lowest end of the curve. It doesn't cost $500 a month. It doesn't cost $250 a month. It costs $150 a month.

Prior to the launch of the oral Wegovy just a few months ago, the GLP-1 market was moving at about 200,000 recorded new scripts per week. The most recent data I looked at this past Friday says that has now moved from 200,000 a week to 300,000 a week in just a few months.

Crazy.

We have to take these incredible learnings. The most logical conclusion is that people are ready, people do want to be proactive, and arming them with the stack that can help us all live an extra decade — really focusing hard on what it takes to close that gap between invention and impact. When I say this trillion-dollar revolution, what that means really is this trillion-dollar reduction in our annual healthcare spend. I think that's really possible now.

四、GLP-1 的科学：从 2 分钟到一周

I want to spend some time explaining what is actually going on with this class of drugs — starting with semaglutide, the single-acting, going to dual-acting, going to the triple-acting one that will be coming out this year or next year.

A lot of people don't realize these medicines have actually been around for 20 years. Some of my first exposure to GLP-1 as a drug class actually happened in my early days entering the industry as a young biotech investor back in 2005 with Amylin Pharmaceuticals, having what we thought at the time was the most miraculous breakthrough.

GLP-1 是什么

GLP-1 is a hormone that our bodies naturally produce. Now the problem is that once it's produced by our bodies, it only lasts for about 2 minutes before it's gone.

If you just pause there — what does that mean practically? If you were to take human GLP-1 and inject it, we'd have to inject ourselves 30 times an hour for the rest of our lives to get the benefit of what we can today with either one pill a day or one injection a week.

The science behind moving from a protein that lasts for 2 minutes to a protein that can last over a week is a dramatic transformation. What's good is it is a hormone that's already in our bodies, and it's a medicine we've got data on for over 20 years — not just because it's been around for 20 years, but it's now been in millions and millions of people.

作用机制

How does it actually work? When we eat food, at some point that food makes its way to our small intestine. When food gets to our small intestine, that's what triggers the release of GLP-1 naturally. That GLP-1 molecule travels all over our bodies:

  • Interacts with receptors in our stomachs → helps to slow the digestion of food
  • Interacts with receptors in our brains → helps us feel fuller longer, feel satiated
  • Interacts with receptors on our pancreas → triggers the release of insulin, plays a big role in not just having that sugar not affect our vasculature, but also making sure it's not destroying our kidneys

The combination of those mechanisms allows us to turn off the food noise, to seek out and consume far fewer calories than we've ever done before.

The mechanism and the science are actually pretty well elucidated. Biologically, we know how it's produced, we understand all the different places in the body it interacts. And when you think about all the places it interacts, it becomes no surprise that it's had such an incredible range of effects — from protecting us from becoming diabetic, to reducing the risk of heart attacks and strokes, to lowering our level of inflammation (because we're consuming less food), all the way through to helping us lose weight (a virtuous cycle in terms of lowering blood pressure).

When you look across the five axes, the five layers of defense that are mission-critical — I fundamentally believe there's no molecule that's more important than GLP-1 across that entire axis.

剂型与频次的演进

We already cracked the most important code — moving from something impractical (30 shots an hour), to the early days with Amylin Pharmaceuticals of a twice-a-day shot, to today: not just longer-acting once-a-week versions but far more potent (meaning we need far less of it) that have incredible pharmaceutical properties.

The next axis isn't just the form (oral or injectable) or the duration — orals we take every day, injectables once a week. There's some amazing ones Pfizer just acquired that look like it might be once a month. Amgen's also working on once-a-month, might be once-a-quarter. The easier it is to take a medicine, the more likely people will stay on it and have the longest-term benefit.

Semaglutide vs Tirzepatide：可互换

How do we think about semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy) and tirzepatide (Zepbound/Mounjaro)? Our team's fundamental perspective is that they all achieve the same goals. They were just novel IP strategies to achieve those same goals. They're pretty much interchangeable.

BMI 分段与"Right to Win"

From an investment and human-impact perspective, the key question is: why does a medicine have a right to win? For a medicine to have a right to win, we're essentially saying this medicine is going to really have a differential impact for people.

We always think about the obesity market as having three main segments: - Overweight — BMIs between 25 and 30 - Obese — BMIs between 30 and 40 - Morbidly obese — BMIs north of 40

For the aperture from BMI 25 up to just under 40, that's where most patients are today that require reactive treatment. But I would argue that as you go from your adolescence to your 50s and 60s, on average people are going to pick up about 5 BMI points. So being able to take a medicine even at low doses — even though they haven't been tested in those populations — I'm willing to bet would help protect people from that 5-point increase that puts them in harm's way.

Where there's still a right to win is really in that BMI zone of 40 and above. Sadly, these people require incredibly long titration to get up to these high levels of the hormone, and they need much more dramatic reduction in their food noise.

单靠饮食行不行？

I have a very basic question. Would there be any difference in outcomes if we simply ate the same exact input we eat on these medicines — if I figured out a psychological way to deal with the food noise? Alcohol addiction seems to be one of the things that gets addressed by these drugs — that seems unrelated to how much I eat.

Eating is definitely a part of it, and it's a major part. If we could all be disciplined and get on a proper diet, you wouldn't really need these medicines for managing our weight. But that's only going to cut at the weight part of it.

Unfortunately, if your biology is wired such that you have a family history of diabetes and you are pre-diabetic, we're starting to be in a place where we're just not producing the insulin required to take up that glucose. Consuming fewer calories can hit part of that, but it's unknown whether that'll be enough to undo your progression to diabetes.

On the addiction side — food is a form of addiction. Consuming fewer calories will help protect us from the vicious cycle of over-consuming food, but in terms of the addiction data coming out over the next year or so, you would lose that free gift of protecting yourself from being addicted to drugs, alcohol, or gambling. That comes from the signaling in the brain — the satiety axis that cuts across food and also some of these other behaviors.

There's an open question on what just consuming food would do for cardiovascular outcomes. We've got compelling data from Novo Nordisk over the last year showing that by taking GLP-1 medicines, there is a north of 20% reduction in our risk of having a heart attack or a stroke. What I thought was profound: that was independent of weight loss. That speaks to me that there's just another axis and another biological driver driving this cardioprotective result.

五、三道阻碍药物发挥真正影响的墙

I want to get to PCSK9 to really drive home the point of existing drugs before we get to all the exciting new stuff. There are existing drugs which could have radical impact if everyone was on them.

There are basically three different dimensions preventing people from getting maximal benefit of a breakthrough medicine: complexity, cost, and convenience (compliance).

Complexity（复杂度）

All of us have had the experience of learning we have some health problem and wanting to do something about it. It was amazing to me to actually have to deal with our health system and all the different branches it took from recognizing I had a cholesterol problem to, two years later, finally getting access to the medicine that could help.

You want to see a doctor because you got a blood test saying you have a problem. Then you learn the next appointment's only six weeks out. Then of course they want to redo the blood test. The friction in our system mounts and mounts. Eventually a lot of people just give up.

Why is it that I can go on my iPhone, go to Amazon, click a button, and the next day have toothpaste — but it takes me an incredible journey, hundreds of phone calls, multiple doctor visits, being pricked and prodded, just to protect myself from having a heart attack?

Cost（成本）

Most medicines we need to be on for the rest of our lives — the chronic care of that defensive health stack — are priced similarly to acute treatments, yet they need to be taken over decades. We have a timeline problem, a horizon problem. There's a lot of innovation that can occur around pricing models if the world is moving from reactively treating to proactively getting out ahead of disease.

Compliance（依从性）

Medicine only works if we take it. Because of the complexity of the system, because of how expensive these things are, and at the end of the day because we're all human, the more frequently we have to take a medicine, the higher the probability we're just going to quit. That's just human nature, myself included.

Compliance is this invisible barrier to all of us realizing our best health outcome. On one hand — GLP-1 medicines are miraculous. We went from twice a day to once a week, and now we've got versions that are once a month, maybe once a quarter. That's a huge unlock.

六、PCSK9：最接近"免费午餐"的药物

Maybe now's the time to tell a second drug story — a far less well-known one than GLP-1, which is PCSK9. It seems closest to a free-lunch drug. And because it deals with cardiovascular disease — something everyone's family or themselves has probably dealt with — it seems incredibly important.

The headline: PCSK9 medicines are amazing. What they do today is they can lower our bad cholesterol — LDL cholesterol — by 50%.

We now have outcome studies: - In patients that have previously had a heart attack or stroke, we can reduce that risk by over 20% in the future - For people at high risk of having a heart attack or stroke, the medicines approved today can lower that risk by about 25%

These are incredible medicines particularly because heart disease is still the number one killer in the US today.

人类遗传学的发现

The way these medicines were discovered is from human genetics. We go around the world and look at different populations and study what are some of the attributes and advantages of different populations.

It turns out there's a population of people in the world that have a genetic mutation conveying a massive advantage — they have a mutation in their PCSK9 gene, which means they don't produce the PCSK9 protein.

What we've learned is that the production of this PCSK9 protein actually interferes with our body's own ability to clear LDL cholesterol. The more PCSK9 we're producing, the fewer particles of LDL we can clear, the higher our cholesterol grows over time, the more those little particles invade our vasculature and mount up until we have a blockage.

Longitudinal studies over 15 years showed that these genetically deficient people had an 88% reduction in the risk of ever developing cardiovascular disease. That was miraculous.

药物工业的复刻

The pharmaceutical industry sought to replicate this — to get that advantage into the hands of people. First came injectable biologic medicines that would find their way to that PCSK9 protein and bind to it so the protein couldn't bind to your LDL receptor.

The next big innovation was moving from injectable biologics (monoclonal antibodies) to a new modality called RNA interference. Another form of subcutaneously injected medicine that makes its way down into your liver — long duration molecules so we don't have to inject ourselves 26 times a year, but maybe only twice.

权衡：GLP-1 vs PCSK9

I'm interested in both PCSK9 and GLP-1 trade-offs. It just seems like there's always a trade-off. What's the skeptic's point of view?

PCSK9s are very much more that free lunch than GLP-1s.

GLP-1s have real toxicity associated with them. We see in the clinical studies significant rates of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea — the first symptoms that cause a lot of people to quit early. If you look at people on longer, there's risk of gallstones and pancreatitis, plus muscle decline — consequences of losing weight quickly.

But when you look at the benefit — the fact that these are so protective across so many different axes: diabetes, obesity, cardiovascular disease — the aggregate of those diseases costs us 5 to 10 years of life. It's very much worth it for people to take the risk.

For PCSK9 — it's pretty much a free lunch. There was a ton of concern in the early days: what happens if your LDL goes down to zero? Doesn't LDL do good things? It turns out that LDL is produced in lots of different places. You can basically pin that PCSK9 bad protein down to zero, and the best we can do in terms of reduction of our LDL — even in animal models — is something in the 80 to 90% zone. Combined with the fact that we've got genetically advantaged people walking around not producing the protein and living long healthy lives, this one is very much a free lunch.

为什么 PCSK9 没有像 GLP-1 一样爆发？

Do you think everyone in the world will be on this drug at some point?

That would be a dream for me. If you project down the road longer term, this medicine should be far bigger in terms of the number of people on it and the revenues associated than GLP-1s. That's not what's playing out right now — for two reasons.

  1. GLP-1s have this amazing attribute: you take them and very quickly start to have some side effects — so you know you're on them, and you start to see the benefits. It's easier for medicines where there's an acute, clear, easily measurable benefit for people to stay on them.

  2. In the setting of cardiovascular disease, you don't feel anything if you have high bad cholesterol. It's this silent killer working in the background, slowly accumulating. You can be at peak physical shape and then all of a sudden have a heart attack or stroke.

Because the reward is so asymmetrically favorable versus the risk — the earlier we intervene with cholesterol accumulation and keep it at bay, the greater the likelihood we get through the rest of our lives, even if other things manifest.

七、神经退行性疾病：阿尔茨海默症的第一道曙光

The number three and four killers after metabolic disorders and cardiovascular are neurodegenerative and cancers. Neurodegenerative in particular — Alzheimer's drugs — seems to have been kind of this wasteland.

It's been a wasteland for decades. I think we've finally cracked the beginning of the code, but not the full answer.

What we found today: in patients with confirmed levels of plaques that have developed over decades in their brains, we now have medicines that will start to break up those plaques and remove them.

Unfortunately — and this will be intuitive — if you get to the patient that late in the disease, a lot of damage has already happened. Today we've got amazing medicines from both Biogen and Eli Lilly: if you are late in the disease progression of Alzheimer's, you can slow the decline by about 30%.

早期介入的潜在突破

If we can identify a patient at risk for Alzheimer's earlier and earlier — if a test is available to do that well — then it makes perfect sense that giving people these medicines as early as possible, not only will you be there in the early stages before a lot of damage is done, but you can think about it as turning off the faucet. As new amyloid plaques are produced, you're attacking them with a medicine that accelerates their clearance.

Maybe that has a dramatic effect on long-term decline — reducing rates by 40, 50 or more percent over a longer period. We might be able to wake up in a world where we live without this disease.

We know this is possible with GLP-1s for diabetes and obesity — if you take them early enough, you will not become diabetic. You will not become obese. We know that's possible for cardiovascular disease — if you start taking a PCSK9 inhibitor earlier in your life (which I think has a risk-reward even more favorable than statins), you won't progress to cardiovascular disease. The same is true for Alzheimer's.

[Ad break: Vanta, Ridgeline]

八、癌症：晚识别与进化的狡猾敌人

Up to this point, we can imagine a future world where everyone can enjoy the benefits of protective defensive measures for these three killers. What about cancer?

If we are successful in mobilizing people around a health stack and we can move from reactively treating disease to helping people be as proactive as possible, the unintended consequence is that as people live longer, eventually something's going to get us. The incidence of cancer as the issue that brings us down could rise.

为什么癌症难

Cancer is hard both because we often identify it too late, and then when we do, cancer is like a sneaky devil.

You might have an angle of attack — a medicine that presents itself early. As soon as you're making progress, all of a sudden the cancer has a survival instinct and it redirects. A medicine that had shrunk a tumor completely to zero has no effect. While that medicine eliminated the cells most vulnerable, the cells left behind — even just a few — now had the advantage in growing rapidly.

早期筛查：Exact Sciences 与 Guardant Health

In terms of identifying cancer early, there are companies like Exact Sciences and Guardant Health that have gone really hard at breakthrough innovations to help us screen for colorectal cancer.

We have the gold standard — a colonoscopy — and I have to go get one next year. But none of us are ever looking forward to that. Because it's something people don't look forward to, most people don't. That's really tragic because colorectal cancer is an incredibly slow-growing cancer — if you just got the colonoscopy, we could very much live in a world with a very low incidence of colorectal cancer.

  • Exact Sciences has a stool-based test with incredible ability to predict people at risk of cancer.
  • Guardant Health took it to the next step — recognizing the commercial complexity of getting people to complete a stool-based test. They came up with the next-gen version as a simple blood test that you can get done just about anywhere.

敏感性与特异性

Not all tests are created equally. When I look at a new diagnostic, I care deeply about two metrics:

  1. Sensitivity — of all the people that could have cancer, what percentage are we actually getting back as accurate diagnoses of cancer? If there are 100 cancers in a sample, what percentage actually get picked up?
  2. Specificity — if there are 100 samples that don't have cancer, what percentage give us a false positive?

多癌种早期检测（MCED）

There's a whole other industry around multi-cancer early detection evolving right now — companies moving from single-cancer tests to multi-cancer tests across large numbers of tumor types.

When you think about the offensive and defensive side of the health stack — what's not in the defensive stack is anything to protect us directly from cancer. That's because I try to attack cancer from the screening perspective. I made that part of offense.

  • At minimum, people should be doing a colonoscopy.
  • The next best-in-class is colonoscopy married with either a blood test or stool-based test with regular periodicity.
  • Over the next 3 to 5 years, particularly with how AI and these tests are converging, we're going to wake up with really awesome multi-cancer early detection tests. When we have them — shame on us if we're not getting them.

治疗端的革命：CAR-T 与 Capstan

The earlier we know we have a cancer, the greater the range of options. Sadly, my brother-in-law's father passed from multiple myeloma almost a decade ago. If he had been diagnosed today, his prognosis is dramatically different.

While cancer medicines began with just pills and injectable biologics, today we have an entirely new class — CAR-T cells — cells we take out of a patient's body and arm with the ability to attack the cancer directly. Companies like Capstan just came up with the next-gen version where they can inject on an IV basis cells that can attack cancer cells directly without having to take cells out of the patient's body.

These medicines have the potential to reduce tumors by 100% and to do that in 70%+ of patients, keeping them at bay for really long periods.

影像学：Neko、Prenuvo

What about imaging — both skin imaging (companies like Neko) and deep body imaging (companies like Prenuvo)?

To me, more data is worth it so long as you can put it in the right context.

The pictures from groups like Prenuvo are powerful because we can see progression over time — particularly as AI increasingly becomes our medical home, arming our AI system with that information will be super helpful.

The thing I worry about: as you measure more and more different dimensions, each with a material false positive rate, the odds that you come out with a false positive that can be disruptive in life becomes material.

For me personally, I'm not yet pursuing those. I'm very much focused on areas where if we're going to do a test, it's going to have a high probability of capturing what I'm testing for and a low probability of giving me a false reading.

九、药物发现的过程与 AI 的介入

Everything we talked about today was discovered and developed by incredibly talented people — scientists, people in the medical field, and business builders. Biology is complicated — it is unfreakingbelievable we can do the things you've been describing.

两大要素

First: what are the two key ingredients for discovery to happen? Scientific instinct married with tremendous amounts of capital. You need both to converge for there to be a breakthrough.

科学方法的循环

A scientist gets inspired to spend their lives trying to crack the code in a certain disease. Just like any consumer or tech product — what's the problem we're trying to solve?

What's developed over time is the scientific method — probably one of the most important methods in the history of humanity. There are discrete steps and tremendous iteration:

  1. Identify the problem
  2. Form a hypothesis — either by observing nature (the PCSK9 example) or combing through thousands of pages of literature
  3. Design experiments to test the hypothesis + predict what should happen
  4. Execute the experiments
  5. Review the data — confirm the hypothesis or loop back

The unfortunate part of the literature is that what's often missing is all the failures, which would be even more informative than the successes.

The process typically moves from: - Literature review → - In vitro experiments (petri dishes) → - Small animals → - Larger animals → - Package presented to regulators → - Toxicity studies → - First in human → - 5-7 years of clinical studies

AI + 自动化：科学的工业革命

I'm curious about two dimensions of shortening that feedback loop: AI, and what I'll call citizen pharmacology.

What you're describing on the AI side is our quest to get to a level of scientific super intelligence.

If scientists are armed with an agentic infrastructure that has a scientific intelligence at an Einstein level or multiple of Einstein level — the force multiplication between a superstar scientist and that agentic resource means they will do science faster, with higher probability, for lower cost, and greater impact.

Human scientists are always going to be constrained by: - What we can retain in our human minds - Our ability to use our human hands to run experiments

AI coming into discovery is one of the biggest unlocks in the history of medicine because we're no longer constrained by our minds. We're no longer constrained by our hands. We can generate all n number of hypotheses that could exist, and run experimentation at a scale that's unprecedented — 24/7.

Timelines that would typically take 3 to 5 years can take less than a couple of years.

今天已经发生的：从模型到分子的一个月

We're in the early innings of being able to discover novel targets. We have not yet in a systematic way been able to discover a target that didn't exist yet. But for targets we know exist but have struggled to develop medicines against, we're starting to see that code broken.

Companies like Lila Sciences and Anaba — you can basically ask the agentic system to develop a molecule against a certain target, and it can crack that code and give us something we can work with and start running experiments on in a month's time (historically would have taken a couple years).

数据护城河：Garbage-in-garbage-out 的问题

When did that curve tip? Very recently.

For most of my career there's been some version of machine learning and the hopes of AI driving drug discovery. I always worried that paradigm required data that didn't exist in any easy place. If all you do is train AI models based on available research, you have garbage in, garbage out.

Unfortunately, a lot of the recorded literature is actually incorrect. If you go try to replicate the experiments in the literature, you don't even get the same results.

The AI companies I believe are going to be most set up for success are those that: - Have incredible AI talent - Have access to significant amounts of capital - Can combine both with a novel way by which they can generate science tokens that don't exist in the public domain

The generation of those tokens becomes a big driver of their moat — and what enables them to get to an increasing level of scientific intelligence that starts to separate from what can be achieved with LLMs without the benefit of those tokens.

5-10 年后：完全自动化的发现过程

If you close your eyes and think 5, 7, 10 years from now — what's your best guess?

I think it's going to be completely automated.

I was up at Lila Sciences the other day. You go into their labs and you see all these robotic arms, petri dishes moving around in an automated fashion. The scale of data required to train these models — trillions of tokens — has got to move beyond the constraints of human hands. It has to be done robotically.

Superstar, brilliant, amazing scientists are no longer going to spend two-thirds of their career at the side of a bench pipetting all day long. They're going to have all that time back to point their energy and their enthusiasm toward figuring out: how do I use the systems and machines I have, and point them in the right direction at the codes we should be cracking?

公民药理学与 FDA 的改革

What do you make of this peptides plus citizen pharmacology side of things? They just said there's going to be 14-something peptides allowed to be compounded.

We live in a world where people want to figure things out that help other people. Now we've got platforms that allow people to share that — the creation of groups that couldn't exist before we had the technology and communities that get formed.

Whether it's the commercial success of GLP-1s or the massive movement around peptides — and honestly not even limited to peptides, you go on Reddit and can see people experimenting across all sorts of dimensions — this is a shared mission where people are saying: "I want to take my future health into my own hands. I'm not willing to wait for scientists to take on this problem through the traditional pathways. I need to know the answers now."

At the same time, there's recognition by the FDA under Marty Makary's real leadership — so long as we have all these frictions going from a hypothesis for a medicine to testing it in people to approval, disease is going to keep winning.

Real-world impact: - Marty brought in an AI system to help comb through thousands of pages of filings in record time versus the armies of people that would have to comb through days and days of time - Challenging every aspect — in what settings do we not need as much animal evidence? In what settings do we no longer need multiple redundant studies?

Anywhere you look, we are seeing a movement to do things more efficiently, faster, and with greater impact.

十、Alex 的个人故事：从 Merrill Lynch 到 Braidwell

I've had this experience with you countless times — I'll introduce you to somebody and somewhere between 30 minutes in and 2.5 hours in, the person has a thought which they rarely say, which is: who the hell is this guy? We've done the same thing again — hours without laying any groundwork for your background.

童年：父亲的企业家精神 + 母亲的韧性

I've just been super lucky. Reflecting back on my childhood, I guess my parents were training me to be a biotech builder and investor since the time I was five.

I had the greatest childhood ever — grew up in a typical American home. My dad was this relentless entrepreneur. He worked harder than anybody I knew. He was so all-in in any business endeavor — our entire family was all in on it. Mom was doing the billing. My brother and I were in the garage refinishing furniture.

Despite being such a relentless businessman, he was never successful really at any of those. Sadly he passed of Parkinson's over a decade ago. One of the things he told me in those last few days is that he felt like he never stuck with anything long enough to see it through over the tougher moments.

Pair my dad with my mom — an incredible, ever-optimist. From my earliest memories, I can remember her drilling into my mind: "We always commit and we never quit." She pretty much convinced me I could do anything I wanted if I put my mind to it.

  • From my dad: I got to experience what it means to take risk and that you can fail and life goes on.
  • From my mom: I developed this sense of resilience.

MIT → Merrill Lynch → 偶然进入生物科技

I was the first in my family to go to college. I graduated from MIT. At MIT, learning about business, economics, science — and connecting it back to working alongside my dad (I'd put $2 into a rocking chair, if we sold it for $200 I thought it was amazing; if we didn't sell it, I was pissed) — I was learning about risk and reward from my dad.

I went on to New York City and Wall Street. Ended up at Merrill Lynch with amazing mentors. Two major stops at Merrill: the capital markets side, and then the derivatives desk.

When I moved to the derivatives desk, I actually never picked biotech. I was just put into biotech. I was assigned to the biotech subgroup and knew nothing about it. But I fell in love with it so quickly — because the risk/reward in that setting was about life and death.

从衍生品到 Deerfield

I came to realize that life sciences investment strategies were actually very similar across firms. Knowing what I knew about derivative securities, it struck me that there was a better way to either maintain the return they needed at lower risk, or maintain the risk and produce a higher rate of return.

I spent nights and weekends writing this beautiful deck. When it was done, I sent it out and told my roommate Don, "My phone is going to be ringing off the hook."

Crickets. I didn't get one single response.

Months went by before my phone finally rang — Jeff Kaplan, today a dear friend and mentor, then the head of trading at a tiny little firm named Deerfield Management (about 12 people) where Jim Flynn had just taken over as general partner.

Jim obsessed over: what is every way our investment firm can have an advantage in producing returns, so we can attract more capital and have more impact on the future of human health? I got hired. I was 24. The next youngest person was probably 40. I'd ask almost a thousand questions a day, and these amazing people would spend the time to teach me.

15 年后的黑暗觉醒

I spent the next 15 years helping to build Deerfield into the go-to source of capital for the industry. Then something really tragic happened.

We had acquired a data source that took us deeper into understanding a patient's journey — from being prescribed the medicine, to getting insurance approval, to how long they stayed on the medicine. Yes, medicine only works if people take it — but they weren't.

That was a really dark period for me. Reflecting on all the time, effort, and capital I was putting behind innovation — I had to start asking: For what purpose? If people need to be on a medicine for the rest of their lives and they're taking it for a year on average and then quitting, are we just optimizing for maximum expected returns but no impact on public health?

I'd been married since 2012. We had our first baby. I was reflecting on just how impactful my parents were, what lessons would I be teaching my daughters? Am I just doing the wrong thing?

The answer was no. What I needed to do was take the innate entrepreneurial energy from my upbringing and the skill set from my apprenticeship at Deerfield, and point those talents not just at the discovery side of invention, but at the impact that's required to actually see these medicines have the impact they're intended to have.

Braidwell 的诞生

That was my inspiration for leaving Deerfield and building Braidwell. One of our dear friends, Brian Kreiter — amazing husband, father, incredibly talented business person — was the chief operating officer for Bridgewater. I was co-running Deerfield.

We decided to spend deep time trying to put our lessons learned and insights together and try to design from a first-principles perspective: what would the operating system for human health look like? And what could we do to be the driving force behind making that happen?

How could we be the force that's not just okay with a once-in-a-decade $100B GLP-1 revolution, but the force behind making sure this once-in-a-lifetime trillion-dollar cost savings revolution happens — because we can be the force behind medicines having their moment where they could prevent diseases that are already preventable today?

投资方法论：三个问题

If you could sum up the investment approach in its simplest form, how would you do it?

I think I just bring you into what we call our morning meeting. At Braidwell, we get together as a team every morning from 9:15 until we're done.

Imagine a big room with an amazingly talented team: scientists, biostatisticians, commercial experts, AI experts, investors, operating people, traders, structured finance people. Everybody has some special superpower.

In order to be a great backer of innovation, we need to answer three questions:

  1. Are the innovations we're getting behind going to work or fail?
  2. Are they ultimately going to be relevant from a market potential perspective?
  3. Is there a way to back this company — invest, enable it to move faster with capital — that produces a return attractive enough for the investors trusting us with their capital?

We want to bring together the most talented minds at each domain to give us an advantage we can replicably deploy day after day — both to back the companies that should be moving forward faster, and to make exist the companies that don't but should.

十一、收官问题：最温暖的礼物

Unfortunately, we're out of time. I'll go to my traditional closing question. What is the kindest thing that anyone's ever done for you?

That's my wife.

Look, I'm pretty darn hard at it every day. Every minute of the day I can be hard at it. That drives me to different levels of stress. I could be so myopically deep down trying to figure something out that I kind of just lose track of everything else around me.

My wife Cass has this amazing ability to just know when I'm on the precipice of that breaking point, and she can find some way to distract me, make me laugh, and get me back to neutral. That's a big force and an advantage for me — because I don't have to worry about going so hard. I've got a partner right there by my side that can help make sure I'm not just going after things I really care about doing, but having some fun along the way.

Knowing Cass — I know that's true. Alex, thanks so much for your time.

Thank you. I had a great time.

[Closing ads: Ramp, Vanta, Ridgeline, Rogo, WorkOS]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：GLP-1 成功的真正原因不是"瘦得更多"，而是"瘦得刚好"
大多数投资者和媒体把 GLP-1 叙事简化为"减肥竞赛"——谁减得更多谁赢。但 Alex 在数据里看到了一个完全相反的信号：**消费者并不在寻找"减肥核弹"，他们在寻找"可持续的微小优势"**。高剂量=高副作用=高脱落率。这意味着整个华尔街的估值模型可能都搭错了轴——他们在用"疗效最大化"给一款"合规性最大化"的药定价。
这里藏着一个更深的跨领域隐喻：**GLP-1 市场的逻辑更接近 SaaS，而不是传统药品**。传统药是一次性"治愈事件"，定价逻辑是急性价值；GLP-1 却像 Netflix 订阅——留存率（compliance）才是真正的 LTV 驱动。当口服版把价格打到 $150/月、新增处方从每周 20 万飙到 30 万时，这不是医药故事，这是一次典型的"价格弹性 × 订阅留存"的消费级拐点。制药工业第一次被迫用消费品思维重新做自己。
## 二、隐藏的系统性真相：医疗创新的真正瓶颈已经从"发现"迁移到"分发"
Alex 反复回到一个让人不安的事实——**过去几十年人类预期寿命曲线几乎

item.title: [All-In Podcast - Videos] Ray Dalio: "AI Is Eating Everything - and It Might Eat Itself"
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast - Videos
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：Dalio 其实在说"民主制度本身成了债务危机的放大器"

表面上这是一次关于债务周期、黄金、AI 泡沫的访谈。但如果把 Dalio 的五大力量拆开重组，会发现他真正在暗示一个让人不安的结论——美国的政治制度结构正在与它的财政现实产生"生理性排异反应"。

他说 DOGE 失败不是因为方法错误，而是因为"在一个无论你做什么都会被批判的社会里，你没有时间把一件痛苦但必要的事做完"。这句话的潜台词极其尖锐：选举周期的长度 < 结构性改革所需的痛苦消化期。这意味着民主制在晚期债务周期中存在一个数学上无解的时间错配问题。这也是他最后几乎是坦白地说"我们需要一个强硬的领导者"——一个被自由主义训练的人听到这句话几乎会本能警觉，但他就是这么说的。

跨领域连接：这正是生物学上"细胞凋亡失灵"的宏观翻版——当组织无法淘汰无效细胞，整体就会走向癌变或衰竭。DOGE 就像一次失败的自噬（autophagy）。

二、被所有人忽略的一句话："税收本身就是通胀"

这是整场对话里最容易被滑过去但最具颠覆性的一句。Dalio 说："如果你的税收上升，这就是通胀。为什么它和房租上涨被区别对待？"

这一句话实际上把过去四十年的 CPI 统计学打了一记耳光。如果接受他的定义，美国过去几十年的"低通胀黄金期"可能是一个统计幻觉——通胀只是从商品价格迁移到了税收、医保、教育等非 CPI 计算口径的支出里。

跨领域连接：这与热力学第二定律的"熵不会消失，只会转移"惊人相似。货币印出来的购买力损耗，不会因为 CPI 没显示就消失，它只是换了马甲藏进了你钱包的其他口袋。黄金从 2900 涨到 5200 不是黄金在涨，是市场开始识别那些被藏起来的熵。

三、最深的埋伏：AI 可能不是泡沫，而是"系统性自我吞噬"

Dalio 用了一个极其精妙的比喻——"AI is eating everything, and it might eat itself"。但他真正埋的钩子是下一句关于中美 AI 哲学差异的描述：

  • 美国：AI 必须盈利 → 资本要回报 → 高定价 → 普及受限
  • 中国：AI 像电一样免费 → 不求利润，只求使用 → 全民生产力跃升

这意味着什么？美国的资本市场结构本身，可能成为 AI 革命中的"拖累器"而不是"加速器"。这是一个几乎没人公开讨论的悖论：自由资本主义催生了 AI，但自由资本主义也可能让 AI 在中国的"准公共品"模式下被反超。

最意外的连接：这和 19 世纪英国为什么输给美国非常像——英国的铁路是私有的、盈利导向；美国的铁路获得了大量联邦土地补贴和公共支持。盈利模型并不总是赢。Dalio 在暗示的是：当一个技术进入"基础设施级别"，私有盈利逻辑就成了自我吞噬的陷阱。

四、一个彩蛋级观察：他对比特币的冷淡暴露了他真正的世界观

很多人会以为 Dalio 贬低比特币是"老派偏见"。但仔细看他的论据——"央行不会买比特币，因为它没有隐私，所有交易都可以被追踪和控制"——你会发现他的货币观的底层逻辑其实是主权视角，而非个人视角。

这是一个惊人的反讽：比特币的支持者认为"不可追踪"是它对抗国家的优势；但在 Dalio 的框架里，"可被追踪"恰恰是它不能成为储备资产的致命伤——因为大国不会把自己的储备放在一个可以被敌对大国监控的账本上。

换句话说：比特币赢得了个人自由的叙事，却输掉了主权储备的叙事。而真正决定资产价格的终极买家，是央行，不是散户。这可能是整场访谈里对加密行业最冷的一盆水。

📝 转录文本

开场

Ray Dalio, welcome back to the All-In podcast. Third Times the Charm. Thanks for being here.

It's always a blast to be here. Thank you for having me.

The last conversation we had was so popular and it was so timely because it was just a few days actually after the inauguration of President Trump, and you had provided some very prescient outlooks for the administration that I think we all thought would be very helpful to get on the record. At the time, you had highlighted—and as you have been for some time—this great debt cycle we're in, the fiscal and monetary policy issues that are driving that debt cycle, and provided some input that if we were able to cut our deficit to GDP to roughly 3%, we may have a shot at a smoother transition here. Today, the CBO estimates that the 2026 deficit to GDP is about 6%.

广告插入：Airwallex
