If you were building a global financial system from first principles today, you wouldn't build it on 50-year-old legacy rails. You'd build Airwallex. It's the single platform for global accounts, cards, and payments that treats the entire world like a local market. Stop paying the legacy tax and start building the future at airwallex.com/allin. Airwallex. Build the future.

一、我们现在走在什么路上？五大力量框架

So the first question I have for you, looking back on the past year of the administration and the actions of Congress and the economy: Are we on a good path? Are we on no different a path than we were say a year ago? Are we moving too slowly? Does Doge tell us something about what's possible at this stage?

I studied these big cycles in history going back 500 years, and there are five big forces that are intertwined to determine the answer to your question:

  1. The debt/money cycle — I'll take you into that in a minute.
  2. Domestic gaps — the wealth and values gaps that are causing irreconcilable differences between the left and the right, affecting how taxes, democracy, and everything works.
  3. International great power conflict — the classic rising of a great power challenging an existing great power and changing the international world order.
  4. Technology — all through these cycles there have been technology.
  5. Acts of nature — droughts, floods, and pandemics.

And when we think of orders, we're talking about there's always a monetary order. And all monetary orders have broken down for the same reasons. All political orders, domestic political orders, they all always change—in the United States less so, we have 250 years here, but they always change. There was one civil war in there. Internationally they always change. All orders change. The international geopolitical order going from a multilateral to a unilateral world order is changing, and certainly technology is changing.

二、政府财政的本质：把国家当成一家公司来看

Okay. So getting that fact that they're all on there, now I'll go down to explain the government's finances and answer your question.

The economics of a country are basically the same as the economics of a company or an individual, except the government has the ability to print money. Look at it like a company or like your own.

  • Basically, it's projected to spend about $7 trillion, take in about $5 trillion.
  • So it's running a 40% deficit — 40% of its spending.
  • It's been running deficits for a long time. So it has a debt that is 600% — six times the amount of money that it takes in.

And we can project that number.

债务循环如何像身体的循环系统

The problem with debt cycles—you could see them transpire. They're almost like the circulatory system of the body. The capital markets bring credit to different parts of the economy, and if that credit is used to be productive and produces an income that pays for the debt service, it's a healthy process.

But what happens is that if the debt service grows relative to the income because it's not paying for it, it's like plaque in the system growing up, and it squeezes out spending.

  • We now have that $2 trillion deficit.
  • Half of that is interest payments.
  • Plus we have to roll over $9 trillion of debt that has been accumulated and is maturing.

As a handy number, 3% of GDP would sort of stabilize the situation. Very unhealthy condition. It's not just unhealthy because it's squeezing out those spendings, but also because there's a supply and a demand. You have to roll over the $9 trillion of debt that's coming due and you have to sell two trillion more.

买家在哪里？

Now you go to the buyers. Who are the buyers? There are some domestic buyers and there are foreign buyers—about a third foreign buyers. And now it's a riskier situation from their point of view.

  • First of all, it's a lot to acquire. Dollar-denominated debt is already a large percentage of their portfolio, larger than it would be if just decided on a prudent basis.
  • But also we have political/geopolitical risks that extend to possibly the risks that the debtor and the creditor will have a conflict. You could imagine that with China. You could imagine that with Europe even. Europeans could wonder whether they will get sanctioned—the debt service payments might not be made as a sanction. And the United States has to worry about whether it's going to bring in that money.

The things that I'm describing have happened repeatedly through history. Particularly in the 1929 to 45 period, you saw this dynamic. So there is this financial piece which in and of itself is not healthy for the US government, but it's also problematic because of the other factors compounding the problem.

三、DOGE 失败了吗？系统性结构的困境

You highlighted this problem. You provided a diagnosis that if we could get to 3% we could soften the effect, but it hasn't happened. We were all very hopeful last year around this time when Elon Musk decided to lead Doge, the Department of Government Efficiency. Did Doge fail because the actions were wrong, or because the system itself cannot be changed at this point in the cycle?

You're talking about taking an inefficient government and making it efficient, and having to do it quick because there are elections, and if people don't like it, you lose your mandate. And in a society in which no matter what you do, you're criticized and torn down...

We have the question: does democracy and our system lend itself toward the sort of executive leadership that both makes it efficient and makes it acceptable for all people? There was a lot of cutbacks—things like school lunch programs—and then trying to do it surgically. How do you do that effectively quickly in a manner that doesn't cause so much controversy that the government falls?

If you look at history—that's why I deal with the political—and even common sense: are you going to have the executive leadership that's going to be able to make this satisfactory with most people? And do that quickly? I think that's a hell of a trick to pull off.

So it might just be structurally a little difficult at this stage.

What an understatement. Structurally a little difficult at this stage.

欺诈与浪费：Minnesota 的案例

Well, there was another big news story recently that there may be quite a lot of fraud going on with public dollars in Minnesota—daycares that don't exist and billions of dollars are flowing to individuals to run these daycares. Do you think that this is a symptom of this stage of the cycle?

Yeah, it's both the stage of the cycle and—if you're going to have something well-managed, are you going to have the government well-manage it? Go to the Department of Motor Vehicles. It's so big and complex, such a mess. Is it a surprise to you that there's all of this inefficiency?

No.

四、黄金与货币的本质

Last time we spoke, you highlighted that a piece of your portfolio was in gold. Since we spoke, gold has climbed from 2900 an ounce to 5200 an ounce. What has happened with gold over the last year?

It's the big cycle. What you have to understand is that gold is not a precious metal that's speculated on, like most people have come to think of it. It is the most established money. It's the second largest reserve currency that central banks hold.

For various reasons—economic, supply/demand, political, geopolitical—central banks themselves have acquired gold to build that up, and individuals and others are looking for an alternative money.

什么是"货币"？

The question is: what is money?

  • Money mechanistically is debt. If you're holding money, you're holding it in the form of a debt instrument.
  • If you're holding a debt instrument, what you're getting is a promise from somebody to deliver you money.
  • The power of central banks when they have too much debt is to print money.

So the question is, Dave: what money do you think is safe?

Asset-backed, right? I want an asset. I want something with some physical known limitation to it.

And particularly what you want is something that can be transferred from one place to another, because money is both a medium of exchange and a storehold of wealth. If one country's central bank wants to pay another government, it can't be in fixed assets like buildings—you have to transact in something you can transfer.

Gold is the only asset—the long-term historic asset—for these reasons:

  • It can be transferred.
  • They can't print a lot of it.
  • It is not dependent on somebody giving you something.

财富 vs. 货币

There's an important thing to distinguish: wealth from money.

  • Wealth is in stuff—buildings, companies, and so on. But you can't spend wealth. You have to sell it, and then you get money to spend.
  • Right now, we have an awful lot of wealth relative to money. And the question is, what is that money? There's the risk that you go to convert your wealth into money and they're going to print money—because that's what they've always done since we've had fiat currencies.

五、泡沫、财富税、与黄金配置

Where are we in terms of folks converting their wealth into gold?

What I look at is literally who has what assets, including central banks. We've seen it go from an extremely small number to something that is a less small number. That price increase and change in composition has brought it almost—not quite, but almost—toward the average of what it's been over a period of time. Total wealth is still so large relative to money, that's a real issue.

泡沫的机制 + 财富税的风险

One question that might be asked: are we in a bubble? Are AI stocks and other such stocks in a bubble?

One of the characteristics of bubbles is that there becomes a need for money that requires people to sell their assets to get money to meet that need. Quite often that need comes from borrowing money to buy those assets. Then the assets go up in price. But it can't be sustained because you have to make the debt service payments.

Now we have a dynamic. The bubble will burst as that dynamic takes place. Just imagine if you put in wealth taxes. Anything—if you put in wealth taxes, and there's a lot of fear of wealth taxes in and of itself—can drive money/wealth to cash. And there's only one way you're going to get the cash from the wealth: either sell it or borrow against it, which causes its own cash flow issues.

配置建议

If you didn't have a view on gold, one should have between 5% and 15% of their portfolio in gold. It's a diversifier. When the shit hits the fan, gold does well and other things don't, generally speaking. I'm not trying to tout people on buying gold, but I would say: what is safe?

六、为什么比特币没跑赢？

Why hasn't Bitcoin performed the same way? Gold climbed 80%, Bitcoin's down 25%.

There are important differentiating characteristics of Bitcoin, and also who owns it and why they buy/sell.

  • Bitcoin does not have privacy. Any transactions can be monitored and indirectly perhaps controlled. Central banks are not going to want to buy Bitcoin and be able to hold it.
  • There are questions about new technologies like quantum computing—can there be issues there?
  • It tends to have a pretty high correlation with tech stocks. If somebody gets squeezed in one thing, they sell whatever else they have.
  • It's a relatively small, relatively controllable market.

A lot of attention has been given to Bitcoin, but as a money, it's small in relationship to gold. There is only one gold.

白银呢？

What about silver?

Silver in its production is a residual commodity. The supply is difficult to increase. Historically, the pound sterling—silver was perceived as a monetary item. But it has also taken on a speculative life of its own. People are hot in it because it's been hot.

七、利率、美联储与 K 型经济

What's your view on where rates are and how the Fed has acted?

Because we have so much federal debt, interest rates are one of the three main considerations: taxes, spending, and interest rates on the debt.

  • You can't make interest rates severely artificially low because one man's debts are another man's assets. Too low for the creditor produces a lot more borrowing—you can fuel a bubble.
  • You can't have them so high that the debtor gets squeezed ineffectively.
  • So there's a balancing act: keep them high enough for the creditor, not so high that the debtor suffers.

K 型经济的挑战

This is made more difficult because of what's called the K economy:

  • Bubble elements in part of the economy—who will be the first to be a trillionaire, the top 1%.
  • Meanwhile, 60% of all Americans have below a sixth-grade reading level, and to make them productive—particularly as AI has replacements for them—is particularly difficult.

The idea of setting an interest rate and having a fiscal and monetary policy for the economy as a whole that doesn't deal with the differences in circumstances is more challenging.

美联储会重新扩表吗？

Is it inevitable that we see a re-expansion of the Fed's balance sheet?

I think it's likely down the road. Right now there's the shortening of maturities as a means to deal with that. Of course, that increases debt rollover risk. Sell less long debt, try to hold the short rate down so the longer rates' attachment helps hold the long rate down. Then try to use the government's power of persuasion on other countries to either buy the debt, hold the debt, or have other forms of capital enter the United States.

对下一任美联储主席的看法

How do you like Kevin Warsh's pick for Fed chair?

It's a very, very big challenge. I think he's a practical man. He understands both sides of the pros and cons. I think it's a tough job.

八、关税、通胀与经济学家的盲区

Looking back on the economic effect of tariffs, what did economists get right and wrong?

税收就是通胀

First, there's the tax revenue part. I think economists make the mistake of not including taxes in inflation.

If your taxes go up, that's inflation. Why should it be any different than if your cost of housing goes up? Why shouldn't it be part of the inflation calculation? It's taking money out of your pocket. It's probably the biggest expense for a lot of people.

Through history, tariffs used to be the biggest source of revenue for government throughout most history and in most countries. It's a totally valid way of raising money, and you get the foreigners paying a portion of it.

空心化问题

As part of the big cycle question: we are not independent. We've had a hollowing out of manufacturing, the middle class, and so on. Are we going to try to build that? What is the plan? Or are we going to continue with large trade deficits and dependence on foreign capital?

  • Unsustainable trade deficits are the other side of capital surpluses (dependence on foreign capital).
  • That's unsustainable—you need some way of rectifying that.
  • Partially that plan can have trade tariffs. I think they're totally valid. But it all has to be part of a greater plan to develop the industries we need.

You need that not only economically but geopolitically—you can't have dependencies. We've moved from a multilateral world order to a power-based confrontational world economy. Everybody's threatening to cut off everything—goods and capital wars. You have to build independence.

3% 方案

I'd say people are misunderstanding that. The important thing is we get the other things right—let's get down to 3%. There's a bipartisan bill on this. The 3% has come out in favor. I'm in favor. The 3% three-part solution: 3% of GDP, three parts—a bit from taxes, a bit from spending, and hopefully interest rates.

关税能取代所得税吗？

President Trump shared his vision that tariffs can completely replace an income tax. Do you think that's feasible?

I don't think it's anywhere near that, both because of the combination of the size and then the impact of that size. Tariffs are regressive. We have to deal with the wealth gap. The biggest problem of the wealth gap, which is a big social problem, is also the productivity gap. You have to make most people productive through infrastructure and so on. I think that needs to be addressed.

九、联邦雇员削减与生产力问题

Nearly half of Americans either work for a government agency or a government service provider. The federal workforce declined by 317,000 employees, roughly 14%. What happens to those individuals?

I haven't studied the numbers, I don't think I can adequately answer that. I would say government is extremely inefficient. It has a role—an important role—but even that role it's handling very inefficiently. Other governments handle that role (maybe education) in a better way. The best thing you could invest in is education.

The one thing that's good about the capitalist system is it doesn't live if somebody either won't bet on it or it doesn't make a profit. So wherever those people go—there are just so many inefficient people and inefficient systems.

成功国家的三个要素

Is the fundamental issue a lack of productivity-driven growth, or that people aren't prepared to be productive?

There are three things basically that you need to do to be successful:

  1. Educate your children well—capable of being productive, and educate them in civility so they are civil with each other.
  2. Create an orderly civil environment where people can compete and work with each other to be productive—that works for the most people.
  3. Stay out of wars—no civil war and no international war.

If you do those three things right, you will have a successful country. That's all throughout history. We're having problems with all three.

十、不可调和的分歧与"需要一个强硬领导者"

Are those three things the antidote to unionization, socialism, wealth taxes?

What we need is to stop fighting. We're now at a stage where we have irreconcilable differences. When the causes people are behind are more important to them than the system, the system is in jeopardy. Our system is in jeopardy because people will not accept the system or the alternatives—so they're going to fight.

We're going to have the midterm elections. Probably the Democrats will take the House. Nobody can succeed because everybody's going to be fighting.

If you go back in history—Plato, around 350 BC, wrote about the cycle of democracies and the threats to them. What's happening now is similar to Julius Caesar and Rome—being stabbed in the Senate. What you need is a bipartisan—you need the country to have a strong, almost a strong leader. We do need a strong leader to get the reforms done to make the country work well.

How do you force this mob of people who are behaving this way—including in the elections—so fragmented, to create order? You need a tough leader who will force them to do difficult things and not fight with each other and focus on being productive.

社会主义还是法西斯主义？

It sounds like there may be this inevitable path between some form of socialism and some form of fascism.

I think we're moving toward that war. We're in that war. We're in what I call stage five of a cycle. In the book I describe the pattern that's happened over and over again. When you get to this position—bad finances combined with large wealth and values gaps, irreconcilable differences, external threats as well as domestic threats—you have this dynamic. That's where we are.

I'm like a mechanic. I'm not ideological. I'm just a practical guy trying to make money in the markets and trying to describe things. That's what it looks like.

十一、AI 泡沫：科技会延续，但公司不一定

When we look at the bubble question on AI, what a lot of people don't realize in bubbles is that through all technologies, they think they are betting on the technology when they buy the stocks and the companies. That's not true.

There's a giant difference between the behavior of the companies and the behavior of the technologies:

  • The norm is that a lot of companies won't survive at the start—very small percentage—and they'll all fight.
  • But the technologies will go on, and it'll be great.

We've seen it with the 2000 bubble. Even in the late '20s—it was unbelievable. The technologies will go on, but the companies won't necessarily go on.

AI 吞噬一切——甚至吞噬自己

Right now it looks to me like AI basically is eating everything, and it might eat itself—not produce adequate profits.

We can't take just a domestic view. We have to look also at what's happening in China and make interesting distinctions.

  • There's a difference in philosophy carried through in the economy of how the US and China work. We have primarily a profit-based system. They have a system in which they might believe that profits are a second consideration—not necessarily needed to achieve the best results.
  • In China, they would say: "Usage of AI is fantastic. It should be like electricity. Let's make it free for everyone and open source for everyone." They might get much higher usage, and they'll get productivity gains through the usage.
  • We have a profit system to pay back.

Now we're in one world. How do you compete in that world? Just imagine their technologies are almost as good as ours—they are, they're not far behind—but you could get them for free, open source. Now you've got to pay it back.

These are systematic risks that enter into the picture of AI.

十二、如果能重写宪法——Dalio 的建议

If you could go back and restructure the United States, be a founding father, what are one to three things you would have done differently?

It's like the marshmallow test. You give a kid the choice between one marshmallow now and two in 20 minutes—the kid who chooses two in 20 minutes will make better decisions. Therein lies our problem: immediate gratification, and not knowing if things are going to be productive.

But the system has been remarkably adaptable too. We've gone through crises, wiped out debts, and gotten past it.

It's a tough question to balance financial prudence with innovative inventions. Take AI now. Nobody knows what's going to come of it. Is it going to pay? Is it not going to pay? What do you write into law that gets you financial prudence and control? Does that lessen experimentation and entrepreneurship?

It's tough to do this with rules. Maybe the main thing is: read history. Read history and know these things and try to get that balance right. Everything's a matter of balance—the pain of failing, or the pain of putting money into something that fails.

结尾

Well, Ray, I want to thank you once again for taking the time to be here. It's always great to catch up, hear your perspective. So much has changed in the last year, and yet so much hasn't.

And thank you for what you guys do. I'm riveted to your program. Conversations like this are really practical help for a lot of people. Thank you for letting me participate, and thank you for what you do for a lot of people.

Thank you. That's right. I'm going all in. I'm going all in.

item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：Dalio 其实在说"民主制度本身成了债务危机的放大器"
表面上这是一次关于债务周期、黄金、AI 泡沫的访谈。但如果把 Dalio 的五大力量拆开重组，会发现他真正在暗示一个让人不安的结论——**美国的政治制度结构正在与它的财政现实产生"生理性排异反应"**。
他说 DOGE 失败不是因为方法错误，而是因为"在一个无论你做什么都会被批判的社会里，你没有时间把一件痛苦但必要的事做完"。这句话的潜台词极其尖锐：**选举周期的长度 < 结构性改革所需的痛苦消化期**。这意味着民主制在晚期债务周期中存在一个数学上无解的时间错配问题。这也是他最后几乎是坦白地说"我们需要一个强硬的领导者"——一个被自由主义训练的人听到这句话几乎会本能警觉，但他就是这么说的。
**跨领域连接**：这正是生物学上"细胞凋亡失灵"的宏观翻版——当组织无法淘汰无效细胞，整体就会走向癌变或衰竭。DOGE 就像一次失败的自噬（autophagy）。
## 二、被所有人忽略的一句话："税收本身就是通胀"
这是整场对话里最容易被滑过去但最具颠覆性的一句。Dalio 说："**如果你的税收上升，

item.title: [Invest Like The Best - Videos] World's Top Researcher on AI, LLMs, and Robot Intelligence
item.dc:creator: Invest Like The Best - Videos
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的核心洞察：机器人学的"摩拉维克陷阱"正在被悄悄反转

这场对话最让我震惊的不是技术细节，而是一个隐藏的范式翻转：Sergey 提到 6 个月前他们发现，机器人的瓶颈已经从"低层物理动作"转移到了"中层语义推理"。这意味着什么？意味着未来训练机器人不再依赖工程师收集天量的遥操作数据，而是任何人都可以"用嘴"教机器人——通过语言标注就能让它泛化得更好。

这是一个被严重低估的拐点。它暗示了一个荒诞的可能性：未来最有价值的"机器人训练师"可能不是机械工程师，而是语言学家、人类学家、甚至幼儿园老师——那些最擅长用语言把复杂场景拆解成可理解步骤的人。机器人行业的人才结构可能会发生我们尚未意识到的剧变。

跨领域连接：Fineman 的"自旋"与企业的"势能"——物理直觉是人类认知的隐秘操作系统

Sergey 引用 Fineman 谈论亚原子粒子"自旋"的类比，以及"那家公司很有势能"这种日常表达，揭示了一个深刻的事实：人类的所有高阶认知，包括理论物理和商业判断，都在偷偷地"借用"物理直觉作为底层运算引擎。

这给我们一个出人意料的预言：如果物理智能真的实现了，它可能不仅会改变机器人，还会反过来改变 LLM 本身。当前 LLM 在"推理"任务上的瓶颈，或许根本不是缺少更多文本，而是缺少 grounded physical understanding。物理智能模型可能成为下一代 AGI 的"小脑"——不是替代 LLM，而是给 LLM 装上身体感。

更有趣的跨领域连接：猴子工具实验中神经元激活转移到工具尖端——这与 Marshall McLuhan "媒介是人的延伸"在神经层面找到了证据。这意味着，当人类长期使用机器人代理执行任务时，我们的大脑可能真的会把机器人视为身体的一部分。届时"远程操控"和"自主行动"的伦理边界会模糊到难以辨识。

隐藏的系统性真相：物理智能公司的真正护城河不是模型，而是"通用性赌注"本身

表面上 PI 在做基础模型，但真正的战略洞察藏在 Sergey 的一句话里："我们决策的核心不是要不要三个摄像头、要不要触觉传感器，而是要保持系统的'可改进的通用性'"。

这是一个反直觉的商业洞察：绝大多数机器人公司都在赌"形态"或"应用"，而 PI 在赌"元层面的可塑性"。这就像 80 年代有人选择做专用文字处理机（王安电脑），而另一些人在做通用 PC——前者短期更有用，但被后者彻底吞噬。

更隐蔽的是 Sergey 对"演示陷阱"的清醒：整个机器人行业被"酷炫演示"绑架了 40 年。Boston Dynamics 跳后空翻的视频累积了数十亿播放，但其商业实质长期为零。这暴露了一个产业级病灶：机器人领域的研究激励、媒体注意力、资本流向都在奖励"特殊化的精彩"，而真正的进步发生在"平凡场景中的稳定泛化"——而后者根本无法在 30 秒短视频里被人类感知到。

这是一个深刻的悖论：最重要的机器人进步，注定无法被普通观众识别。直到某天它突然出现在你家厨房——而那一刻，所有人都会问"为什么这么晚？"——答案是：它一直都在，只是你看不见它。

📝 转录文本

World's Top Researcher on AI, LLMs, and Robot Intelligence

嘉宾：Sergey Levine（Physical Intelligence 联合创始人）

开场介绍

My guest today is Sergey Lavine, one of the co-founders and researchers at physical intelligence. As a disclaimer, I'm an investor in physical intelligence because I believe it's one of the most important companies tackling the problem of robotics.

As you hear us discuss today, robotics has what I would call a scarecrow problem. All of these amazing physical devices are becoming ever more possible in all sorts of cool permutations, but what they all really need is an intelligence, a brain, and that is what they're developing at physical intelligence. They are trying to develop foundation models that can make any physical robot do any task in any environment.

That challenge is daunting and has required many of the world's best researchers, Sergey as one of those leaders, coming together to try to solve this problem. The nature of our conversation today is all of the problems facing robotics and all of the promise of solving these problems across the world. I hope you enjoy this great conversation with Sergey Lavine.

一、什么是 Physical Intelligence？

This is going to be a real treat and a blast to learn about possibly the most exciting impactful area of technology being developed. Just to set the stage before we go back in time, maybe you could just define physical intelligence as you see it.

Fundamentally, the goal of physical intelligence is to develop robotic foundation models that can control basically any embodied system to do any task. But broadly speaking, you could imagine that in the same way that a language model is kind of rapidly evolving towards a system that can do any task that can be expressed in language, what we would like is to build a new class of models that can do any task that can be done by a physical actuated device.

And part of the thesis of this company is that we believe that doing it at the full level of generality might actually in the long run be easier than trying to special case very specific narrow application domains. Again, in much the same way that for language models, it turned out to be easier in some ways to solve natural language tasks in their full generality than to narrowly target like machine translation or sentiment analysis or whatever.

That may not be obvious why you would make that bet versus a robot that just does your dishes or something. So what are the key trade-offs to understand and why make the decision that you made?

Maybe I can give you like a two-part answer for this. First is how it relates kind of the analogy to language models, and the second one is what that means in the robotics world.

So the first one is kind of a little bit more informed by evidence. In the world of natural language we saw that there were a lot of efforts to develop domain specific solutions that tackled specific problems like — you know, somebody would spend a lot of time thinking about how English differs from French and then build a machine translation system. The reason that language models actually kind of took over for all of those different application domains is because they can leverage much broader sources of data.

And it's not even as simple as saying like, "oh, we had this data for this application, this data for this application, let's merge everything." It's actually more than that. When you can leverage weakly labeled data — like data that, in the case of language models, you just mine from the web — you actually learn more about the world. So you establish a foundation of world understanding, and then on top of that foundation it turns out to be much more effective to build out different applications.

So to bring this into robotics — obviously the calculus doesn't look quite the same, because in robotics we don't have an internet-size dataset that we can just draw on. But this notion of understanding the world, if anything, is actually more important in robotics. Because if you have many different tasks, maybe even many different physical systems, then you can go from training individual dishwashing specialists or laundry folding specialists, and instead train a model that actually understands physical interaction.

People can master new skills very very rapidly because we understand physical interaction. We can intuitively grasp what's going to happen in a new unfamiliar situation, and that lets us bootstrap things really really quickly. So if we can draw on data from many sources, many applications, many robots, then we can have a model that has a physical understanding, and then it'll be much much easier to put new applications on top of that platform.

二、通用方法的最大挑战：泛化无法被"演示"

What is the hardest part about building in this way for you when you see other approaches that are more maybe legible to the average person? Like "oh, there's a robot moving around doing this one specific thing, it looks a certain way." What's the hardest part about this approach as you're doing it?

I think this has actually been kind of an issue in my whole career. Because when you work on robotic learning — and the more general it gets, the more this becomes important — effective robotic learning, effective generalization, isn't actually the optimal way to have a really exciting demo.

The way to have a really exciting demo is to pick a really cool task, control everything else in the environment, set it up so that it's perfectly clean, perfectly pristine, and just make it work in that one setting. That's the way you make a robot demo. And generalization, you can't just show it in one spot. The point of generalization is that it does something relatively mundane that any human could do, but it does it in any situation.

So we had some demos that we released last April where we showed our robot cleaning kitchens. I think it's kind of cool, but if you watch an individual video out of context, it's just like, "okay, it's picking up plates, anybody can pick up plates" — except that we just put it into that home just for that demo and it never had training data from that setting. So obviously you kind of have to understand what's going on to appreciate why this is actually pushing the frontier.

三、成功意味着什么：解锁机器人的"寒武纪大爆发"

What is your model for the stakes of what you're doing? If you are successful — I'm curious for you to define what that would mean. Successful other than just "we cross this chasm of general physical intelligence." But if you cross that line, then what?

One of the things that I think would be really really exciting that would be enabled by a general purpose embodied foundation model is the ability to unlock people's imagination in how they build robots and other embodied systems.

Personal computers were a really big deal in my mind because it made it possible for lots of people to hack together all sorts of really cool stuff, and there was this kind of Cambrian explosion of amazing applications that started in like the '90s and so on, and then was further accelerated by the internet.

I think something like that might happen in the world of robotics, but it can't happen today, because if you want to put together some cool new robotics application, some cool new robotics idea, you kind of have to build this monstrous stack and you need to basically solve the intelligence problem.

But if there is a solution that someone can build on top of — there's a foundation model that you can prompt that'll provide basic functionality and then you can maybe fine-tune it a little bit or adjust it in some way to your application — now it actually makes it a lot more tractable for lots of people, lots of companies, lots of individuals to try out all sorts of different things.

We sometimes think that robots are going to be like one thing — like, "yeah, there's people, and now we're going to make metal people, and that'll be robots." But I don't think that's how it's going to be, because no technology has been like that. It's going to be more like a toolkit where you can put together all sorts of really cool applications. Get really creative with it. Maybe I'm going to make a robot with five arms and this one's going to hang from the ceiling and figure out the right thing to tackle your domain. Maybe also experiment with software. But you need the right platform on top of which to do that — and I think the foundation model can be that thing.

四、关于人形机器人的反思

What are, in your mind, the pros and cons of the humanoid approach to robotics?

One pro of it is that it's really cool. You can show it to somebody and they're like, "Yeah, I get it."

Yeah. You hear a lot about the Optimus hand.

But it is cool. I think there's a lot of value to that. There's a lot of value to capturing the imagination, and a lot of value to getting people to think about what the future might look like in a way that's understandable.

But in my mind, it's one of many possible kinds of robots that we're likely to have. And fundamentally the challenge of intelligence looks very similar for all these different robots. I don't think we should be tackling intelligence in the context of one specific body. I think we should handle it in a general way, because otherwise it's just really hard to get a handle on this. We need lots of data.

The cool thing about being able to build robots is that ultimately they don't have to be constrained to look like humans at all. You can build the right tool for the job. You could imagine that you're building a house with a robot that is a swarm of 10,000 quadcopters.

In the future we'll have a robotic foundation model which can then be adapted to all sorts of applications, and it might really run the gamut from bulldozers to humanoids to robotic arms like this thing. Maybe it would need to be adapted to each one. Maybe it would need to be fine-tuned. Maybe we would need something in context to understand how that body works. But the fundamentals of how you interact with objects, how things move in the world, how causality works — that's all conserved for all of these different systems.

Do you have a favorite example of what might be possible with true general intelligence that might not be possible with say like a humanoid only intelligence?

There are a few things worth thinking about. One is that we can make machines that are very big and machines that are very small. In the long run — this is not a short-term thing — there's lots of really exciting applications in medicine, in surgery, where we not only might in the long run not be limited to robots that look like humans, we might not be limited to robots that can even be controlled by humans.

Currently, for example, in robotic surgery, it's done entirely through tele-operations. So you need something that a person can control in real time with the right level of dexterity. That limitation holds for current learning-enabled systems too. But in the long run we could imagine addressing that.

五、机器人学的历史里程碑

If you think about the most important hash marks on the timeline of robotics research that have gotten us to here — I always think it's super helpful to set the historical context before we talk about what state is today and where we're going. Could you walk us through the relevant hashmarks on the history timeline?

At some level doing end-to-end control for robotic systems is a very very old idea. The first autonomous driving systems that used end-to-end learning existed in the 1980s — ALVINN was, I think, 1986 or 87, and that was a driving system demonstrated to drive on highways, controlled by a neural network from a camera. The neural network was tiny.

So I think there are some very venerable concepts. But historically what has been really difficult in robotic learning is that you need a system that: - Handles the application you want to address - Is cost-effective to train (you don't need a huge amount of data for every single application) - Handles long-tail scenarios with common sense - For the thing it's actually supposed to do, is robust, fast, and reliable

Getting all those things together is very very hard. With machine learning, it works best when there's a lot of data. So if you naively approach a robotic problem and say "I want to do washing dishes," the obvious thing is to collect an enormous amount of data of washing dishes. But that's not cost-effective, because then you go on to the next application and you go through that process all over again.

So being able to train general-purpose models that can handle many tasks is essential, because now you need a lot less data for each new task. But then even further — and this is the thing that has probably changed the most in the last few years — you also then need to handle the unusual scenarios.

For unusual scenarios, you are probably not going to have experience. What you need to rely on is knowledge that you've acquired from other sources that you can ground in that new situation. People are extremely good at this. If you're driving a car and there's something going on in the middle of the road and someone put up a sign saying "don't go here, there's a gas leak" — you've probably never experienced that before, but you can put these things together and figure out what you're supposed to do because you have common sense.

This has been a huge mystery in the robotic learning world: where do you get that common sense? This is what's changed in the last few years, because it turns out that multimodal language models are really good at pulling in knowledge and articulating that knowledge. They're not very good at grounding that knowledge in physical situations, but they know stuff. So now there is a path to get that kind of common sense by leveraging the knowledge that is contained in multimodal LLMs — but there's also a challenge, because you have to somehow plug into that knowledge in the right way.

You can't just show it a picture and say "what would you do here," because it doesn't have the context. It doesn't know that you are a robot, this is what you look like, this is what's going on. So that's a technological challenge. We've made some headway in addressing it; the research community in general has. But most importantly, it's kind of that light at the end of the tunnel of "okay, now we have this way of pulling in lots of knowledge which can help us handle those long-tail scenarios."

关键里程碑

Are there hashmark equivalents on the timeline of like AlexNet or the transformer? Are there big events that you think everyone will point to when writing the history books about this?

That's a good question. I think it's very early on right now to answer that definitively. Certainly you have to look back at least 10 years to have something like that. Probably the first end-to-end learning systems in the 80s, that's definitely a milestone. I think the first deep reinforcement learning systems in the early 2010s, those are probably a milestone — because deep reinforcement learning gives us a way to go beyond human-level performance, which I think will be essential for robotic systems.

And then there's the more recent stuff, but that's in the last few years. I don't know how that's going to shake out as far as whether that's something people will point to. But I do think the advent of multimodal LLMs that can be adapted to robotic control to bring in that common sense — I do think that's a really important advance. We're probably going to see quite a few important advances in the next few years, and maybe those will be the things people point to.

六、Sergey 的个人研究历程

Can you tell us your own personal history of approaching the problem? The origin of when you first became interested and why, and then how you've decided what to spend your personal time and attention on ever since?

I started working in robotics in 2014. That was actually after I finished my graduate degree and started a postdoc with Professor Pieter Abbeel at UC Berkeley. I actually hadn't worked on robots before, but I figured I should get a little bit more education after finishing my degree. His lab worked on robots, so I tried to apply what I had learned previously to robotics. Before that, I worked on computer graphics actually.

The thing that I've always wanted to really figure out is how to get AI systems that get better and better the more they do things, because I think that's tremendously powerful. If you can have a system that gets better and better the more it does something and it just keeps getting better, then there's sort of no limit. Then it can master all the skills you wanted to do.

Initially I tried to approach it in a very blank-slate way — meaning you start with nothing, you practice a particular skill, and you get better at that skill. That was kind of okay; you can do that in a limited setting and you get something that works, but it's very hard to turn that into a general system that can work in open-world settings. Because if I practice something over here and then it goes over there, now something is different and it needs to practice all over again.

The next thing I tried — when I worked at Google afterwards — I tried to see if we can do that, but now parallelize it across many robots. Collective learning: can you put 20 robots in a room and have them all learn together? That works, and it generalizes, but it's very hard for that to handle these tail cases, these edge cases. Because now it becomes this kind of savant of this particular task, and that's all it knows in the world.

The next step, what I mentioned before, is combining this ability to practice skills with lots of prior knowledge. That's actually a really really hard problem. It's not just in robotics where it's a hard problem; I think it's a hard problem in all of AI. Because arguably the two big impressive results in AI over the last few decades have been generative AI and deep reinforcement learning. If you want a single example to epitomize this — generative AI is LLMs, deep reinforcement learning is AlphaGo. They're both very impressive, and they're impressive for very different reasons.

Generative AI is impressive because it can reproduce some of the things that humans can do — it can draw pictures that look like human pictures, write text. DRL is impressive for the opposite reason: it does things humans hadn't thought of, like move 37.

Exactly.

So the big challenge — and this is what I hope we'll figure out here at physical intelligence — is how to combine those threads. How to bring in all of that knowledge that you get with generative AI but also go beyond just human-level performance with reinforcement learning. I haven't figured out yet, but I think we've made some good progress on that.

七、PI 的具体技术路径：VLA 模型 + 思维链 + 强化学习

So what literally have you done and are you doing to make that happen?

In the past few years we started off first by developing the basic foundations. The basic foundation is what's called a vision-language-action model. A vision-language-action model you can think of as an LLM that has been adapted for robotic control. The way these things are trained is they're first trained on text data, then they're adapted with lots of image data from the web to understand images, and then they're adapted to robots with lots of very diverse robot data.

That's a starting point. That's a way to take all of that web knowledge, get it into a model that can control robots, and get some interesting behaviors out of it. From there, we studied two threads: 1. How to get this thing to handle unusual situations with common sense 2. How to get it to improve with reinforcement learning

The way you get common sense is by essentially using chain of thought. The robot enters a scene and instead of directly starting to move, it thinks about what it was asked to do. So if it was told "clean up the kitchen," it looks at the scene and says, "okay, based on this I should pick up the plate." It literally talks to itself. It says "pick up the plate," and then it goes and does it. That unlocks all this prior knowledge, because those intermediate inferences benefit from the web-scale pre-train. That handles edge cases.

The reinforcement learning part comes in after you've practiced it a few times — you can keep getting better and better at the task directly through your experience. For example, we had this demo on making espresso. That system practiced making those espressos many many times and used that to improve robustness, improve speed, improve throughput. We're not done with that — there's a lot more to do — but we have the starting point.
